questions.question: Joseph Smith's First Vision — The Foundation of the Restoration
questions.answer:
In the spring of 1820, a fourteen-year-old boy named Joseph Smith went into a grove of trees near his home in upstate New York to pray. He had been troubled by the religious confusion of his day — different churches all claiming to teach the truth, yet contradicting each other. He turned to the Bible, where he read in James 1:5: "If any of you lack wisdom, let him ask of God, that giveth to all men liberally, and upbraideth not; and it shall be given him." That promise settled into his heart. He decided to ask God which church was right.

When he knelt and began to pray, a thick darkness gathered around him — a force he described as almost overwhelming. He prayed through it. Then a pillar of light descended, brighter than the noon sun, and he saw two Personages standing above him in the air. One of them spoke, pointing to the other, and said: "This is My Beloved Son. Hear Him!"

Joseph asked which church to join. He was told to join none of them — that they had all gone astray from the original gospel. He was also told that his sins were forgiven, and that in time the fulness of the gospel would be made known to him.

This event answers three questions that Christianity had debated for centuries. First: Does God hear and answer individual prayer? Yes — He came Himself. Second: Are the Father and the Son separate, distinct Beings? Yes — Joseph saw them both. Third: Is the original Church of Christ still on the earth? No — it had been lost, and a restoration was needed.

The Bible itself prepares us for this kind of vision. Stephen saw the Father and the Son standing at God's right hand just before he was martyred. Acts 7:55–56 Paul was caught up into visions and revelations of the Lord. 2 Corinthians 12:1 The God of the Bible speaks, appears, and calls prophets. Joseph Smith's experience fits the biblical pattern completely.

The First Vision is the foundation of the entire restoration. If it happened, everything else follows. The Book of Mormon, the priesthood, the temples, the living prophet — all of it rests on that morning in the grove. Joseph Smith did not build a religion on philosophy or tradition. He built it on a personal visit from God the Father and His Son Jesus Christ.

missionary.simple: In 1820, fourteen-year-old Joseph Smith prayed to know which church was true. God the Father and Jesus Christ appeared to him in person — two separate, glorified Beings. This is the foundation of the restored Church.
missionary.explain: 'The Father pointed to the Son and said: This is My Beloved Son — Hear Him. Joseph did not see a vision of one person. He saw two. The nature of God was clarified in that single moment, and the need for a restoration was confirmed.'
missionary.why: If God appeared to Joseph Smith, then the restored gospel is real and the Church of Jesus Christ is exactly what it claims to be. This is the question everything else depends on.
missionary.invite: Would you read Joseph Smith's account in Joseph Smith History chapter 1 and ask God in prayer whether it is true?

questions.question: The Family: A Proclamation to the World
questions.answer:
We, the First Presidency and the Council of the Twelve Apostles of The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints, solemnly proclaim that marriage between a man and a woman is ordained of God and that the family is central to the Creator's plan for the eternal destiny of His children.

All human beings — male and female — are created in the image of God. Each is a beloved spirit son or daughter of heavenly parents, and, as such, each has a divine nature and destiny. Gender is an essential characteristic of individual premortal, mortal, and eternal identity and purpose.

In the premortal realm, spirit sons and daughters knew and worshipped God as their Eternal Father and accepted His plan by which His children could obtain a physical body and gain earthly experience to progress toward perfection and ultimately realize their divine destiny as heirs of eternal life. The divine plan of happiness enables family relationships to be perpetuated beyond the grave. Sacred ordinances and covenants available in holy temples make it possible for individuals to return to the presence of God and for families to be united eternally.

The first commandment that God gave to Adam and Eve pertained to their potential for parenthood as husband and wife. We declare that God's commandment for His children to multiply and replenish the earth remains in force. We further declare that God has commanded that the sacred powers of procreation are to be employed only between man and woman, lawfully wedded as husband and wife.

We declare the means by which mortal life is created to be divinely appointed. We affirm the sanctity of life and of its importance in God's eternal plan.

Husband and wife have a solemn responsibility to love and care for each other and for their children. "Children are an heritage of the Lord" Psalms 127:3. Parents have a sacred duty to rear their children in love and righteousness, to provide for their physical and spiritual needs, and to teach them to love and serve one another, observe the commandments of God, and be law-abiding citizens wherever they live. Husbands and wives — mothers and fathers — will be held accountable before God for the discharge of these obligations.

The family is ordained of God. Marriage between man and woman is essential to His eternal plan. Children are entitled to birth within the bonds of matrimony, and to be reared by a father and a mother who honour marital vows with complete fidelity. Happiness in family life is most likely to be achieved when founded upon the teachings of the Lord Jesus Christ. Successful marriages and families are established and maintained on principles of faith, prayer, repentance, forgiveness, respect, love, compassion, work, and wholesome recreational activities. By divine design, fathers are to preside over their families in love and righteousness and are responsible to provide the necessities of life and protection for their families. Mothers are primarily responsible for the nurture of their children. In these sacred responsibilities, fathers and mothers are obligated to help one another as equal partners. Disability, death, or other circumstances may necessitate individual adaptation. Extended families should lend support when needed.

We warn that individuals who violate covenants of chastity, who abuse spouse or offspring, or who fail to fulfil family responsibilities will one day stand accountable before God. Further, we warn that the disintegration of the family will bring upon individuals, communities, and nations the calamities foretold by ancient and modern prophets.

We call upon responsible citizens and officers of government everywhere to promote those measures designed to maintain and strengthen the family as the fundamental unit of society.

Proclaimed by the First Presidency and Quorum of the Twelve Apostles of The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints, September 23, 1995.

missionary.simple: The Family Proclamation is an official declaration by the First Presidency and Quorum of the Twelve that marriage between a man and a woman is ordained of God and that the family is central to His eternal plan.
missionary.explain: 'God did not leave the definition of family to culture or politics. He declared it through living apostles and prophets. This is His word on the matter — and it has never changed.'
missionary.why: The family is the most important organization in time and eternity. Understanding God's design for it changes how you see every relationship in your life.
missionary.invite: Would you like to read the Family Proclamation and ask God whether it reflects His true design for families?

questions.question: The Living Christ: The Testimony of the Apostles
questions.answer:
As we commemorate the birth of Jesus Christ two millennia ago, we offer our testimony of the reality of His matchless life and the infinite virtue of His great atoning sacrifice. None other has had so profound an influence upon all who have lived and will yet live upon the earth.

He was the Great Jehovah of the Old Testament, the Messiah of the New. Under the direction of His Father, He was the creator of the earth. "All things were made by him; and without him was not any thing made that was made" John 1:3. Though sinless, He was baptized to fulfil all righteousness. He "went about doing good" Acts 10:38, yet was despised for it. His gospel was a message of peace and goodwill. He entreated all to follow His example. He walked the roads of Palestine, healing the sick, causing the blind to see, and raising the dead. He taught the truths of eternity, the reality of our premortal existence, the purpose of our life on earth, and the potential for the sons and daughters of God in the life to come.

He instituted the sacrament as a reminder of His great atoning sacrifice. He was arrested and condemned on spurious charges, convicted to satisfy a mob, and sentenced to die on Calvary's cross. He gave His life to atone for the sins of all mankind. His was a great vicarious gift in behalf of all who would ever live upon the earth.

We solemnly testify that His life, which is central to all human history, neither began in Bethlehem nor concluded on Calvary. He was the Firstborn of the Father, the Only Begotten Son in the flesh, the Redeemer of the world.

He rose from the grave to "become the firstfruits of them that slept" 1 Corinthians 15:20. As Risen Lord, He visited among those He had loved in life. He also ministered among His "other sheep" John 10:16 in ancient America. In the modern world, He and His Father appeared to the boy Joseph Smith, ushering in the long-promised "dispensation of the fulness of times" Ephesians 1:10.

Of the Living Christ, the Prophet Joseph wrote: "His eyes were as a flame of fire; the hair of his head was white like the pure snow; his countenance shone above the brightness of the sun; and his voice was as the sound of the rushing of great waters, even the voice of Jehovah, saying: I am the first and the last; I am he who liveth, I am he who was slain; I am your advocate with the Father" (D&C 110:3–4).

Of Him the Prophet also declared: "And now, after the many testimonies which have been given of him, this is the testimony, last of all, which we give of him: That he lives! For we saw him, even on the right hand of God; and we heard the voice bearing record that he is the Only Begotten of the Father" (D&C 76:22–23).

We declare in words of solemnity that His priesthood and His Church have been restored upon the earth — "built upon the foundation of apostles and prophets, Jesus Christ himself being the chief corner stone" Ephesians 2:20. We testify that He will someday return to earth. "And the glory of the Lord shall be revealed, and all flesh shall see it together" Isaiah 40:5. He will rule as King of Kings and reign as Lord of Lords, and every knee shall bend and every tongue shall speak in worship before Him. Each of us will stand to be judged of Him according to our works and the desires of our hearts.

We bear testimony, as His duly ordained Apostles — that Jesus is the Living Christ, the immortal Son of God. He is the great King Immanuel, who stands today on the right hand of His Father. He is the light, the life, and the hope of the world. His way is the path that leads to happiness in this life and eternal life in the world to come. God be thanked for the matchless gift of His divine Son.

The First Presidency and Quorum of the Twelve Apostles of The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints, January 1, 2000.

missionary.simple: The Living Christ is the official testimony of the current First Presidency and Quorum of the Twelve Apostles that Jesus Christ is literally alive today — the immortal Son of God.
missionary.explain: 'These are not ancient words. These are living apostles — men we sustain as witnesses of Christ today — declaring under their own names that He lives. That testimony carries the same weight as Peter's on the day of Pentecost.'
missionary.why: If Jesus Christ is literally alive today, everything He taught and everything His restored church offers is real and available to you right now.
missionary.invite: Would you read the Living Christ testimony and ask God whether Jesus Christ is truly alive and whether He knows you personally?

questions.question: 0. What are the general beliefs of The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints? — The Articles of Faith
questions.answer:
Written in 1842 by the Prophet Joseph Smith, these 13 statements explain the basic doctrines and practices of The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints.

1. We believe in God, the Eternal Father, and in His Son, Jesus Christ, and in the Holy Ghost.

2. We believe that men will be punished for their own sins, and not for Adam's transgression.

3. We believe that through the Atonement of Christ, all mankind may be saved, by obedience to the laws and ordinances of the Gospel.

4. We believe that the first principles and ordinances of the Gospel are: first, Faith in the Lord Jesus Christ; second, Repentance; third, Baptism by immersion for the remission of sins; fourth, Laying on of hands for the gift of the Holy Ghost.

5. We believe that a man must be called of God, by prophecy, and by the laying on of hands by those who are in authority, to preach the Gospel and administer in the ordinances thereof.

6. We believe in the same organization that existed in the Primitive Church, namely, apostles, prophets, pastors, teachers, evangelists, and so forth.

7. We believe in the gift of tongues, prophecy, revelation, visions, healing, interpretation of tongues, and so forth.

8. We believe the Bible to be the word of God as far as it is translated correctly; we also believe the Book of Mormon to be the word of God.

9. We believe all that God has revealed, all that He does now reveal, and we believe that He will yet reveal many great and important things pertaining to the Kingdom of God.

10. We believe in the literal gathering of Israel and in the restoration of the Ten Tribes; that Zion (the New Jerusalem) will be built upon the American continent; that Christ will reign personally upon the earth; and, that the earth will be renewed and receive its paradisiacal glory.

11. We claim the privilege of worshiping Almighty God according to the dictates of our own conscience, and allow all men the same privilege, let them worship how, where, or what they may.

12. We believe in being subject to kings, presidents, rulers, and magistrates, in obeying, honoring, and sustaining the law.

13. We believe in being honest, true, chaste, benevolent, virtuous, and in doing good to all men; indeed, we may say that we follow the admonition of Paul — We believe all things, we hope all things, we have endured many things, and hope to be able to endure all things. If there is anything virtuous, lovely, or of good report or praiseworthy, we seek after these things.

missionary.simple: The Articles of Faith are thirteen statements written by Joseph Smith in 1842 that explain the basic doctrines and practices of the restored Church of Jesus Christ.
missionary.explain: 'Each article connects directly to the Bible. We believe in God, in Christ, in the Holy Ghost — as separate beings. We believe in the same organization as the primitive church. We believe the Bible and the Book of Mormon are both the word of God.'
missionary.why: The Articles of Faith give any sincere person a clear, honest summary of what we actually believe — with nothing hidden and nothing to be embarrassed about.
missionary.invite: Would you read the Articles of Faith and ask God whether they describe a church that He would restore in these latter days?

questions.question: 5. Why does your church baptize children at age 8? Isn't that too young to choose?
questions.answer:
The Church does not baptize infants. Baptism is done at the age of eight — the age of accountability — when a child is old enough to understand right from wrong, to feel genuine remorse, and to make a real choice before God.

Baptism is for the remission of sins. Acts 2:38 Paul wrote that “where there is no law, there is no transgression.” Romans 4:15 Little children live under no such law. They carry no sin. Christ Himself took little children in His arms and blessed them — He did not baptize them. If a child has no sin, there is nothing to remit. Baptizing an infant contradicts what baptism is actually for.

Peter said, “Repent and be baptized.” Acts 2:38 Repentance comes before baptism. And repentance requires understanding. A baby cannot repent. An eight-year-old can. Christ said, “He that believeth and is baptized shall be saved.” Mark 16:16 Belief comes first. Eight is old enough to believe, old enough to choose, old enough to enter a covenant with God.

Mormon said it plainly: “Little children are whole, for they are not capable of committing sin.” Moroni 8:8 And: “He that supposeth that little children need baptism is in the gall of bitterness.” Moroni 8:14 Eight is not too young to choose. It is exactly the age when a real choice becomes possible.

missionary.simple: We baptize at age 8 because children under that age are innocent before God — they have no sin. Baptism is for the forgiveness of sins, so a sinless child does not need it yet.
missionary.explain: Ask them: Can a baby choose to sin? No. So baptizing a baby makes no sense. At age 8, a child begins to understand right from wrong and can make a real choice.
missionary.why: It shows that God is fair. He does not hold children accountable until they are old enough to understand.
missionary.invite: Does that make sense to you? Would you like to know what baptism means for you personally?

questions.question: 22. Why do we perform baptism for the dead if they are already dead?
questions.answer:
Paul asked it himself: “Else what shall they do which are baptized for the dead, if the dead rise not at all? Why are they then baptized for the dead?”1 Corinthians 15:29 Paul did not condemn this practice. He used it as evidence of the resurrection. The saints were doing it, and Paul pointed to it as proof of their belief in eternal life.

Christ told Nicodemus, “Except a man be born of water and of the Spirit, he cannot enter into the kingdom of God.”John 3:5 That is a universal requirement. But millions of God's children died before hearing the gospel. A just God does not hold people accountable for failing to obey a law they never had the chance to hear.

Peter taught that Christ, after His crucifixion, went and preached to “the spirits in prison.”1 Peter 3:18–20 And 1 Peter 4:6 states that "the gospel was preached also to them that are dead." The dead can hear the gospel in the spirit world. But baptism is an ordinance of the flesh — it must be performed in the body. So we, the living, act as proxy for those who died without that opportunity.

Hebrews 11:40 makes it plain: "God having provided some better thing for us, that they without us should not be made perfect." They need us. We need them. This is the work that ties all generations together.

Joseph Smith received further light on this doctrine through revelation. In the Doctrine and Covenants, the Lord declared: "The greatest responsibility in this world that God has laid upon us is to seek after our dead."D&C 128:15 And again: "They without us cannot be made perfect — neither can we without our dead be made perfect."D&C 128:18 The living and the dead need each other. This is the doctrine of sealing — binding generations together.

🕮 Scripture Evidence: Spirit World & Salvation for the Dead

  • 1 Corinthians 15:29 — “Else what shall they do which are baptized for the dead, if the dead rise not at all? why are they then baptized for the dead?”
  • 1 Peter 3:18-20 — Christ was “put to death in the flesh, but quickened by the Spirit: By which also he went and preached unto the spirits in prison.”
  • 1 Peter 4:6 — “For for this cause was the gospel preached also to them that are dead, that they might be judged according to men in the flesh, but live according to God in the spirit.”
  • Luke 16:22-23 — The beggar Lazarus was carried to Abraham’s bosom after death — showing distinct realms in the afterlife.
  • Luke 23:43 — Jesus tells the thief: “To day shalt thou be with me in paradise.”
  • Ecclesiastes 12:7 — “The spirit shall return unto God who gave it.”
  • Philippians 1:23 — Paul expresses desire “to depart, and to be with Christ” — confirming conscious spirit world existence.
  • Hebrews 12:23 — References “the spirits of just men made perfect” — confirming ongoing spirit progression.

missionary.simple: Because the dead deserve the same chance to accept the gospel as the living. We perform the ordinance on their behalf, but they choose freely whether to accept it.
missionary.explain: Think of it like signing a permission form on behalf of someone who could not sign it themselves. The choice to honor it still belongs to them.
missionary.why: God loves every person who ever lived — not just those born in the right time or place. This work makes salvation available to all.
missionary.invite: Do you have ancestors you love who may not have heard the gospel? Would you like to learn how this work connects you to them?

questions.question: 25. Do people need to be rebaptized before joining the church? Isn't that wrong?
questions.answer:
Paul addresses this directly in Acts 19:1–6. He found disciples in Ephesus who had been baptized — but only with the baptism of John. John held the Aaronic Priesthood — the lesser, preparatory priesthood. That authority allowed him to baptize for repentance, but it did not include the power to confer the gift of the Holy Ghost. That required the Melchizedek Priesthood, which the apostles of Jesus Christ held. When he asked them if they had received the Holy Ghost since they believed, they had not. Paul then baptized them again — in the name of the Lord Jesus — and laid his hands on them, and they received the Holy Ghost.

The issue is not the sincerity of the person being baptized. It is the priesthood authority of the person performing the baptism. Christ said, “As my Father hath sent me, even so send I you.”John 20:21 No man takes this honour upon himself. "He that is called of God, as was Aaron" — Aaron was called by Moses, a prophet. Hebrews 5:4 He did not call himself.

In Acts 8, Philip baptized many in Samaria. But when the apostles heard, they came and laid hands on them — because the gift of the Holy Ghost had not yet been given. Authority is specific. It is not transferred by reading a book or attending a seminary.

We therefore rebaptize not because a person's previous faith was insincere, but because baptism performed without divine authority is not recognized of God. This is the pattern established in the New Testament by the apostles themselves.

Christ himself established the precise form and authority of baptism when He appeared in the Americas. He taught: "Whoso believeth in me, and is baptized, the same shall be saved; and they are they who shall inherit the kingdom of God."3 Nephi 11:33 He then gave His disciples specific authority and words to use in baptism — the same pattern restored through Joseph Smith. 3 Nephi 11:23–26 Authority was always part of the covenant.

missionary.simple: Because the authority behind a baptism matters. A baptism performed without God's proper authority is sincere — but not recognized by God. Paul rebaptized people for exactly this reason.
missionary.explain: Imagine signing a legal document with someone who had no authority to notarize it. The signature is real, but the document is not valid. Authority matters.
missionary.why: We are not saying your previous faith was wrong. We are offering you the complete ordinance — the same way Paul did for those disciples in Ephesus.
missionary.invite: Would you be willing to read Acts 19:1–6 and pray about what it means for you?

questions.question: 26. Why does your church require confirmation after baptism? Isn't baptism enough?
questions.answer:
Baptism and confirmation are two separate ordinances, and the scriptures treat them that way. In Acts 8:12–17, Philip baptized many people in Samaria. Then Peter and John came down from Jerusalem and laid their hands on them — because they had "as yet fallen upon none of them" — meaning the Holy Ghost had not yet been given. Baptism had already been performed. But something else was still needed.

Paul listed both in Hebrews 6:1–2 as foundational principles of the gospel — "the doctrine of baptisms" and "laying on of hands." He listed them separately because they are separate. One without the other is incomplete.

Christ himself was baptized by John in the water and then received the Holy Ghost descending upon Him like a dove. Matthew 3:16 Both happened. Both were necessary. We follow that same pattern.

John the Baptist said it plainly: “I indeed baptize you with water unto repentance: but he that cometh after me... shall baptize you with the Holy Ghost.”Matthew 3:11 There are two baptisms — one of water and one of the Spirit. We administer both, exactly as the primitive church did.

The resurrected Christ confirmed this same pattern when He ministered to the Nephites. After baptism, He commanded His disciples to confer the Holy Ghost by the laying on of hands. 3 Nephi 18:36–37 The Doctrine and Covenants also gives the exact words to be used in confirmation: "Receive the Holy Ghost" — pronounced by one holding the Melchizedek Priesthood. D&C 20:41 Both ordinances are required. Neither is optional.

missionary.simple: Because baptism and receiving the Holy Ghost are two separate gifts. Water baptism cleanses you. The laying on of hands gives you the Holy Ghost as a constant companion.
missionary.explain: When Philip baptized people in Samaria, the apostles still had to come and lay hands on them to give the Holy Ghost. Two different acts — both necessary.
missionary.why: The Holy Ghost is God's personal guide for your life. Without confirmation, you only have half of what God wants to give you.
missionary.invite: Would you like to learn what the gift of the Holy Ghost has meant in the lives of members you could speak with?

questions.question:
49. Why does your church claim authority through Joseph Smith when no one can verify that John the Baptist or Peter, James, and John
physically appeared to him?
questions.answer:
No one could verify that Christ appeared to Paul either — and yet Paul's apostolic authority is widely accepted across Christianity. He saw a light. He heard a voice. He reported an experience that no third party witnessed. And the entire Christian world has built on his testimony.

John the Baptist was beheaded. Matthew 14:10 He died before the resurrection. Moses and Elijah appeared on the Mount of Transfiguration as spiritual beings — men long deceased yet present and speaking with the living Christ. Matthew 17:3 Spiritual appearances are not new in scripture. The Lord "appeared" to Abraham, to Moses, to Isaiah, to Ezekiel. These were not public events with notarized witnesses.

The test is not whether the experience was publicly observed. The test Moses gave us is this: Does the man speak in the name of the Lord, and do his words come to pass? Deuteronomy 18:21–22 The prophecy of the Civil War came to pass. The prophecy of the Rocky Mountains came to pass. The organization of a church with apostles and prophets, the bringing forth of scripture, the gathering of Israel — all of it is being fulfilled.

The authority of Joseph Smith is established the same way the authority of Paul is established — by the fruits. “By their fruits ye shall know them.”Matthew 7:16 No other man in this dispensation has produced what Joseph Smith produced. And no other church on earth claims the authority that was restored through him.

missionary.simple: Because the same question was raised about Paul — no one witnessed his call on the road to Damascus either. Prophets are known by their fruits, not by who was watching.
missionary.explain: Paul's authority was accepted across Christianity even though no committee verified it. The test is the same: what did Joseph Smith produce? Fulfilled prophecy. Restored scripture. Millions of changed lives.
missionary.why: God has always called prophets in private, through personal vision. That pattern has never changed.
missionary.invite: Would you be willing to read about Joseph Smith's experience and ask God directly if he was a prophet?

questions.question: 69. Why do you teach that little children who die before baptism are saved? The Bible says all must be born again.
questions.answer:
Paul wrote plainly: “As by one man sin entered into the world... even so by the righteousness of one the free gift came upon all men unto justification of life.”Romans 5:18 The sin of Adam — which is physical death — was paid for by Christ for all. No child is condemned for the sin of Adam. Christ paid that debt for every soul.

Paul further wrote, “Where there is no law, there is no transgression.”Romans 4:15 A child who has not yet reached the age of accountability has no law. He has no transgressions. He has nothing to repent of. Baptism is for remission of sins. A sinless child has no sins to remit.

Christ himself said, "Of such is the kingdom of heaven," when speaking of little children. Matthew 19:14 He did not say "of such, once they are baptized." He said "of such is the kingdom." Present tense. As they are. Pure. Innocent. Covered by His atonement without condition.

The God described in the Bible is a God of justice and mercy — and little children are covered by His atonement without condition. God is just. He is merciful. And little children who die before accountability are in His arms — fully redeemed by the blood of Christ, without need of any ordinance.

The Book of Mormon makes this explicit. Mormon wrote: "Little children cannot repent; wherefore, it is awful wickedness to deny the pure mercies of God unto them, for they are all alive in him because of his mercy."Moroni 8:19 And the Lord Himself confirmed through Joseph Smith that children who die before the age of accountability are saved in the celestial kingdom. D&C 137:10 No child is lost.

missionary.simple: Little children who die before age 8 are fully saved by Christ's atonement — automatically, with no ordinance needed. They are pure and innocent.
missionary.explain: Jesus said of little children: 'of such is the kingdom of heaven.' He did not say 'of such, once they are baptized.' They are already His.
missionary.why: This is one of the most comforting doctrines of the restored gospel. No child is lost. God is perfectly just and perfectly merciful.
missionary.invite: Does that bring you peace? Would you like to know more about what the restored gospel teaches about God's love for all people?

questions.question: 70. Why do you practice laying on of hands for healing? Is that really still valid today?
questions.answer:
James wrote it clearly: “Is any sick among you? let him call for the elders of the church; and let them pray over him, anointing him with oil in the name of the Lord: And the prayer of faith shall save the sick, and the Lord shall raise him up.”James 5:14–15 That is not a suggestion. It is an instruction. It is in the Bible now, in your hands, and it has never been repealed.

Mark 16:17–18 records Christ's promise: "These signs shall follow them that believe... they shall lay hands on the sick, and they shall recover." Christ himself said this. If the signs do not follow in a church, something is missing — not the promise, but the faith and the authority.

The apostles healed. Peter healed the lame man at the gate of the temple. Acts 3:6–7 Paul raised Eutychus from the dead. Acts 20:9–12 These were not isolated performances. They were the regular operation of the Church in the power of the Holy Ghost.

We have elders today who anoint the sick with oil, lay their hands upon them, and call down the blessings of heaven by the authority of the Melchizedek Priesthood. We do not announce it. We do not publicize it. We do not turn it into a performance. But it happens — daily — among the members of this Church. And the Lord honours it, because the priesthood authority to perform it was restored through His prophet.

missionary.simple: Yes, healing by the laying on of hands is real and practiced today. James commanded it. Christ promised it. We follow the same pattern as the New Testament church.
missionary.explain: This is not a ceremony. Elders with the Melchizedek Priesthood anoint with oil, lay hands on the person's head, and call on God by priesthood authority. It happens regularly among our members.
missionary.why: Signs follow those who believe. A church without this gift is missing something the original church had.
missionary.invite: Have you ever experienced or witnessed a blessing of healing? Would you like to know more about the priesthood authority that makes this possible?

questions.question: 76. Why do you believe the priesthood must be conferred by the laying on of hands? Can't God give it directly?
questions.answer:
God can do whatever He chooses. But the pattern He has consistently chosen is to work through order, through authority, and through appointed servants. He could have given the Law to Israel directly — but He gave it through Moses. He could have sent the Holy Ghost without the apostles — but He sent it through the laying on of their hands. Acts 8:17 He could have ordained Aaron without Moses — but He ordained Aaron through Moses. Exodus 28:1

Paul wrote that “no man taketh this honour unto himself, but he that is called of God, as was Aaron.”Hebrews 5:4 Aaron did not receive his priesthood from God directly. He received it through Moses — a prophet who had received his authority from God through an appointed line. The authority flows through people, by the laying on of hands, in an unbroken chain from Christ to His apostles to those they ordained.

That chain was broken during the apostasy. When the last apostle died without passing his authority to a successor, the chain was cut. Sincerity, education, and devotion are precious — but they cannot restore an authority that was lost. Only God could do that. Only God can do that — by sending messengers with the authority to restore it. And that is exactly what He did, through John the Baptist and through Peter, James, and John, who conferred that authority upon Joseph Smith.

missionary.simple: Because that is God's pattern in every dispensation. Authority flows from God through appointed servants — not from self-appointment or reading a book.
missionary.explain: Aaron did not decide to be a priest. Moses, a prophet, ordained him. The same chain — God to prophet to ordained servant — has never changed.
missionary.why: Without proper authority, ordinances are sincere but not binding before God. Authority is what makes them real.
missionary.invite: Would you like to know how the priesthood authority was restored to the earth today and who holds it?

questions.question: 90. Why do you perform ordinances by proxy for the dead? Isn't it wrong to make religious decisions for others?
questions.answer:
We do not make decisions for others. We provide them an opportunity. The dead are not compelled to accept proxy baptism any more than the living are compelled to accept missionary teaching. In the spirit world, they hear. They consider. They choose. God does not override agency in the spirit world any more than He overrides it here. 1 Peter 4:6

What we do provide is the ordinance. Baptism must be performed in the flesh — it is a physical act. The dead cannot perform it for themselves. So we act as proxies — giving them the ordinance so that if they choose to accept it, the required covenant has been made on their behalf and in their name.

Paul wrote in Hebrews 11:39–40 that the faithful dead "received not the promise... that they without us should not be made perfect." They need something from us. We provide it through proxy ordinance work. They are not made perfect without our participation.

This is the most just doctrine in all of religion. No person is held accountable for not receiving a gospel they never heard. No one is sent to eternal darkness because they were born in the wrong century or the wrong country. God gives every soul an equal opportunity — in this life or in the next. The decision remains theirs. The opportunity is His gift to all.

This doctrine was revealed in full through Joseph Smith. The Doctrine and Covenants records that in the spirit world, "the dead who repent will be redeemed, through obedience to the ordinances of the house of God."D&C 138:58 President Joseph F. Smith saw in vision that the dead are taught the gospel by faithful missionaries — and given the same opportunity to accept or reject it as the living. D&C 138:30–34 This is God's mercy operating with perfect justice.

🕮 Scripture Evidence: Spirit World & Salvation for the Dead

  • 1 Corinthians 15:29 — “Else what shall they do which are baptized for the dead, if the dead rise not at all? why are they then baptized for the dead?”
  • 1 Peter 3:18-20 — Christ was “put to death in the flesh, but quickened by the Spirit: By which also he went and preached unto the spirits in prison.”
  • 1 Peter 4:6 — “For for this cause was the gospel preached also to them that are dead, that they might be judged according to men in the flesh, but live according to God in the spirit.”
  • Luke 16:22-23 — The beggar Lazarus was carried to Abraham’s bosom after death — showing distinct realms in the afterlife.
  • Luke 23:43 — Jesus tells the thief: “To day shalt thou be with me in paradise.”
  • Ecclesiastes 12:7 — “The spirit shall return unto God who gave it.”
  • Philippians 1:23 — Paul expresses desire “to depart, and to be with Christ” — confirming conscious spirit world existence.
  • Hebrews 12:23 — References “the spirits of just men made perfect” — confirming ongoing spirit progression.

missionary.simple: We do not make decisions for the dead. We give them an opportunity. They freely choose whether to accept the ordinance performed for them.
missionary.explain: Think of it as opening a door for someone who was locked out through no fault of their own. They still decide whether to walk through it.
missionary.why: This is the most just doctrine in all of religion. No one is condemned for missing a gospel they never had the chance to hear.
missionary.invite: Would you like to learn how you can participate in this work for your own ancestors?

questions.question: 94. Why do you teach that the Holy Ghost can be permanently given by the laying on of hands? In the Bible it came and went freely.
questions.answer:
In the Old Testament, the Spirit of God would come upon individuals for specific purposes and depart again. Samuel anointed Saul and “the Spirit of God came upon him.”1 Samuel 10:10 Later it departed. 1 Samuel 16:14 That was the Aaronic, preparatory administration of the Spirit — not the gift of the Holy Ghost as Christ established it.

Christ promised His disciples something permanent and different. “I will pray the Father, and he shall give you another Comforter, that he may abide with you for ever.”John 14:16 Abide with you forever. That is not a temporary visitation. That is a permanent gift.

The gift of the Holy Ghost — as distinct from the influence of the Spirit — was made available after Christ's resurrection through the authority of the apostleship. Before His ascension He breathed on the disciples and said, “Receive ye the Holy Ghost.”John 20:22 After His ascension the apostles conferred it by the laying on of hands. Acts 8:17 The gift is permanent — available to those who receive it and remain worthy of it.

The condition is faithfulness. Those who grieve the Holy Spirit can lose the gift. Ephesians 4:30 But the gift itself, conferred properly by proper authority, is not a temporary event. It is a permanent endowment — the constant companionship of a member of the Godhead as a guide, a witness, and a comforter throughout this life.

missionary.simple: The Holy Ghost is a permanent gift given after baptism. In the Old Testament the Spirit came and went. Christ promised something better — a Comforter who stays with you.
missionary.explain: John the Baptist baptized with water. Christ baptizes with the Holy Ghost. Two different baptisms — one temporary, one permanent. We administer both.
missionary.why: Having the Holy Ghost as a constant companion changes everything — how you make decisions, how you find peace, how you recognize truth.
missionary.invite: Would you like to know what it feels like to receive the gift of the Holy Ghost?

questions.question: 135. Why do you believe that Elijah literally returned to the earth? Didn't Christ say John the Baptist fulfilled that prophecy?
questions.answer:
Christ said, “Elias is indeed come already, and they knew him not.”Matthew 17:12 He was speaking of John the Baptist — who came in the spirit and power of Elias to prepare the way for the Lord's first coming. But He also said in the same passage: "Elias truly shall first come, and restore all things." Future tense. A coming still ahead.

These are two separate missions — one of preparation before the first coming, one of restoration before the second. John fulfilled the first. Elijah himself — the same one taken in the whirlwind in 2 Kings 2:11 — was to fulfil the second.

John himself denied being Elijah. The Jews asked him plainly, "Art thou Elias?" He said, “I am not.”John 1:21 If John were the complete fulfilment of the Elijah prophecy, why did he deny it? Because there was another fulfilment yet to come.

Malachi 4:5–6 says Elijah would come before the "great and dreadful day of the Lord" and turn the hearts of the fathers to the children and the children to the fathers. John turned the hearts of Israel to the Messiah. But the turning of hearts between living and dead generations — the sealing work of the temples — was not accomplished by John. It was accomplished when Elijah appeared to Joseph Smith in the Kirtland Temple on April 3, 1836, and restored the sealing keys. That is the fulfilment Malachi described.

missionary.simple: John the Baptist fulfilled one part of the Elijah prophecy — preparing the way for Christ's first coming. Elijah himself fulfilled the second part: restoring the sealing power before Christ's second coming.
missionary.explain: John himself said he was not Elijah when asked directly. That is because there were two separate missions — one before the first coming, one before the second.
missionary.why: Elijah's return was not just a prophecy about John. It was a promise that family sealing power would be restored in the last days — and it has been.
missionary.invite: Would you like to know what it means that families can be sealed together for eternity because of this restoration?

questions.question: 146. Why do you say that the Holy Ghost can witness truth to anyone, even before baptism?
questions.answer:
Because that is how every conversion in the New Testament worked. The people at Pentecost received Peter's testimony, were "pricked in their hearts" — felt the witness of the Spirit — and then asked what they must do. Acts 2:37 The witness came before the baptism.

Cornelius and his household — Gentiles who had not yet received the gospel ordinances — received the Holy Ghost while Peter was still speaking. Peter's reaction was astonishment: “Can any man forbid water, that these should not be baptized, which have received the Holy Ghost as well as we?”Acts 10:47 The Spirit moved before the ordinance.

The gift of the Holy Ghost — the permanent, covenant-sealed companionship — comes through the laying on of hands after baptism. But the influence of the Spirit — the witness to truth, the prompting toward repentance, the light that prepares a person for the covenant — is available to all.

Joseph Smith described the two as distinct: "There is a difference between the Holy Ghost and the gift of the Holy Ghost. Cornelius received the Holy Ghost before he was baptized, which was the Holy Ghost falling upon him; but Cornelius received not the gift of the Holy Ghost until after he was baptized." Every honest seeker in every nation can receive the prompting of the Spirit toward truth. The full gift comes through the covenant. That distinction honours both the universal love of God and the importance of the ordinance.

missionary.simple: Because God wants everyone to know the truth for themselves. The Spirit witnesses truth to any sincere seeker — even before they are baptized.
missionary.explain: Cornelius received the Holy Ghost while Peter was still talking — before any baptism. The Spirit prepares people for the covenant. It does not wait for the ordinance first.
missionary.why: You do not have to take our word for it. You can know for yourself. God will answer a sincere prayer.
missionary.invite: Will you ask God in prayer whether what you have been learning is true?

questions.question: 236. Why do I need to be baptised again before joining the Church if I have already been baptised in my former church?
questions.answer:
Because the validity of a baptism depends not only on the sincerity of the person being baptised, but on the priesthood authority of the person performing it.

Paul found this situation in Ephesus. He met disciples who had already been baptised — with the baptism of John. John held the Aaronic Priesthood — the lesser, preparatory priesthood. His baptism was not invalid, but it was preparatory. He had authority to baptize for repentance, but not to confer the gift of the Holy Ghost — that required the higher Melchizedek Priesthood, which the apostles held. He asked them, "Have ye received the Holy Ghost since ye believed?" They said they had not. Paul baptised them again — this time in the name of the Lord Jesus — and then laid his hands on them, and they received the Holy Ghost. Acts 19:1–6

Their previous baptism was sincere. Their belief was real. But the baptism lacked the priesthood authority that connected it to the gift of the Holy Ghost and the covenant Paul was administering. So Paul baptised them again — not to insult their previous experience, but to complete what was missing.

The same situation applies today. When a person is baptised in a church that does not hold the authority of the Melchizedek Priesthood — the authority restored through Joseph Smith by Peter, James, and John — that baptism is sincere but unauthorised. It is like a document signed by someone who had no legal authority to sign it. The signature is genuine. The authority behind it is not.

Paul wrote that no man takes the priesthood honour upon himself — “but he that is called of God, as was Aaron.”Hebrews 5:4 Aaron was called by Moses — a prophet. He did not call himself. Every minister in every church who baptised by his own authority, or by the authority of a church that received its authority from another unauthorised church, is in the same position — sincere, but without the divine authorisation that makes the ordinance binding before God.

We do not rebaptise to disrespect your previous faith. We rebaptise because we love you enough to give you the ordinance in its complete and valid form — exactly as Paul did for the disciples in Ephesus.

missionary.simple: Because a baptism's validity depends on the authority of the person performing it — not just the sincerity of the person receiving it. Paul rebaptized believers in Ephesus for exactly this reason.
missionary.explain: Their previous baptism was sincere and real. But Paul baptized them again because it lacked the priesthood authority that connects the ordinance to God's covenant. He then laid hands on them and they received the Holy Ghost.
missionary.why: We do not rebaptize to disrespect your previous faith. We rebaptize because we love you enough to give you the complete and valid ordinance.
missionary.invite: Would you read Acts 19:1–6 this week and ask God whether this applies to you?

questions.question: 237. What do you mean by laying on of hands to receive the Holy Ghost?
questions.answer:
After a person is baptised by water, they receive a second ordinance — the laying on of hands for the gift of the Holy Ghost. A person who holds the Melchizedek Priesthood places his hands on the head of the newly baptised member, calls them by name, and by the authority of the priesthood, confirms them a member of the Church and says "receive the Holy Ghost."

This is not a metaphor or a ceremony without content. It is a real conferral of a real gift — the right to the constant companionship of the Holy Ghost as long as the person remains worthy.

John the Baptist distinguished clearly between the baptism of water and the baptism of the Spirit. He said: “I indeed baptise you with water unto repentance: but he that cometh after me is mightier than I... he shall baptise you with the Holy Ghost.”Matthew 3:11 Two separate baptisms. Two separate gifts. Two separate ordinances.

When Philip baptised the people of Samaria, they received the ordinance of water — but the Holy Ghost had not yet fallen upon them. When Peter and John arrived from Jerusalem and laid their hands on them, they received the Holy Ghost. Acts 8:14–17 This was not automatic or invisible. Simon the Sorcerer saw it happen and wanted to buy the power to do the same. The effect was visible and real.

Paul listed the laying on of hands alongside baptism as a foundational principle of the gospel. Hebrews 6:1–2 It is not optional. It is not decorative. It is the ordained method by which God gave the gift of the Holy Ghost through His apostles in the primitive Church — and through His restored priesthood holders today.

The laying on of hands is not magic. It is authority. The person laying on hands acts as the instrument through which God confers His Spirit upon the recipient. The gift belongs to God. The hands are the channel He has chosen.

Nephi taught this same doctrine centuries before Christ's earthly ministry. He wrote that after baptism by water, one must receive the Holy Ghost, after which the Father speaks with the voice of the Son: "Yea, the words of my Beloved are true and faithful. He that endureth to the end, the same shall be saved."2 Nephi 31:15 The pattern — water baptism followed by the gift of the Holy Ghost — was known from the beginning. 2 Nephi 31:13–14

missionary.simple: After baptism, a Melchizedek Priesthood holder places his hands on your head and confers the gift of the Holy Ghost. This gives you the right to the constant companionship of the Holy Ghost.
missionary.explain: When Philip baptized people in Samaria, the Holy Ghost had not yet come. Peter and John arrived and laid their hands on them — and then they received the Holy Ghost. Two separate acts. Both matter.
missionary.why: The Holy Ghost is your personal guide from God. Confirmation is how you receive that gift officially and permanently.
missionary.invite: Would you like to meet with someone who can share what receiving the Holy Ghost meant in their life?

questions.question: 245. How can I join the Church?
questions.answer:
The door to The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints is open to every person on earth who sincerely wants to follow Jesus Christ and receive the fulness of His gospel.

The path is the same path Christ established in the New Testament. Peter preached on the day of Pentecost and the people asked him, "What shall we do?" He answered: “Repent, and be baptized every one of you in the name of Jesus Christ for the remission of sins, and ye shall receive the gift of the Holy Ghost.”Acts 2:37–38

The steps are these. First, meet with the missionaries. They will teach you the restored gospel — the nature of God, the mission of Jesus Christ, the apostasy and restoration, the Book of Mormon, and the principles and ordinances of the gospel. Second, read and pray about the Book of Mormon — asking God sincerely if it is true. Third, repent — turn to God with a full purpose of heart and a commitment to follow His commandments. Fourth, be baptised by immersion by one who holds the proper authority. Fifth, receive the Holy Ghost by the laying on of hands.

You can request missionaries by visiting comeuntochrist.org, speaking to any member of the Church, or finding the nearest chapel on the Church's website at churchofjesuschrist.org.

The invitation is as simple and as profound as anything in the scriptures: “Come unto me.”Matthew 11:28 That is still the message. Come. Ask. Receive.

Christ extended that same invitation after His resurrection when He appeared to the Nephites: "Come unto me and be baptized in my name, that ye may receive a remission of your sins."3 Nephi 27:16 The path is unchanged across all dispensations. The door is always open.

missionary.simple: You meet with missionaries, learn the restored gospel, pray about it, repent, and then be baptized by immersion by someone holding proper authority.
missionary.explain: On the day of Pentecost, the people asked Peter what they should do. His answer is still the answer: repent and be baptized. Then receive the gift of the Holy Ghost.
missionary.why: The door is open to every person on earth. God has no favorites. The path is the same for everyone.
missionary.invite: Would you be willing to take the next step and meet with the missionaries to learn more?

questions.question: 246. What should I do after baptism?
questions.answer:
Baptism is the beginning of the covenant journey — not the end of it. Christ did not say "come unto me and be baptised and you are done." He said, “Come, follow me.”Luke 18:22 The following is the life's work.

After baptism, the most important things are straightforward. Attend church every Sunday. The sacrament — taken weekly — renews the covenant you made at baptism and keeps you connected to the promise of the Spirit's companionship. 3 Nephi 18:7 Do not skip it without cause.

Pray every day. Morning and night. The relationship with God you began in your conversion is a relationship that must be maintained through regular, honest conversation. Families that pray together stay together. Souls that pray daily stay connected to heaven.

Read the scriptures daily. Even fifteen minutes a day transforms a person over months and years. Start with the Book of Mormon. Read it from cover to cover. Then read it again. The Spirit will teach you something new in it every time.

Find a calling — a way to serve in the Church. Service is the antidote to spiritual passivity. Paul wrote that every member of the body has a function. 1 Corinthians 12:12–27 Find yours and fulfil it.

Build relationships with other members. The ward is your covenant family. They will support you in hard times and celebrate with you in good ones if you invest in them.

Set a goal to receive your endowment and, if married, to be sealed in the temple. That is the path to the fulness of the covenant God has offered you.

You have started well. Keep going.

Nephi captured the path in one verse: "Wherefore, ye must press forward with a steadfastness in Christ, having a perfect brightness of hope, and a love of God and of all men. Wherefore, if ye shall press forward, feasting upon the word of Christ, and endure to the end, behold, thus saith the Father: Ye shall have eternal life."2 Nephi 31:20 That is the whole journey in one scripture. Press forward. Endure. Receive eternal life.

missionary.simple: Baptism is the beginning of a covenant relationship with God — not the end. The next steps are attending church, taking the sacrament weekly, reading scriptures daily, and serving others.
missionary.explain: Christ did not say 'be baptized and you are done.' He said 'come, follow me.' Following is a lifelong journey, not a single event.
missionary.why: The habits you build after baptism shape who you become. Daily prayer and scripture study transform a person over months and years.
missionary.invite: Would you be willing to attend church this Sunday and see what it feels like to take that next step?

questions.question: 6. Why don't you use the cross as a symbol of your faith like other Christians?
questions.answer:
We reverence what Christ did on the cross with everything in us. His atonement is the foundation of all we teach and all we believe. But we choose to focus on the living Christ — the resurrected Christ who came out of that tomb with a body of flesh and bone, who showed His hands and His feet to His apostles, and who lives today.

Paul wrote, “Christ being raised from the dead dieth no more; death hath no more dominion over him.”Romans 6:9 The cross was not the end of His story. The resurrection was. We do not carry a symbol of death. We carry a witness of life.

If we were to reduce our faith to a symbol, we would choose the empty tomb over the cross — because Christ himself made the resurrection the central proof of His divinity. He said to Thomas, “Reach hither thy finger, and behold my hands.”John 20:27 He presented himself alive — not as a reminder of suffering.

Our testimony is not that He died. It is that He lives. And that is a truth no symbol carved from wood can fully contain.

When the resurrected Christ appeared to the Nephites, He invited them to feel the wounds in His hands and feet — living proof of His sacrifice and His resurrection both. 3 Nephi 11:14–15 He came not as a memory on a cross but as a living, glorified, resurrected Lord. That is the Christ we worship.

missionary.simple: We focus on the living, risen Christ rather than the cross. Our symbol is not death — it is resurrection and life.
missionary.explain: We reverence everything Christ did on the cross. But we choose to remember that He rose — that He is alive today. An empty tomb matters more to us than a cross.
missionary.why: Our testimony is not that He died. It is that He lives. That changes everything.
missionary.invite: Would you like to know who Jesus Christ is to us and why His resurrection is the center of what we believe?

questions.question: 24. Why don't the women in the church cover their hair with cloth, as directed by the Bible?
questions.answer:
Paul's instruction in 1 Corinthians 11 on head coverings was addressed to a specific culture at a specific time in Corinth — a city where the covering or uncovering of a woman's head carried specific social meaning tied to that culture. Paul consistently distinguished between what was commandment from the Lord and what was his own judgment suited to circumstances. 1 Corinthians 7:6, 10, 12

The same Paul who wrote about head coverings also wrote that in Christ “there is neither male nor female.”Galatians 3:28 His intent was always order, modesty, and the avoidance of things that brought dishonour in that cultural setting — not the establishment of a universal dress code binding on all women in all times.

If 1 Corinthians 11 is read as a binding universal law, the verses before it must also apply — that a man dishonours Christ by covering his head during prayer. Many Christian traditions today do not require men to pray bareheaded. They have already applied cultural judgment to that same passage.

We teach modesty, reverence, and respect in worship. We follow the spirit of Paul's instruction. Where the Lord has given specific commandments through His living prophet, we observe them exactly.

The Book of Mormon reflects the same principle — that modesty and the honour of womanhood are eternal values, not cultural decorations. Jacob rebuked the men of his day for causing grief to women through their unfaithfulness, declaring their conduct before God as grossly wicked. Jacob 2:35 God cares about how we treat and honour women — the spirit behind Paul's instruction has always been about dignity and reverence.

missionary.simple: Paul's head covering instruction was cultural guidance for Corinth — not a universal law for all women in all times. We follow the principle behind it: modesty and reverence.
missionary.explain: The same Paul who wrote about head coverings also wrote that in Christ there is neither male nor female. He was addressing a specific cultural context, not making an eternal dress code.
missionary.why: We apply the spirit of scripture, not just the surface form. God cares about the heart behind your worship.
missionary.invite: Would you like to know how our Sunday meetings work and what we focus on in worship?

questions.question: 27. Why doesn't your church speak in tongues like other churches do?
questions.answer:
We do have the gift of tongues. It is one of the gifts of the Spirit that Christ promised would follow believers. Mark 16:17 But the purpose of that gift has always been specific — to communicate the gospel across language barriers, not to produce unintelligible sounds in a meeting.

Paul addressed this directly in 1 Corinthians 14. He asked, “If I come unto you speaking with tongues, what shall I profit you, except I shall speak to you either by revelation, or by knowledge, or by prophesying, or by doctrine?”1 Corinthians 14:6 He went further: “If any man speak in an unknown tongue... let one interpret. But if there be no interpreter, let him keep silence in the church.”1 Corinthians 14:27–28

Paul said he would rather speak five words with understanding than ten thousand words in an unknown tongue. 1 Corinthians 14:19 That is not an endorsement of what passes for tongue-speaking in many churches today. Paul was providing the scriptural standard — speaking in a way that edifies the congregation.

The gift of tongues functions in our church exactly as the scriptures describe. Our missionaries go to nations whose languages they do not know and are given remarkable ability to learn and communicate. That is the gift in its proper operation. Paul himself said that unintelligible speech in a congregation serves no one. 1 Corinthians 14:19

Moroni listed the gift of tongues as one of the genuine gifts of the Spirit given to those who believe: "And all these gifts come by the Spirit of Christ; and they come unto every man severally, according as he will."Moroni 10:17 The gifts are real. But Moroni also warned that they must be sought sincerely and exercised in the Lord's way — not performed for show. Moroni 10:8–17

missionary.simple: We do have the gift of tongues. But its true purpose is communicating the gospel across language barriers — not making unintelligible sounds. Paul said so directly.
missionary.explain: Paul said he would rather speak five words people can understand than ten thousand in an unknown tongue. Our missionaries go to foreign countries and receive remarkable ability to learn new languages. That is the gift at work.
missionary.why: Every gift of God has a purpose — to edify, to testify, to build the kingdom. We follow Paul's standard.
missionary.invite: Would you like to hear from a missionary how they experienced the gift of tongues in learning a new language?

questions.question: 55. Why don't Latter-day Saints use the Apostles' Creed or the Nicene Creed like other Christians?
questions.answer:
We follow scripture rather than creeds because the creeds were not written by apostles or prophets. They came from councils of men — assembled not under divine commission but under political direction — who tried to settle theological disputes by majority vote. The Church of Jesus Christ is not governed by committee. It is governed by revelation.

The Nicene Creed was formulated at the Council of Nicaea in 325 AD, called by the Emperor Constantine. He called that council not out of spiritual conviction but to unify his empire politically. His personal life did not reflect gospel standards. That is the historical origin of the creed that shapes the understanding of God in most of Christianity today.

The Athenasian Creed describes God as "incomprehensible." But the Bible says eternal life is to know God. John 17:3 A God who cannot be known and a God whose knowledge is eternal life are very different things.

Christ prayed. He wept. He was tempted. He grew in wisdom. Luke 2:52 He cried out on the cross to His Father. None of that fits a God described as without parts, without passions, and beyond understanding. We hold to the God of the Bible — personal, knowable, loving, and real — rather than to a philosophical concept developed centuries after the apostles were gone.

Nephi recorded hearing the voice of the Father bear witness of the Son after the pattern of the Holy Ghost descending like a dove — the same three distinct, knowable beings revealed at Christ's baptism. 2 Nephi 31:15 And when Christ appeared to the Nephites, there was no ambiguity about His nature — He was a physical, resurrected, glorified being whom thousands touched with their own hands. 3 Nephi 11:14–15

missionary.simple: Because creeds were written by uninspired councils of men — not by apostles or prophets. We follow scripture and living revelation, not man-made definitions.
missionary.explain: The Nicene Creed calls God incomprehensible. But Jesus said eternal life is to know God. A God you cannot know and a God whose knowledge brings eternal life are very different beings.
missionary.why: We worship the God of the Bible — personal, knowable, loving, and real — not a philosophical concept invented centuries after the apostles died.
missionary.invite: Would you like to know what we believe about the nature of God and why it matters for how you relate to Him?

questions.question: 56. Why do you partake of the sacrament every Sunday? Isn't that too frequent?
questions.answer:
Paul wrote, “For as often as ye eat this bread, and drink this cup, ye do shew the Lord's death till he come.”1 Corinthians 11:26 "As often as" — not once a month, not once a quarter, not on special occasions only. The early saints gathered on the first day of the week specifically to break bread. Acts 20:7 That was their pattern. That is our pattern.

The sacrament is a renewal of the covenant we made at baptism. Every week we carry the weight of our failures, our weaknesses, and our shortcomings. Every week we need the reminder that His blood was shed, that His body was broken, and that through His grace our sins can be forgiven if we come to Him with a broken heart and a contrite spirit.

Taking it frequently is not a diminishment of its sacred weight — it is a recognition that we need His grace constantly. The more we remember Him, the closer His Spirit draws to us — and that is precisely the promise in the sacrament prayer itself.

missionary.simple: Because we need the reminder of Christ's sacrifice constantly — not just occasionally. The sacrament renews our baptismal covenant every single week.
missionary.explain: Paul said 'as often as ye eat this bread.' The early saints broke bread every first day of the week. We follow that same pattern because we need God's grace every week, not once a month.
missionary.why: Weekly sacrament keeps you connected to the covenant you made at baptism and to the promise of the Spirit's companionship.
missionary.invite: Would you like to attend a sacrament meeting and experience that for yourself?

questions.question: 57. Why do your men dress in white attire to administer the sacrament? Where is that in the Bible?
questions.answer:
White in scripture has always represented purity, holiness, and consecration. The priests of the tabernacle wore white linen garments when performing sacred ordinances. Exodus 28:39–43 John saw the elders around the throne clothed in white raiment. Revelation 4:4 The angels at the tomb were clothed in white. John 20:12 White is the colour of the covenant.

Those who administer the sacrament act in the name of Jesus Christ. They hold the Aaronic Priesthood — the same priesthood held by Aaron and his sons, who wore white linen when they ministered at the altar of God. The principle has not changed. The clothing represents the seriousness of what they are doing and the purity required to do it.

Beyond that, we baptize in white. We are married in white in the temple. We dress our dead in white for burial. White is the thread that runs through every sacred ordinance of the gospel — representing the cleansing that Christ offers to all who come unto Him. There is nothing arbitrary about it, and every member is free to dress according to their own conscience.

missionary.simple: White in scripture always represents purity and holiness. Those who administer the sacrament dress in white because they are performing a sacred ordinance in the name of Christ.
missionary.explain: The priests of Israel wore white linen at the altar. Angels at the tomb were clothed in white. The principle has not changed. White is the color of covenant and consecration.
missionary.why: How we dress for sacred things reflects how seriously we take them. It is an act of reverence, not ceremony.
missionary.invite: Would you like to attend a Sunday sacrament meeting and see the reverence we bring to our worship?

questions.question: 59. Why do you hold Fast Sunday once a month? Where is that in the Bible?
questions.answer:
Isaiah 58:6–7 describes the fast the Lord has chosen — to loose the bands of wickedness, to deal bread to the hungry, to bring the poor into your house. That is exactly what we do. We fast. We donate what we would have spent on food. That fast offering goes directly to those in need — no administrative costs, no overhead charity model. It goes to the poor.

Christ himself fasted. He fasted forty days and forty nights before beginning His ministry. Matthew 4:2 He taught His disciples that certain things come forth only “by prayer and fasting.”Matthew 17:21 Paul fasted. 2 Corinthians 11:27 The primitive church fasted as a regular spiritual discipline.

The prophet Joel commanded, “Sanctify ye a fast.”Joel 1:14 The early church observed fasts together as a body. Acts 13:2–3 We do the same — on the first Sunday of each month. We deny ourselves food and water for two meals, we pray, and we give generously to care for the poor. That is not a tradition of men. That is a biblical practice preserved in its purity.

missionary.simple: Fasting means going without food and water for two meals once a month, then donating what you would have spent to care for the poor. It is also a spiritual discipline that draws you closer to God.
missionary.explain: 'Fasting is not about suffering. It is about making room for God. When your stomach is empty, your heart has space to hear Him.'
missionary.why: Fasting connects personal sacrifice with caring for the poor — exactly the kind of religion God says He wants.
missionary.invite: Would you be willing to try fasting once and see what God says to you during that time?

questions.question: 73. Why does your church teach that the Sabbath was changed to Sunday? The commandment says the seventh day.
questions.answer:
This is a question worth examining carefully from scripture. The Sabbath commandment was given to a specific people at a specific time as part of the Mosaic covenant. Paul wrote in Colossians 2:16, "Let no man therefore judge you in meat, or in drink, or in respect of an holyday, or of the new moon, or of the sabbath days." He placed the Sabbath among the things the Mosaic law covered and the New Covenant superseded.

He wrote to the Galatians, "The law was our schoolmaster to bring us unto Christ: but after that faith is come, we are no longer under a schoolmaster."Galatians 3:24–25 The schoolmaster — including its Sabbath requirements — was fulfilled in Christ.

John, the beloved apostle, refers to "the Lord's Day" in Revelation 1:10. The disciples gathered on the first day of the week after the resurrection. John 20:19, 26 The sacrament was observed on the first day. Acts 20:7 Offerings were collected on the first day. 1 Corinthians 16:2 The resurrection itself happened on the first day. The early Church followed the pattern the Lord set through His resurrection — and we do the same.

The deeper principle behind the Sabbath has never changed — setting aside time to rest, worship, and draw close to God. That principle is eternal. The specific requirements of the Mosaic Sabbath were fulfilled in Christ, who is our true Sabbath rest. Hebrews 4:9–10

missionary.simple: The Sabbath commandment was fulfilled in Christ. Paul placed it among the things the old covenant covered. We worship on Sunday because that is the day Christ rose from the dead.
missionary.explain: 'The principle behind the Sabbath has never changed — set aside one day for God. The specific day changed because the resurrection changed everything.'
missionary.why: Keeping Sunday holy is not a burden. It is a weekly reminder that Christ rose and that you belong to Him.
missionary.invite: Would you be willing to try keeping one Sunday focused on God and see how it affects your week?

questions.question: 77. Why do Latter-day Saints not observe the Lord's Supper with fermented wine? Didn't Christ use wine at the Last Supper?
questions.answer:
The command was to drink of the cup — not of wine specifically. In Matthew 26:27 He said, "Drink ye all of it." In verse 29 He spoke of the "fruit of the vine." The cup contained a drink made from grapes. Whether it was fermented is not the issue — the issue is what the ordinance represents and what its purpose is.

The original Greek text uses the word for wine to cover everything from fresh grape juice to fully fermented wine. Paul wrote in Romans 14:21 that it is "good neither to eat flesh, nor to drink wine, nor any thing whereby thy brother stumbleth." He also wrote in 1 Timothy 5:23 to use "a little wine for thy stomach's sake." The same apostle writing both epistles — meaning the word "wine" covered a range of drinks. There is no conflict here once we understand that grape juice and fermented wine were both called by the same word.

Beyond the linguistic point — we have living prophets. Through revelation given to the prophet Joseph Smith, water was substituted for wine in the sacrament. The reason is plain: the purpose of the sacrament is remembrance and covenant renewal — not the specific liquid. Paul himself said, “As often as ye eat this bread, and drink this cup, ye do shew the Lord's death till he comes.”1 Corinthians 11:26 The cup. Not the content of the cup. The covenant matters. The ordinance matters. The drink is secondary.

missionary.simple: Christ said 'drink of the fruit of the vine,' not specifically fermented wine. Through living prophets, water was substituted. The covenant matters, not the liquid.
missionary.explain: 'The purpose of the sacrament is remembrance and covenant renewal. Christ himself told the Nephites to use water. The promise is the same either way.'
missionary.why: God speaks through living prophets to adapt His ordinances to the needs of His people without changing their meaning.
missionary.invite: Would you like to attend a sacrament meeting and experience for yourself what that weekly covenant renewal feels like?

questions.question: 116. Why do you believe the Lord's Prayer was not meant to be repeated word for word?
questions.answer:
Because Christ said so. Just before giving the prayer, He warned against “vain repetitions, as the heathen do.”Matthew 6:7 Then He said, "After this manner therefore pray ye." After this manner — meaning use this as a pattern, a model, a guide for what prayer should include. Not as a script to be repeated.

James wrote, “If any of you lack wisdom, let him ask of God, that giveth to all men liberally, and upbraideth not; and it shall be given him.”James 1:5 Ask God — your words, your needs, your heart. Not a formula borrowed from scripture.

Paul wrote, “The Spirit itself maketh intercession for us with groanings which cannot be uttered.”Romans 8:26 Real prayer is personal, specific, and flows from the actual condition of the soul. A repeated liturgical formula — however beautiful — does not produce that kind of prayer. It produces familiarity. And familiarity, Christ warned, produces the very vain repetition He cautioned against.

We teach our members to pray as a conversation with a loving Father — honestly, personally, with gratitude and petition shaped by their own lives. That is the spirit of the prayer Christ modelled. The Lord's Prayer shows what prayer covers — praise, petition, forgiveness, protection — not the exact words that exhaust its meaning for every person in every circumstance.

missionary.simple: Christ taught us to pray personally, not to repeat a script. The Lord's Prayer is a pattern — a model of what prayer should include, not words to recite over and over.
missionary.explain: 'God wants to hear your voice, your words, your actual life. He is not impressed by memorized phrases. He wants a real conversation.'
missionary.why: Personal prayer is how you build a real relationship with God — not a ritual performance, but a genuine conversation with your Father.
missionary.invite: Would you be willing to pray in your own words tonight and tell God exactly what is on your heart?

questions.question: 239. Why don't you dance and sing aloud to praise God in Sunday meetings? Why does your church worship so quietly compared to others?
questions.answer:
We do sing. Every sacrament meeting includes congregational hymns — sung together as an act of worship. And we do dance — the Church actively encourages wholesome recreational dancing in appropriate settings. (Luke 15:25 records that dancing was part of the celebration in Christ's parable of the prodigal son.) Dancing in proper, chaperoned settings has always been part of Latter-day Saint culture.

What we do not do is turn Sunday sacrament meeting into a concert or an entertainment event. And the reason for that is found in the purpose of the meeting itself.

The sacrament — the ordinance of the Lord's Supper — is the centrepiece of our Sunday worship. Paul wrote that when they came together to eat the Lord's Supper, disorder and irreverence did not honour the spirit of the occasion. 1 Corinthians 11:20–22 He commanded that everything be done “decently and in order.”1 Corinthians 14:40

The Lord himself was quiet in sacred settings. He taught in a still, small voice. 1 Kings 19:12 He did not shout from temple steps. He went into the desert to pray. He found a garden in Gethsemane. The Spirit of God does not typically operate through noise and excitement. It operates through peace and reverence. Paul said God is “not the author of confusion.”1 Corinthians 14:33

The loud, physically expressive style of worship found in some churches is sincere and we do not mock it. But sincerity does not determine correctness. The Pharisees prayed loudly and publicly. Christ said pray privately. Matthew 6:5–6 The form of worship matters. We follow the form the Lord established.

missionary.simple: We do sing in our meetings. But our sacrament meeting is centred on the Lord's Supper, not performance. Paul commanded that everything be done decently and in order.
missionary.explain: 'The Spirit of God often speaks in a still, small voice. We create silence so people can actually hear Him. Reverence is not coldness — it is attention.'
missionary.why: Worship that produces real spiritual experience requires space for the Spirit to move — and the Spirit moves in peace, not noise.
missionary.invite: Would you like to attend a Sunday meeting and experience the kind of reverence we bring to worship?

questions.question: 240. Why don't your women cover their heads while attending Sunday meetings?
questions.answer:
