Paul's instruction in 1 Corinthians 11 on head coverings addressed a specific cultural reality in Corinth — where the covering or uncovering of a woman's head carried specific social meaning tied to that culture. His purpose was modesty and order in worship — not the establishment of a universal dress code for all women in all cultures for all time.

Paul himself distinguished between what was "commandment from the Lord" and what was his own judgment given to specific circumstances. 1 Corinthians 7:6, 10, 12 The head covering instruction belongs in the second category — a cultural application of the principle of modesty, not a binding eternal law.

Consider the verse just before that instruction. Paul said that a man dishonours the occasion if he prays with his head covered. 1 Corinthians 11:4 Most Christian churches today already apply cultural judgment to that same verse — accepting that the underlying principle applies while the specific cultural form does not bind every believer in every age.

We apply the same reasoning consistently. We teach modesty. We teach reverence in worship. We teach women to dress in a way that honours God and their own dignity. We do not require a specific garment whose original social meaning has no correspondence in our culture.

Where the Lord has given specific commandments through His living prophet — such as the Word of Wisdom or the law of chastity — we observe them precisely. Where Paul gave culturally specific counsel, we honour the principle behind it.

missionary.simple: Paul's head covering instruction was specific to the culture of Corinth. We apply the principle — modesty and reverence in worship — not the specific cultural form.
missionary.explain: 'Paul was solving a specific social problem in one city at one time. His principle is eternal: honor God in how you worship. The specific application varies by culture.'
missionary.why: God cares about the heart behind your worship far more than the cloth on your head.
missionary.invite: Would you like to know what we do focus on in Sunday worship and what it means for you personally?

questions.question: 241. Why do your men always clean-shave? Is that part of Church doctrine? In the Bible, people kept beards.
questions.answer:
Clean-shaven appearance is not a doctrinal requirement in the same sense that baptism or the Word of Wisdom is. It is a standard of personal appearance that has been encouraged — particularly for missionaries and for those serving in certain leadership capacities — because of its association with neatness, professionalism, and the way the Church presents itself to the world.

You are correct that men in the Bible kept beards. Abraham had a beard. Moses had a beard. Christ almost certainly had a beard. The Mosaic law actually forbade shaving the corners of the beard. Leviticus 19:27 We do not teach that beards are inappropriate.

What we do teach is that physical appearance matters in the ministry. Paul wrote, “I have made myself servant unto all, that I might gain the more.”1 Corinthians 9:19 Part of that is meeting people where they are — presenting yourself in a way that does not create unnecessary barriers to the message you are carrying.

The standard has been practical and cultural rather than doctrinal. In recent years the Church's guidance on facial hair has been relaxed for general membership, while missionaries and some temple workers are still encouraged to be clean-shaven as part of a consistent, professional appearance.

The underlying principle is that our appearance is a reflection of how we regard our calling. That principle is scriptural even if the specific application varies by culture and context.

missionary.simple: Clean shaving for missionaries and some leaders is a standard of appearance — not doctrine. The principle is that how we present ourselves reflects how we regard our calling.
missionary.explain: 'Abraham had a beard. Moses had a beard. The principle is not about facial hair — it is about presenting yourself in a way that does not create unnecessary barriers to the message you carry.'
missionary.why: The gospel deserves to be heard by everyone, and sometimes our appearance either opens or closes that door.
missionary.invite: Would you like to know more about what it means to represent Jesus Christ as a missionary?

questions.question: 242. Why don't members clap hands in Church meetings?
questions.answer:
Applause in the Church is replaced by a different form of expression — the sustaining vote. When a leader is called, the congregation sustains them by raising their right hand. This is not silence. It is participation — a solemn, unified covenant that the congregation will support and sustain the person being called.

The pattern comes from the primitive church. Acts 6:5 records that the congregation chose the seven ministers — implying a congregational process of recognition and approval. The raised hand in a sustaining vote is the modern equivalent of that pattern.

Beyond the specific question of clapping — our meetings are conducted with reverence because the Spirit of God is invited to be present, and the Spirit operates in an atmosphere of peace rather than noise. Paul's instruction — “let all things be done decently and in order”1 Corinthians 14:40 — governs our approach to meeting conduct.

The absence of applause is not an absence of joy. It is an expression of the kind of joy that does not require noise — the “peace of God, which passeth all understanding.”Philippians 4:7 In a sacrament meeting where the body and blood of Christ are symbolically remembered, reverence is not coldness. It is the appropriate response to a sacred moment.

Clapping at a concert or a sporting event is natural and right in its context. Clapping in a sacrament meeting would shift the atmosphere from worship to performance — and Christ warned against turning worship into a performance. Matthew 6:1–5 The absence of applause is our way of keeping the meeting what it is meant to be.

missionary.simple: In our meetings, we sustain leaders by raising our right hand — a solemn covenant to support them. Applause is not our expression of agreement; the raised hand is.
missionary.explain: 'When the congregation raises their hands together, it is not a vote — it is a promise. Every person is committing to support and sustain the person called.'
missionary.why: Sustaining leaders by covenant is more meaningful than applause. It creates real accountability and real unity.
missionary.invite: Would you like to attend a meeting where you can see this practice for yourself?

questions.question: 247. Why do members not buy and sell on Sunday, yet they pay for transportation?
questions.answer:
The Sabbath is a day set apart for rest and worship. The Lord commanded Israel to remember the Sabbath and keep it holy — refraining from their own work and their own commercial activity. Exodus 20:8–10 Isaiah taught that keeping the Sabbath means “not doing thine own ways, nor finding thine own pleasure, nor speaking thine own words.”Isaiah 58:13

The principle behind the commandment is the dedication of the day to God — not to commerce, not to personal business, not to the ordinary rhythms of worldly life. Buying and selling as a habit on Sunday shifts the day's focus from God to mammon. It supports commercial systems that require other people to work on a sacred day. It treats the Sabbath as a convenience rather than a covenant.

Paying for transportation to get to church is a different matter entirely. The purpose of the payment is to fulfil the Sabbath — not to conduct worldly business. The destination is the house of God. The payment is incidental to that act of worship, not an act of commerce in its own right.

Christ taught that it is lawful to do good on the Sabbath. Matthew 12:12 If getting to church requires paying a fare, the payment serves the worship. The same principle applies to purchasing medicine for a sick child or paying for emergency care. Necessity in the service of righteousness is not a violation of the Sabbath. Unnecessary commerce for personal entertainment or convenience is.

The distinction is one of purpose and spirit. The question every member should ask is not "can I get away with this on Sunday?" but "does this honour the covenant I made with God to keep this day holy?"

missionary.simple: The Sabbath is set apart from commercial activity. Paying transport to get to church serves the Sabbath — it is not commerce. Necessity in the service of worship is different from convenience.
missionary.explain: 'The question is not what you can get away with on Sunday. The question is: does this honor my covenant with God to keep this day holy?'
missionary.why: The Sabbath is a covenant. How you keep it reflects how seriously you take your relationship with God.
missionary.invite: Would you like to know what a Sabbath day focused entirely on God looks and feels like in a member's home?

questions.question: 28. Why were Black members not given the priesthood for so long? Isn't that racial discrimination?
questions.answer:
The restriction on Black members holding the priesthood was not a doctrine of scripture. In 1852, President Brigham Young publicly announced that men of black African descent could no longer be ordained to the priesthood. The origin of this restriction is not fully understood, and the Church's official Gospel Topics Essay states plainly that "the origins of the priesthood restriction are not fully understood." It was lifted by revelation through President Spencer W. Kimball in 1978. Every worthy male member, regardless of race, now holds the priesthood and every blessing of the gospel is available to all.

The reasons offered by past leaders for the restriction were theories — personal opinions attempting to explain something they did not fully understand. None of those explanations was ever canonised as official doctrine. The Church has explicitly disavowed them. What was practiced was a restriction. What was offered as explanation was the opinion of men.

The God we worship is the God Paul described in Acts 17:26 — who "hath made of one blood all nations of men." He is the God who told Peter in a vision that He is “no respecter of persons.”Acts 10:34 He is the God who said through Paul, “There is neither Jew nor Greek, there is neither bond nor free... for ye are all one in Christ Jesus.”Galatians 3:28

We do not defend the restriction. We acknowledge that God's people are imperfect and sometimes carry the cultural limitations of their time into the Church. What we do affirm is that God corrected it through revelation — as He has corrected errors throughout the history of His dealings with men.

The Book of Mormon contains God's own statement on this matter: "He inviteth them all to come unto him and partake of his goodness; and he denieth none that come unto him, black and white, bond and free, male and female."2 Nephi 26:33 That is the God we worship. The restriction was a deviation from His own revealed word. His word has now been restored in full.

missionary.simple: The restriction was a policy — not scripture. It was wrong, and the Church has officially said so. In 1978 it was lifted by revelation. Every worthy person now holds the priesthood equally.
missionary.explain: God is no respecter of persons. He made of one blood all nations of men. The restriction reflected the cultural assumptions of men, not the revealed will of God. God corrected it.
missionary.why: God's people are imperfect and sometimes carry cultural limitations into the Church. What matters is that God corrects them — and He did.
missionary.invite: Would you like to read the Church's official statement on this and ask God about His love for every person regardless of race?

questions.question: 41. Why do you have a lay clergy? Doesn't the Bible require trained, educated ministers?
questions.answer:
The scriptures are clear on this point.

Peter was a fisherman. Andrew was a fisherman. James and John were fishermen. Matthew was a tax collector. Paul was a tentmaker. None of the original apostles attended a theological school before Christ called them. He called them. He trained them. He gave them authority. Their qualification was not education — it was the call of God and the authority of the priesthood.

Paul wrote that no man takes the priesthood honor upon himself — “but he that is called of God, as was Aaron.”Hebrews 5:4 Aaron did not go to school to become a priest. Moses, a prophet, called him. That is the pattern. The credential is the call, not the certificate.

The professional clergy system was introduced centuries after the apostles died. It was part of the drift Paul warned about — when men began to speak perverse things and draw disciples after themselves. Acts 20:30 A man who appoints himself, without a divine call through those who hold authority, has taken the honour upon himself. A man called by those who hold divine authority, ordained by the laying on of hands — that is the pattern of the New Testament.

Our bishops, stake presidents, and local leaders have professions. They have families. They serve without pay. That is the model the apostles lived, and we seek to preserve it.

The Book of Mormon confirms this same pattern. Alma taught that priests were ordained "after the holy order of God" — not by self-appointment or academic training but by divine call, foreordained before the foundation of the world. Alma 13:1–3 The credential was always the call of God, not the certificate of men.

missionary.simple: Because the apostles — Peter, James, John, Paul — were fishermen, tentmakers, and tax collectors. Christ called them. He did not send them to school first.
missionary.explain: A man takes the priesthood honour by the call of God through those who hold authority — not by earning a degree. That was the New Testament pattern and it is ours.
missionary.why: Our bishops serve without pay, with real jobs, serving real families. That is the model Christ established and we preserve it.
missionary.invite: Would you like to meet our local bishop and see for yourself what a lay minister looks like?

questions.question: 62. Why do you believe the Melchizedek Priesthood is still on the earth today?
questions.answer:
Because Christ is “a priest for ever after the order of Melchisedec.”Hebrews 7:17 Not was. Is. Present tense. An eternal priesthood, held by an eternal high priest, does not expire when a generation dies. It continues.

Paul explained in Hebrews 7:3 that this priesthood is "without father, without mother, without descent, having neither beginning of days, nor end of life." It has no end. If it has no end, it is on the earth today — or it was lost through the apostasy and needed to be restored. We say it was lost and has been restored. That is the question every sincere seeker must consider.

Paul further wrote that no man takes this honour upon himself. “He that is called of God, as was Aaron.”Hebrews 5:4 Aaron was called by Moses — a prophet. Not by reading the scriptures. Not by attending a school. By the laying on of hands through a man who held the authority to confer it.

Peter, James, and John held that priesthood. They received it from Christ. After the apostasy and the death of the last apostle, that authority was gone from the earth. In 1829 those same three apostles — Peter, James, and John — restored it to Joseph Smith by the laying on of hands. That is the line of authority through which every elder in this Church traces his ordination today.

The Lord confirmed through Joseph Smith that this priesthood "continueth in the church of God in all generations" and that without it "the power of godliness is not manifest unto men in the flesh." D&C 84:17–18 Without it, ordinances are performed but the power of God is absent. With it, heaven and earth are connected.

missionary.simple: Because Christ is a priest forever after the order of Melchizedek — present tense. That priesthood has no end. It either still exists on the earth or it was lost and needed to be restored.
missionary.explain: We say it was lost in the apostasy and has been restored through Joseph Smith by Peter, James, and John — the same men who held it in the New Testament.
missionary.why: If the priesthood is real, authority matters. A church without it performs sincere ordinances that God does not recognize. We offer the real thing.
missionary.invite: Would you like to know the line of authority through which the priesthood was restored and who holds it today?

questions.question: 96. Why do you teach that the priesthood has specific offices — deacon, teacher, priest, elder, high priest? Isn't that just rank?
questions.answer:
It is not rank. It is function. Every office in the priesthood has a specific purpose, specific responsibilities, and specific authority. Paul listed them in Ephesians 4:11 — apostles, prophets, evangelists, pastors, teachers. In 1 Corinthians 12:28 he added helps and governments. He listed them because they were distinct. Different functions, different callings, different contributions to the body of Christ.

The deacon in the Aaronic Priesthood is learning to serve. He passes the sacrament and takes care of the temporal needs of the congregation. The teacher prepares the sacrament and visits members. The priest blesses the sacrament and can baptize. The elder administers the higher ordinances — confirmation, healing, blessings. The high priest presides over the Church in its fullness.

This is not career advancement. A 12-year-old deacon is not inferior to a high priest. They hold different offices for different seasons and responsibilities. Twelve-year-old boys pass the sacrament in our meetings — administering one of the most sacred ordinances of the gospel — because the Lord trusts them with it and their ordination is real.

Paul told the Corinthians, “The eye cannot say unto the hand, I have no need of thee.”1 Corinthians 12:21 Every office is needed. Every one contributes. The body of Christ requires all its parts — and God organised the priesthood with exactly the parts it needs to function.

missionary.simple: Each priesthood office has a specific function — deacon, teacher, priest, elder, high priest — just as Paul listed apostles, prophets, teachers, and helpers as distinct roles in the church.
missionary.explain: 'A 12-year-old boy passes the sacrament. An elder seals families in the temple. Different offices, different responsibilities — all needed, none inferior.'
missionary.why: God is organized, not random. His church has specific roles because each one fulfills a specific purpose in building His kingdom.
missionary.invite: Would you like to know what priesthood office someone your age or background would hold and what it means practically?

questions.question: 104. Why do Latter-day Saints not have paid professional clergy at the local level?
questions.answer:
Because Christ's apostles did not. Peter was a fisherman. John was a fisherman. Paul was a tentmaker. Matthew was a tax collector. None of them drew a salary for preaching the gospel. Paul wrote, “What is my reward then? Verily that, when I preach the gospel, I may make the gospel of Christ without charge.”1 Corinthians 9:18 Without charge. That was his deliberate choice — to demonstrate that the gospel was not merchandise.

He warned Timothy and Titus that bishops and elders must not be “greedy of filthy lucre.”1 Timothy 3:3; Titus 1:7 The character qualification for church leadership explicitly excluded love of money. A system that pays men to preach creates an incentive that Paul identified as dangerous to the integrity of the ministry.

Every bishop in our Church has a profession. He earns his living outside the Church and serves his congregation without pay — spending evenings and weekends counselling families, presiding over meetings, visiting the sick, and ministering to the poor. Our stake presidents serve the same way. Our missionaries pay their own way.

Christ said, “The laborer is worthy of his hire.”Luke 10:7 We agree. The recompense was food and shelter — not salary and benefit packages. The apostles were fed by those they served. They were not compensated by institutions. That is the model of the primitive church. It is the model of this Church.

missionary.simple: Because Christ's apostles did not take salaries. Peter was a fisherman. Paul was a tentmaker. They served at personal cost because the gospel is not merchandise.
missionary.explain: 'Our bishop has a job, a family, and a life — and he gives his evenings and weekends to serve his congregation for free. That is the New Testament model and we have kept it.'
missionary.why: A paid minister has a financial incentive that can corrupt the purity of the calling. An unpaid minister serves because he loves God.
missionary.invite: Would you like to meet our local leader and see what a lay minister looks like in real life?

questions.question: 147. Why do you teach that priesthood keys are required to govern the Church? Doesn't the Spirit govern?
questions.answer:
Church leaders have taught that keys are the authority to direct the use of priesthood power — not separate from the Spirit, but working alongside it. Keys and the Spirit work together — they are not alternatives. The Spirit confirms truth. Keys authorise its administration. Without both, the Church cannot function as Christ organised it.

Matthew 16:19 — "I will give unto thee the keys of the kingdom of heaven." Christ gave specific, named, transferable authority to Peter. Keys — not just the Spirit. Not just faith. Keys. An authority that could be held, exercised, passed, and in the apostasy, lost.

These keys were specific. The key of baptism for the remission of sins — given to John the Baptist and restored through him to Joseph Smith. The key of the Melchizedek Priesthood — given by Peter, James, and John. The key of the sealing power — given by Elijah. The key of the gathering of Israel — given by Moses. Each key unlocks a specific power of God in the Church.

Without keys, sincere people can preach, worship, and serve — and God will bless them for it. But the ordinances they perform carry no binding authority before God. Simon the Sorcerer had the desire to administer the Holy Ghost — but not the keys. Peter corrected him clearly. Acts 8:18–23 Desire and sincerity do not substitute for authority. The Spirit guides. The keys authorise. Both are essential in the Church Christ built.

missionary.simple: Keys are the authority to direct God's work — to baptize, to seal, to gather. Christ gave keys to Peter. Those keys were lost in the apostasy and restored through Joseph Smith.
missionary.explain: 'Keys are not just power — they are direction. Without keys, a sincere person can preach and pray, but the ordinances they perform are not authorized by God.'
missionary.why: The keys make the difference between a church that God recognizes and one that is sincere but unauthorized.
missionary.invite: Would you like to know who holds these keys today and what they authorize in your life?

questions.question: 153. Why do you believe that the oath and covenant of the priesthood is one of the most binding obligations in the gospel?
questions.answer:
Because the Lord Himself said so. "For whoso is faithful unto the obtaining these two priesthoods of which I have spoken, and the magnifying their calling, I give unto them a promise... And also all they who receive this priesthood receive me, saith the Lord; for he that receiveth my servants receiveth me; and he that receiveth me receiveth my Father." (Doctrine and Covenants 84:33–37.)

To receive the priesthood is to receive Christ. To receive Christ is to receive the Father. That chain of reception is sealed by an oath and a covenant — among the most serious and far-reaching commitments a person can make before God. And the consequence of breaking it is correspondingly serious.

Hebrews 7:21 records that God swore an oath to make Christ a priest for ever after the order of Melchisedec. An oath from God. The same oath was part of the covenant made with Abraham. Hebrews 6:13–17 The oath and covenant of the priesthood places the priesthood holder within that same covenant framework — accountable to God in the most direct and personal way possible.

Paul wrote that those who "sin wilfully after that we have received the knowledge of the truth" face a fearful consequence. Hebrews 10:26–27 Men who receive the Melchizedek Priesthood, enter into its covenant, and then wilfully turn against it face the most serious spiritual consequences of any act in this life. We do not take that lightly. We teach it clearly. And those who magnify their callings receive promises that no other covenant offers.

missionary.simple: To receive the Melchizedek Priesthood is to enter a covenant with God that carries the most serious promises and responsibilities available to a man in this life.
missionary.explain: 'God swore an oath about this priesthood — the same kind of oath He made with Abraham. When a man receives it, he enters that same covenant lineage.'
missionary.why: The oath and covenant connects every priesthood holder to the promises God made to Abraham — and to every blessing that flows from faithfulness.
missionary.invite: Would you like to understand what it would mean for you personally to hold this authority?

questions.question: 160. Do women not hold the priesthood in your church?
questions.answer:
Women in this Church are not ordained to priesthood offices. But to say they have no connection to the priesthood would be inaccurate. In the temple, women perform sacred ordinances — washings, anointings, and other rites — under the authority of the priesthood. They serve in callings that require spiritual gifts and revelation. They exercise the power of God through faith, prayer, and the gifts of the Spirit.

Paul wrote, “Your sons and your daughters shall prophesy.”Acts 2:17 Daughters prophesy. The gift of prophecy — one of the highest spiritual gifts — belongs to women as fully as to men.

In the primitive church, women prophesied. Philip the evangelist had “four daughters, virgins, which did prophesy.”Acts 21:9 Deborah judged Israel as a prophetess. Judges 4:4 Huldah delivered God's word to the king of Israel. 2 Kings 22:14–20

The question of priesthood offices and ordination is a different question from the question of spiritual gifts, spiritual power, and access to God. Women in this Church lead, teach, serve, counsel, and minister. They officiate in the holiest ordinances of the gospel in the temple. They hold leadership positions over the largest women's organisation in the world.

God has organised His Church in a specific way — with specific responsibilities distributed according to His wisdom, not human preference. We trust His wisdom. The question of why women are not ordained to priesthood offices is a question we put to God — and the answer He has given through His living prophets is the answer we accept.

missionary.simple: Women are not ordained to priesthood offices, but they absolutely exercise priesthood power through their covenants, their spiritual gifts, and their sacred work in the temple.
missionary.explain: 'Your daughters shall prophesy. Women in the early church prophesied, served, and led. The gift is real. The office structure follows God's revealed order — not cultural preference.'
missionary.why: God values women's contributions fully and gives them real access to His power. The organization of those responsibilities follows His wisdom, not human opinion.
missionary.invite: Would you like to speak with a woman in our congregation about what her role in the Church means to her personally?

questions.question: 200. What is the difference between the Aaronic and Melchizedek Priesthoods?
questions.answer:
The Aaronic Priesthood — also called the Levitical Priesthood — is the preparatory priesthood. It was given through Aaron and his sons in Israel and was responsible for the outward ordinances of the Mosaic law — the animal sacrifices, the washings, the maintenance of the tabernacle. Paul described it in Hebrews 7:11 as insufficient alone to bring a person to perfection.

In the restored Church, the Aaronic Priesthood is given to young men beginning at age twelve. It carries the keys of the ministering of angels, the gospel of repentance, and the baptism by immersion for the remission of sins. (Doctrine and Covenants 13.) Its holders administer and pass the sacrament, perform baptisms, and learn to serve.

The Melchizedek Priesthood is the greater, higher priesthood — named after the ancient king-priest Melchizedek to whom Abraham paid tithes. Hebrews 7:1–4 Christ is a “priest forever after the order of Melchisedec.”Hebrews 7:17 This priesthood holds the keys of all the spiritual blessings of the Church — the gift of the Holy Ghost, the healing of the sick, the temple endowment, eternal marriage, and all the higher ordinances of the gospel.

Paul wrote that the Aaronic Priesthood was a schoolmaster bringing Israel to Christ — and that when Christ came, the higher priesthood replaced it as the governing authority. Galatians 3:24; Hebrews 7:12 Both priesthoods exist in the Church today. The Aaronic prepares. The Melchizedek perfects.

missionary.simple: The Aaronic Priesthood handles outward ordinances — baptism, the sacrament. The Melchizedek Priesthood handles the higher ordinances — the Holy Ghost, temple blessings, eternal marriage.
missionary.explain: 'The Aaronic is the preparatory priesthood. The Melchizedek is the fulness. One prepares you for the covenant. The other seals you into it.'
missionary.why: Understanding these two priesthoods explains the whole structure of ordinances in the restored church and what each one does in your life.
missionary.invite: Would you like to know what it means that these two priesthoods have been restored and are active on the earth today?

questions.question: 212. Why are local Bishops — who are not trained therapists — allowed to counsel victims of sexual trauma?
questions.answer:
A bishop's role in counselling is pastoral — not clinical. He does not claim to be a therapist. He claims to be a called and ordained servant of God who holds priesthood keys and can offer priesthood blessings, spiritual guidance, repentance interviews, and the hope of the atonement. These are things no licensed therapist can provide.

When a victim of sexual trauma comes to a bishop, the bishop's role is to receive that person with love, connect them with professional help if needed, support them spiritually, and help them access the healing power of the atonement of Jesus Christ. He is not a replacement for a therapist. He is an addition to one.

James 5:14–15 commanded that those who are sick — physically, emotionally, spiritually — call for the elders of the Church. That command was not conditional on the elders also holding a state licence. The pastoral care of a priesthood holder is a gift of God — different from and complementary to professional mental health care.

The Church has significantly expanded its professional support systems in recent years, with licensed therapists and social workers available through Church-sponsored services in many areas. The bishop does not stand alone. He is part of a system of care.

In cases where individual bishops have not handled sensitive situations well, that reflects the limitations of individuals rather than a flaw in the system itself. Every bishop is a human being who serves voluntarily. The Church provides clear guidelines requiring professional referral for cases of abuse. The system, when followed, protects members. Continued improvement in training and accountability is an ongoing commitment of Church leadership.

missionary.simple: A bishop's role is pastoral, not clinical. He offers priesthood blessings, spiritual guidance, and the healing power of the atonement — things no licensed therapist can provide.
missionary.explain: 'The bishop does not replace a therapist. He adds what a therapist cannot give — the authority of God, the power of the atonement, and the covenant community's support.'
missionary.why: God designed His church to care for the whole person — spiritually, emotionally, and practically.
missionary.invite: Would you like to know how our congregation cares for people who are going through difficult times?

questions.question: 227. If women perform priesthood ordinances like washings in the temple, why can't they hold priesthood offices in the local ward?
questions.answer:
This question reflects a genuine and important distinction in how priesthood power operates. Women who officiate in the washing and anointing ordinances in the temple do so as part of the endowment ceremony — sacred ordinances that directly relate to the covenants and blessings available to women in the temple context.

Church leaders have clarified that faithful women who are endowed exercise priesthood power in performing these ordinances. This is an official recognition, not a concession. Women have always had access to priesthood power — through their covenants, through their callings, and through their exercise of spiritual gifts.

The difference between this and holding a priesthood office — such as bishop, elder, or stake president — is the difference between exercising priesthood power in specific contexts and holding the keys and authority that govern the Church's administration. God has organised those governing responsibilities through ordained male priesthood holders. That is His revealed order for this dispensation.

We do not fully know all the reasons. We know what has been revealed. And what has been revealed is sufficient to understand that God values women's contributions, gives them real and substantive access to priesthood power, and has organised the government of His Church in a specific way that He has not seen fit to change.

missionary.simple: Women who have received the temple endowment exercise priesthood power when they perform sacred ordinances in the temple. That is official Church teaching, not tradition.
missionary.explain: 'The temple is where God's covenant people exercise the full range of priesthood blessings. Women officiate there in real ordinances. The distinction is in office structure, not in access to God's power.'
missionary.why: The restored gospel gives women real and substantive access to God's power — far more than most of Christianity acknowledges.
missionary.invite: Would you like to learn about what happens in the temple and what it means for women who go there?

questions.question: 228. Why did the Presiding Bishopric shift from spiritual judges to managing financial assets?
questions.answer:
The Presiding Bishopric's responsibilities have always included both spiritual and temporal administration. In the primitive Church, the seven men chosen in Acts 6 were set apart to care for the temporal needs of the widows — so the apostles could focus on prayer and the ministry of the word. That is the original pattern — temporal care as a sacred responsibility distinct from but complementary to spiritual leadership.

As the Church has grown to millions of members across 190 countries, the temporal responsibilities have grown proportionately. Managing the consecrated resources of that many members — temples, chapels, welfare programs, humanitarian aid, and investment funds held in reserve for future need — is an enormous and sacred stewardship. The Presiding Bishopric serves as the Church's presiding administrative authority over those temporal matters.

The spiritual dimension has not disappeared. The bishop is still the judge in Israel — the one who conducts worthiness interviews, holds the keys of the Aaronic Priesthood, and oversees the temporal and spiritual care of the ward. At the local level, that spiritual role is as prominent as ever. At the general Church level, the growth in temporal responsibilities reflects the growth in the Church itself.

Paul taught that the Church should have "helps and governments" among its officers. 1 Corinthians 12:28 Government of temporal things is a sacred function — not a descent from the spiritual. God cares about how His consecrated funds are managed.

missionary.simple: The Presiding Bishopric manages the temporal resources of the Church — exactly as the seven men chosen in Acts 6 cared for the widows while apostles focused on the ministry.
missionary.explain: 'Managing consecrated funds for millions of members across 190 countries is a sacred stewardship. God cares how His resources are used.'
missionary.why: Temporal care is not less spiritual than preaching — it is a different expression of the same love for God's children.
missionary.invite: Would you like to know how the Church uses its resources to care for the poor and build the kingdom worldwide?

questions.question: 238. Why does your Church claim to have the only right priesthood authority from God?
questions.answer:
Because that is what the scriptures teach — not our preference, but God's stated method.

Paul wrote that “no man taketh this honour unto himself, but he that is called of God, as was Aaron.”Hebrews 5:4 Aaron did not read the scriptures and decide to be a priest. Moses, a prophet, ordained him. The authority was conferred by a person who had it — not assumed by a person who wanted it.

Christ did the same. He chose twelve apostles. He gave them power and authority. Luke 9:1 He told them, “As my Father hath sent me, even so send I you.”John 20:21 He sent them. They did not send themselves. And they, in turn, ordained others — laying hands on Timothy 1 Timothy 4:14, on Matthias Acts 1:26, on the seven chosen to serve the widows Acts 6:6. The chain of authority was continuous, specific, and transmitted by the laying on of hands.

When the last apostle died, that chain was broken. No institution can pass on what it does not have. A broken chain cannot be repaired by reading the Bible more carefully or by sincerity of belief. It can only be repaired by going back to the original source.

That is exactly what happened. John the Baptist — who held the Aaronic Priesthood — came to Joseph Smith in 1829 and laid hands on him, conferring that priesthood. Peter, James, and John — who held the Melchizedek Priesthood — came and laid hands on Joseph Smith and conferred the higher priesthood. This is the same pattern God has always used. He sends a messenger with authority. He does not leave men to invent their own.

We do not say this to diminish sincere ministers in other churches. We say it because it is true and because every soul deserves the ordinances in their valid form. A minister who truly loves the people he baptises should want those baptisms to be recognised by God. The authority that makes them recognised before God is what we are offering — not criticism, but completion.

missionary.simple: Because that is what the scriptures teach. No man takes this honour upon himself. Christ gave it to His apostles. They passed it on. It was lost in the apostasy and restored through Joseph Smith.
missionary.explain: We are not saying this to dismiss other churches. We are saying it because we love the people in them. Everyone deserves ordinances that are recognized by God — and that requires proper authority.
missionary.why: The chain of authority was broken when the last apostle died. Only God could restore it. He sent messengers. He did.
missionary.invite: Would you be willing to pray and ask God whether this Church holds the authority He restored to the earth?

questions.question: 243. Why don't you pay your bishops and other leaders who sacrifice their time?
questions.answer:
Because that is the pattern Christ established and Paul preserved.

Peter was a fisherman. Andrew was a fisherman. James and John were fishermen. Matthew was a tax collector. Paul was a tentmaker. None of them received a salary for preaching the gospel. Paul said explicitly: “What is my reward then? Verily that, when I preach the gospel, I may make the gospel of Christ without charge, that I abuse not my power in the gospel.”1 Corinthians 9:18 Without charge. That was his deliberate choice — because he understood that a paid ministry creates incentives that corrupt the purity of the calling.

Christ sent His disciples out with specific instructions: carry no purse, no scrip, no shoes — and eat what is given to you by those you serve. Luke 10:4–8 The recompense was hospitality, not salary. The apostles did not build financial empires. They served and were fed.

Paul warned Timothy and Titus that a bishop must not be “greedy of filthy lucre.”1 Timothy 3:3; Titus 1:7 He listed love of money as a disqualifying character flaw for Church leadership. A system that pays leaders to lead creates precisely the incentive Paul identified as dangerous.

Our bishops have professions. They earn their livings outside the Church. They spend their evenings counselling families, their weekends presiding over meetings, and their early mornings serving before the rest of the world wakes up — all without compensation. That is not a burden the Church imposes carelessly. It is the model Christ gave — and it produces leaders who serve because they love God, not because they are employed.

The sacrifice is real. We acknowledge it. But it is also the highest form of consecration — the giving of time, which is the one thing that cannot be replaced. Every bishop who gives those hours to God lays up the kind of treasure Christ described — not here, where moth and rust corrupt, but in heaven, where nothing can take it away. Matthew 6:19–21

missionary.simple: Because Christ established it that way. Paul preached without charge. Peter was a fisherman. Every unpaid leader in this Church follows the pattern of the New Testament exactly.
missionary.explain: 'Our bishop sacrifices hundreds of hours every year — not for a salary, but because he loves God and loves people. That sacrifice is itself a testimony.'
missionary.why: The kingdom of God is built by consecrated people, not employees. That model has never changed.
missionary.invite: Would you like to meet someone who serves without pay and ask them why they do it?

questions.question: 248. Why are patriarchs not allowed to serve in other callings in the Church?
questions.answer:
The office of patriarch is unique in the Church. A patriarch is ordained to a specific and sacred calling — to give patriarchal blessings to the members of his stake. That calling requires spiritual preparation, prayerfulness, and a level of spiritual attentiveness that can be difficult to maintain if the patriarch is simultaneously serving in a demanding administrative calling.

Paul described the gifts of the Spirit as distributed specifically — “to one is given by the Spirit the word of wisdom; to another the word of knowledge.”1 Corinthians 12:8 The patriarch's gift is specifically prophetic and personal — speaking by the Holy Ghost the inspired blessings of individuals. That gift is best exercised when the patriarch's spiritual focus is not divided.

There is also a principle of stewardship. Each calling in the Church has a specific purpose and a specific stewardship. A bishop oversees his ward. A stake president oversees his stake. A patriarch gives patriarchal blessings. Mixing those stewardships can dilute the effectiveness of each.

The Lord organised His Church with specific offices for specific purposes. 1 Corinthians 12:28 The patriarch holds an office that is best fulfilled when it is held exclusively — given the sacred and personal nature of the work he does.

questions.question: 7. Do Latter-day Saints believe they can become gods? Isn't that against the Bible?
questions.answer:
Christ himself said it. In John 10:34–35 He told the Jews, "Is it not written in your law, I said, ye are gods? If he called them gods, unto whom the word of God came, the scripture cannot be broken." That is the mouth of the Savior of the world — not the mouth of Joseph Smith.

Paul wrote that we are “heirs of God and joint-heirs with Christ.”Romans 8:17 If we are joint-heirs with Christ, what do we inherit? All that He has.

Peter wrote that we are to become “partakers of the divine nature.”2 Peter 1:4 That is not a small claim. It is the promise of scripture.

John wrote, “Beloved, now are we the sons of God, and it doth not yet appear what we shall be: but we know that, when he shall appear, we shall be like him.”1 John 3:2 Like Him. That is not our doctrine inventing something new. That is the apostle John speaking under inspiration.

We do not believe we will ever be equal to God the Father or replace Him. We believe that through the grace and atonement of Jesus Christ, faithful children of God can grow to resemble their Father — because that is precisely what He has promised.

missionary.simple: The patriarch's calling is to give prophetic, personal blessings to members of his stake. That sacred focus is best maintained when it is not divided with other demanding leadership roles.
missionary.explain: 'Each calling in the Church has a specific stewardship. The patriarch's gift is prophetic and personal — it requires focused spiritual attentiveness that is best protected by keeping it undivided.'
missionary.why: God organizes His church with specific roles for specific purposes because each one matters and each one serves the whole.
missionary.invite: Would you like to know what a patriarchal blessing is and how receiving one could speak personally to your own life and mission?

questions.question: 15. Do Latter-day Saints believe Jesus and Satan are brothers? How is that possible?
questions.answer:
We believe that all spirit children of God the Father lived with Him before coming to this earth. That includes Lucifer, who rebelled and was cast out. It includes every person born on this earth. And it includes our Lord and Savior Jesus Christ.

Paul wrote in Hebrews 12:9, "We have had fathers of our flesh which corrected us... shall we not much rather be in subjection unto the Father of spirits and live?" The Father of spirits — meaning all spirits came from Him.

The Lord told Jeremiah, “Before I formed thee in the belly I knew thee; and before thou camest out of the womb I sanctified thee.”Jeremiah 1:4–5 Paul wrote that God “hath determined the times before appointed, and the bounds of their habitation.”Acts 17:26 These things were determined before birth because there was a life before birth.

In that pre-mortal life, Christ was not merely a brother in passing — He was the firstborn, the mightiest, the one through whom all creation came. Colossians 1:15–16 Lucifer was among the sons of the morning who chose rebellion over obedience. Isaiah 14:12 The distinction between them is infinite. To say they are spirit brothers is not to diminish Christ. It is to acknowledge that God the Father is the Father of all.

missionary.simple: Yes — because all spirit children of God the Father lived with Him before this earth. That includes every person born here, including Jesus Christ and Lucifer.
missionary.explain: God is called the Father of spirits. All spirits came from Him. Christ was the firstborn and greatest — infinite above Lucifer in every way. Sharing a Father does not make them equal.
missionary.why: Understanding this explains the war in heaven, why evil exists, and why Christ's sacrifice was a choice — not an accident.
missionary.invite: Would you like to understand what the pre-mortal life means and how it changes the way you see your purpose on earth?

questions.question: 30. Why do you believe God has a physical body? The Bible says God is a Spirit.
questions.answer:
John 4:24 says "God is a Spirit" — and Christ said this while speaking to pagan Samaritans who were worshipping idols. His point was that God is not a carved image, not a graven statue. He was correcting idol worship, not giving a complete description of God's physical nature.

The same scriptures say “God is love”1 John 4:8, “God is light”1 John 1:5, and “God is a consuming fire.”Hebrews 12:29 Do you believe God is literally made of fire? Of course not. These are descriptions of His attributes, not definitions of His substance.

Stephen, filled with the Holy Ghost, looked up into heaven and “saw the glory of God, and Jesus standing on the right hand of God.”Acts 7:55–56 He saw two distinct beings. If God were only a spirit with no form, what exactly did Stephen see?

Christ, after His resurrection, said to His apostles, “Behold my hands and my feet, that it is I myself: handle me, and see; for a spirit hath not flesh and bones, as ye see me have.”Luke 24:39 He rose with a body. He ascended with that body. Acts 1:9–11 Hebrews 1:3 tells us He is the "express image" of His Father. If Christ's resurrected body is the express image of the Father, then the Father has a body.

The God of the Bible has form. He appeared to Moses. He walked with Adam. He showed His back to Moses on Sinai. Exodus 33:23 A God with no form, no presence, no image — that is the god of Greek philosophy, not the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob.

🕮 Scripture Evidence: God Has Been Seen

  • Moses — Exodus 33:11: The LORD spoke to Moses face to face, as a man speaks to his friend.
  • Moses — Exodus 33:18-23: Moses is permitted to see God's back, confirming a directional, tangible presence.
  • Moses & the elders — Exodus 24:9-11: Moses, Aaron, and seventy elders saw the God of Israel and ate and drank in His presence.
  • Jacob — Genesis 32:30: “I have seen God face to face, and yet my life is preserved.”
  • Isaiah — Isaiah 6:1-5: Isaiah beheld the Lord sitting upon a throne, high and lifted up.
  • Ezekiel — Ezekiel 1:26-28: Ezekiel describes the appearance of the likeness of the glory of the LORD as a human form.
  • Abraham — Genesis 18:1-2: The LORD appeared to Abraham in visible, tangible form at the oaks of Mamre.
  • Job — Job 42:5: “My eyes have seen you.”

missionary.simple: God has a glorified, physical, resurrected body. That is what the Bible shows. Stephen saw Him. Moses saw Him. Christ showed His hands and feet after the resurrection.
missionary.explain: Christ said a spirit does not have flesh and bones as He has. He rose with a body. Hebrews says He is the express image of His Father's person. If the Son has a body, the Father does too.
missionary.why: A God with a body is a God who can truly understand what it means to live, to suffer, and to love. He is our Father — not an abstract force.
missionary.invite: Would you like to know how this understanding of God changes the way you pray and relate to Him?

questions.question: 31. Why do Latter-day Saints believe in three separate Gods? Isn't that polytheism?
questions.answer:
We do not believe in three Gods. We believe in one Godhead — three separate and distinct beings who are perfectly united in purpose, in will, in testimony, and in truth.

Christ prayed to the Father in Gethsemane. He asked if "this cup" might be removed from him. Matthew 26:39 If He were the Father, He was praying to himself — a reading that does not fit the plain meaning of the text. When He was baptized, a voice came from heaven saying “This is my beloved Son.”Matthew 3:17 The Father was speaking. The Son was standing in the water. The Holy Ghost descended as a dove. Three distinct presences, at the same moment, in the same place.

Christ said in John 17:20–21 that He wanted His disciples to be one "as thou, Father, art in me, and I in thee." He was praying that His disciples would be one — the same way He and the Father are one. Does that mean He wanted His disciples to merge into a single being? No. He meant unity of purpose. That is what He and the Father share.

John 8:17–18 records Christ himself telling the Jews, "It is also written in your law, that the testimony of two men is true. I am one that bear witness of myself, and the Father that sent me is another." He compared himself and the Father to two men. His own words.

Three beings, perfectly unified in purpose — that is not polytheism. That is the Godhead as Christ himself described it.

🕮 Scripture Evidence: God Has Been Seen

  • Moses — Exodus 33:11: The LORD spoke to Moses face to face, as a man speaks to his friend.
  • Moses — Exodus 33:18-23: Moses is permitted to see God's back, confirming a directional, tangible presence.
  • Moses & the elders — Exodus 24:9-11: Moses, Aaron, and seventy elders saw the God of Israel and ate and drank in His presence.
  • Jacob — Genesis 32:30: “I have seen God face to face, and yet my life is preserved.”
  • Isaiah — Isaiah 6:1-5: Isaiah beheld the Lord sitting upon a throne, high and lifted up.
  • Ezekiel — Ezekiel 1:26-28: Ezekiel describes the appearance of the likeness of the glory of the LORD as a human form.
  • Abraham — Genesis 18:1-2: The LORD appeared to Abraham in visible, tangible form at the oaks of Mamre.
  • Job — Job 42:5: “My eyes have seen you.”

missionary.simple: We believe in one Godhead — three separate beings who are perfectly united in purpose, will, and love. That is not polytheism. That is exactly what the New Testament shows.
missionary.explain: At Christ's baptism, the Father spoke from heaven while the Son stood in the water and the Holy Ghost descended as a dove — three separate presences at the same moment. That is the Godhead.
missionary.why: Understanding the true nature of God changes your relationship with Him. He is not a mystery. He is your Father.
missionary.invite: Would you like to read John 17 and ask God what it means that He and Christ are one?

questions.question: 40. Why do you pray to Heavenly Father instead of praying to Jesus?
questions.answer:
Because Christ told us to. When His disciples asked Him how to pray, He said, “After this manner therefore pray ye: Our Father which art in heaven...”Matthew 6:9 He directed prayer to the Father. He did not say pray to me. He said pray to the Father, in my name.

In John 16:23 He said, "Whatsoever ye shall ask the Father in my name, he will give it you." The pattern is clear — we address the Father. We approach through the Son. That is not our invention. It is Christ's own instruction.

Christ himself prayed to the Father. In Gethsemane He said, “O my Father, if it be possible, let this cup pass from me.”Matthew 26:39 At His baptism the Father spoke from heaven while the Son stood in the water. They are not the same person. Prayer directed to the Father, in the name of the Son, through the power of the Holy Ghost — that is the pattern Christ established. We follow it.

missionary.simple: Because Christ told us to. He said pray 'Our Father which art in heaven.' He directed prayer to the Father and told us to approach through Him.
missionary.explain: Christ prayed to the Father in Gethsemane. He did not pray to Himself. We follow the pattern He established — address the Father, come through the Son, guided by the Holy Ghost.
missionary.why: This is not a small detail. It shapes every prayer you pray. You speak to your Father in heaven by name.
missionary.invite: Would you be willing to pray to Heavenly Father tonight and ask Him if He is really there?

questions.question: 46. Why do you believe God and Christ are separate beings? Didn't Christ say "I and my Father are one"?
questions.answer:
He did say that. And then He explained what He meant — in John 17:20–21. He was praying for His disciples, and He said, "That they all may be one; as thou, Father, art in me, and I in thee, that they also may be one in us." He prayed that His disciples would be one — the same way He and the Father are one. He was not praying that His disciples would merge into a single being. He meant unity of purpose, will, and testimony.

If "I and my Father are one" means they are literally the same single being, then we have a serious problem in Acts 7:55–56. Stephen, filled with the Holy Ghost, "looked up stedfastly into heaven, and saw the glory of God, and Jesus standing on the right hand of God, and said, Behold, I see the heavens opened, and the Son of man standing on the right hand of God." He saw two. Distinct. Separate. One standing at the right hand of the other.

At the baptism of Christ, the Father spoke from heaven, the Son stood in the water, and the Holy Ghost descended as a dove — simultaneously. Three separate presences, at the same moment, in the same place. Matthew 3:16–17

Christ himself told Philip, “If ye had known me, ye should have known my Father also.”John 14:7 He also asked, in Gethsemane, "O my Father, if it be possible, let this cup pass from me." If He were the Father, He was begging mercy from himself. Christ's own prayer is the clearest proof of two separate beings.

missionary.simple: Christ said 'I and my Father are one' — meaning one in purpose and love, not one single being. He proved they are separate in dozens of places throughout the scriptures.
missionary.explain: Stephen saw God and Jesus standing at His right hand — two separate beings. At Christ's baptism, both the Father and the Son were present simultaneously. One being cannot be in two places at once.
missionary.why: The simplest reading of the New Testament is that the Father and the Son are two distinct, separate, loving beings — united in everything that matters.
missionary.invite: Would you read John 17 and Acts 7:55–56 and ask God what they teach about His nature?

questions.question: 52. Why do you believe in a Heavenly Mother? Where is that in the Bible?
questions.answer:
Paul wrote in Acts 17:28–29, "For in him we live, and move, and have our being; as certain also of your own poets have said, For we are also his offspring. Forasmuch then as we are the offspring of God..." We are His offspring. Offspring require two parents. If we have a Heavenly Father, the logic of biology — which God himself established — points to a Heavenly Mother.

Paul wrote to the Ephesians, “I bow my knees unto the Father of our Lord Jesus Christ, of whom the whole family in heaven and earth is named.”Ephesians 3:14–15 A family in heaven. Families have mothers.

We do not pray to a Heavenly Mother. We do not have detailed revealed doctrine about Her nature and role beyond what has been given. But the principle that we are God's literal offspring — born of heavenly parents — is consistent with every scriptural description of our relationship to God as Father and children.

The Bible says very little about many true things. The existence of the spirit world is barely explicit. The three degrees of glory are described in scattered verses. The nature of pre-mortal life is hinted at rather than detailed. The silence of the Bible on a topic is not proof that the topic does not hold up under scrutiny. It is often proof of how much was lost in the apostasy — and how much still needs to be restored.

missionary.simple: Paul said we are the offspring of God and that the whole family in heaven and earth is named after the Father. Offspring require two parents. A family in heaven has a mother.
missionary.explain: We do not have detailed revealed doctrine about Her role. But the principle is clear from scripture — we are God's literal children, and families have mothers.
missionary.why: You are not an orphan. You have a Heavenly Father who knows you and a divine heritage that makes you more than you know.
missionary.invite: Would you like to know more about what it means that you are literally a child of God?

questions.question: 53. Why do you believe that God was once a man? Isn't God eternal and uncreated?
questions.answer:
Christ said, “The Son can do nothing of himself, but what he seeth the Father do: for what things soever he doeth, these also doeth the Son likewise.”John 5:19 What did the Son do? He lived as a man. He died. He rose with a glorified, physical, resurrected body. And He said He does only what He sees the Father do.

He went further: “As the Father hath life in himself; so hath he given to the Son to have life in himself.”John 5:26 The Father and the Son have the same kind of life — in themselves. One is in the image of the other. One does what the other has done.

This is not a doctrine that diminishes God. It exalts man. It means the Father has passed through experiences that qualify Him to be our perfect, merciful, understanding Father — not a distant, incomprehensible force, but a being who knows what it means to suffer, to love, to overcome. The Athenasian Creed calls God "incomprehensible." The Bible calls Him knowable. John 17:3 Those are not the same God.

We do not claim full knowledge of how or when. We claim what the scriptures show — that the Father and the Son share the same nature, the same power, and the same kind of life. And that life includes a resurrected, glorified, physical body of flesh and bone.

🕮 Scripture Evidence: God Has Been Seen

  • Moses — Exodus 33:11: The LORD spoke to Moses face to face, as a man speaks to his friend.
  • Moses — Exodus 33:18-23: Moses is permitted to see God's back, confirming a directional, tangible presence.
  • Moses & the elders — Exodus 24:9-11: Moses, Aaron, and seventy elders saw the God of Israel and ate and drank in His presence.
  • Jacob — Genesis 32:30: “I have seen God face to face, and yet my life is preserved.”
  • Isaiah — Isaiah 6:1-5: Isaiah beheld the Lord sitting upon a throne, high and lifted up.
  • Ezekiel — Ezekiel 1:26-28: Ezekiel describes the appearance of the likeness of the glory of the LORD as a human form.
  • Abraham — Genesis 18:1-2: The LORD appeared to Abraham in visible, tangible form at the oaks of Mamre.
  • Job — Job 42:5: “My eyes have seen you.”

missionary.simple: Christ said the Son does only what He sees the Father do. The Son lived, suffered, died, and rose with a glorified body. If the Son does what the Father did, the implication is clear.
missionary.explain: This is not a doctrine that makes God smaller. It makes God more knowable, more personal, more merciful. A Father who understands from experience is a better Father.
missionary.why: The God of the Bible is not an incomprehensible force. He is your Father — a being who knows what mortality feels like.
missionary.invite: Does the idea of a God who truly understands your life change how you feel about turning to Him?

questions.question: 79. Why do you believe that the Holy Ghost is a separate personage and not just a force or influence?
questions.answer:
Because Christ described Him as a person. He said, “When he, the Spirit of truth, comes, he will guide you into all truth: for he shall not speak of himself; but whatsoever he shall hear, that shall he speak.”John 16:13 "He." "Himself." "He shall hear." "He shall speak." These are not descriptions of a force. They are descriptions of a person — a being with will, knowledge, speech, and purpose.

Christ also said, “It is expedient for you that I go away: for if I go not away, the Comforter will not come unto you.”John 16:7 One had to depart before the other could come. If the Holy Ghost were simply the influence or presence of Christ, this statement makes no sense. You cannot separate a person from his influence by leaving the room. But two separate personages can be in different places at the same time — which is exactly what this verse requires.

At the baptism of Christ, the Father spoke from heaven, the Son stood in the water, and the Holy Ghost descended as a dove. Three separate, simultaneous, distinct presences. Matthew 3:16–17 That is the Godhead — three beings, united in purpose, separate in person. The Holy Ghost is the third member of that Godhead — a personage of spirit, distinct from the Father and the Son, carrying the most personal witness available to man.

🕮 Scripture Evidence: God Has Been Seen

  • Moses — Exodus 33:11: The LORD spoke to Moses face to face, as a man speaks to his friend.
  • Moses — Exodus 33:18-23: Moses is permitted to see God's back, confirming a directional, tangible presence.
  • Moses & the elders — Exodus 24:9-11: Moses, Aaron, and seventy elders saw the God of Israel and ate and drank in His presence.
  • Jacob — Genesis 32:30: “I have seen God face to face, and yet my life is preserved.”
  • Isaiah — Isaiah 6:1-5: Isaiah beheld the Lord sitting upon a throne, high and lifted up.
  • Ezekiel — Ezekiel 1:26-28: Ezekiel describes the appearance of the likeness of the glory of the LORD as a human form.
  • Abraham — Genesis 18:1-2: The LORD appeared to Abraham in visible, tangible form at the oaks of Mamre.
  • Job — Job 42:5: “My eyes have seen you.”

missionary.simple: Christ called the Holy Ghost 'He' — a person who hears, speaks, and guides. Not a force. Not an influence. A distinct person and the third member of the Godhead.
missionary.explain: Christ said it is expedient that He go away so the Comforter can come. You cannot separate a person from their influence by leaving the room. This requires two separate beings.
missionary.why: The Holy Ghost is your personal witness of truth. When you feel peace about something you are learning, that is Him speaking to you.
missionary.invite: Have you ever felt a strong sense of peace or certainty about something spiritual? That may have been the Holy Ghost. Would you like to know more?

questions.question: 91. Why do you believe Christ was Jehovah of the Old Testament? The Old Testament clearly distinguishes between God the Father and Christ.
questions.answer:
Because Jehovah and God the Father are not always clearly distinguished by name in the Old Testament — a fact that creates real confusion until the restored gospel clarifies who is who.

The scriptures show in Psalms 110:1–2 and 1 Corinthians 15:24–25 that the one who would put all enemies under His feet was Christ. Since those verses use the name LORD in capitals — which represents Jehovah — Jehovah is Christ.

Isaiah 12:1–2 calls the Lord Jehovah "my strength and my song" and "my salvation." Acts 4:12 declares that "there is salvation by none other" than Jesus Christ — "for there is none other name under heaven given among men, whereby we must be saved." If Jehovah was Isaiah's salvation and Jesus is Peter's salvation, and Jesus is the only name given for salvation, then Jesus is Jehovah.

Zechariah 12:10 records Jehovah speaking and saying, "They shall look upon me whom they have pierced." John 19:37 applies that prophecy to the crucifixion of Christ. Jehovah was pierced. Christ was pierced. Jehovah is Christ.

Revelation 1:8 and 22:12–16 taken together show that the Alpha and Omega is both Jehovah and Jesus. The titles are identical. The person is the same. This was understood by the early Church — it was the apostasy and the creeds that confused it.

missionary.simple: Jehovah of the Old Testament is Jesus Christ. The scriptures show it clearly — every title, every prophecy, every act attributed to Jehovah was fulfilled by Jesus.
missionary.explain: 'Zechariah said they would look on Jehovah whom they pierced. John applies that prophecy directly to the crucifixion of Christ. Jehovah was pierced. Christ was pierced. They are one and the same.'
missionary.why: When you read the Old Testament knowing Christ is Jehovah, the entire Bible becomes a unified testimony of Jesus Christ.
missionary.invite: Would you like to trace that connection through the scriptures for yourself and see how clearly it is taught?

questions.question: 93. Why does your church teach that God has a name — Elohim — and Christ has a different name — Jehovah? Isn't God just one name?
questions.answer:
This is not our invention. It comes directly from the Hebrew text of the Bible. Elohim is the Hebrew word translated as "God" throughout the Old Testament, appearing thousands of times — every time you read "God" in the King James Old Testament, the underlying Hebrew word is almost always Elohim, specifically referring to God the Father. Its very first appearance opens the whole Bible: Genesis 1:1, "In the beginning God created the heaven and the earth." Elohim is itself a plural Hebrew noun, and that plurality becomes visible even in English translation when God says, "Let us make man in our image, after our likeness."Genesis 1:26 Jehovah — rendered LORD in capital letters in most English translations — refers specifically to the God of Israel who led the covenant people, which we have established is Christ. When God revealed His name to Moses at the burning bush, He said, "I AM THAT I AM,"Exodus 3:14 and then identified Himself by name as "the LORD God of your fathers... this is my name for ever."Exodus 3:15

Genesis 1:1 uses Elohim — God the Father directing the creation. Genesis 2:4 introduces Jehovah Elohim — the Lord God — who is Christ working under the direction of the Father. Scholars have wrestled with this distinction for centuries. The restored gospel resolves it.

This distinction is important because it explains apparent contradictions in the Old Testament — cases where God seems to speak to another God, or where the Lord appears to a man while the Father remains in the heavens. They are different beings. The Bible's own Hebrew names for them confirm it.

The Athenasian Creed describes God as "incomprehensible." But the Hebrew text of the Bible gives Him a name. A God who cannot be known has no name. The God of Israel has many names — each one revealing something of His character and mission. Elohim. Jehovah. El Shaddai. I Am. Every name is an invitation to know Him — not a mystery to admire from a distance.

missionary.simple: These are two different Hebrew names for two different beings. Elohim is God the Father. Jehovah is Jesus Christ. The Bible's own Hebrew text makes this distinction thousands of times.
missionary.explain: 'Genesis 1 uses Elohim — the Father directing the creation. Genesis 2 introduces Jehovah Elohim — Christ working under the Father's direction. Two persons, working together, from the very first pages.'
missionary.why: Understanding these names clarifies the nature of the Godhead and shows that the Father and Son were always distinct, always unified, always working together.
missionary.invite: Would you like to see how these two names appear throughout the Old Testament and what they reveal about God?

questions.question: 112. Why do you say that God the Father has a body? Spirit is higher than flesh.
questions.answer:
The idea that spirit is inherently superior to flesh is Greek philosophy — specifically Neo-Platonism — not Bible doctrine. It entered Christianity through the great apostasy as pagan thinking was absorbed into Christian theology. The Bible teaches the opposite.

The resurrection — the reuniting of body and spirit in a glorified, permanent state — is the crowning gift of the atonement. Paul wrote that Christ “shall change our vile body, that it may be fashioned like unto his glorious body.”Philippians 3:21 A glorified body. Not a discarded body. A body fashioned like Christ's — which was “flesh and bones.”Luke 24:39

Christ's resurrection was the first resurrection — the pattern of what ours will be. He kept His body. He showed it to the apostles. He ate with them. He ascended with it. Hebrews 1:3 says He is the "express image" of His Father's person. If the Son has a glorified, physical, resurrected body, and He is the express image of the Father, then the Father also has a glorified, physical, resurrected body.

A God who had no body would have no hands to create, no face for Moses to glimpse, no form for Stephen to see in vision, no voice from heaven at the baptism of Christ. The God who appears in the Bible has a presence, a voice, a glory, and a form. Greek philosophy stripped Him of it. The restoration gave it back.

🕮 Scripture Evidence: God Has Been Seen

  • Moses — Exodus 33:11: The LORD spoke to Moses face to face, as a man speaks to his friend.
  • Moses — Exodus 33:18-23: Moses is permitted to see God's back, confirming a directional, tangible presence.
  • Moses & the elders — Exodus 24:9-11: Moses, Aaron, and seventy elders saw the God of Israel and ate and drank in His presence.
  • Jacob — Genesis 32:30: “I have seen God face to face, and yet my life is preserved.”
  • Isaiah — Isaiah 6:1-5: Isaiah beheld the Lord sitting upon a throne, high and lifted up.
  • Ezekiel — Ezekiel 1:26-28: Ezekiel describes the appearance of the likeness of the glory of the LORD as a human form.
  • Abraham — Genesis 18:1-2: The LORD appeared to Abraham in visible, tangible form at the oaks of Mamre.
  • Job — Job 42:5: “My eyes have seen you.”

missionary.simple: The idea that spirit is higher than flesh is Greek philosophy, not Bible doctrine. God has a glorified, physical, resurrected body — and the resurrection is the crowning gift of the atonement.
missionary.explain: 'Paul said Christ will change our bodies to be like His glorious body. A glorified body is not discarded — it is the destination. God's body is not a limitation. It is perfection.'
missionary.why: A God with a body is a God who fully entered our experience and glorified it. Your body is not a prison — it is a sacred gift with an eternal future.
missionary.invite: Would you like to understand what the resurrection really means and what your own body will become?

questions.question: 117. Why do you believe that God weeps over His children? Doesn't that imply He is weak?
questions.answer:
Enoch saw God weep over the wickedness of His children and asked, “How is it that thou canst weep, seeing thou art holy, and from all eternity to all eternity?”Moses 7:28–29 It is the right question. The answer God gave was that these were His children — all of them — and He had given them agency, and they had used it to destroy each other, and He mourned it.

John 11:35 — "Jesus wept." The shortest verse in the Bible. At the tomb of Lazarus, knowing He was about to raise him from the dead, the Son of God wept. Not from lack of power. Not from ignorance. He wept because He loved Lazarus. Because He felt the grief of those around Him. Because the suffering of His children was real to Him.

Isaiah 63:9 — "In all their affliction he was afflicted." When Israel suffered, God suffered with them. Not because He was powerless to stop it, but because love feels what the beloved feels.

A God who cannot weep is a God who cannot love. A God who cannot love is not the God of the Bible. The strength of God is not the cold indifference of a distant power. It is the capacity to love infinitely — and love inevitably weeps over what it cannot force to be better without destroying the agency that makes goodness possible.

missionary.simple: God weeps because these are His children — and He loves them. Perfect love feels what the beloved feels. God's capacity to weep is not weakness; it is the proof of His perfect love.
missionary.explain: 'When Enoch saw God weeping over His children, he asked the same question you might ask. God answered: they are mine. I gave them agency. And I mourn when they destroy each other.'
missionary.why: A God who weeps is a God who truly loves you — not from a distance, but personally, fully, and with feeling.
missionary.invite: Does the idea that God personally grieves over suffering in your life change how you feel about turning to Him?

questions.question: 149. Why do you believe that Christ is literally the Son of God — not just metaphorically?
questions.answer:
Luke 1:35 records the angel's message to Mary: "The Holy Ghost shall come upon thee, and the power of the Highest shall overshadow thee: therefore also that holy thing which shall be born of thee shall be called the Son of God." The Son of God. Not the symbol of God. Not the metaphor of God. The Son — born of a mortal mother, conceived by the power of God the Father.

John 3:16 — "For God so loved the world, that he gave his only begotten Son." Begotten. That word means something. It means the Father was not just the inspiring force behind Jesus. He was the literal Father of the being born to Mary. That is the doctrine of the New Testament — not metaphor, not symbol, but literal divine sonship.

Paul wrote, “God sent forth his Son, made of a woman, made under the law.”Galatians 4:4 Made of a woman. Born into mortality. A real person, with a real father and a real mother — one divine, one mortal. That combination — the fullness of the Godhead in a mortal body — is what made the atonement possible. Only a being who was both fully mortal and fully divine could suffer all that mortality offers and still have the power to overcome it.

The Athenasian Creed attempts to make this "incomprehensible." The Bible makes it plain. God the Father is the Father. Jesus Christ is His Son. The relationship is real — as real as any father and son relationship on this earth, and infinitely more glorious.

🕮 Scripture Evidence: God Has Been Seen

  • Moses — Exodus 33:11: The LORD spoke to Moses face to face, as a man speaks to his friend.
  • Moses — Exodus 33:18-23: Moses is permitted to see God's back, confirming a directional, tangible presence.
  • Moses & the elders — Exodus 24:9-11: Moses, Aaron, and seventy elders saw the God of Israel and ate and drank in His presence.
  • Jacob — Genesis 32:30: “I have seen God face to face, and yet my life is preserved.”
  • Isaiah — Isaiah 6:1-5: Isaiah beheld the Lord sitting upon a throne, high and lifted up.
  • Ezekiel — Ezekiel 1:26-28: Ezekiel describes the appearance of the likeness of the glory of the LORD as a human form.
  • Abraham — Genesis 18:1-2: The LORD appeared to Abraham in visible, tangible form at the oaks of Mamre.
  • Job — Job 42:5: “My eyes have seen you.”

missionary.simple: Christ is literally the Son of God — conceived by Mary and by the power of God the Father. Not a metaphor. Not a symbol. A literal divine Son born into mortality.
missionary.explain: 'John 3:16 says God gave His only begotten Son. Begotten means something. It means the Father was the actual Father of Jesus — not just an inspiring influence.'
missionary.why: The literal sonship of Christ is what made the atonement possible. Only a being who was both fully mortal and fully divine could do what He did.
missionary.invite: Would you like to know what it means that Christ was both the Son of God and the Son of Man — and why that matters for you?

questions.question: 183. Do you really believe God the Father was once a mortal man who lived on another planet?
questions.answer:
We do not claim that God lived on "another planet" in the science-fiction sense. What we do teach is that the Father has passed through experiences that qualify Him to be the perfect, understanding, empathetic Father He is.

Christ said, “The Son can do nothing of himself, but what he seeth the Father do: for what things soever he doeth, these also doeth the Son likewise.”John 5:19 What did the Son do? He was born. He lived. He suffered. He died. He rose. And He said He does only what He sees the Father do. The implication is clear.

We have established that God has a body of flesh and bone — glorified, exalted, resurrected. Luke 24:39; Hebrews 1:3 A resurrected body is a body that has passed through death and been raised. That is what resurrection means. If the Father has a resurrected body, He has passed through something.

What exactly that means in terms of pre-existent worlds, timelines, and the eternal nature of God — we are humble. We do not have all the details. What we have is the principle: God is not an incomprehensible, formless force. He is our Father — a being who loves us because He knows from experience what we face. His omniscience is not cold and distant. It is warm and personal. And the reason it is personal is that He has lived it.

🕮 Scripture Evidence: God Has Been Seen

  • Moses — Exodus 33:11: The LORD spoke to Moses face to face, as a man speaks to his friend.
