He said, “When ye shall see all these things, know that it is near, even at the doors.”Matthew 24:33 Learn the parable of the fig tree — when the branch is tender and puts forth leaves, summer is near. The signs are given precisely so that the faithful will recognise the season and prepare.

Paul wrote, “But ye, brethren, are not in darkness, that that day should overtake you as a thief.”1 Thessalonians 5:4 The world will be taken by surprise. The faithful should not be. The difference is attention to the signs and preparation for what they announce.

The signs the scriptures describe are not arbitrary. Wars, earthquakes, plagues, the gospel preached in all the world, the gathering of Israel, the building of Zion, the return of Elijah — these are the prophesied markers of the season. Many are being fulfilled today. We watch, we prepare, and we work. We do not set dates — but we do not pretend to be blind to what is unfolding.

missionary.simple: No one knows the day or hour. Christ said so. But He commanded us to know the season — to watch the signs He gave, because those who are prepared will not be taken by surprise.
missionary.explain: 'Paul told the Thessalonians that the day should not overtake them as a thief. The world will be surprised. Those who watch the signs will not be. We watch without setting dates.'
missionary.why: The signs Christ gave are unfolding right now — the gathering of Israel, the gospel going to all nations, the building of temples. We are in the season He described.
missionary.invite: Would you like to study the signs of the times and see how many are being fulfilled in our day?

questions.question: 126. Why do you believe that revelation continues line upon line and precept upon precept?
questions.answer:
Isaiah wrote it. “Precept upon precept, precept upon precept; line upon line, line upon line; here a little, and there a little.”Isaiah 28:10 God gives truth incrementally — not because He is withholding it, but because His children can only receive and process it in portions. The container must be enlarged before more can be poured in.

Paul wrote to the Corinthians, “I have fed you with milk, and not with meat: for hitherto ye were not able to bear it, neither yet now are ye able.”1 Corinthians 3:2 They were not ready for more. God does not force truth on an unprepared mind. He gives what can be received, prepares the receiver, and gives more.

Proverbs 4:18 — "The path of the just is as the shining light, that shineth more and more unto the perfect day." More and more. The light increases. It does not arrive all at once and then stay fixed.

This is why the restoration did not come in a single revelation. Joseph Smith received truth over years — the first vision in 1820, the Aaronic Priesthood in 1829, the Melchizedek Priesthood in 1829, the organisation of the Church in 1830, revelation upon revelation through the following years. Each one built on what came before. The pattern is exactly what Isaiah described — line upon line, precept upon precept, the perfect day still coming.

missionary.simple: Isaiah said God gives truth line upon line, precept upon precept. God gives what we can receive, prepares us for more, and keeps giving. That is how revelation has always worked.
missionary.explain: 'Paul told the Corinthians he fed them milk because they were not ready for meat. God does not dump all truth on an unprepared mind. He gives enough to take the next step, then the next.'
missionary.why: You do not need to understand everything before you can start. God meets you where you are and leads you forward from there.
missionary.invite: What is the next line of truth you feel God is giving you right now? Would you be willing to act on it?

questions.question: 131. Why do you believe that we should seek a personal testimony? Isn't the Church's testimony enough?
questions.answer:
Paul wrote to the saints in Thessalonica — people who already belonged to the Church — “Prove all things; hold fast that which is good.”1 Thessalonians 5:21 Prove all things. Not assume. Not defer to leadership. Prove — personally, individually, through study, prayer, and spiritual experience.

Moroni closes the Book of Mormon with a personal invitation: "And when ye shall receive these things, I would exhort you that ye would ask God, the Eternal Father, in the name of Christ, if these things are not true." Ask God. Personally. The promise of a personal witness is offered to every soul who will sincerely seek it.

James gave the same invitation: “If any of you lack wisdom, let him ask of God, that giveth to all men liberally, and upbraideth not; and it shall be given him.”James 1:5 Every person has access to God. Every person can receive a personal witness of truth from the Holy Ghost.

The Church does not ask anyone to believe on institutional authority alone. We ask them to believe on the testimony of witnesses, study the scriptures, and then take their questions directly to God. The answer they receive will be personal — tailored to their soul — and no power on earth or hell can take that witness away from them once it is given.

missionary.simple: Because the Holy Ghost is the witness God promised to every sincere seeker — and the Church's testimony, however strong, cannot substitute for your own personal witness from God.
missionary.explain: 'Moroni closed the Book of Mormon with a direct challenge: ask God. Not ask a missionary. Not ask a bishop. Ask God. He promises to answer every sincere, real-intent prayer.'
missionary.why: A testimony that belongs to you personally cannot be taken away. A testimony borrowed from someone else can be shaken by the first difficult question.
missionary.invite: Will you ask God directly — tonight, in your own words — whether the Book of Mormon is true and whether this is His restored church?

questions.question: 137. Why do you believe in patriarchal blessings? Isn't that like fortune-telling?
questions.answer:
Patriarchal blessings are not fortune-telling. Fortune-telling claims to reveal fixed futures. A patriarchal blessing declares the spiritual potential and divine destiny of a person — conditioned on faithfulness, not mechanical and predetermined.

The pattern is as old as the Bible. Jacob called his twelve sons and blessed each one according to his character and calling. (Genesis 49.) Moses blessed each of the twelve tribes before his death. (Deuteronomy 33.) Isaac blessed Jacob and Esau — and his words carried weight and consequence. These were not predictions from a crystal ball. They were inspired declarations of potential and covenant identity from a man who held priesthood authority.

Paul wrote that “prophecy is for edification, and exhortation, and comfort.”1 Corinthians 14:3 A patriarchal blessing does all three. It declares a person's lineage in Israel — placing them in the covenant family of Abraham. It identifies gifts and callings that God recognises in that individual. It warns, encourages, and comforts.

The blessing is conditional, not absolute. It is a map, not a guarantee. It shows what is available to the person if they live faithfully. Some elements may be fulfilled in this life, others in the next, others in ways the person never expected. It is a gift of the Holy Ghost through the Patriarch — exactly the kind of prophetic blessing the Bible describes from beginning to end.

questions.question: 155. Why do you declare that you know the Church is true — how can anyone know something spiritual with certainty?
questions.answer:
Paul wrote, “The Spirit itself beareth witness with our spirit, that we are the children of God.”Romans 8:16 The Spirit witnesses. Not suggests. Not implies. Bears witness — the same Greek word used for legal testimony, for the testimony of an eyewitness, for the declaration of something seen and known.

John wrote, “We know that we have passed from death unto life, because we love the brethren.”1 John 3:14 Know. Present tense. Certain knowledge. Not hope, not estimate, not probability. Know.

Peter declared on the day of Pentecost, “Therefore let all the house of Israel know assuredly that God hath made that same Jesus, whom ye have crucified, both Lord and Christ.”Acts 2:36 Know assuredly. That is the language of certainty — not arrogance, but the certainty of a man who had walked with Christ, seen Him die, seen Him risen, and received the Holy Ghost.

The knowledge of spiritual things is different in kind from the knowledge of mathematics or science — but it is not less certain. Christ himself promised, “If any man will do his will, he shall know of the doctrine, whether it be of God, or whether I speak of myself.”John 7:17 Doing produces knowing. The test is not intellectual. It is lived. Every person who has planted the seed of faith and nurtured it with obedience, prayer, and sincere seeking knows what Alma described — the seed swells, it grows, it produces fruit. The fruit is the evidence. The Spirit is the witness. And the witness is sure.

We bear our testimony not to impress or to claim superiority. We bear it because we know — and because the God who gave us this knowledge commanded us to share it with every creature in every nation. That is why we are here.

missionary.simple: The Holy Ghost bears witness — not suggests, not hints. Peter said the Spirit witnesses with our spirit that we are children of God. That is a legal term meaning certain testimony.
missionary.explain: 'John said we know we have passed from death unto life. Know — present tense, certain. This is not arrogance. It is the confident testimony of someone who has received a witness from the third member of the Godhead.'
missionary.why: You can have this same certainty. It is not reserved for special people. It is available to everyone who seeks it with a sincere heart.
missionary.invite: Would you be willing to seek a personal witness from God — and refuse to stop seeking until you receive one?

questions.question: 188. Why is there a patriarchal blessing, and is it like a horoscope?
questions.answer:
The answer is straightforward. A patriarchal blessing is not a horoscope. A horoscope is based on the position of celestial bodies at the time of birth — a mechanical system with no divine involvement. A patriarchal blessing is an inspired declaration given by a man ordained to the office of patriarch, speaking by the power and gift of the Holy Ghost, specifically and personally about the person in front of him.

The pattern is throughout scripture. Jacob called his twelve sons and blessed each one according to his inspired perception of their character and calling. (Genesis 49.) Moses blessed each tribe before his death. (Deuteronomy 33.) Isaac blessed Jacob and Esau — and his words had weight and consequence. These were not horoscopes. They were the inspired declarations of prophetic men speaking under the influence of the Spirit.

A patriarchal blessing declares a person's tribe in Israel — placing them in the covenant lineage of Abraham. It identifies gifts, warnings, callings, and promises conditional on faithfulness. No two blessings are alike. They are specific, personal, and given without preparation or prior knowledge of the person's life.

Members cherish their patriarchal blessings as personal scripture — a communication from a loving Father through an inspired servant. That is as far from a horoscope as the God of the universe is from the movements of Jupiter.

missionary.simple: A patriarchal blessing is not a horoscope. It is an inspired, personal declaration of your lineage in the house of Israel, your spiritual gifts, and God's promises to you — given by a man ordained by priesthood authority.
missionary.explain: 'Jacob called his twelve sons and blessed each one according to his inspired understanding of them. Moses blessed each tribe before his death. A patriarchal blessing follows that same ancient, scriptural pattern.'
missionary.why: A patriarchal blessing is a personal scripture for your life — a message from a loving Father who knows you by name.
missionary.invite: Would you like to know what it takes to receive a patriarchal blessing and what one has meant in the life of someone you could speak with?

questions.question: 229. Why does a patriarchal blessing assign you to a specific Tribe of Israel? Is this literal DNA or symbolic adoption?
questions.answer:
Both — and this is one of the most beautiful doctrines of the restoration. Paul wrote in Galatians 3:27–29 that those who are baptised into Christ have "put on Christ" and are therefore "Abraham's seed, and heirs according to the promise." The covenant transforms the relationship. A Gentile who is baptised into the covenant of Christ genuinely becomes part of the house of Israel — not by blood alone, but by covenant.

At the same time, the Lord told Jeremiah before his birth that He knew him, sanctified him, and ordained him to be a prophet. Jeremiah 1:4–5 The foreordination was real — tied to Jeremiah's actual spirit and actual lineage. Paul declared that God “hath determined the times before appointed, and the bounds of their habitation.”Acts 17:26 The lineage a person is born into is not random. God placed spirits in the bodies and nations where they needed to be.

The tribe declared in a patriarchal blessing is both a statement of actual spiritual lineage — the tribe through whose line that spirit came into this world — and a declaration of covenant adoption where literal lineage is mixed or secondary. Most members are declared of the tribe of Ephraim — reflecting the responsibility given to Ephraim to carry the gospel to the nations in the last days, a role that both born-Ephraimites and adopted covenant-members fulfil together.

missionary.simple: Membership councils are pastoral, not criminal. Their purpose is to protect members, facilitate repentance, and determine whether formal action serves the person's spiritual welfare — not to punish.
missionary.explain: 'The council is private to protect the member. The bishop has no financial stake and no adversarial role. His goal is restoration — exactly what James commanded when he said confess your faults to one another.'
missionary.why: God designed His church to help people come back to Him, not to condemn them. The council is a tool of mercy, not judgment.
missionary.invite: Would you like to understand how the Church supports members through serious mistakes and helps them return fully to God?

questions.question: 232. Is the Prophet infallible, or just a man of his time when his past teachings are proven wrong?
questions.answer:
Neither extreme is correct. The Prophet is not infallible — and the Church has never taught that he is. He is a mortal man, called by God, fallible in personal opinions and subject to the cultural assumptions of his time like any human being. The history of the Church demonstrates this. Some teachings of past prophets have been revised, corrected, and in some cases disavowed.

But he is also not merely "a man of his time" in all things — because if everything he teaches can be dismissed as culturally conditioned, then prophetic revelation means nothing and the whole claim of restoration collapses.

The distinction is between a prophet speaking as a prophet and a prophet speaking as a man. Joseph Smith himself made this distinction. "Not every word a president speaks is the word of God." (Joseph Fielding Smith.) The standard for binding revelation is clear: the First Presidency and Quorum of the Twelve acting unanimously, sustained by the body of the Church, and canonised in the standard works. Official declarations and solemn proclamations carry prophetic weight. Personal opinions, cultural assumptions, and informal teachings do not automatically carry the same weight.

Where past prophets taught things that reflected cultural bias rather than revelation — as in the racial theories surrounding the priesthood restriction — the Church has acknowledged those as the opinions of men, not the word of God. Where past prophets taught things that were later confirmed by revelation to be correct — as in the Word of Wisdom's warnings about tobacco decades before medical science confirmed them — the divine origin is plain.

The test is the same one Moses gave: does it come to pass? Does it bear good fruit? The prophetic track record of this Church — evaluated honestly — is remarkable. We trust the office. We also hold the man within it to the standard God established.

questions.question: 249. How can I pray to know the truth?
questions.answer:
The promise is from the Book of Mormon — and it is as direct as any promise in scripture. Moroni wrote: "And when ye shall receive these things, I would exhort you that ye would ask God, the Eternal Father, in the name of Christ, if these things are not true; and if ye shall ask with a sincere heart, with real intent, having faith in Christ, he will manifest the truth of it unto you, by the power of the Holy Ghost." Moroni 10:4

The conditions are three. Sincerity — you must genuinely want to know, not look for reasons to dismiss. Real intent — you must be willing to act on whatever answer you receive. Faith in Christ — you must approach God through His Son, trusting that God is willing and able to answer.

James said it plainly: “If any of you lack wisdom, let him ask of God, that giveth to all men liberally, and upbraideth not; and it shall be given him.”James 1:5 God does not mock a sincere question. He answers it.

Find a quiet place. Kneel before God. Tell Him honestly what you are seeking. Ask whether the Book of Mormon is true, whether Joseph Smith was a prophet, whether the Church is His restored Church. Then be still and listen.

The answer will not always come like thunder. More often it comes as Paul described — “the peace of God, which passeth all understanding.”Philippians 4:7 A quiet certainty. A settled conviction. A warmth in the heart that the Holy Ghost uses to say — this is true.

Act on whatever light you receive. More will follow.

missionary.simple: Find a quiet place. Kneel. Address Heavenly Father by name. Ask sincerely — with real intent to act on the answer — whether the Book of Mormon is true and whether this is His church.
missionary.explain: 'Moroni promised that God will manifest the truth by the power of the Holy Ghost to everyone who asks with a sincere heart and real intent. The conditions are yours to meet. The answer is His to give.'
missionary.why: You do not have to take our word for anything. God made Himself available to answer you personally. That offer has never been withdrawn.
missionary.invite: Will you kneel and pray about this tonight — and keep seeking until you receive your answer?

questions.question: 8. Why does your church have temples that others are not allowed to enter?
questions.answer:
The Lord himself restricted access to sacred places throughout all of scripture. The tabernacle in the wilderness had courts that only the Levites could enter. The inner court was for priests only. The Holy of Holies was entered by the High Priest alone, and only once a year. (Exodus 26; Leviticus 16.) That was not exclusion — that was consecration.

Solomon built a temple. (1 Kings 6.) The Lord filled it with His glory. Not everyone walked in freely. The pattern of the Lord has always been to set apart a holy place for holy ordinances.

Ezekiel foresaw the restoration of the Lord's house and described in detail who would be permitted to enter and who would not. Ezekiel 44:9 The Lord does not change that principle simply because men have grown uncomfortable with it.

Our temples are for those who have made specific covenants with God and who are living those covenants. They are not locked against seekers of truth — they are set apart for those who have prepared themselves. Any worthy member of any nation, language, or background may receive a temple recommend and enter. The requirement is worthiness, not birth or privilege.

The Lord promised in revelation: "All the pure in heart that shall come into it shall see God."D&C 97:16 The temple is not a private club. It is the house of God — and He promises His presence to those who are prepared to meet Him there.

missionary.simple: God has always restricted sacred places. Only the high priest entered the Holy of Holies. The temple requires preparation — not exclusion. Any worthy person from any nation may enter.
missionary.explain: 'The tabernacle in Moses's day had courts that only priests could enter. The innermost court was for the High Priest alone. That is not exclusion — that is consecration. Our temples work the same way.'
missionary.why: The preparation required to enter the temple is not a barrier — it is an invitation to live in a way that qualifies you for God's greatest blessings.
missionary.invite: Would you like to know what it takes to prepare to enter the temple and what happens inside?

questions.question: 9. What really happens inside your temples? Why is it kept private?
questions.answer:
The ordinances performed in the temple are sacred — not secret. There is a difference. Sacred things are held with reverence and not shared in common settings. That is a principle found throughout scripture.

Paul was "caught up to the third heaven" and heard things “not lawful for a man to utter.”2 Corinthians 12:2–4 Did that make Paul dishonest or secretive? No. Some things are sacred, and treating them as sacred is an act of reverence, not concealment.

Christ instructed His disciples, “Give not that which is holy unto the dogs, neither cast ye your pearls before swine.”Matthew 7:6 That is not secrecy. That is the proper handling of sacred things.

In the temple, we make covenants with God. We receive instruction about our relationship to the Father, the Son, and the eternal plan of salvation. Families are sealed together for time and eternity. Proxy work is performed for those who died without the opportunity to receive gospel ordinances. These are the most holy ordinances of the gospel of Jesus Christ — and we treat them accordingly. We do not hide them out of shame. We protect them out of reverence.

Jacob testified: "The keeper of the gate is the Holy One of Israel; and he employeth no servant there."2 Nephi 9:41 The covenants of the temple are administered under the direction of the Holy One of Israel Himself. What happens inside is between the soul and God.

🕮 Scripture Evidence: Spirit World & Salvation for the Dead

  • 1 Corinthians 15:29 — “Else what shall they do which are baptized for the dead, if the dead rise not at all? why are they then baptized for the dead?”
  • 1 Peter 3:18-20 — Christ was “put to death in the flesh, but quickened by the Spirit: By which also he went and preached unto the spirits in prison.”
  • 1 Peter 4:6 — “For for this cause was the gospel preached also to them that are dead, that they might be judged according to men in the flesh, but live according to God in the spirit.”
  • Luke 16:22-23 — The beggar Lazarus was carried to Abraham’s bosom after death — showing distinct realms in the afterlife.
  • Luke 23:43 — Jesus tells the thief: “To day shalt thou be with me in paradise.”
  • Ecclesiastes 12:7 — “The spirit shall return unto God who gave it.”
  • Philippians 1:23 — Paul expresses desire “to depart, and to be with Christ” — confirming conscious spirit world existence.
  • Hebrews 12:23 — References “the spirits of just men made perfect” — confirming ongoing spirit progression.

missionary.simple: Sacred ordinances are made in the temple — covenants with God about your life, your family, and your eternal future. They are held in reverence, not secrecy.
missionary.explain: 'Paul heard things in the third heaven that were not lawful to utter. Sacred things belong in sacred settings. The covenants we make in the temple are too precious to be treated as casual conversation.'
missionary.why: What happens in the temple transforms lives — families sealed together for eternity, individuals empowered with God's promises for their journey.
missionary.invite: Would you like to prepare yourself to one day enter the temple and receive those covenants for yourself?

questions.question: 36. Why do you believe marriage is eternal? Christ said there is no marriage in heaven.
questions.answer:
Christ said that to a Sadducee — a man who did not even believe in the resurrection. Matthew 22:23 He was answering a question about a hypothetical scenario the Sadducees used to challenge the resurrection, not providing a complete theology of eternal marriage. He said they would not marry in the resurrection — meaning the ceremony cannot be performed after death. It must be performed before. That is exactly what we teach.

He answered a Pharisee very differently on the same subject. When the Pharisees asked about divorce, Christ said, “Whatsoever God hath joined together, let not man put asunder.”Matthew 19:6 If God joins two people together, what power does death have over that? Ecclesiastes 3:14 says, "I know that, whatsoever God doeth, it shall be for ever."

Peter told the saints that husbands and wives are “heirs together of the grace of life.”1 Peter 3:7 Paul wrote, “Neither is the man without the woman, neither the woman without the man, in the Lord.”1 Corinthians 11:11 In the Lord. Not just in this life.

Christ gave His apostles the power to bind on earth what would be bound in heaven. Matthew 16:19; 18:18 If that power is real — and Christ said it was — then a marriage sealed by that authority is sealed in both worlds. The Sadducee's wife was never sealed to any of her seven husbands by divine authority. That is why Christ said she would not belong to any of them.

The Lord revealed through Joseph Smith: "If a man marry a wife by my word, and it is sealed unto them by the Holy Spirit of promise... it shall be done unto them in all things whatsoever my servant hath put upon them, in time, and through all eternity."D&C 132:19 The sealing power is real. Death has no jurisdiction over what God has bound.

missionary.simple: Christ said that to a Sadducee who denied the resurrection entirely. His point was that the ceremonial Mosaic marriage ends at death. A marriage sealed by divine authority — given by God — stands forever.
missionary.explain: 'Christ said what God has joined together, let not man put asunder. If God joins two people by His own authority, what power does death have over that? Ecclesiastes says whatever God does shall be forever.'
missionary.why: Your family does not have to end at death. The sealing ordinance makes eternal family relationships real and binding.
missionary.invite: Would you like to understand what it means to be sealed to your family forever — and what it takes to receive that blessing?

questions.question: 45. Why does your church spend so much money on temples and buildings instead of helping the poor?
questions.answer:
The Lord commanded the building of temples. He commanded Moses to build the tabernacle. He commanded Solomon to build the temple. He commanded the Israelites returning from Babylon to rebuild the temple before attending to their own houses. Haggai 1:4–8 Sacred buildings for sacred ordinances are not an extravagance — they are an obedience.

Christ did not rebuke the woman who poured precious ointment on His feet. When Judas complained that it should have been sold and given to the poor, Christ answered, “Why trouble ye the woman? for she hath wrought a good work upon me. For ye have the poor always with you; but me ye have not always.”Matthew 26:10–11 The worship of God and the care of the poor are both commanded. One does not cancel the other.

As for caring for the poor — the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints operates one of the largest private welfare and humanitarian systems in the world. It feeds the hungry, rehabilitates addicts, provides clean water, assists in disaster relief across every continent, and does it all without passing a collection plate or seeking credit. Matthew 25:34–40 is not our decorative verse. It is our operating instruction.

Nephi himself built a temple among the very first acts of his people in the promised land. 2 Nephi 5:16 The pattern is consistent: where God's covenant people gather, they build His house first. The temple is always the priority — and service to the poor follows naturally from the covenants made inside it.

missionary.simple: God commanded Moses to build the tabernacle. He commanded Solomon to build the temple. Sacred buildings for sacred ordinances are not extravagance — they are obedience.
missionary.explain: 'When Judas complained that the ointment should have been sold for the poor, Christ said the poor you always have with you. The worship of God and the care of the poor are both commanded — neither cancels the other.'
missionary.why: The Church also operates one of the largest private humanitarian programs in the world — feeding, clothing, and caring for the poor on every continent.
missionary.invite: Would you like to see both sides of this — the temples we build and the service we give — and ask God whether this is how He wants His resources used?

questions.question: 92. Why do you believe that only those married in the temple can reach the highest degree of glory?
questions.answer:
Paul wrote, “Neither is the man without the woman, neither the woman without the man, in the Lord.”1 Corinthians 11:11 The highest form of eternal life — to dwell with God and Christ in the celestial kingdom — requires the fulfilment of all the covenants of the gospel, including eternal marriage. Not because God is exclusive, but because the nature of that kingdom includes family relationships sealed by divine authority.

Christ gave Peter the power to "bind on earth" what would be “bound in heaven.”Matthew 16:19 If that power exists — and Christ said it does — then a marriage sealed by that authority is sealed in both realms. A marriage performed without that authority ends at death. "Till death do you part" is not our covenant — because death does not have the final word over what God has joined.

For those who did not have the opportunity to receive this ordinance in this life — through no fault of their own — temple work after death makes it available to them. A just God does not condemn a person for failing to receive an ordinance they never had the chance to perform. He provides the opportunity. The decision remains theirs.

The requirement is not exclusion. It is completion. The highest kingdom requires the fullest covenant. And for those who seek it sincerely, the Lord provides every opportunity to receive it — whether in this life or in the next.

The Lord declared through Joseph Smith: "In the celestial glory there are three heavens or degrees; And in order to obtain the highest, a man must enter into this order of the priesthood — meaning the new and everlasting covenant of marriage."D&C 131:1–3 The highest degree requires the highest covenant. This is the doctrine of God — not tradition of men.

missionary.simple: The highest degree of eternal life with God requires the fulness of all covenants — including eternal marriage. Not because God is exclusive, but because that kingdom includes families sealed by His authority.
missionary.explain: 'Paul said neither is the man without the woman, neither the woman without the man, in the Lord. The highest kingdom is a family kingdom. You cannot inherit it alone — you need to be sealed.'
missionary.why: This is the most hopeful doctrine about marriage in all of religion. Death is not the end of your family. The sealing makes it eternal.
missionary.invite: Would you like to know what eternal marriage actually involves and how you can receive that ordinance?

questions.question: 97. Why do you believe that families can be together forever? Doesn't the Bible say relationships end at death?
questions.answer:
Show us where the Bible says that. Not the Sadducee's hypothetical scenario — which Christ answered by pointing out their ignorance of “the scriptures and the power of God.”Matthew 22:29 Show us the scripture that says love ends, relationships cease, and families dissolve at the moment of death.

It is not there — because God never said it.

What you find instead is Christ promising that what is bound on earth by divine authority is bound in heaven. Matthew 16:19 What you find is Paul writing that neither death “nor life... nor things present, nor things to come... shall be able to separate us from the love of God which is in Christ Jesus our Lord.”Romans 8:38–39 If the love of God cannot be separated by death, why would the love between His children be separated by death?

What you find is Peter telling husbands and wives that they are “heirs together of the grace of life.”1 Peter 3:7 Together. In the grace of life. That does not end when mortality does.

What you find is Paul writing that "the whole family in heaven and earth" is named after the Father. Ephesians 3:14–15 A family in heaven. Heaven has families. The doctrine of eternal families is not our invention. It is the implication of every scripture that speaks of God as Father, Christ as the Son, and us as His children — related to Him and to each other by bonds that no grave can dissolve.

missionary.simple: Show us where the Bible says relationships end at death. It is not there. What the Bible does say is that what God joins cannot be put asunder and that whatever God does shall be forever.
missionary.explain: 'Peter said husbands and wives are heirs together of the grace of life — together. Paul said the whole family in heaven and earth is named after the Father. Heaven has families.'
missionary.why: The people you love most do not have to be temporary. The sealing ordinance makes the most important relationships in your life permanent.
missionary.invite: Is there someone in your family you would want to be with forever? Would you like to know what it takes to make that possible?

questions.question: 139. Why does your church believe that the millennium will involve temple work and gospel preaching? Won't everything be settled by then?
questions.answer:
Not everything will be settled. Billions of people have lived and died without hearing the gospel in its fullness. The millennium — a thousand years of Christ's personal reign on the earth — provides the time and conditions to complete that work.

Isaiah described the millennium as a time when “the earth shall be full of the knowledge of the Lord, as the waters cover the sea.”Isaiah 11:9 That fullness has not yet been reached. It will be reached during the millennium as the gospel is preached to every soul — living and dead.

Hebrews 11:40 — the faithful dead "without us should not be made perfect." The work of perfecting them requires us. The ordinances for the dead — baptism, confirmation, endowment, sealing — cannot all be completed before the millennium. There are too many. The millennium is the time appointed to complete them.

Christ will reign personally. Satan will be bound. The veil between the mortal and spirit world will be much thinner. The dead and the living will cooperate in ways not possible in this fallen world. The temples will operate continuously. Families will be united. The great work of redemption will reach its completion — "that they without us should not be made perfect, and that we without them should not be made perfect." (D&C 128:18.) Both are needed. Both will be there.

🕮 Scripture Evidence: Spirit World & Salvation for the Dead

  • 1 Corinthians 15:29 — “Else what shall they do which are baptized for the dead, if the dead rise not at all? why are they then baptized for the dead?”
  • 1 Peter 3:18-20 — Christ was “put to death in the flesh, but quickened by the Spirit: By which also he went and preached unto the spirits in prison.”
  • 1 Peter 4:6 — “For for this cause was the gospel preached also to them that are dead, that they might be judged according to men in the flesh, but live according to God in the spirit.”
  • Luke 16:22-23 — The beggar Lazarus was carried to Abraham’s bosom after death — showing distinct realms in the afterlife.
  • Luke 23:43 — Jesus tells the thief: “To day shalt thou be with me in paradise.”
  • Ecclesiastes 12:7 — “The spirit shall return unto God who gave it.”
  • Philippians 1:23 — Paul expresses desire “to depart, and to be with Christ” — confirming conscious spirit world existence.
  • Hebrews 12:23 — References “the spirits of just men made perfect” — confirming ongoing spirit progression.

missionary.simple: Billions of people have lived and died without hearing the gospel. The millennium provides 1,000 years for that work to be completed — temple ordinances performed, the gospel taught to all who never heard it.
missionary.explain: 'Hebrews says the faithful dead without us should not be made perfect. They need something from us — the ordinances we perform in the temple. The millennium is when that work reaches completion for all generations.'
missionary.why: God's plan leaves no one behind. Every person who ever lived will have a full and fair opportunity to receive the gospel.
missionary.invite: Would you like to understand the full scope of God's plan for every person who has ever lived — and your role in it?

questions.question: 150. Why do you teach that families sealed in the temple remain together after death? What about people who were never given that opportunity?
questions.answer:
For those who had the opportunity and accepted it — their sealing is eternal. What God has joined, death cannot dissolve. Matthew 16:19; Ecclesiastes 3:14 The covenant made at the altar of the temple is a covenant for time and for all eternity — bound by the sealing power that Elijah restored, that Peter held, that Christ conferred.

For those who never had the opportunity — God in His infinite mercy provides it. That is the entire purpose of temple work for the dead. Every person who lived without the opportunity to receive the sealing ordinance will have that opportunity extended to them — either through proxy work by the living or through the ordinances performed during the millennium.

The God of perfect justice does not assign eternal loneliness to a faithful wife who spent her life in a culture where the temple was not available. He does not separate children from parents because of geography or timing. He provides the ordinance — and He leaves the choice to the individual.

Joseph Smith wrote, “The greatest responsibility in this world that God has laid upon us is to seek after our dead.”Documentary History of the Church 6:313 The responsibility is ours — to provide the ordinances. The decision is theirs — to accept or reject. God's justice and mercy both operate perfectly in this system. Not one soul who sought God will be lost for want of an ordinance. That is the promise.

🕮 Scripture Evidence: Spirit World & Salvation for the Dead

  • 1 Corinthians 15:29 — “Else what shall they do which are baptized for the dead, if the dead rise not at all? why are they then baptized for the dead?”
  • 1 Peter 3:18-20 — Christ was “put to death in the flesh, but quickened by the Spirit: By which also he went and preached unto the spirits in prison.”
  • 1 Peter 4:6 — “For for this cause was the gospel preached also to them that are dead, that they might be judged according to men in the flesh, but live according to God in the spirit.”
  • Luke 16:22-23 — The beggar Lazarus was carried to Abraham’s bosom after death — showing distinct realms in the afterlife.
  • Luke 23:43 — Jesus tells the thief: “To day shalt thou be with me in paradise.”
  • Ecclesiastes 12:7 — “The spirit shall return unto God who gave it.”
  • Philippians 1:23 — Paul expresses desire “to depart, and to be with Christ” — confirming conscious spirit world existence.
  • Hebrews 12:23 — References “the spirits of just men made perfect” — confirming ongoing spirit progression.

missionary.simple: For those who kept their covenants — the sealing is eternal. For those who never had the opportunity — God provides it through temple work after death. His plan leaves no one without an opportunity.
missionary.explain: 'Joseph Smith said the greatest responsibility God has laid upon us is to seek after our dead. The living provide the ordinances. The dead make the choice. God's justice and mercy both operate perfectly.'
missionary.why: Not one soul who sincerely wanted God will be lost for want of an ordinance they never had the chance to receive.
missionary.invite: Do you have ancestors who may not have heard the gospel? Would you like to learn how you can help them receive these blessings?

questions.question: 178. Why are the Temple ceremonies so similar to Masonic rituals?
questions.answer:
Joseph Smith joined the Freemasons in March 1842, and the temple endowment was introduced shortly after. Surface similarities exist — ceremonial clothing, symbolic gestures, and certain formulas. Joseph Smith's own explanation was that ancient sacred ceremonies, including those of Solomon's temple, had been preserved over centuries in fragmented and imperfect form across various traditions, including Freemasonry. The restoration brought those ordinances back in their complete and pure form through revelation.

This pattern is consistent throughout scripture. Moses grew up in Egypt surrounded by Egyptian religious ceremony, yet the ordinances he established for Israel came by revelation from God. The presence of similarities between one tradition and another does not mean one copied the other. It means both drew from a common ancient divine source.

Scholars have identified clear parallels between the temple endowment and ancient Egyptian initiation texts — texts that were not translated until decades after Joseph Smith's death and were inaccessible to him in 1842. This rules out simple borrowing. The endowment reflects an ancient pattern of sacred ceremony that predates Freemasonry by centuries.

The temple endowment is a series of sacred covenants made with God. Members who have received it testify that it is spiritually deep, personally meaningful, and unlike anything they have encountered elsewhere. It is not a religious novelty — it is a restoration of what God has always taught His covenant people.

questions.question: 179. Why do members wear temple garments, and what is their actual purpose?
questions.answer:
The wearing of sacred clothing is a principle as old as the Aaronic priesthood. Aaron and his sons wore specific garments when performing sacred ordinances. (Exodus 28.) The garment had specific design elements — a breastplate, an ephod, a robe, a mitre — each with spiritual significance. To wear the garment was to be marked as set apart for holy service.

Temple garments in our Church are worn by members who have received the endowment. They serve as a personal reminder of the covenants made in the temple — to serve God, to keep His commandments, to walk in righteousness. They are a constant, physical symbol of the covenant — worn close to the body as a reminder that the covenant is carried with the person at all times.

Paul wrote, “Put on the whole armour of God.”Ephesians 6:11 The armour of God is both spiritual and symbolic — representing the protection that comes from living the gospel. The garment is a physical expression of that spiritual armour. Members who wear it faithfully report a sense of spiritual protection and constant covenant consciousness.

We do not claim the garment is magic. We claim it is sacred. There is a difference. Sacred things change the person who treats them with reverence. The garment changes the wearer — not by mystical power, but by the daily reminder of who they are and whose they are.

missionary.simple: Temple garments are worn by members who have received the endowment as a physical reminder of their covenants — carried close to the body because the covenant covers the whole person.
missionary.explain: 'Aaron and his sons wore specific garments as a mark of their consecration to God. Paul wrote about putting on the whole armour of God. The garment is a physical expression of that spiritual commitment.'
missionary.why: Sacred things that are carried on your person become daily reminders of who you are and whose you are.
missionary.invite: Would you like to learn more about the covenants made in the temple and what they mean for the way a member lives every day?

questions.question: 180. Are there secret handshakes or passwords required to enter heaven?
questions.answer:
This deserves a thoughtful and respectful answer.

In the temple endowment, members make covenants and receive instruction about their relationship with God. Part of that instruction involves symbolic tokens and signs — physical gestures that accompany the covenant-making, much as a handshake seals a covenant between men. These are not passwords that unlock a mechanical gate. They are symbols of the covenants made — the outward signs of an inward commitment.

The pattern of token and sign in covenant-making is throughout scripture. The rainbow was a token of the covenant with Noah. Genesis 9:12–13 Circumcision was a token of the covenant with Abraham. Genesis 17:11 The Passover lamb's blood on the doorposts was a token that protected Israel. Sacred ordinances have always had physical signs that accompany the spiritual covenant.

When a resurrected, glorified being stands before God, the question is not whether they know a password. The question is whether they have kept the covenants those tokens represent — whether their character has been shaped by those commitments into something fit to dwell in God's presence. The tokens and signs are representations of inward realities. We treat them with reverence, not as mechanical keys.

missionary.simple: The tokens and signs in the temple are symbolic representations of the covenants made — not passwords that unlock a mechanical gate. They are the outward signs of inward commitments.
missionary.explain: 'The rainbow was a token of God's covenant with Noah. Circumcision was a token of the covenant with Abraham. Sacred covenants have always had physical signs that accompany them. The temple follows this eternal pattern.'
missionary.why: The covenants themselves are commitments to live the gospel — chastity, consecration, obedience. The signs represent those inward realities.
missionary.invite: Would you like to understand what the covenants in the temple actually involve and how they bless the lives of those who keep them?

questions.question: 181. Why were the penalties — symbolic gestures of death — removed from the temple ceremony in 1990?
questions.answer:
The temple ceremony has been adjusted several times since Joseph Smith introduced it. The Church has consistently said that the endowment can be refined and updated by revelation through the living prophet — because we have a living prophet who holds the keys to do exactly that.

The penalties that were removed in 1990 were symbolic gestures representing the seriousness of covenant-breaking — drawn across the throat and chest. They were not threats. They were dramatic representations of the spiritual consequences of breaking sacred covenants — the same way that the symbolic tearing of garments in the Old Testament represented mourning and serious consequence.

When the Church determined that these gestures were being misunderstood and were creating unnecessary obstacles to the spiritual purposes of the ceremony, the prophet acted under revelation to remove them. The covenants remain. The seriousness remains. The symbolic gestures that were causing distraction were removed.

Paul wrote, “Let all things be done decently and in order.”1 Corinthians 14:40 The living prophet has the authority and the responsibility to ensure that the ordinances of the temple serve their purpose — to bring members closer to God, not to distract them. That is what he did in 1990. The keys of the kingdom include the authority to administer the ordinances in the way the Lord directs for each dispensation.

missionary.simple: The penalties — symbolic gestures representing the seriousness of covenant-breaking — were removed in 1990 because the Church determined they were being misunderstood and creating unnecessary obstacles. The covenants remain completely intact.
missionary.explain: 'Living prophets hold the keys to administer the ordinances in the way the Lord directs for each dispensation. The covenants are eternal. The specific ceremonial forms can be refined.'
missionary.why: The fact that the prophet updated the ceremony is evidence that we have a living prophet — not that the ordinances are made up.
missionary.invite: Would you like to understand what covenants members actually make in the temple and what those commitments mean for everyday life?

questions.question: 182. Is it true that members take oaths in the temple that they cannot reveal to outsiders?
questions.answer:
Members make sacred covenants in the temple. They commit to keep those covenants confidential — not because the covenants are shameful or dangerous, but because they are sacred. The principle is the same one we cited from Matthew 7:6 — "Give not that which is holy unto the dogs, neither cast ye your pearls before swine." Sacred things are protected by reverence, not because they cannot bear examination.

Paul himself received sacred things in vision that were “not lawful for a man to utter.”2 Corinthians 12:4 That did not make him secretive or deceptive. It made him a steward of sacred things that required a sacred setting to be properly received.

The covenants themselves are not dangerous or sinister. They are commitments to live the law of the gospel — chastity, consecration, obedience to God's commandments, and dedication to the building of God's kingdom. These are the same principles taught in every gospel setting. The temple simply provides the sacred setting in which those commitments are made in their most formal, binding, and eternal form.

What members protect is the specific language and symbols of the ceremony — not the principle. We freely tell the world we covenant to keep the law of chastity. We freely tell the world we covenant to consecrate our time and talents to God. What we keep sacred is the exact form in which those covenants are made — because the form is sacred, not because it is incriminating.

missionary.simple: Members commit to keep temple covenants confidential — not because they are shameful, but because sacred things deserve sacred settings. The covenants themselves are commitments to live the gospel.
missionary.explain: 'Paul heard things in the third heaven that were not lawful to utter. Sacred things handled carelessly lose their power. The covenants we protect are commitments to chastity, consecration, and obedience to God — nothing sinister.'
missionary.why: Protecting sacred things is an act of reverence, not concealment. What we protect is precious — not incriminating.
missionary.invite: Would you like to know what the temple covenants involve and how members who keep them describe the change they bring to daily life?

questions.question: 195. What is the Second Anointing, and is it a secret ritual for high-level leaders?
questions.answer:
The Second Anointing — also called the Second Endowment or Calling and Election Made Sure — is a sacred ordinance in which a person's exaltation is sealed by divine authority. Peter described it in 2 Peter 1:10 as making your "calling and election sure." Paul described it in Hebrews 6:19–20 as an anchor for the soul, entering within the veil.

It is not a secret ritual for elite members. It is a sacred ordinance that the Lord has not made universally available in this dispensation and which has historically been given sparingly. It is not advertised because it is sacred. It is not discussed publicly because the Lord has not directed it to be a matter of public discussion.

Joseph Smith taught that it was the privilege of every faithful member to eventually have their calling and election made sure — their salvation sealed by the Holy Spirit of Promise. The ordinance formalises what the Spirit can also witness directly — that a person has been found faithful and their place in the celestial kingdom is secure.

It is not a club for insiders. It is not a guarantee that a person can sin without consequence afterwards. It is a sacred confirmation of a life of faithfulness — given by God through His appointed servants to those He chooses to honour in this way.

missionary.simple: The Second Anointing is a sacred ordinance in which a person's exaltation is sealed by the Holy Spirit of Promise. Peter described it as making your calling and election sure.
missionary.explain: 'It is not a club for insiders. It is a sacred confirmation of lifelong faithfulness — given by God through His appointed servants to those He chooses to honor. Joseph Smith taught it is the privilege of every faithful member to eventually receive this.'
missionary.why: The restored gospel offers ordinances that bind on earth and in heaven — from baptism all the way to the sealing of your eternal standing before God.
missionary.invite: Would you like to understand the complete covenant path the restored gospel offers — from baptism to the fulness of what God has prepared?

questions.question: 196. Why does the Church use the all-seeing eye and other Masonic symbols on some older temples?
questions.answer:
The all-seeing eye is not exclusively a Masonic symbol. It appears in Christian art going back centuries as a representation of divine omniscience — the eye of God that sees all things. It appears in the design of the Great Seal of the United States for the same reason.

Many of the symbols found on older LDS temple exteriors — the sun, moon, and stars, the all-seeing eye, the beehive — are drawn from the same reservoir of ancient symbolic language that Masonry also drew from. Joseph Smith understood that ancient ceremonial traditions had preserved fragments of a once-complete sacred symbolism. He used that symbolic vocabulary in the design of the temples.

The beehive represents industry and cooperative work — the same qualities the Lord commended in Proverbs 6:6 through the ant. The sun, moon, and stars represent the three degrees of glory Paul described in 1 Corinthians 15. The all-seeing eye represents the omniscience of God — not the surveillance of a secret society.

Symbols carry the meaning assigned to them by context. In the context of a temple dedicated to the Lord Jesus Christ, built by His covenant people, the all-seeing eye means exactly what it has meant in Christian art for centuries: the watchful, loving care of a God who sees all things.

questions.question: 203. What is Celestial Marriage?
questions.answer:
Celestial marriage is marriage performed by the authority of the Melchizedek Priesthood, in the holy temple, with the sealing power restored through Elijah — binding two people together for time and for all eternity, not just until death.

Christ gave Peter the power to bind on earth what would be bound in heaven. Matthew 16:19 If that power is real — and Christ said it was — then a marriage performed by that power is not dissolved by death. “What therefore God hath joined together, let not man put asunder.”Matthew 19:6 If God joins two people together by divine authority, no power on earth — and no power of death — can sunder it.

Ecclesiastes 3:14 — "I know that, whatsoever God doeth, it shall be for ever." If God seals a marriage, it is sealed forever. That is celestial marriage — the eternal union of a man and woman in the presence of God, sealed by the authority He restored through His living prophet.

Paul wrote, “Neither is the man without the woman, neither the woman without the man, in the Lord.”1 Corinthians 11:11 In the Lord. Not just in this life. The highest degree of the celestial kingdom — life with God in the fulness of eternal glory — is available only to those who have made and kept the covenants of celestial marriage. That is not our requirement. It is God's. And He has provided every opportunity for every soul to receive it — in this life or in the next.

missionary.simple: Celestial marriage is performed by the authority of the Melchizedek Priesthood in the temple, sealing two people together for time and all eternity — not just until death.
missionary.explain: 'Christ gave Peter the power to bind on earth what would be bound in heaven. If that power is real — and Christ said it is — then a marriage performed by that power is sealed in both worlds. Death cannot dissolve it.'
missionary.why: The most important relationship of your mortal life does not have to end when mortality does. Celestial marriage makes it eternal.
missionary.invite: Would you like to understand what it would take for you to one day receive this ordinance and what it would mean for your family?

questions.question: 216. Why do temple garments have specific marks sewn into them?
questions.answer:
The marks in temple garments are symbols of the covenants made in the temple — reminders, worn close to the body, of specific commitments made before God. They are not decorations. They are not arbitrary. Each mark corresponds to a specific covenant and a specific principle.

The same principle governs the garments of the Aaronic priesthood in the Old Testament. The breastplate of Aaron had twelve stones representing the twelve tribes — carried literally over his heart as he ministered. Exodus 28:15–21 The hem of the priestly robe had bells and pomegranates alternating — each with specific symbolic meaning. Exodus 28:33–34 Every element of the priestly garment was symbolic and purposeful.

The marks in modern temple garments serve the same function — they are physical symbols of spiritual commitments, worn as a constant reminder that the covenant covers the whole person, in every part of their life, at every hour of the day and night. To wear the garment faithfully is to acknowledge continuously that one belongs to God and has made binding promises to serve Him.

We do not discuss the specific meaning of the marks outside the temple because they are sacred — given in a sacred context to those who have prepared themselves to receive them. That is not secrecy. That is appropriate reverence for sacred things.

missionary.simple: The marks in temple garments are symbols of specific covenants made before God — worn close to the body as a constant reminder that the covenant covers the whole person at every hour.
missionary.explain: 'Aaron's breastplate had twelve stones representing the twelve tribes — worn literally over his heart as he ministered. Every element of priestly garments was symbolic. The principle is ancient and biblical.'
missionary.why: What you carry on your person shapes who you become. The garment is a daily covenant reminder that belongs to the whole life.
missionary.invite: Would you like to understand what the temple covenants involve and how wearing the garment changes the way members think about their daily choices?

questions.question: 218. What happens to the sealing of a family if one parent leaves the Church?
questions.answer:
The sealing performed in the temple is a binding ordinance — performed by divine authority and valid before God. A person's choice to leave the Church does not automatically cancel the sealing any more than a person's choice to stop attending church cancels their baptism.

The sealing is a covenant. Covenants have conditions. The full blessings of the sealing are conditional on the faithfulness of both parties. But the ordinance itself is not dissolved by one party's departure. It remains — like a valid legal contract that one party has chosen not to honour. The consequences of breaking a covenant fall on the one who breaks it, not on the covenant itself.

For the faithful spouse and children — the sealing remains in full force. The Lord is perfectly just. He will not penalise a faithful wife whose husband apostatised for the faithfulness she maintained throughout her life. He will not leave a child without eternal connection to the parent who remained true because the other parent walked away.

For the children — a sealing is a promise made to them and about them, not only by them. They will have every opportunity to choose whether to honour it and claim its blessings. The parent who left does not have the power to remove that from them. God is more powerful than apostasy. His covenants outlast human faithlessness.

missionary.simple: The sealing remains valid even when one spouse leaves the Church. The faithful spouse and children are not penalized for another's choices. God's covenant is stronger than human faithlessness.
missionary.explain: 'The sealing is a covenant. One party breaking a covenant does not dissolve it — it places the consequences on the one who broke it. The faithful spouse inherits the full blessing. God's justice is perfect.'
missionary.why: God does not punish loyalty. The faithful member who holds to their covenants when a spouse walks away will receive every blessing they were promised.
missionary.invite: Is there someone in your life navigating a difficult family situation? Would you like to understand what God promises those who remain faithful?

questions.question: 220. Why does the temple ceremony involve a physical veil with specific marks, and what do they represent?
questions.answer:
The veil of the temple is one of the most profound symbols in all of scripture. In the tabernacle of Moses and in Solomon's temple, the veil separated the holy place from the Holy of Holies — the place where God dwelt. Exodus 26:33 No one could pass through that veil except the High Priest, and only once a year, with blood for the atonement.

When Christ died on the cross, “the veil of the temple was rent in twain from the top to the bottom.”Matthew 27:51 That was not an accident of physics. It was a divine declaration — the separation between God and man had been removed by the atoning sacrifice of the Son of God. Access to God was now open to all who came through Christ.

In the temple ceremony, the veil represents the boundary between mortality and the presence of God. Passing through it represents the completion of the mortal journey and the entry into God's presence. The marks on the veil — corresponding to the marks in the garment — represent the covenants made, carried through mortality to the moment of entering God's presence.

It is the most vivid and personal possible enactment of what the atonement of Jesus Christ accomplished. Christ tore the veil. He opened the way. The ceremony represents every soul following Him through that opening — having kept their covenants, having endured to the end, entering at last into the rest of the Lord.

missionary.simple: The veil in the temple represents the boundary between mortality and God's presence. Passing through it represents completing the mortal journey and entering fully into God's presence — made possible by Christ's atonement.
missionary.explain: 'When Christ died, the veil of the temple was torn from top to bottom. That was God's declaration that the barrier between Himself and mankind had been removed by the atonement. The temple ceremony enacts that reality personally for every participant.'
missionary.why: The temple makes the atonement personal and tangible. Every person who goes through it is enacting the moment when Christ's sacrifice becomes their own.
missionary.invite: Would you like to know what it means to pass through the veil — and how preparing to do so changes the way you live every day?

questions.question: 221. What happens to the eternal family chain if a child is born to parents who are not sealed in the temple?
questions.answer:
God's provision is comprehensive and just. A child born to parents who are not sealed in the temple is not spiritually orphaned. When the parents are later sealed in the temple — whether in this life or through proxy work after death — their children can be sealed to them as well. Children who are born before the sealing can be sealed to their parents in a separate ceremony called a "sealing of children to parents."

Children who are born after the parents are sealed are "born in the covenant" — meaning the sealing covers them from birth. The covenant reaches both directions — backward to cover children already born, forward to include children yet to be born.

For children whose parents never receive the gospel — the provision is the spirit world and temple work by descendants or Church members who identify and perform the work. No child of God is left without access to eternal family connections if those connections are sought.

Hebrews 11:40 applies here as it applies everywhere: "They without us should not be made perfect." The living have a responsibility to the dead. The living do the work. The dead make the choice. The family chain that was broken in mortality can be completed in eternity through the ordinances God has provided.

🕮 Scripture Evidence: Spirit World & Salvation for the Dead

  • 1 Corinthians 15:29 — “Else what shall they do which are baptized for the dead, if the dead rise not at all? why are they then baptized for the dead?”
  • 1 Peter 3:18-20 — Christ was “put to death in the flesh, but quickened by the Spirit: By which also he went and preached unto the spirits in prison.”
  • 1 Peter 4:6 — “For for this cause was the gospel preached also to them that are dead, that they might be judged according to men in the flesh, but live according to God in the spirit.”
  • Luke 16:22-23 — The beggar Lazarus was carried to Abraham’s bosom after death — showing distinct realms in the afterlife.
  • Luke 23:43 — Jesus tells the thief: “To day shalt thou be with me in paradise.”
  • Ecclesiastes 12:7 — “The spirit shall return unto God who gave it.”
  • Philippians 1:23 — Paul expresses desire “to depart, and to be with Christ” — confirming conscious spirit world existence.
  • Hebrews 12:23 — References “the spirits of just men made perfect” — confirming ongoing spirit progression.

questions.question: 34. Why do you believe in three degrees of glory? Doesn't everyone just go to heaven or hell?
questions.answer:
Paul saw it himself. In 2 Corinthians 12:2 he wrote, "I knew a man in Christ above fourteen years ago... such a one caught up to the third heaven." The third heaven. That requires a first and a second.

In 1 Corinthians 15:40–42 Paul wrote, "There are also celestial bodies, and bodies terrestrial... There is one glory of the sun, and another glory of the moon, and another glory of the stars: for one star differeth from another star in glory. So also is the resurrection of the dead." Paul is directly describing different degrees of glory in the resurrection.

Christ himself said, “In my Father's house are many mansions.”John 14:2 Not one mansion. Many. A God of perfect justice does not apply the same reward to the faithful saint and the casual unbeliever and the violent criminal. He judges every man according to his works. Revelation 20:12–13

The Doctrine and Covenants gives the fullest description of these kingdoms ever revealed. Joseph Smith and Sidney Rigdon saw the celestial, terrestrial, and telestial glories in vision — the sun, the moon, and the stars Paul described — and recorded in detail who inherits each. D&C 76:50–113 God’s justice is perfect. Every soul receives exactly what they have prepared themselves to receive.

The doctrine of everyone going to either heaven or hell is a simplification that the scriptures do not support. It came from tradition, not from the word of God. Paul described what he saw. The Doctrine and Covenants reveals it in detail.

missionary.simple: Children born before parents are sealed can be sealed to them in a separate temple ceremony later. Children born after a sealing are automatically covered. No child is left without the opportunity.
missionary.explain: 'The sealing reaches both directions — backward to children already born, forward to children yet to be born. God's covenant is comprehensive. No family member is excluded if the ordinance is sought.'
missionary.why: Every child of God deserves the opportunity to be sealed to an eternal family, and the restored gospel provides a way to make that possible for everyone.
missionary.invite: Would you like to understand what the sealing ordinance means and how it can connect your family together for eternity?

questions.question: 37. Why do you believe in a spirit world after death? Doesn't the Bible say the dead are simply asleep?
questions.answer:
The dead do rest. The scriptures use sleep as a metaphor for death because the body lies still. But sleep and unconsciousness are not the same thing. We dream in sleep. We are aware. And the spirit, while the body rests, is very much alive and active.

Moses died in Moab. Deuteronomy 34:5 He had been dead for hundreds of years when he appeared on the Mount of Transfiguration — talking with Christ, Peter, James, and John. Matthew 17:3 Christ had not yet been resurrected. Moses was not resurrected. He was present in spirit — awake, aware, and able to communicate. The dead know nothing in the grave because the spirit is not in the grave. The spirit returned to God who gave it. Ecclesiastes 12:7

Peter taught that Christ, in the days His body lay in the tomb, “went and preached unto the spirits in prison.”1 Peter 3:18–20 Christ was active, preaching, ministering — while His body was in the tomb. That is the spirit world.

Luke 16 records Christ's parable of the rich man and Lazarus — with full awareness, conversation, memory, and feeling on both sides of death. Whatever your view of that parable, Christ chose that setting to teach about the afterlife. It is not the picture of unconscious sleep.

Alma the Younger gave the most complete scriptural description of the spirit world. The spirits of the righteous are received into paradise — a state of happiness and rest — while the wicked are in a state of outer darkness, awaiting the resurrection. Both are conscious and aware. Alma 40:11–14 The spirit world is not empty silence. It is an active place where the work of salvation continues.

🕮 Scripture Evidence: Spirit World & Salvation for the Dead

  • 1 Corinthians 15:29 — “Else what shall they do which are baptized for the dead, if the dead rise not at all? why are they then baptized for the dead?”
  • 1 Peter 3:18-20 — Christ was “put to death in the flesh, but quickened by the Spirit: By which also he went and preached unto the spirits in prison.”
  • 1 Peter 4:6 — “For for this cause was the gospel preached also to them that are dead, that they might be judged according to men in the flesh, but live according to God in the spirit.”
  • Luke 16:22-23 — The beggar Lazarus was carried to Abraham’s bosom after death — showing distinct realms in the afterlife.
  • Luke 23:43 — Jesus tells the thief: “To day shalt thou be with me in paradise.”
  • Ecclesiastes 12:7 — “The spirit shall return unto God who gave it.”
  • Philippians 1:23 — Paul expresses desire “to depart, and to be with Christ” — confirming conscious spirit world existence.
  • Hebrews 12:23 — References “the spirits of just men made perfect” — confirming ongoing spirit progression.

missionary.simple: The spirit is separate from the body. When the body dies, the spirit goes to the spirit world — a real place where people are conscious, aware, and continuing to learn and grow.
missionary.explain: 'Moses appeared on the Mount of Transfiguration — centuries after his death. He was present, talking, and engaged. He was not asleep and unconscious. The spirit world is active, not dormant.'
missionary.why: Death is not the end — not of consciousness, not of love, not of learning. The spirit world is the continuation of your journey, not its conclusion.
missionary.invite: Would you like to know what the restored gospel teaches about what happens immediately after death and what that means for people you love?

questions.question: 47. Why do you believe in a literal, physical resurrection? Don't we rise as spirits?
questions.answer:
Christ answered this himself, after His resurrection, when His apostles were terrified and thought He was a ghost. He said, “Behold my hands and my feet, that it is I myself: handle me, and see; for a spirit hath not flesh and bones, as ye see me have.”Luke 24:39 He then ate fish and honeycomb in front of them. Luke 24:42–43 Spirits do not eat. Bodies do.

Paul wrote in 1 Corinthians 15:20 that "Christ is risen from the dead, and become the firstfruits of them that slept." Firstfruits — meaning the first of a coming harvest. His physical resurrection was the pattern and promise of ours.

He then described the resurrection in detail in 1 Corinthians 15:42–44 — the body is sown in corruption, raised in incorruption. Sown in dishonour, raised in glory. Sown in weakness, raised in power. It is the same body — transformed, glorified, but physical.

Acts 1:9–11 records that Christ ascended into heaven with His physical body. Angels told the watching apostles, "This same Jesus, which is taken up from you into heaven, shall so come in like manner as ye have seen him go into heaven." The same Jesus. The same body. He will return as He left — in a glorified, physical, resurrected body.

Alma taught that the resurrection is universal, complete, and literal: “the soul shall be restored to the body, and the body to the soul; yea, and every limb and joint shall be restored to its body; yea, even a hair of the head shall not be lost; but all things shall be restored to their proper and perfect frame.”Alma 40:23 The resurrection is not symbolic. Every part of you is restored — perfected and glorified forever.

🕮 Scripture Evidence: God Has Been Seen

  • Moses — Exodus 33:11: The LORD spoke to Moses face to face, as a man speaks to his friend.
  • Moses — Exodus 33:18-23: Moses is permitted to see God's back, confirming a directional, tangible presence.
  • Moses & the elders — Exodus 24:9-11: Moses, Aaron, and seventy elders saw the God of Israel and ate and drank in His presence.
  • Jacob — Genesis 32:30: “I have seen God face to face, and yet my life is preserved.”
  • Isaiah — Isaiah 6:1-5: Isaiah beheld the Lord sitting upon a throne, high and lifted up.
  • Ezekiel — Ezekiel 1:26-28: Ezekiel describes the appearance of the likeness of the glory of the LORD as a human form.
  • Abraham — Genesis 18:1-2: The LORD appeared to Abraham in visible, tangible form at the oaks of Mamre.
  • Job — Job 42:5: “My eyes have seen you.”

missionary.simple: Christ rose from the dead with a physical body of flesh and bone — and showed it to His disciples, invited them to touch it, and ate with them. That is the pattern for every person's resurrection.
missionary.explain: 'A spirit does not have flesh and bones as I have — those are Christ's own words, spoken after His resurrection. He rose with the same body. We will too. The resurrection is physical, universal, and permanent.'
missionary.why: Your body is not a burden or a prison — it is an eternal gift. The resurrection restores it glorified and perfected forever.
missionary.invite: Would you like to know what your resurrected body will actually be like and what kind of existence awaits you after this life?

questions.question: 65. Why do you believe in eternal progression? Can a person keep growing after this life?
questions.answer:
Paul wrote, “Eye hath not seen, nor ear heard, neither have entered into the heart of man, the things which God hath prepared for them that love him.”1 Corinthians 2:9 What God has prepared is beyond what we can currently imagine. That is not a picture of static, frozen existence. It is the picture of something vast and growing.

Peter wrote that we are to become “partakers of the divine nature.”2 Peter 1:4 He wrote that we should “add to your faith virtue; and to virtue knowledge; and to knowledge temperance.”2 Peter 1:5–6 That is progression — deliberate, purposeful growth toward God. The very nature of godliness involves becoming something more than we are.

John wrote, “It doth not yet appear what we shall be: but we know that, when he shall appear, we shall be like him.”1 John 3:2 We shall be like Him. We are not like Him now. There is a distance between what we are and what we shall be — and that distance is covered through eternal progression.

God himself declared to Moses, “Worlds without number have I created.”Moses 1:33 A God who creates without end, redeems without end, and perfects without end is not a God who locks His children into an unchanging state for eternity. He is a God of eternal increase — and He invites His children to share in it.

missionary.simple: Paul said eye hath not seen nor ear heard what God has prepared. Peter said we become partakers of the divine nature. That is not a picture of static existence — it is a picture of eternal growth.
missionary.explain: 'Peter told us to add faith, then virtue, then knowledge — always growing toward God. That pattern does not stop at death. The intelligence we gain in this life rises with us in the resurrection.'
missionary.why: Eternity is not an endless Sunday afternoon. It is an expanding journey toward becoming everything God is — and He wants to take you there.
missionary.invite: Would you like to understand what eternal progression actually means and what your life could look like a thousand years from now?

questions.question: 85. Why do you believe in a New Jerusalem to be built in America? Isn't the New Jerusalem in the Bible in the Middle East?
questions.answer:
John saw the New Jerusalem “coming down from God out of heaven.”Revelation 21:2 He described it in detail — twelve gates, twelve foundations, a city of extraordinary size and glory. He placed it on a new earth, after the old earth had passed away. That is not the earthly Jerusalem of the Middle East. That is a celestial city descending to a renewed earth.

But beyond John's vision, Ether 13:3–6 in the Book of Mormon records that a New Jerusalem would be built on this Western continent by the remnant of the house of Joseph. The prophet Micah saw the Lord's house established “in the top of the mountains.”Micah 4:1 Isaiah saw the same. The Rocky Mountains — where the Church is headquartered — are the tops of those mountains.

Joseph Smith received revelation identifying Independence, Missouri as the centre place of Zion — where a temple will eventually stand. (Doctrine and Covenants 57:3.) This is not a replacement for the gathering of Israel to the Holy Land. Both will happen. Zion in the West for the house of Joseph. Jerusalem in the East for the house of Judah. Two centres. One King. One return of Christ — to a world prepared by the gathering of all His covenant people.

Isaiah 2:2–3 describes it: "Out of Zion shall go forth the law, and the word of the Lord from Jerusalem." Zion and Jerusalem — two distinct locations from which the Lord will govern in the millennium. Both are real. Both are coming.

missionary.simple: The New Jerusalem will be built in America — in the land of the tribe of Joseph, whose blessing in Genesis promised his descendants would go across the sea to the great mountain ranges.
missionary.explain: 'John saw the New Jerusalem coming down from God out of heaven. Moroni recorded that a New Jerusalem would be built on the American continent. Two witnesses for the same glorious event.'
missionary.why: We are living in the dispensation where all these ancient promises are being fulfilled. The gathering of Israel, the building of Zion — these are happening now.
missionary.invite: Would you like to understand how the prophecies about the New Jerusalem connect to what is happening in the world right now?
