questions.question: 87. Why do you believe in a literal millennium with Christ reigning on earth for a thousand years?
questions.answer:
Revelation 20:1–6 describes it precisely. An angel bound Satan with a great chain and cast him into the bottomless pit for a thousand years. The saints who had been martyred lived and reigned with Christ for a thousand years. John called this "the first resurrection." After the thousand years, Satan was loosed for a season. This is not figurative language buried in poetry. It is prophetic narrative from the apostle John.

Isaiah saw it. “The wolf also shall dwell with the lamb, and the leopard shall lie down with the kid... They shall not hurt nor destroy in all my holy mountain: for the earth shall be full of the knowledge of the Lord.”Isaiah 11:6–9 That has not happened. The knowledge of the Lord does not fill the earth today. This awaits a future fulfilment.

Paul wrote of Christ reigning “till he hath put all enemies under his feet.”1 Corinthians 15:25 The last enemy is death — which will be destroyed at the end of the millennium. 1 Corinthians 15:26 The sequence is: Christ reigns, enemies fall, death is destroyed, then the end comes. That sequence requires a literal reign on the earth.

Zechariah 14:4–9 records that Christ's feet will stand on the Mount of Olives, the mountain will split, and "the Lord shall be king over all the earth." Literal feet. Literal mountain. Literal kingship. A literal thousand years of peace.

missionary.simple: Revelation 20 describes it precisely — Satan bound for 1,000 years, the saints reigning with Christ, the first resurrection. John saw it. We believe him.
missionary.explain: 'Isaiah saw the wolf dwelling with the lamb and the earth full of the knowledge of the Lord. That has not happened yet. It awaits a future fulfillment — and the millennium is when it arrives.'
missionary.why: Christ will literally reign on the earth. The suffering and injustice of this world will end. Every wrong will be righted. That is the promise.
missionary.invite: Does it change anything for you to know that a time is coming when Christ personally governs this earth in peace for 1,000 years?

questions.question: 88. Why do you teach that the sun, moon, and stars represent different kingdoms of glory? Isn't that reading too much into Paul?
questions.answer:
Paul wrote it. We are reading it. "There are also celestial bodies, and bodies terrestrial: but the glory of the celestial is one, and the glory of the terrestrial is another. There is one glory of the sun, and another glory of the moon, and another glory of the stars: for one star differeth from another star in glory. So also is the resurrection of the dead." 1 Corinthians 15:40–42

He was not speaking about astronomy. He was speaking about the resurrection — about the different qualities of glorified bodies that people will receive based on what they did in this life. Three degrees of glory, illustrated by the three degrees of brightness in the heavens: sun, moon, stars.

Paul also wrote that he was caught up to “the third heaven.”2 Corinthians 12:2 A third heaven requires a first and a second. Three heavens. Three degrees. The same number Paul used in 1 Corinthians 15.

Christ said, “In my Father's house are many mansions.”John 14:2 Not one. Many. Different places, different glories, for different people. A God of perfect justice does not assign the same outcome to every soul regardless of how they lived. His mercy is as real as His justice — and His rewards reflect both. Paul saw that clearly. The restored gospel simply takes Paul at his word and follows where the scripture leads.

missionary.simple: Paul compared the resurrection to the glory of the sun, moon, and stars — three different glories for three different kingdoms. He used the heavens themselves as the illustration.
missionary.explain: 'Paul was not doing astronomy. He was describing the resurrection — that different people will receive different glories based on how they lived. The sun, moon, and stars illustrate the differences.'
missionary.why: God's justice is perfect. His rewards are not one-size-fits-all. Everyone receives exactly what their faith and choices qualify them for.
missionary.invite: Would you like to know what qualifies a person for the highest glory and what that kind of life actually looks like?

questions.question: 107. Why do you believe that the Holy Ghost was not available in full before Christ's resurrection?
questions.answer:
Because Christ said so. “The Holy Ghost was not yet given; because that Jesus was not yet glorified.”John 7:39 The full gift of the Holy Ghost — as a permanent, indwelling companion — was not available before the resurrection and ascension of Christ. That is John's statement, not ours.

In the Old Testament, the Spirit of God came upon individuals temporarily for specific purposes. Saul received it when Samuel anointed him and lost it through disobedience. 1 Samuel 10:10; 16:14 David prayed "take not thy holy spirit from me" — which only makes sense if the Spirit could be taken. Psalms 51:11 It was not yet a permanent gift.

After the resurrection, Christ breathed on the apostles and said, “Receive ye the Holy Ghost.”John 20:22 After the ascension, He sent the Comforter on the day of Pentecost. (Acts 2.) From that point, Peter preached, “Repent, and be baptized... and ye shall receive the gift of the Holy Ghost.”Acts 2:38 The gift. Permanent. Available to all who entered the covenant.

The atonement of Christ opened a new dispensation of spiritual experience. The veil of the temple was torn from top to bottom at His death — symbolising the new access to God's presence that His sacrifice provided. The fullness of the Holy Ghost came through His glorification. That is the clear teaching of the New Testament.

missionary.simple: Christ himself said the Holy Ghost was not yet given because He had not yet been glorified. The full, permanent gift of the Holy Ghost became available after Christ's resurrection and ascension.
missionary.explain: 'In the Old Testament, the Spirit came and went. Saul received it and lost it. David prayed take not thy Holy Spirit from me — meaning it could be taken. After Christ's ascension, it became a permanent gift through the laying on of hands.'
missionary.why: The gift of the Holy Ghost you receive through confirmation is not a temporary visit — it is a permanent endowment available to you for life.
missionary.invite: Would you like to understand what makes the gift of the Holy Ghost different from any other spiritual experience you have had?

questions.question: 119. Why do you believe that the earth itself will be renewed and become a celestial sphere? Where is that in the Bible?
questions.answer:
Peter wrote, “We, according to his promise, look for new heavens and a new earth, wherein dwelleth righteousness.”2 Peter 3:13 A new earth. Not a destroyed earth. A renewed, glorified, celestialized earth — where righteousness dwells permanently.

John saw it in vision: “I saw a new heaven and a new earth: for the first heaven and the first earth were passed away.”Revelation 21:1 The earth does not disappear. It passes away — as in, it is transformed. The celestial city descends to it. God's throne comes to it. The earth becomes the dwelling place of God and His exalted children.

Isaiah saw the same: “For, behold, I create new heavens and a new earth: and the former shall not be remembered, nor come into mind.”Isaiah 65:17 A creation — not a destruction. The earth is glorified, not annihilated.

Paul wrote of “the redemption of our body.”Romans 8:23 The body is redeemed — not discarded. The same principle applies to the earth. It was created for a purpose. It fell when Adam fell. It will be redeemed when the redemption is complete. The earth itself groans and travails, “waiting for the manifestation of the sons of God.”Romans 8:19–22 When the sons of God are glorified, the earth is glorified with them.

missionary.simple: Peter and John both saw a new earth where righteousness dwells. The earth itself will be renewed, glorified, and become the celestial home of God and His exalted children.
missionary.explain: 'Paul said the whole creation groans and travails, waiting for the manifestation of the sons of God. The earth groans along with humanity. When the sons of God are glorified, the earth is glorified with them.'
missionary.why: This earth, this creation, has an eternal future. You will live on a glorified earth with God and your family — not in a cloud somewhere.
missionary.invite: Would you like to understand the full scope of the plan of salvation and where this earth and your life fit into God's eternal design?

questions.question: 133. Why does your church teach that the resurrection is physical and universal? Some believe only in spiritual resurrection.
questions.answer:
Paul does not leave room for ambiguity. “For since by man came death, by man came also the resurrection of the dead. For as in Adam all die, even so in Christ shall all be made alive.”1 Corinthians 15:21–22 All. Every person born into mortality will be resurrected. Physical death came to all through Adam. Physical resurrection comes to all through Christ.

Christ's own resurrection was physical. He said so himself. “Behold my hands and my feet, that it is I myself: handle me, and see; for a spirit hath not flesh and bones, as ye see me have.”Luke 24:39 He invited Thomas to touch the wounds. He ate fish and honeycomb. He appeared to more than 500 brethren at one time. 1 Corinthians 15:6 These were not spiritual apparitions. They were physical encounters with a physically resurrected being.

In Matthew 27:51–53 — at the moment of Christ's death, "the graves were opened; and many bodies of the saints which slept arose, and came out of the graves after his resurrection, and went into the holy city, and appeared unto many." Bodies. Out of graves. Into a city. Appearing to many. That is a literal, physical, bodily resurrection — recorded in your own Bible, in the same gospel of Matthew.

The resurrection is the crowning gift of the atonement. It is universal. It is physical. And it is the foundation of everything we believe about the eternal nature of human existence.

🕮 Scripture Evidence: God Has Been Seen

  • Moses — Exodus 33:11: The LORD spoke to Moses face to face, as a man speaks to his friend.
  • Moses — Exodus 33:18-23: Moses is permitted to see God's back, confirming a directional, tangible presence.
  • Moses & the elders — Exodus 24:9-11: Moses, Aaron, and seventy elders saw the God of Israel and ate and drank in His presence.
  • Jacob — Genesis 32:30: “I have seen God face to face, and yet my life is preserved.”
  • Isaiah — Isaiah 6:1-5: Isaiah beheld the Lord sitting upon a throne, high and lifted up.
  • Ezekiel — Ezekiel 1:26-28: Ezekiel describes the appearance of the likeness of the glory of the LORD as a human form.
  • Abraham — Genesis 18:1-2: The LORD appeared to Abraham in visible, tangible form at the oaks of Mamre.
  • Job — Job 42:5: “My eyes have seen you.”

missionary.simple: Paul said as in Adam all die, even so in Christ shall all be made alive. All means all. The resurrection is universal, physical, and unconditional — for every person who has ever lived.
missionary.explain: 'Christ rose with a body of flesh and bone and showed it to His disciples. That is the pattern. Every person who has ever lived will receive their body back — glorified and perfected — in the resurrection.'
missionary.why: The resurrection is not a metaphor or a symbol. It is a literal, physical, universal gift that Christ purchased for every person through His own resurrection.
missionary.invite: Would you like to know what your resurrected body will be like and what the resurrection actually means for your eternal future?

questions.question: 138. Why do you believe the spirit world is near the earth? Where is that in the Bible?
questions.answer:
Christ told the thief on the cross, “Today shalt thou be with me in paradise.”Luke 23:43 And three days later He told Mary Magdalene, “Touch me not; for I am not yet ascended to my Father.”John 20:17 If paradise were heaven, Christ had already been there and was back — in which case His statement to Mary makes no sense. Paradise must therefore be a different place — close enough to return from in three days, but separate from the presence of the Father in the highest celestial glory.

Peter taught that Christ went and preached to spirits in prison. 1 Peter 3:19 Where are those spirits? The Bible does not give coordinates, but the logic of Christ's three-day visit, His return, and His ascension to the Father as a separate event suggests that the spirit world is near, accessible, and distinct from the final celestial glory.

Luke 16 records Christ's parable of the rich man and Lazarus — Lazarus in Abraham's bosom, the rich man in torment, a gulf between them, but both aware of each other and able to converse. The proximity is part of the teaching. The spirit world is not at the end of the universe. It is the condition that exists after death and before resurrection — near enough to this world that the missionary work described in 1 Peter 4:6 can proceed.

The restoration has given us clarity about this that the Bible alone could only suggest.

🕮 Scripture Evidence: Spirit World & Salvation for the Dead

  • 1 Corinthians 15:29 — “Else what shall they do which are baptized for the dead, if the dead rise not at all? why are they then baptized for the dead?”
  • 1 Peter 3:18-20 — Christ was “put to death in the flesh, but quickened by the Spirit: By which also he went and preached unto the spirits in prison.”
  • 1 Peter 4:6 — “For for this cause was the gospel preached also to them that are dead, that they might be judged according to men in the flesh, but live according to God in the spirit.”
  • Luke 16:22-23 — The beggar Lazarus was carried to Abraham’s bosom after death — showing distinct realms in the afterlife.
  • Luke 23:43 — Jesus tells the thief: “To day shalt thou be with me in paradise.”
  • Ecclesiastes 12:7 — “The spirit shall return unto God who gave it.”
  • Philippians 1:23 — Paul expresses desire “to depart, and to be with Christ” — confirming conscious spirit world existence.
  • Hebrews 12:23 — References “the spirits of just men made perfect” — confirming ongoing spirit progression.

missionary.simple: Christ told the thief on the cross he would be with Him in paradise that day — yet three days later told Mary He had not yet ascended to the Father. Paradise is not heaven. It is the spirit world, close and real.
missionary.explain: 'Moses appeared on the Mount of Transfiguration — present, talking, engaged — centuries after his death. He was not far away. The spirit world is near this earth, not at the edge of the universe.'
missionary.why: The people you love who have passed are not gone to some distant place. They are near, aware, and continuing their journey in the spirit world.
missionary.invite: Would you like to know what the restored gospel teaches about the spirit world and what your loved ones who have passed are doing right now?

questions.question: 140. Why do you believe that Christ will return to Independence, Missouri as well as Jerusalem?
questions.answer:
The scriptures describe two centres of the Lord's millennial government. Zechariah 14 describes Christ standing on the Mount of Olives in Jerusalem — His feet literally touching the earth there. Isaiah 2:3 and Micah 4:2 describe the law going forth from Zion and the word of the Lord from Jerusalem. Two locations. One king. One government.

The identity of Zion as a location in the Americas comes through the restoration. Joseph Smith received revelation that Independence, Missouri is "the centre place" of Zion — where the New Jerusalem will be built. (Doctrine and Covenants 57:3.) Jacob prophesied that Joseph's branch would go "over the wall" — over the sea — to “the utmost bound of the everlasting hills.”Genesis 49:22–26 The everlasting hills of the Americas are the fulfilment of that blessing.

John saw the New Jerusalem “coming down from God out of heaven.”Revelation 21:2 Ether 13:3–4 in the Book of Mormon records Moroni's description of a New Jerusalem to be built on the American continent by the remnant of Joseph, from which the old Jerusalem across the sea would receive its counterpart. Two holy cities. Both are scriptural. Both are coming.

The God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob is big enough to have two capitals for His millennial kingdom. He governs worlds without number. Two cities on one small earth is not a theological problem. It is the fulfilment of the promise made to both Judah and Joseph.

questions.question: 197. If someone leaves the Church, can they still go to a Kingdom of Glory if they keep the commandments?
questions.answer:
Paul described three degrees of glory in 1 Corinthians 15:40–42 — celestial, terrestrial, and telestial. Every person who has ever lived will be resurrected and assigned to a kingdom of glory appropriate to their faithfulness and their response to the light they received.

The terrestrial kingdom — represented by the moon — is described in Doctrine and Covenants 76 as the inheritance of "honourable men of the earth, who were blinded by the craftiness of men... who received not the testimony of Jesus in the flesh, but afterwards received it." Good people who lived well but did not receive the fulness of the gospel will receive a glory that surpasses human imagination.

A person who leaves the Church and continues to live uprightly — keeping the moral commandments, serving others, living with integrity — will be judged by the light they received and how they responded to it. God is perfectly just. He will not condemn a man to outer darkness because he disagreed with the Church's position on a specific issue and sincerely sought God outside of it.

What we do teach is that the fulness of exaltation — the celestial kingdom in its highest degree — requires the fulness of the gospel, the ordinances of the temple, and faithfulness to those covenants. It is not available through another path — because the path was established by God, not by us. But lesser kingdoms of glory are available to those who have lived lesser laws. God's mercy is vast.

missionary.simple: Yes. Every person who lived honorably — even outside the restored gospel — will receive a kingdom of glory that surpasses human imagination. God's mercy reaches every sincere soul.
missionary.explain: 'Paul described the terrestrial kingdom — honourable men who lived well but did not receive the fulness of the gospel. Their glory exceeds anything on this earth. God's mercy is vast.'
missionary.why: No sincere, honest person who lived with integrity will be lost. God sees every heart. He judges with perfect knowledge and perfect mercy.
missionary.invite: Is there someone you love who lived a good life but was not a member of the Church? Would you like to know what the restored gospel teaches about where they are?

questions.question: 217. Is Spirit Prison a literal place or a state of mind?
questions.answer:
Peter described it in literal terms. Christ, after His crucifixion and before His resurrection, “went and preached unto the spirits in prison.”1 Peter 3:19 He went — implying movement, destination, arrival. There is a place. Whether it has walls and floors as we understand them — that is beyond our current knowledge. But it is not merely a metaphor for a psychological state.

Christ's parable of the rich man and Lazarus places both the blessed and the suffering in specific locations — one in Abraham's bosom, one in torment, with a great gulf between them. They are aware of each other. They are aware of their condition. They communicate. Luke 16:22–26 That is not the description of a state of mind. It is the description of a conscious, aware existence in a distinct place.

Spirit Prison — what the Doctrine and Covenants calls "spirit prison" — is the place where spirits who did not receive the gospel in mortality await the opportunity to hear it. It is not a place of torment by definition. It is a place of waiting — of people who are teachable, who can hear missionaries sent by the Lord, and who can accept or reject the gospel. 1 Peter 4:6

Those who accept it have proxy ordinances performed on their behalf and can eventually move out of that condition into the full blessing of the spirit world paradise. The condition ends at the resurrection, when all will be assigned to their kingdom of glory based on what they did with the light they received.

🕮 Scripture Evidence: Spirit World & Salvation for the Dead

  • 1 Corinthians 15:29 — “Else what shall they do which are baptized for the dead, if the dead rise not at all? why are they then baptized for the dead?”
  • 1 Peter 3:18-20 — Christ was “put to death in the flesh, but quickened by the Spirit: By which also he went and preached unto the spirits in prison.”
  • 1 Peter 4:6 — “For for this cause was the gospel preached also to them that are dead, that they might be judged according to men in the flesh, but live according to God in the spirit.”
  • Luke 16:22-23 — The beggar Lazarus was carried to Abraham’s bosom after death — showing distinct realms in the afterlife.
  • Luke 23:43 — Jesus tells the thief: “To day shalt thou be with me in paradise.”
  • Ecclesiastes 12:7 — “The spirit shall return unto God who gave it.”
  • Philippians 1:23 — Paul expresses desire “to depart, and to be with Christ” — confirming conscious spirit world existence.
  • Hebrews 12:23 — References “the spirits of just men made perfect” — confirming ongoing spirit progression.

missionary.simple: Spirit prison is a real condition — a place of waiting for spirits who did not receive the gospel in mortality. It is not a place of torment for the sincere. It is a place of learning and opportunity.
missionary.explain: 'Christ spent three days after His death preaching to spirits in prison. Not punishing them — preaching to them. The gospel reaches people even after death so the judgment can be perfectly fair.'
missionary.why: God leaves no one without an opportunity. Every sincere soul who missed the gospel in mortality will hear it before the final judgment.
missionary.invite: Do you have loved ones who passed away without hearing the gospel? Would you like to know what the restored gospel teaches about the hope available to them?

questions.question: 225. Can someone eventually move from the Telestial Kingdom to the Celestial Kingdom over eternity?
questions.answer:
The Church has no official doctrine of progression between kingdoms. What has been taught consistently is that each kingdom represents the eternal condition of those who inherit it — not a temporary rest stop on the way to something greater.

Doctrine and Covenants 76 describes the inhabitants of each kingdom in terms that do not suggest temporary residence. Those in the celestial kingdom dwell with God and Christ forever. Those in the terrestrial kingdom receive the presence of Christ but not the Father. Those in the telestial kingdom receive the ministering of the Holy Ghost and are eventually redeemed from Satan's power — but the language does not describe them as progressing to a higher kingdom.

What we do teach is that before the final judgment — during the spirit world period and the millennium — progression and learning and growth are available to every soul. The final kingdoms are assigned at the resurrection, after which conditions appear to be permanent.

However — the Church has also been careful not to draw hard lines on what God has not explicitly closed. Some leaders have suggested that eternal progression within each kingdom is possible. Whether the ceiling of one kingdom eventually reaches the floor of the next — that is not revealed doctrine. We are humble about what we do not know. God is just. His arrangements for eternity will be perfectly calibrated to every soul's capacity and every choice they made with the light they received.

missionary.simple: We do not teach officially that progression between kingdoms after the final judgment is possible. What we do teach is that God's mercy in assigning kingdoms is perfect — and every kingdom exceeds human imagination.
missionary.explain: 'Some leaders have suggested progression within kingdoms is possible. Whether the ceiling of one reaches the floor of the next is not revealed. What is clear is that God's arrangements for eternity will be perfectly calibrated to every soul.'
missionary.why: God's justice and mercy together ensure that every person receives exactly what their choices and desires have prepared them to receive.
missionary.invite: Would you like to understand the three degrees of glory and what each one involves — so you can understand what God has truly prepared for those who seek Him?

questions.question: 32. Why do you believe in a pre-mortal life? Where is that in the Bible?
questions.answer:
The Lord told Jeremiah, “Before I formed thee in the belly I knew thee; and before thou camest forth out of the womb I sanctified thee, and I ordained thee a prophet unto the nations.”Jeremiah 1:4–5 God knew Jeremiah before he was born. He ordained him before he was born. That requires a prior existence.

Paul wrote that God “hath chosen us in him before the foundation of the world.”Ephesians 1:4 Chosen us — before the world was created. We existed to be chosen.

John 9:1–3 records the disciples asking Christ about a man born blind — "who did sin, this man, or his parents, that he was born blind?" The disciples considered it possible that the man himself had sinned before his birth. That question only makes sense if pre-mortal existence was an accepted concept among them.

Paul wrote to the Hebrews, “We have had fathers of our flesh which corrected us, and we gave them reverence: shall we not much rather be in subjection unto the Father of spirits, and live?”Hebrews 12:9 The Father of spirits. Our spirits came from a Father. Fathers and children share a relationship that existed before birth.

Job 38:4–7 records the Lord asking Job, "Where wast thou when I laid the foundations of the earth... when the morning stars sang together, and all the sons of God shouted for joy?" The sons of God were present at the creation — shouting for joy. They had to exist before the earth was formed to be present when it was laid.

Abraham received a vision confirming this directly. He saw the spirits of all people awaiting their mortal birth, and God declared: "These I will make my rulers; for he stood among those that were spirits, and he saw that they were good."Abraham 3:23 You were there. You were known. You were prepared before you came to this earth. Abraham 3:22–23

missionary.simple: God told Jeremiah He knew him before he was formed. Paul said God chose us before the foundation of the world. We existed as spirit children of God before this life began.
missionary.explain: 'Job's friends were told they were present when God laid the foundations of the earth, shouting for joy. You were there. You chose to come here. This life is not your beginning.'
missionary.why: Knowing you lived before this life changes everything — your sense of purpose, your understanding of trials, your confidence that God knows you personally.
missionary.invite: Would you like to understand what the pre-mortal life means and how it connects to the challenges and gifts of your life today?

questions.question: 54. Why do Latter-day Saints believe in a literal gathering of Israel? Isn't that symbolic?
questions.answer:
It is not symbolic. The Lord told Abraham that in his seed all the nations of the earth would be blessed. Genesis 22:18 He told Moses that Israel would be scattered to the four corners of the earth and then gathered again. Deuteronomy 30:1–5 Isaiah saw Israel gathered from the north, the south, the east, and the west. Isaiah 43:5–6 Ezekiel saw the two records of Judah and Joseph brought together in one hand at the time of the gathering. Ezekiel 37:16–22

These are specific, literal, geographical promises. The Jewish people have been gathered to their land since 1948 — fulfilling prophecies written thousands of years ago. That is not symbolic. It is happening before our eyes.

But the gathering is broader than the Jewish nation. Paul wrote in Galatians 3:29, "If ye be Christ's, then are ye Abraham's seed, and heirs according to the promise." Through baptism into the covenant, people of every nation become part of the house of Israel — literally adopted into the covenant lineage — and are gathered to Zion wherever it is established.

Our missionaries are engaged in that gathering today. Every soul baptized into the covenant is gathered to Israel. Malachi's prophecy of Elijah turning hearts — fulfilled in our temples — seals living and dead generations together as part of that same gathering. This is not metaphor. It is the fulfillment of covenants God made with our fathers.

The risen Christ told the Nephites that the gathering of Israel was the sign of the Father's covenant being fulfilled: "The Father hath commanded me that I should give unto you this land, for your inheritance."3 Nephi 20:14 He then prophesied that the remnants of Israel would be gathered from the four corners of the earth to lands of promise. 3 Nephi 20:13–14 We are watching the fulfillment of those words today.

missionary.simple: The gathering of Israel is not symbolic — it is happening right now. Jewish people returning to their land, covenant people gathering to temples and congregations worldwide. Every prophecy is being fulfilled.
missionary.explain: 'Moses promised Israel would be scattered to the four corners of the earth and then gathered again. Isaiah saw them returning from north, south, east, and west. We are watching that fulfillment in our lifetime.'
missionary.why: We are living in the most prophetically significant time in human history. The gathering you can participate in right now is part of God's eternal plan.
missionary.invite: Would you like to understand your role in the gathering of Israel and how baptism connects you to that ancient covenant people?

questions.question: 60. Why do Latter-day Saints believe in free agency when God already knows what we will do?
questions.answer:
God's foreknowledge does not destroy man's freedom. Knowing what a person will choose is not the same as forcing them to choose it. A wise teacher may know that a student will fail an exam — that knowledge does not cause failure.

Joshua said plainly, “Choose you this day whom ye will serve.”Joshua 24:15 That is a choice. A real choice. Not a performance. Not a script. God created man capable of genuine choice — that is what it means to be made in His image. The image of God includes moral agency.

Paul wrote to the Galatians, “Stand fast therefore in the liberty wherewith Christ hath made us free.”Galatians 5:1 Christ died to make us free. He did not die to make us automatons who only appear to choose while every outcome was forced from the beginning.

The plan of salvation requires an agency. Without the ability to genuinely choose God, there is no virtue in choosing Him. It is an agency that makes faith possible, repentance meaningful, and obedience honourable. God honours our choices — even when they break His heart — because He will not destroy the freedom, He gave us to become like Him.

Lehi taught that this agency was central to God's plan from the beginning: "Wherefore, men are free according to the flesh; and all things are given them which are expedient unto man. And they are free to choose liberty and eternal life, through the great Mediator of all men, or to choose captivity and death."2 Nephi 2:27 The choice is real. The consequences are real. And the Mediator — Jesus Christ — stands ready to redeem every honest choice toward God.

missionary.simple: God knows everything you will choose — but knowing is not forcing. A wise parent knows a child will make a mistake without causing the mistake. Knowledge and compulsion are completely different.
missionary.explain: 'God created beings who can genuinely choose because without real choice, there is no real virtue. He honors your choices — even the painful ones — because overriding them would destroy the very thing He is trying to build in you.'
missionary.why: Your choices are real. Your faith is real. Your agency is the gift that makes your love for God and your growth toward Him genuinely meaningful.
missionary.invite: Would you like to know how the restored gospel explains the purpose of this life and why genuine agency is at the center of it?

questions.question: 71. Why does your church teach that God chose us before we were born? Doesn't that destroy equality?
questions.answer:
Paul wrote, “He hath chosen us in him before the foundation of the world.”Ephesians 1:4 That is Paul's pen, not ours. God chose us — before this world existed. And He determined "the times before appointed, and the bounds" of every person's habitation on this earth. Acts 17:26 These are not random assignments. They are foreordinations based on what God knew of each person in the pre-mortal life.

Foreordination is not the same as predestination. Predestination says the outcome is fixed regardless of choice. Foreordination says God called certain people to certain responsibilities based on His perfect foreknowledge of their faithfulness — but they still must choose to fulfil those callings. Esau was born with a birthright and sold it. Every foreordained calling can be accepted or rejected through the exercise of free agency.

Jeremiah was foreordained to be a prophet before he was born. Jeremiah 1:4–5 That did not mean Jeremiah had no choice. It meant God knew him well enough in the life before to trust him with the calling. That is not inequality. That is the same principle a wise employer uses when assigning responsibility — those who have proven their reliability receive more.

missionary.simple: Foreordination means God called certain people to certain responsibilities based on what He knew of them in the pre-mortal life. But they still freely choose whether to fulfill those callings.
missionary.explain: 'Esau was born with a birthright and sold it. Foreordination is not forced destiny — it is a calling extended by a God who knew you well enough to trust you with it. You still choose whether to accept it.'
missionary.why: Your life is not random. God placed you here, in this time, in this family, because He knew you and trusted you with a specific purpose.
missionary.invite: Would you like to know what it means that God knew you before this life and what He may have called you to do?

questions.question: 78. Why do you believe there was a Council in Heaven before this world was created? Where is that in the Bible?
questions.answer:
Job 38:4–7 — the Lord asked Job, "Where wast thou when I laid the foundations of the earth... when the morning stars sang together, and all the sons of God shouted for joy?" The sons of God were present at the creation, shouting for joy. They had to exist before the earth was formed to be there when it was laid.

Isaiah 14:12–15 records the fall of Lucifer — "How art thou fallen from heaven, O Lucifer, son of the morning... For thou hast said in thine heart, I will ascend into heaven, I will exalt my throne above the stars of God." He had a throne. He had ambitions. He was cast down from heaven before this world's history began. That war happened somewhere — and the scriptures call it heaven.

Revelation 12:7–9 describes it plainly: "There was war in heaven: Michael and his angels fought against the dragon; and the dragon fought and his angels... and the dragon was cast out into the earth, and his angels were cast out with him." Michael — who is Adam — led the faithful against Lucifer. There was a war. There were sides. There were choices made before mortality began.

Christ was “the Lamb slain from the foundation of the world.”Revelation 13:8 He was foreordained to be the Saviour before the world was made. That foreordination required a plan — and a plan requires a council. The scriptures support every element of it.

missionary.simple: Job 38 records God asking Job where he was when the morning stars sang together and the sons of God shouted for joy. We were there — present at the creation, as spirit children of God.
missionary.explain: 'Isaiah records the fall of Lucifer from heaven, his ambition to exalt his throne above God. Revelation records the war in heaven where Michael led the faithful against the dragon. These events were real.'
missionary.why: You are here because you chose the right side in that war. You were faithful before this life began. That heritage matters.
missionary.invite: Would you like to understand the pre-mortal life and the war in heaven and how they connect to the choices you face today?

questions.question: 80. Why do you believe we lived with God before we were born? Doesn't the Bible say God forms us in the womb?
questions.answer:
God does form us in the womb. Our physical bodies are formed there. But the body and the spirit are two different things. Isaiah 42:5 distinguishes between them clearly: "He that created the heavens and stretched them out... He that giveth breath unto the people upon it, and spirit to them that walk therein." Breath is given to the body. Spirit is given to the person. These are two separate gifts.

Ecclesiastes 12:7 — "Then shall the dust return to the earth as it was: and the spirit shall return unto God who gave it." The spirit returns to God who gave it. It existed with God before it was placed in a body. It returns to the same God after the body dies. The logic of "returning" requires a prior existence — you can only return to a place you have been before.

Hebrews 12:9 names God "the Father of spirits." We have earthly fathers of our flesh. We have a heavenly Father of our spirits. Just as the flesh came from earthly parents, the spirit came from a heavenly Parent. Paul wrote that God chose us “in him before the foundation of the world.”Ephesians 1:4 You cannot be chosen before the world is made unless you exist before the world is made.

The forming of the body in the womb is the beginning of mortality — not the beginning of existence.

missionary.simple: Our bodies are formed in the womb — but the spirit that animates them existed with God before birth. The forming of the body is the beginning of mortality, not the beginning of existence.
missionary.explain: 'Ecclesiastes says at death the spirit returns to God who gave it. You can only return to somewhere you have been. The spirit returns to God because it came from Him in the first place.'
missionary.why: You are more than your body. Your spirit is eternal, known by God, and has a destination that goes far beyond this mortal life.
missionary.invite: Would you like to understand what your spirit is, where it came from, and where it is going after this life?

questions.question: 113. Why do you believe in a literal war in heaven before this world began?
questions.answer:
Because John saw it. "There was war in heaven: Michael and his angels fought against the dragon; and the dragon fought and his angels, and prevailed not; neither was their place found any more in heaven. And the great dragon was cast out, that old serpent, called the Devil, and Satan... and his angels were cast out with him." Revelation 12:7–9

That is not poetry about the interior struggle of the soul. That is a prophetic record of an event involving real beings — Michael, the dragon, angels with sides and weapons and outcomes. A third of the host of heaven was cast out with Satan. Revelation 12:4 One third — a specific number implying a real count of real beings who made a real choice.

Isaiah saw the same event: “How art thou fallen from heaven, O Lucifer, son of the morning... thou hast said in thine heart, I will ascend into heaven, I will exalt my throne above the stars of God.”Isaiah 14:12–13 He had a throne. He had ambition. He was cast down for it.

Christ himself said to the seventy who returned from their missions, “I beheld Satan as lightning fall from heaven.”Luke 10:18 He was there. He saw it. He reported it in past tense. The war was real. Satan's fall was real. And every person born into mortality already lived through that war on the right side — because those who chose the wrong side never came to earth. They were cast out. We are here because we chose Christ.

missionary.simple: John saw the war in heaven — Michael and his angels fighting the dragon. A third of the host of heaven was cast out. These were real beings making real choices before this world began.
missionary.explain: 'Isaiah records Lucifer's ambition: I will exalt my throne above God. He was cast down for it. The same war between light and darkness that began then continues now — in your choices, every day.'
missionary.why: You are here because you chose Christ in that pre-mortal war. That choice brought you to this life with a purpose worth living.
missionary.invite: Would you like to understand the plan of salvation from before this world began to the end of eternity — and where you fit in it?

questions.question: 141. Why do Latter-day Saints believe that the Ten Tribes of Israel will be gathered from the north? Where did they go?
questions.answer:
The Ten Tribes were carried away by Assyria around 721 BC. 2 Kings 17:6 They went to the region of Halah and Habor, by the river of Gozan, "in the cities of the Medes." From that point, the historical record of where they went is incomplete — which is exactly what the scriptures describe.

Jeremiah prophesied their return: “I will bring them from the north country, and gather them from the coasts of the earth.”Jeremiah 31:8 Isaiah saw them returning from the north and the west and the south. Isaiah 43:5–6 The specific direction of "north" is repeated enough in the prophets to be significant.

Doctrine and Covenants 133:26–34 reveals that the tribes are in the "north countries" and will come forth in the last days — highways will be cast up, mountains laid low, ice will flow before them. These are not figurative images. They describe a literal gathering of a real people who have been kept somewhere until the time appointed for their return.

We do not know the exact location. The restoration has given us the truth that they will come and that their records will be joined with ours. 2 Nephi 29:13 Ezekiel's vision of the valley of dry bones — the whole house of Israel restored — includes them. Their gathering is part of the great latter-day work. Where exactly they are today — that is one of the things the fullness of time will reveal.

missionary.simple: The Ten Tribes were taken captive by Assyria around 721 BC and have been in the 'north countries' ever since. Their return is one of the great latter-day prophecies still being fulfilled.
missionary.explain: 'We do not know exactly where they are. What we know is that God has promised they will come — with records, with highways cast up, with a reunion of all Israel. That gathering is underway now.'
missionary.why: We are living in the dispensation when all the ancient promises of Israel's gathering are being fulfilled. You can be part of that gathering.
missionary.invite: Would you like to understand the gathering of Israel — and how your baptism into the covenant makes you part of this ancient and glorious fulfillment?

questions.question: 152. Why do Latter-day Saints believe that moral agency must be preserved at all costs — even allowing wickedness?
questions.answer:
Because that is the principle upon which salvation is built. Moral agency — the ability to genuinely choose — is the foundation of everything. Without it, faith is meaningless, repentance is impossible, and obedience carries no virtue.

God could have created beings who always obey. He did not. He created beings who can choose — knowing that the ability to choose evil is the same ability that makes choosing good worth anything. An obedience that has no alternative is not obedience. It is machinery.

Lehi taught, “It must needs be, that there is an opposition in all things.”2 Nephi 2:11 Without the possibility of wrong, the choice of right is not a choice. Without darkness, light cannot be perceived as light. Without the option of sin, righteousness has no content.

Paul wrote that even God does not compel — “God is not mocked: for whatsoever a man soweth, that shall he also reap.”Galatians 6:7 He does not intercept the consequences. He allows the harvest to reflect the planting. That is the deepest form of respect God can show His children — treating them as genuine agents with genuine consequences for genuine choices.

The wickedness in this world is not God's failure. It is the necessary condition of a world in which real goodness is possible. The atonement does not eliminate the consequences of wickedness — it offers redemption to the wicked who repent. That is both perfect justice and perfect mercy operating simultaneously.

missionary.simple: God preserves moral agency even when it allows wickedness because agency is the foundation of everything — faith, repentance, love, and growth all require the freedom to genuinely choose.
missionary.explain: 'God does not intercept consequences — He allows the harvest to match the planting. That is the deepest form of respect He can show His children. Treating you as a genuine moral agent means letting choices have real weight.'
missionary.why: Your choices matter eternally — not just personally. God built a universe where genuine goodness is possible precisely because genuine evil is also possible.
missionary.invite: Would you like to understand the plan of salvation and why moral agency is at the center of everything God is trying to build in you?

questions.question: 172. Why did past prophets teach that Black people were less valiant in the pre-existence?
questions.answer:
This question deserves an honest answer. Past leaders of the Church offered theories — personal explanations — for the priesthood restriction. The idea that Black members of the Church were "less valiant" in the pre-mortal life was one such theory. It was never canonised as official doctrine. It was the opinion of men attempting to explain something they did not fully understand.

The Church's 2013 Gospel Topics Essay on this subject states clearly that the Church "disavows the theories advanced in the past that black skin is a sign of divine disfavour or curse, or that it reflects actions in a premortal life." That disavowal is official. The theories were mistaken. The Church has said so plainly.

The God of the Bible is not a respecter of persons. Acts 10:34 He made of one blood all nations of men. Acts 17:26 He sent His Son to die for every soul of every race. The restriction that existed in the Church for a time was not accompanied by a revealed explanation — and the theories men invented to explain it were not from God. They were from men whose thinking was shaped by the racial assumptions of their culture.

We do not defend those theories. We acknowledge them as errors. We are grateful that God corrected the restriction through revelation in 1978 and that the fulness of gospel blessings is now available to every worthy member regardless of race.

The Book of Mormon contains God’s own statement on who is welcome before Him: “he inviteth them all to come unto him and partake of his goodness; and he denieth none that come unto him, black and white, bond and free, male and female.”2 Nephi 26:33 Those words were written centuries before the restoration. God’s love for every person of every race has never changed. The restriction was a deviation — not His word.

missionary.simple: Those theories were never official doctrine, were never canonized, and have been officially disavowed by the Church. They were the mistaken opinions of men shaped by their cultural assumptions — not the word of God.
missionary.explain: 'God made of one blood all nations of men. Every person born into mortality came here because they chose Christ in the pre-mortal life. Skin color says absolutely nothing about a person's eternal worth or standing before God.'
missionary.why: The God we worship loves every person of every race with perfect and equal love. His church today fully reflects that love.
missionary.invite: Would you like to know what the Church officially teaches today about the equal standing of every person before God — and how that shapes how we welcome everyone?

questions.question: 201. What is a Dispensation?
questions.answer:
A dispensation is a period of time in which God gives His fulness of gospel truth and authority to men on the earth through a chosen prophet. In each dispensation, the Lord calls a prophet, restores His gospel and priesthood, and gives the people the opportunity to receive and live His truth.

Paul referred to "the dispensation of the fulness of times" — the last and greatest dispensation, in which God would “gather together in one all things in Christ, both which are in heaven, and which are on earth.”Ephesians 1:10 This is the dispensation we live in.

Previous dispensations include Adam's, Enoch's, Noah's, Abraham's, Moses's, and Christ's original meridian dispensation. In each case, truth was given. In each case, the people eventually rejected it, and the dispensation ended in apostasy. The keys of each dispensation are held by the prophet who presided over it.

In this final dispensation — this dispensation of times — all the keys of all previous dispensations have been restored and gathered together in one. Elijah restored the sealing keys. Moses restored the keys of the gathering of Israel. Elias restored the keys of the Abrahamic covenant. Peter, James, and John restored the Melchizedek Priesthood. The fulness of every previous dispensation is present in this one. That is what makes it this dispensation of times — because it contains everything.

The Lord confirmed this through Joseph Smith: all the keys of all previous dispensations have been committed to this final dispensation — from Adam, Noah, Abraham, Moses, Elias, Elijah, and Peter, James, and John. D&C 27:12–13 Nothing given in any previous age is absent from this one.

missionary.simple: A dispensation is a period when God restores His full gospel and priesthood authority to the earth through a chosen prophet. We live in the last and greatest dispensation — the dispensation of the fulness of times.
missionary.explain: 'Every previous dispensation — Adam, Enoch, Noah, Abraham, Moses, Christ — eventually fell into apostasy. This final dispensation contains all the keys of every previous one, gathered together before Christ's second coming.'
missionary.why: You were born into the most significant dispensation in the history of the world. Understanding that changes how you see your purpose and your potential.
missionary.invite: Would you like to understand what it means that you are living in the dispensation of the fulness of times — and what God is asking you to do in it?

questions.question: 202. What is the Fulness of Times?
questions.answer:
Paul described it precisely in Ephesians 1:10 — "That in the dispensation of the fulness of times he might gather together in one all things in Christ, both which are in heaven, and which are on earth; even in him."

The fulness of times is the period in which God gathers back to the earth every truth, every priesthood key, every restored gospel principle that was ever given in any previous dispensation. It is not just a new revelation added to previous revelations. It is the gathering of all previous revelations into one unified, complete restoration.

We are living in that dispensation now. The gospel has been restored. The priesthood has been restored. The temples are built. The gathering of Israel is underway. The Book of Mormon — the stick of Joseph — has been joined to the Bible — the stick of Judah — exactly as Ezekiel prophesied. The hearts of the children are being turned to the fathers through temple work. Elijah has come.

All that remains is the completion of the gathering, the building of Zion, the Second Coming of Christ, the millennium, and the final judgment. Every piece is in place. The fulness of times is the last act of God's plan for this earth — the act that precedes the Saviour's return and the transformation of the earth into its celestial glory.

Joseph Smith declared the purpose plainly: “that in the dispensation of the fulness of times we might gather together all things in one, both which are in heaven, and which are in earth.”D&C 128:18 Every temple, every missionary, every sealed family is part of that gathering. The work is urgent. The time is now.

questions.question: 13. Why did your church stop practicing polygamy if it was once allowed?
questions.answer:
God gave polygamy for a specific purpose and withdrew it by the same authority by which He gave it. This is entirely consistent with the pattern of God throughout scripture. In Jacob 2:30, the Lord himself states the condition: "For if I will raise up seed unto me, I will command my people; otherwise they shall hearken unto these things." When the Lord commanded it, it was right. When He withdrew the command, obedience required stopping.

The Book of Mormon itself defines the standard. In Jacob 2:30, the Lord states: "For if I will raise up seed unto me, I will command my people; otherwise they shall hearken unto these things" — meaning monogamy is the divine default, and plural marriage was a specific exception given for a specific season. Its withdrawal was as authoritative as its institution.

The Lord gave David his wives. 2 Samuel 12:8 He permitted Abraham, Jacob, and many righteous men to take plural wives. None of this was condemned in its proper season.

In 1890, President Wilford Woodruff received revelation from God that the season for plural marriage had ended. He issued what is known as the Manifesto — now recorded as Official Declaration 1 in the Doctrine and Covenants. President Woodruff explained: "The Lord showed me by vision and revelation exactly what would take place if we did not stop this practice." The Manifesto was sustained by the Church in general conference and remains the authoritative end of the practice.

Today any member found practicing plural marriage faces membership withdrawal. President Gordon B. Hinckley stated plainly: "If any of our members are found to be practicing plural marriage, their membership is withdrawn — the most serious action the Church can take." The standard of the Church is monogamy — as it always has been as the default, with plural marriage having been a specific, now-withdrawn exception.

missionary.simple: Having many churches does not prove any one of them is right. It proves that what was once one has become divided — exactly what Paul warned against and exactly what the apostasy produced.
missionary.explain: 'Paul besought the saints that there be no divisions among them. He wrote that because divisions had already appeared. Divisions are not God's blessing — they are the evidence of the problem He sent the restoration to fix.'
missionary.why: The restoration did not create another denomination. It restored the one church Christ built — with His authority, His ordinances, and His structure intact.
missionary.invite: Would you like to know what makes this Church different from a new denomination — and why the difference matters eternally?

questions.question: 156. Are you part of the Jehovah's Witnesses or Seventh-day Adventists?
questions.answer:
No. We are members of The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints. We are not affiliated with the Jehovah's Witnesses, the Seventh-day Adventists, or any other denomination. We do not share their doctrines, their organisational structure, their authority, or their origins.

The Jehovah's Witnesses were founded by Charles Taze Russell in the 1870s. The Seventh-day Adventists grew from the Millerite movement of the 1840s. Both were founded by men who took existing Bible interpretations and built new organisations around them. Neither claims a living prophet who received authority from heaven. Neither claims the restoration of apostolic keys. Neither has temples, a Melchizedek Priesthood, or the fulness of the everlasting gospel.

These are sincere Christian groups, and we respect the faith of their members. However, each of them was founded by individuals who took existing Bible interpretations and built new organisations around them. None of them claim a living prophet who received authority directly from heaven. None of them claim the restoration of apostolic keys, a Melchizedek Priesthood, or the fulness of the everlasting gospel as it existed in the primitive church.

We are separate from all of them. Our claim is not reform. It is not reinterpretation. It is restoration — of the original Church organised by Christ, with the same authority, the same structure, the same ordinances, and the same living prophets He placed at its head.

missionary.simple: We are not Jehovah's Witnesses or Seventh-day Adventists. We are members of The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints — a distinct restored church with apostles, living prophets, temples, and priesthood authority.
missionary.explain: 'Both those churches were founded by men who reinterpreted the Bible. We make a different claim entirely — that God restored the original church through the prophet Joseph Smith by sending actual messengers from heaven.'
missionary.why: The distinction matters because restoration is fundamentally different from reformation. We did not start a new church — we restored the original one.
missionary.invite: Would you like to understand specifically what makes the restored church different from any other denomination — and why that difference matters for you?

questions.question: 168. Why was plural marriage kept secret from Emma Smith for so long?
questions.answer:
Emma Smith knew of plural marriage from relatively early in its practice. The historical record shows she was informed, that she gave her consent at certain times, and that she struggled deeply with it at others. She was a woman of extraordinary strength and faith, and her struggle with this principle was real and documented. We do not diminish it.

The secrecy surrounding plural marriage in the early Church was not a deception toward Emma alone. It was a response to the violent persecution the saints faced. Joseph Smith had been arrested, tarred and feathered, imprisoned, and driven from states. The government of Illinois was moving against the Church. The practice of plural marriage — incomprehensible to 19th-century American culture — would have accelerated that persecution dramatically.

The Lord commanded secrecy in the same way He commanded Nephi to keep certain things from being written. 1 Nephi 14:28 Not everything from God is revealed immediately to the whole world. Some things are held back until the time is right. That is the pattern of the Lord throughout scripture — not deception, but stewardship of sacred things until they can be received.

Plural marriage caused Emma deep pain, and we do not minimize the complexity of that history. But pain in the practice of a divine commandment does not make the commandment false. Abraham's willingness to sacrifice Isaac caused deep pain. God does not always make His commandments easy. He makes them true.

missionary.simple: Emma knew about plural marriage from relatively early and struggled deeply with it. Her struggle was real, honored, and documented. The gospel has never required that following God's commands is painless.
missionary.explain: 'Abraham's household was complicated too. Sarah's pain was real. God's commands have never guaranteed comfort. What matters is whether the command came from God — and whether the fruits of Joseph's ministry confirm his calling.'
missionary.why: Difficulty in following a divine command is not evidence that the command is false. It is evidence that faith costs something real.
missionary.invite: Would you be willing to look honestly at the fruits of Joseph Smith's ministry — the scripture, the prophecies, the church — and ask God directly whether he was called by God?

questions.question: 170. Do you believe polygamy will be practiced in the Celestial Kingdom?
questions.answer:
We believe that the principles governing the celestial kingdom are governed by God — and that what He has sanctioned in eternity, He has the right to sanction. We do not make specific declarations about the exact domestic arrangements of exalted beings in eternity. That is God's business.

What we do teach is that eternal marriage is essential to the highest degree of the celestial kingdom. Paul wrote that “neither is the man without the woman, neither the woman without the man, in the Lord.”1 Corinthians 11:11 The family unit — husband, wife, and children — is eternal in the celestial kingdom.

As for whether plural marriage will be practised there — the scriptures tell us that God gave plural wives to the great prophets and said of David's wives, “I gave them thee.”2 Samuel 12:8 He added, through Ecclesiastes 3:14, that "whatsoever God doeth, it shall be for ever." If God gave something and declared it forever, that has implications.

But rather than speculate about eternal arrangements beyond what has been revealed, we simply say this: whatever God has in store for His exalted children will be more glorious, more just, and more full of joy than anything human imagination can construct. We trust Him with those details. What He has not revealed, we do not presume to define.

missionary.simple: We do not make specific declarations about the domestic arrangements of exalted beings in eternity. What we teach is that eternal marriage is essential to the highest degree of the celestial kingdom.
missionary.explain: 'God gave David his wives through the prophet Nathan. Ecclesiastes says whatever God does shall be for ever. Whatever God has sanctioned in this life He has the authority to sanction in eternity. Beyond that, we trust Him with the details.'
missionary.why: What God has prepared for the faithful far exceeds what human imagination can construct. We hold to what is revealed and trust God with what is not.
missionary.invite: Would you like to understand what eternal marriage actually involves and what God promises those who receive and keep that covenant?

questions.question: 171. Why can a man be sealed to more than one woman if they are deceased or divorced, but a woman cannot be sealed to more than one man?
questions.answer:
This reflects the Lord's established order for sealings, not a statement about the lesser worth of women. The sealing ordinance is not merely a social arrangement — it is a priesthood ordinance that binds families under divine law. The pattern governing it was given by revelation.

The practical reality is this: a man whose wife dies and who remarries can be sealed to his second wife because both sealings are valid and the Lord will sort out the eternal arrangement. A woman who remarries after the death of her first husband can have her civil marriage but cannot be sealed to the second husband while still sealed to the first. She can have the sealing to the first cancelled and be sealed to the second — but not hold two simultaneous sealings.

This asymmetry reflects the same pattern established in ancient Israel, where God sanctioned plural wives for men but did not sanction plural husbands for women. It is not a statement about women's worth. It is the revealed order of the sealing ordinance.

We acknowledge that this creates real emotional complexity — particularly for women who love a second husband with their whole heart. The comfort is the same comfort God gives in every difficult doctrinal area: He is just. He sees every heart. He knows the full story of every marriage and every sealing. His arrangements for eternal families will reflect that perfect knowledge and perfect love. We trust His judgment over our own comfort.

questions.question: 173. Does the Church still believe the Curse of Cain or Curse of Ham applies to certain races?
questions.answer:
No. The Church has explicitly and officially repudiated this teaching. The 2013 Gospel Topics Essay states that the Church "disavows" these racial theories and that they "were never" adopted as official Church doctrine.

The "Curse of Cain" and "Curse of Ham" as applied to Black Africans was a widespread 19th-century American Christian interpretation — shared by Baptists, Methodists, Presbyterians, and others — used to justify slavery and racial hierarchy. Some past LDS leaders adopted language from that cultural context. It was mistaken. The Church has said so directly.

The scriptures themselves do not support the racial application of these narratives. The mark given to Cain was a protection — “lest any finding him should kill him.”Genesis 4:15 It was not described as a racial curse passed to descendants. The curse of Ham in Genesis 9:25 was spoken by Noah over Canaan — not over all African peoples. Applying these texts to justify racial hierarchy requires a level of interpretive distortion that the plain text does not support.

Every person born into mortality came here because they chose Christ in the pre-mortal life. Every soul is loved by the same Father. Every soul has equal access to the atonement of the same Saviour. The colour of a person's skin is not a spiritual statement. It never was, and the Church today is clear about that.

missionary.simple: No. The Church has officially and explicitly disavowed those theories. They were the opinions of men shaped by their cultural time — not the word of God.
missionary.explain: 'God made of one blood all nations of men. Paul said it plainly. Every person born into mortality came here because they chose Christ in the pre-mortal life. Skin color says nothing about eternal worth.'
missionary.why: God loves every person of every race with equal and perfect love. His church now reflects that love fully — every blessing available to every worthy person.
missionary.invite: Would you like to know what the Church officially teaches today about race and the equal standing of every person before God?

questions.question: 174. What is the Church's official stance on same-sex marriage and LGBTQ+ rights?
questions.answer:
The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints teaches that marriage between a man and a woman is ordained of God and is essential to His eternal plan. This teaching comes from the first pages of Genesis — “male and female created he them... Therefore shall a man leave his father and his mother, and shall cleave unto his wife: and they shall be one flesh.”Genesis 1:27; 2:24 Christ himself quoted these verses when He taught about marriage. Matthew 19:4–6 It is not a cultural preference. It is the revealed order of creation.

Paul wrote in Romans 1:26–27 that same-sex relations are contrary to nature and represent a departure from the truth of God. He listed them alongside other sins whose consequences affect both the individual and the society. We teach what Paul taught. We do not apologise for it, and we do not soften it to match contemporary culture.

At the same time, every person — regardless of their attractions, their identity, or their history — is a beloved child of God. Every person has access to the atonement of Jesus Christ. Every person is welcomed through the doors of our chapels. The distinction the Church makes is between same-sex attraction — which is not a sin — and same-sex sexual behaviour, which falls outside the law of chastity. This distinction is scriptural, consistent, and pastoral.

We love every person. We do not compromise the law of God.

missionary.simple: Marriage between a man and a woman is ordained of God and essential to His eternal plan. We teach what Christ taught, what Paul taught, and what the first pages of Genesis teach.
missionary.explain: 'We love every person regardless of their attractions or history. Every person is welcome in our chapels. The distinction we make is between attraction and action — exactly the same standard we apply to every member.'
missionary.why: We do not compromise the law of God — but we also do not compromise on loving every person. Both are commands. Both are kept.
missionary.invite: Would you like to know how we walk the line between holding God's standard and genuinely loving every person who walks through our doors?

questions.question: 175. Can an openly gay person remain a member in good standing if they do not marry?
questions.answer:
Yes. A person who experiences same-sex attraction, who lives the law of chastity, who keeps the commandments and participates faithfully in the life of the Church, is in full good standing. Their attraction does not define their standing before God. Their choices do.

The law of chastity applies equally to all members of the Church regardless of sexual orientation. It requires that sexual relations occur only between a man and a woman who are legally and lawfully married. This is the standard for every unmarried person — heterosexual or homosexual. Chastity is not a special burden placed on gay members. It is the universal law of the gospel applied consistently.

Paul listed both fornication and homosexual behaviour in the same breath in 1 Corinthians 6:9–11 — and then said, "And such were some of you: but ye are washed, but ye are sanctified, but ye are justified in the name of the Lord Jesus, and by the Spirit of our God." Were some of you — past tense. The implication is that the atonement of Christ covers the full range of human experience and struggle. No person is beyond the reach of that atonement if they turn to God with sincerity and purpose.

The Church welcomes people who are struggling, who are seeking, and who are trying to live the gospel despite genuine difficulty. That is all any of us are doing.

missionary.simple: Yes — fully. A person who experiences same-sex attraction, lives the law of chastity, and keeps the commandments is in complete good standing in the Church.
missionary.explain: 'The law of chastity applies equally to every unmarried person — straight or gay. It is the same standard. The experience of same-sex attraction is a temptation, not a sin. The choice is what matters.'
missionary.why: God sees your full heart and your full struggle. No person is condemned for the temptations they face — only for the choices they make in response.
missionary.invite: Would you like to know how members who navigate this struggle have found peace, community, and a full life in the gospel?

questions.question: 176. How does the Church view feminism and the Ordain Women movement?
questions.answer:
The Church recognises the equal worth and divine potential of every woman. Women are daughters of a Heavenly Father who loves them. They are co-creators with God in the most sacred of all human work — the bearing and raising of children. They hold callings of enormous responsibility, lead organisations of millions, and officiate in the holiest ordinances performed on earth — in the temples of God.

The Ordain Women movement asked the Church to ordain women to the priesthood. The President of the Church — as the Lord's prophet — addressed this prayerfully and declared that the Lord's answer is no. That is not a cultural decision. It is a revelatory one. And the same obedience we would have given to Peter, to Moses, or to Christ is the obedience we give to the living prophet.

Paul wrote, “Let your women keep silence in the churches: for it is not permitted unto them to speak.”1 Corinthians 14:34 And yet he also wrote that women could prophesy in the congregation. 1 Corinthians 11:5 These are not contradictions — they reflect the same principle we teach today. Women have gifts, callings, and responsibilities fully equal in spiritual significance to those of men. The organisation of those responsibilities differs by divine design, not by cultural prejudice.

The activism of the Ordain Women movement treated a matter of revelation as a matter of policy to be changed by social pressure. The Church does not operate that way. Policy follows revelation. And the Lord has not revealed that women should be ordained to priesthood offices.

missionary.simple: The Church fully recognizes the equal worth and divine potential of every woman. The Ordain Women movement asked for ordination to priesthood offices. The prophet prayerfully answered that the Lord's answer is no.
missionary.explain: 'Your daughters shall prophesy. Women prophesy, serve, lead, counsel, and officiate in the holiest ordinances of the gospel in the temple. The organization of governing responsibilities follows God's revealed order — not social pressure.'
missionary.why: God organized His church in a specific way for reasons that reflect His wisdom. We trust that wisdom even when we do not fully understand every reason.
missionary.invite: Would you like to speak with a woman in our congregation about what her role in the Church means to her and whether she feels valued and empowered?

questions.question: 177. What is the Church's position on gender identity and transgender individuals?
questions.answer:
The Church teaches that gender is an essential characteristic of individual premortal, mortal, and eternal identity and purpose. "The Family: A Proclamation to the World" states that "gender is an eternal characteristic of each individual." This is a doctrinal statement — not a cultural preference.

Every person is a child of God. Every person deserves to be treated with dignity, respect, and Christlike love — regardless of the struggles they face or the questions they carry. The Church does not condemn people who experience gender dysphoria. That experience is real and often deeply painful, and it is treated with compassion.

At the same time, the Church teaches that biological sex is not separate from eternal identity. We do not support surgical transition as a solution to gender dysphoria because we believe it alters what God has given and what will be restored in the resurrection. The resurrection reunites the spirit with the body in its perfected, glorified form — the same body created by God, eternal in its gender.

The pastoral approach is one of love, inclusion, and patient discipleship — helping every person find their identity and worth in their relationship with God rather than in cultural categories. These are questions the Lord is still working through with His people. The Church approaches them carefully, prayerfully, and with the compassion Christ showed to every person whose life was complex.

missionary.simple: The Church teaches that gender is an eternal characteristic of individual identity. Gender dysphoria is real and often deeply painful — and every person experiencing it deserves dignity, compassion, and Christlike love.
missionary.explain: 'We do not condemn people who experience gender dysphoria. We walk alongside them. What we teach is that the body God gave you has an eternal significance that extends through the resurrection — and that His love for you is complete and personal.'
missionary.why: God knows every person by name, in every struggle. His love does not depend on having everything figured out. He meets you exactly where you are.
missionary.invite: Would you like to know how our congregation walks alongside people navigating difficult personal questions with love and without judgment?

questions.question: 194. What is the Church's stance on abortion in cases of rape or incest?
questions.answer:
The Church teaches that human life is sacred from conception. The soul of a child does not become sacred at a certain trimester or after a certain threshold of development. It is sacred from the beginning because God is its Father.

At the same time, the Church recognises specific exceptions — not as endorsements, but as acknowledgements of tragic human circumstances. In cases of rape, incest, serious risk to the life of the mother, or a severely defective foetus that cannot survive outside the womb, the Church teaches that the decision is a deeply personal one between the individual, their family, their doctor, and God — made prayerfully and with great care.

The Church does not list these exceptions to make abortion easy. It lists them because the God who commands "thou shalt not kill" also commands compassion for the victim. A woman whose pregnancy resulted from rape is not in the same moral position as someone who aborted for convenience. God sees that difference. The Church acknowledges it.

But outside these specific exceptions — abortion for convenience, for financial reasons, for career reasons, or simply because a child was not wanted — the Church is clear: life is sacred, agency was given to us to choose righteousness, and the taking of an innocent life is a serious moral weight that God will not overlook.

missionary.simple: The Church teaches that human life is sacred from conception. In cases of rape, incest, serious risk to the mother's life, or severe fetal defect, the decision is deeply personal — made prayerfully with God.
missionary.explain: 'God formed you before He put you in the womb. Every life has divine worth from its beginning. Outside the specific difficult exceptions, using abortion for convenience treats a sacred life as disposable — and God sees that differently.'
missionary.why: Every child is known by God before birth. How we treat the most vulnerable among us reflects what we actually believe about the worth of human souls.
missionary.invite: Would you like to understand how the Church's approach to this difficult question reflects both respect for human life and compassion for those in impossible situations?

questions.question: 208. Why is the Adam-God Theory taught by Brigham Young now considered incorrect?
questions.answer:
Brigham Young's discourse on this subject has been examined carefully by Church historians. The discourse was given in a specific context and used an unusual narrative approach to explain the relationship between Adam, God, and the creation. Some who heard it misunderstood the intent, and subsequent leaders clarified that it did not represent official doctrine.

What we now say clearly is this: Adam is not God the Father — the God we worship, the Father of Jesus Christ. That is not our doctrine. It has never been canonised doctrine. Brigham Young's discourse was a complex theological reflection that some of his contemporaries misunderstood and that some subsequent leaders clarified needed correction.

The living prophet has the authority to clarify the teachings of past leaders when those teachings have created confusion. That is one of the purposes of a living prophet. (Ephesians 4:14 — that we be "no more children, tossed to and fro... with every wind of doctrine.") The clarity that has been given is: Adam is Michael, the archangel, the father of the human race, a great and noble being. He is not God the Eternal Father. Those are two separate beings.

The incident demonstrates the importance of living prophets and continuing revelation. Without them, one speech from one leader in one meeting in 1852 could have become permanent, calcified doctrine. With living prophets, the Lord can correct, clarify, and guide the Church into all truth — exactly as He promised. John 16:13

missionary.simple: The Adam-God theory taught in one of Brigham Young's discourses was never canonized as doctrine, has been officially clarified as incorrect by subsequent Church leaders, and does not represent our teaching.
