missionary.explain: 'Living prophets have the authority to clarify the teachings of past leaders when those teachings have created confusion. That is exactly what happened here — and it is one of the reasons we need living prophets.'
missionary.why: The fact that the Church corrects its own leaders when they err is evidence of divine oversight — not of confusion.
missionary.invite: Would you like to understand what we actually teach about Adam and God — and why having living prophets means the Church can correct course when needed?

questions.question: 213. Why does the Church categorise acting on same-sex attraction as a sin, but not the attraction itself?
questions.answer:
Because the same principle applies to every person on earth, regardless of their specific temptations. A heterosexual man who feels attracted to a woman who is not his wife has not sinned by feeling the attraction. He sins if he acts on it in violation of the law of chastity. The attraction is not the sin. The choice is the sin.

Christ said, “Whosoever looketh on a woman to lust after her hath committed adultery with her already in his heart.”Matthew 5:28 He did not say that noticing a woman's beauty is adultery. He said that cultivating lust — deliberate, dwelling desire — is the sin. The distinction between involuntary attraction and cultivated desire applies to same-sex attraction exactly as it applies to any other form of temptation.

Paul wrote, “There hath no temptation taken you but such as is common to man: but God is faithful, who will not suffer you to be tempted above that ye are able.”1 Corinthians 10:13 Temptation is universal. The experience of same-sex attraction is a temptation that some members of the Church face — just as others face different temptations. No person is condemned for the temptation they experience. Every person is accountable for the choices they make in response to it.

We do not say this lightly or without compassion. We know this standard requires real sacrifice from some members. So does celibacy for unmarried heterosexual members. So does fidelity in a difficult marriage. The law of chastity is demanding for many people. It is the same law for all.

missionary.simple: Because the same principle applies to every person on earth. A man who feels attracted to a woman who is not his wife has not sinned by the attraction. He sins if he acts on it in violation of the law of chastity.
missionary.explain: 'Temptation is not sin. Christ was tempted in all points as we are — and He never sinned. The attraction is the temptation. The choice is the moral act. This distinction applies equally to everyone, regardless of what form their temptation takes.'
missionary.why: God does not condemn you for the temptations you face. He asks you to choose righteousness in response to them — and He gives you the power to do so.
missionary.invite: Would you like to know how members who experience same-sex attraction have found peace, purpose, and full community within the gospel?

questions.question: 214. Does the Church still believe that Lamanites will one day have their skin turned white and delightsome?
questions.answer:
No. This phrasing — drawn from 2 Nephi 30:6 in earlier editions of the Book of Mormon — has been updated. The current edition reads "pure and delightsome" rather than "white and delightsome," reflecting both a textual restoration to an earlier manuscript reading and a recognition that the verse speaks of spiritual transformation, not racial skin colour change.

The Book of Mormon itself is a record given to and for the remnants of the house of Israel in the Americas. Its promise is that when those peoples embrace the gospel of Jesus Christ, they will be "a pure and delightsome people." That is a promise of spiritual and moral transformation — the same promise made to every people in every nation who turn to God with full purpose of heart.

The idea that this verse predicted literal skin colour change was a misinterpretation. It was an extrapolation from racial assumptions common in the 19th century. The text itself does not require that interpretation. The restored reading — "pure and delightsome" — removes the ambiguity and returns to the spiritual meaning the verse has always carried.

The remnants of the house of Joseph in the Americas are a magnificent people with a glorious heritage and a glorious destiny. Their dignity, their worth, and their divine potential have nothing to do with the colour of their skin. It has everything to do with the covenants God made with their fathers — covenants that are being fulfilled in this dispensation as the gospel goes to every nation, kindred, tongue, and people.

missionary.simple: This phrasing from older editions has been updated to read 'pure and delightsome' — returning to an earlier manuscript reading. The verse speaks of spiritual transformation, not racial skin color change.
missionary.explain: 'The Book of Mormon's promise is that when any people embrace the gospel of Jesus Christ, they become pure and delightsome before God — spiritually transformed, regardless of race or background. That is the plain meaning of the text.'
missionary.why: God's promises in the Book of Mormon are for every person of every background who comes to Christ. The text has always been about spiritual renewal, not race.
missionary.invite: Would you like to read 2 Nephi 30 in its context and ask God what His promises in the Book of Mormon mean for you personally?

questions.question: 219. How can God be no respecter of persons if only a tiny fraction of humanity has ever heard the Restored Gospel?
questions.answer:
This is the very question that baptism for the dead answers. If God were no respecter of persons — and He is not — then every person who ever lived must have an equal opportunity to receive the gospel. If most people who ever lived died without that opportunity, then God is either not just or the gospel must reach them somehow.

Peter taught that the gospel was preached to the dead. 1 Peter 4:6 Christ preached to the spirits in prison. 1 Peter 3:19 The provision for those who died without the gospel is the spirit world — where missionaries carry the gospel to every soul who did not receive it in mortality.

Paul wrote in Hebrews 11:40 that the faithful dead could not be made perfect without us. The proxy ordinances performed in temples complete what they could not do for themselves in mortality. The ordinances are provided. The choice is theirs. Every soul gets an equal opportunity.

Acts 10:34–35 — "God is no respecter of persons: but in every nation he that feareth him, and worketh righteousness, is accepted with him." In every nation. Not just nations where the missionaries have gone. Every nation. The acceptance is based on the response to whatever light a person has — and God gives sufficient light to all who seek Him sincerely. Those who respond to that light in mortality or in the spirit world will receive the fulness when it is available to them.

No soul is lost for want of opportunity. God's plan is comprehensive enough, merciful enough, and just enough to reach every one of His children. That is the testimony of the restored gospel.

🕮 Scripture Evidence: Spirit World & Salvation for the Dead

  • 1 Corinthians 15:29 — “Else what shall they do which are baptized for the dead, if the dead rise not at all? why are they then baptized for the dead?”
  • 1 Peter 3:18-20 — Christ was “put to death in the flesh, but quickened by the Spirit: By which also he went and preached unto the spirits in prison.”
  • 1 Peter 4:6 — “For for this cause was the gospel preached also to them that are dead, that they might be judged according to men in the flesh, but live according to God in the spirit.”
  • Luke 16:22-23 — The beggar Lazarus was carried to Abraham’s bosom after death — showing distinct realms in the afterlife.
  • Luke 23:43 — Jesus tells the thief: “To day shalt thou be with me in paradise.”
  • Ecclesiastes 12:7 — “The spirit shall return unto God who gave it.”
  • Philippians 1:23 — Paul expresses desire “to depart, and to be with Christ” — confirming conscious spirit world existence.
  • Hebrews 12:23 — References “the spirits of just men made perfect” — confirming ongoing spirit progression.

missionary.simple: Because God loves every person of every race and background equally. Every person who ever lived will have a full and fair opportunity to hear and receive the gospel — in this life or in the spirit world.
missionary.explain: 'Peter declared that God is no respecter of persons. Paul said in every nation, he that fears God and works righteousness is accepted. The restored gospel's provision for the dead through temple work makes this perfectly true — no one is excluded.'
missionary.why: The most just doctrine in all of religion is that no soul who sincerely wanted God will be turned away because of where or when they were born.
missionary.invite: Would you like to understand how the restored gospel makes salvation available to every person who has ever lived — not just those born in the right time or place?

questions.question: 222. Is it ethical for the Church to hold reserves while asking for tithing from members in developing nations?
questions.answer:
The Church holds reserves because it is a responsible steward of the sacred funds it has been given. That is not greed. That is wisdom. Joseph in Egypt stored seven years of grain against seven years of famine. (Genesis 41.) His reserves saved not just Egypt but the surrounding nations. He was not criticised for holding those reserves. He was honoured for the wisdom that made them possible.

Malachi 3:10 commands bringing tithes into the storehouse — creating exactly the kind of reserves some question. The storehouse exists to care for the poor, to fund the missionary work, to build temples and chapels, to provide education, and to respond to humanitarian disasters worldwide. Without reserves, the Church would be incapable of doing any of those things when they are needed most.

The Church's humanitarian programme — funded in significant part by these reserves — operates in more than 190 countries. It does not publicise its donations because Christ commanded against it. Matthew 6:1–4 The left hand is not to know what the right hand gives. The scale of what is given quietly and without fanfare would answer this question clearly.

Asking a member in a developing nation for tithing is not exploitation. It is invitation — to enter into a covenant with God that comes with its own promise: “I will... open you the windows of heaven, and pour you out a blessing, that there shall not be room enough to receive it.”Malachi 3:10 Every faithful tithe-payer in every nation is a recipient of that promise. The Church's reserves are the stewardship of those consecrated funds — managed for the glory of God and the blessing of His children.

missionary.simple: The Church holds reserves because it is a responsible steward — exactly as Joseph stored grain in Egypt for seven years before seven years of famine. Reserves make compassionate service sustainable across generations.
missionary.explain: 'God commanded bringing tithes into the storehouse to create reserves for the poor. The Church operates humanitarian programs in 190 countries funded by those reserves — quietly, without fanfare, because Christ said not to announce what you give.'
missionary.why: The scale of what the Church gives quietly and without publicity would answer every question about whether consecrated funds are used for God's purposes.
missionary.invite: Would you like to know what the Church does with its resources and how those resources reach people in need around the world?

questions.question: 226. Is the folk legend that Cain is still wandering the earth as Bigfoot based on actual leadership statements?
questions.answer:
There is a documented account from the journal of David W. Patten — an apostle — recording that he encountered a very large, dark, hairy man who identified himself as Cain and asked to be killed. This was reported in Lycurgus Wilson's 1900 biography of Patten. Later leaders — including Spencer W. Kimball — referenced this account.

This is not canonised doctrine. It is a historical account from the journal of one apostle — recorded by a biographer, referenced by later leaders, but never included in the standard works or official Church publications as doctrine.

The association of this account with "Bigfoot" is a folk legend — a popular cultural overlay added by others, not taught by the Church. The Church has never officially taught that Bigfoot is Cain. The original account — if taken at face value — describes a singular encounter with a specific being, not an ongoing supernatural presence in the forests of North America producing blurry photographs.

We take the account of David Patten seriously as a historical record of his experience. We do not canonise it as doctrine. And we certainly do not teach that it has any connection to the Bigfoot phenomenon. The two things have been linked by popular culture, not by the Church.

missionary.simple: There is a documented account from an apostle's journal of encountering a person who identified himself as Cain. This is not canonized doctrine, and its association with 'Bigfoot' is popular culture — not Church teaching.
missionary.explain: 'We take the historical account seriously as one man's recorded experience. We do not teach that it is ongoing, universal, or connected to any cultural legend. The Church has never officially taught anything about Bigfoot.'
missionary.why: The Church distinguishes carefully between recorded historical accounts, personal opinions of leaders, and official canonized doctrine — and that distinction matters.
missionary.invite: Would you like to understand how the Church determines what is doctrine versus what is personal opinion or historical account?

questions.question: 230. Why are Church disciplinary councils conducted in private without a legal advocate for the accused?
questions.answer:
A membership council — which is what the Church now calls a disciplinary council — is not a criminal continuing. It has no power to imprison, fine, or compel anything. Its purpose is pastoral — to help a person assess the seriousness of their actions, to facilitate repentance, and to determine whether formal Church discipline serves the member's spiritual welfare and the protection of others.

The privacy of the continuing protects the member. Their struggle, their sin, their circumstances are not made public. The council consists of the bishop and two counsellors — or, in cases involving potential membership withdrawal, the stake presidency and high council. These are men who are called, who have covenanted before God to serve faithfully, and who have no personal stake in the outcome.

The accused member may speak, present their perspective, bring support, and respond to the concerns raised. They may bring a companion from the Church for support. What they may not bring is an adversarial legal advocate — because the continuing is not adversarial. It is pastoral.

The Church is not a court of law. It has no coercive power. The only consequence it can impose is a change in Church membership status. A person who disagrees with the outcome is free to appeal. A person who considers their membership worthless can simply leave. The council exists to help — not to punish. Where individual councils have fallen short of that ideal, the Church's guidance on conducting them has been consistently updated to better protect and serve the member.

missionary.simple: A membership council is pastoral — not a criminal trial. It exists to help a person assess their standing, facilitate repentance, and restore them to full fellowship. Privacy protects the member.
missionary.explain: 'The council has no power to fine, imprison, or compel anything. Its only authority is over membership status. The member can speak, respond, and appeal. The goal is always restoration — exactly the pattern James described.'
missionary.why: God designed His church to bring people back to Him, not push them away. The council is an instrument of mercy used only when mercy requires honest accountability.
missionary.invite: Would you like to understand how the Church walks alongside members through serious mistakes and what restoration actually looks like in practice?

questions.question: 231. Did the Church ever officially apologise for teaching that dark skin was a mark of divine displeasure?
questions.answer:
The Church has not used the word "apology" in the formal statement it released. What it has done is publish an official Gospel Topics Essay — representing the position of the First Presidency and Quorum of the Twelve — that explicitly disavows the theories that were taught. That essay states that the Church "disavows the theories advanced in the past that black skin is a sign of divine disfavour or curse, or that it reflects actions in a premortal life."

Whether that constitutes an apology in the formal sense — or whether the word "disavow" carries the weight that "apologise" would — is a question of pastoral and political language that reasonable people can assess differently. What is not in question is the substance: the Church officially, in its own words, says those teachings were wrong.

Some feel that a stronger, more direct apology is needed. That is a legitimate feeling and we do not dismiss it. The pain caused by those teachings — to Black members who were told their skin colour reflected something about their eternal worth — is real, lasting, and deserving of acknowledgement.

What we can say with confidence is this: the God we worship does not hold a person's race against them. He never did. The restriction was a human failing carried into the Church by men shaped by their times. God corrected it. The Church has acknowledged it. And every blessing of the gospel is now fully available to every child of God regardless of race.

missionary.simple: The Church published an official Gospel Topics Essay that explicitly disavows the past theories about dark skin being a mark of divine disfavor. The substance of the statement is a clear acknowledgment that those theories were wrong.
missionary.explain: 'The essay states plainly that the Church disavows these theories. Whether the word apology was used matters less than the substance — the Church has said, in its own words, that those teachings were the opinions of men, not the word of God.'
missionary.why: God's people acknowledge error honestly. That honesty is a sign of divine oversight — a church willing to correct itself when it has erred.
missionary.invite: Would you like to read the Church's official position on this and ask God whether a church willing to acknowledge past error deserves your honest investigation?

questions.question: 233. Did the Church ever officially apologise for the Mountain Meadows Massacre?
questions.answer:
Yes. In 2007, at the 150th anniversary of the Mountain Meadows Massacre — in which approximately 120 emigrants were attacked and killed in Utah Territory on September 11, 1857 — with only 17 small children spared — then-First Presidency Counsellor Henry B. Eyring stood at the massacre site and spoke directly to the descendants of the victims. He said: "What was done here long ago by members of our Church represents a terrible and inexcusable departure from Christian teaching and conduct... I express profound regret for the massacre carried out in this valley 150 years ago today and for the undue and untold suffering experienced by the victims then and by their relatives to the present time."

That is an official apology from a member of the First Presidency — the highest governing body of the Church — speaking on behalf of the Church at the site of the event, to the families of those killed. It is as official an apology as any institution can give.

The massacre was a tragedy — an act carried out by local Church members and militia in circumstances that Church historians have studied extensively. Brigham Young did not order it. The evidence points to local leaders acting without authorisation during a period of intense anti-government and anti-Church tension that does not excuse what was done but does explain the environment in which it happened.

The victims were innocent. The act was a tragedy. The Church has said so plainly.

questions.question: 12. Why do Latter-day Saints not drink coffee or tea? Where is that in the Bible?
questions.answer:
The Word of Wisdom was given by revelation in 1833 — not because coffee and tea appear by name in the Bible, but because God has always given health commandments suited to the needs of His people at the time He addresses them.

Paul wrote that our bodies are the temple of the Holy Ghost, and that we are to glorify God in our body. 1 Corinthians 6:19–20 That is the principle. The specific application comes through living prophets.

This is precisely the purpose of continuing revelation. Coffee and tea did not exist in the days of Moses or Paul. A scripture written in 50 AD cannot address every health question that arises in 1833 or today. If it could, there would be no need for living prophets — which is exactly the argument being used against us.

Proverbs 20:1 says, "Wine is a mocker, strong drink is raging." The principle of protecting the body from harmful substances runs throughout scripture. The Word of Wisdom is the Lord applying that eternal principle to the specific conditions of our time. And the fruits of it are plain. Matthew 7:16 still applies: by their fruits ye shall know them.

The Lord gave the Word of Wisdom through Joseph Smith in 1833, promising those who obeyed it: "And all saints who remember to keep and do these sayings, walking in obedience to the commandments, shall receive health in their navel and marrow to their bones."D&C 89:18 And further — they shall find wisdom and great treasures of knowledge. D&C 89:19 The fruits of that law, observed over generations, are plain.

missionary.simple: Yes. In 2007, First Presidency Counsellor Henry B. Eyring stood at the massacre site and expressed profound regret on behalf of the Church. It was an official, direct apology.
missionary.explain: 'The massacre was a tragedy carried out by local members without authorization. Brigham Young did not order it. The Church has acknowledged it plainly and stood at the site to say so.'
missionary.why: God's people are imperfect and sometimes fail terribly. What matters is that God corrects His church and His church acknowledges error honestly.
missionary.invite: Would you like to know how the Church has handled difficult moments in its history — and whether that honesty strengthens or weakens your confidence in it?

questions.question: 16. Why don't you celebrate some traditional Christian practices the same way as other churches?
questions.answer:
We follow the scriptures rather than tradition where the two conflict. Paul commanded the saints to “keep the ordinances, as I delivered them to you.”1 Corinthians 11:2 The question is not what tradition has taught, but what the Lord originally delivered.

The Lord's Supper was instituted with bread and a cup. Christ specifically said He would not drink of the “fruit of the vine.”Matthew 26:29 Paul clarified: “For as often as ye eat this bread and drink this cup, ye do show the Lord's death till he come.”1 Corinthians 11:26 It is a remembrance, not a transformation.

Many Christian traditions entered the church not through scripture but through uninspired councils, pagan influence, and the gradual drift that Paul, Peter, and John all foretold. The Nicene Creed was composed in the third century by men acting at the command of Constantine — a pagan emperor. That is not apostolic authority.

We do not reject tradition out of rebellion. We restore the original, because Christ established one — and the plain and precious parts of it were lost.

Nephi foresaw this very thing: "After the book of the Lamb of God... there are many plain and precious things taken away from the book."1 Nephi 13:28 The Restoration did not add to the Bible. It restored what had been removed — including the ordinances, the authority, and the structure that Christ originally established.

questions.question: 17. Why do missionaries focus so much on conversion instead of just helping people?
questions.answer:
Christ sent His apostles to preach the gospel, not just to feed the poor. In Matthew 28:19–20 He said, "Go ye therefore and teach all nations, baptizing them in the name of the Father, and of the Son, and of the Holy Ghost." That is a command to convert — to bring people into a covenant relationship with God.

Paul said, “Woe is unto me if I preach not the gospel.”1 Corinthians 9:16 He understood that physical comfort without eternal truth leaves a person helped today but lost forever. The most loving thing a person can do for another is to share truth that leads to eternal life.

We also help people. The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints has one of the largest welfare and humanitarian programs in the world — built on scripture and run without paid ministry. Matthew 25:34–40 is the foundation of that work. Feeding the hungry, clothing the naked, visiting the sick — these are commandments we take seriously. But Christ did not come to make the world comfortable. He came to save it. We follow Him — in both the service He commanded and the preaching He commanded.

Nephi's brother Jacob declared: "We labored diligently among our people, that we might persuade them to come unto Christ."Jacob 1:7 That is the missionary purpose in one sentence. Persuade people to come unto Christ — not simply to become better people or to receive food or shelter, but to enter into a covenant relationship with the living God.

missionary.simple: Christ commanded His apostles to go into all the world and preach the gospel to every creature. Sharing the most important truth you have ever found is not aggression — it is love.
missionary.explain: 'Paul said woe is unto me if I preach not the gospel. He felt a debt to every person he had not yet reached. If you had a cure for a terminal illness, would you keep it to yourself out of politeness?'
missionary.why: Missionary work is an act of love, not salesmanship. Every person on earth deserves the opportunity to hear the restored gospel and choose for themselves.
missionary.invite: Would you be willing to hear what missionaries actually teach — the full message — and decide for yourself whether it is true?

questions.question: 18. Why do you keep records and genealogies so seriously in your church?
questions.answer:
The entire Old Testament rests on genealogy. Matthew 1 and Luke 3 both record the lineage of Jesus Christ in detail — to establish His right to be the Messiah through the seed of David and the house of Judah. You cannot separate genealogy from scripture without destroying half of it.

Malachi 4:5–6 prophesied that Elijah would come to "turn the hearts of the fathers to the children, and the hearts of the children to their fathers." That prophecy was not fulfilled by John the Baptist — John himself denied being that Elijah. John 1:21 It required a future fulfillment. Elijah came to Joseph Smith in 1836 and restored the keys of that sealing work.

Paul wrote in Hebrews 11:39–40 that the faithful dead "received not the promise: God having provided some better thing for us, that they without us should not be made perfect." They cannot be made perfect without us. We cannot be made perfect without them. Family history work is not a hobby. It is a covenant obligation that ties the living and the dead together in the bonds of the gospel.

The Doctrine and Covenants declares: "The earth will be smitten with a curse unless there is a welding link of some kind or other between the fathers and the children."D&C 128:18 Temple and genealogy work is not optional — it is the welding link God requires between generations. Without it, the earth cannot be fully redeemed.

questions.question: 19. Why are your meetings structured and scheduled instead of free-flowing like other churches?
questions.answer:
Paul instructed clearly: “Let all things be done decently and in order.”1 Corinthians 14:40 Order is not a human invention imposed on the gospel. It is a commandment.

The primitive church met on the Lord's day, partook of the sacrament, heard instruction, and was edified according to a pattern. (Acts 20:7; 1 Corinthians 16:2.) Paul's epistles themselves were written to be read in order to the assembled saints — that is structured worship.

The gifts of the Spirit were to be exercised in order, not in confusion. Paul devoted an entire chapter to regulating the use of gifts in meetings because disorder had entered the church at Corinth. (1 Corinthians 14.) His instruction was not to abandon structure but to restore it.

God is not the author of confusion. 1 Corinthians 14:33 Where confusion governs worship, something other than the Spirit of God is at work. Our meetings are ordered so that every person can be edified, the sacrament can be administered properly, and the Spirit of the Lord can be present.

The Doctrine and Covenants gives the exact pattern for how meetings of the Church are to be conducted: "And the members shall be received by baptism... and they shall partake of the sacrament... and there shall be instructors."D&C 20:37–42 Order in worship is a commandment — not a preference.

questions.question: 33. Why do you practice tithing? Isn't that an Old Testament law that was done away?
questions.answer:
Christ himself endorsed tithing. In Matthew 23:23 He said to the Pharisees, "Ye pay tithe of mint and anise and cumin, and have omitted the weightier matters of the law, judgment, mercy, and faith: these ought ye to have done, and not to leave the other undone." He said not to leave tithing undone.

Abraham paid tithes to Melchizedek — and this was before the Law of Moses existed. Genesis 14:20; Hebrews 7:4–6 Tithing is older than the Mosaic law. It was not created by Moses and therefore was not done away by Christ.

Paul taught in 2 Corinthians 9:6–7 that he who sows sparingly reaps sparingly, and he who sows bountifully reaps bountifully. "Every man according as he purposeth in his heart, so let him give; not grudgingly, or of necessity: for God loveth a cheerful giver." The principle of giving to God is not an Old Testament relic. It is a covenant principle that runs through both testaments.

Malachi 3:10 — "Bring ye all the tithes into the storehouse... and prove me now herewith, saith the Lord of hosts, if I will not open you the windows of heaven." Christ came to fulfill the law, not to make giving to God obsolete.

missionary.simple: Christ himself endorsed tithing — He said not to leave it undone. Abraham paid tithes to Melchizedek before the Law of Moses existed. It is older than any church tradition.
missionary.explain: 'Malachi challenged Israel to prove God by bringing the full tithe and watching what He does. The promise is open to anyone. Faithful tithe-payers in every country testify that the ninety percent, under God's blessing, goes further than the hundred percent ever did.'
missionary.why: Tithing is not a tax — it is a covenant. It connects you to God's storehouse and opens the windows of heaven over your own life.
missionary.invite: Would you be willing to try paying a full tithe for three months and see what God does with the ninety percent?

questions.question: 50. Why do Latter-day Saints do missionary work so aggressively? Isn't it disrespectful to other faiths?
questions.answer:
Paul wrote, “I am debtor both to the Greeks, and to the Barbarians; both to the wise, and to the unwise. So, as much as in me is, I am ready to preach the gospel.”Romans 1:14–15 He felt a debt to every human being to share what he knew to be true.

Christ commanded it. “Go ye into all the world, and preach the gospel to every creature.”Mark 16:15 He did not say go only to those who invite you. He said go. The disciples went to synagogues. They went to marketplaces. They went to homes. They went to prisons. They went to the Areopagus in Athens. They were not timid, and they were not invited.

Respect for people and their agency is real. We do not force anyone. We share and we move on if rejected. Christ himself said that where they were not received, the disciples were to "shake off the dust" from their feet and go elsewhere. Matthew 10:14 We follow that instruction.

But to say nothing when you believe you have the truth that leads to eternal life — that is not respect. That is indifference dressed as courtesy. If I knew the building you were in was on fire and I said nothing out of politeness, that would not be kindness. It would be cowardice.

missionary.simple: Paul said he was debtor to every person he had not yet reached. He went to synagogues, marketplaces, and homes. He was not invited everywhere. Christ sent the apostles — they did not wait to be asked.
missionary.explain: 'If a building was on fire, silence out of politeness would not be kindness. Sharing the gospel is not disrespect to your faith — it is the deepest kind of love from someone who genuinely believes it is true.'
missionary.why: Every missionary who has ever knocked on your door was doing it because they believe you deserve to know what they know. That sincerity deserves respect, even in disagreement.
missionary.invite: Would you be willing to hear what missionaries actually teach — with no obligation — and make your own honest assessment?

questions.question: 58. Why do you call your leaders "President" and not "Pastor" or "Bishop" as the Bible says?
questions.answer:
We have both bishops and presidents — and the Bible supports both. The word "president" simply means one who presides. Paul wrote that elders who rule well are “worthy of double honour, especially they who labour in the word and doctrine.”1 Timothy 5:17 To preside is to rule, to lead, to govern. The title reflects the function.

The word "Pope" appears nowhere in the Bible. Neither does "Cardinal," "Archbishop," or "Reverend." Yet the churches represented here use all of those titles without a moment of hesitation. If titles not found in the Bible are acceptable for them, then a title derived from the scriptural concept of presiding is certainly acceptable for us.

Our bishops serve local congregations exactly as the bishop Paul described in 1 Timothy 3:1–7. Our stake presidents preside over regions of the Church as the apostles presided over regions in the primitive church. Our prophet and president leads the entire Church as Peter led the primitive church after Christ's ascension — by revelation, by authority, and by the keys Christ conferred.

The title does not define the office. The authority does.

missionary.simple: Because the title reflects the function — to preside. President is a scriptural principle, not a corporate invention. Our bishops serve locally exactly as the New Testament bishop Paul described.
missionary.explain: 'Pope, Cardinal, and Archbishop appear nowhere in the Bible either. The title is far less important than the authority and function behind it. Our bishop has every qualification Paul listed — and serves without pay.'
missionary.why: The structure of the restored church matches the primitive church in function — what matters is the authority, not the label.
missionary.invite: Would you like to compare the organization of the primitive church as Paul described it with the structure of the restored church today?

questions.question: 64. Why do Latter-day Saints observe the Word of Wisdom but eat meat? Doesn't the Bible also restrict meat?
questions.answer:
The Word of Wisdom, given in 1833, does address meat. It teaches that flesh of beasts and fowls was ordained for the use of man — but “only in times of winter, or of cold, or famine.”Doctrine and Covenants 89:12–13 It also teaches that grain is the staff of life for man. These principles are a guide for health, not an absolute prohibition.

Paul addressed the question of meat directly: “For every creature of God is good, and nothing to be refused, if it be received with thanksgiving: for it is sanctified by the word of God and prayer.”1 Timothy 4:4–5 He also warned against those who would command “to abstain from meats, which God hath created to be received with thanksgiving.”1 Timothy 4:3 Paul named excessive meat restriction as a sign of apostasy — a doctrine of devils.

The principle is moderation, gratitude, and wisdom — not prohibition. We do not eat meat with gluttony or waste. We receive it as God's provision. And we follow the broader counsel of the Word of Wisdom — avoiding tobacco, alcohol, coffee, and tea — which science has confirmed over and over again as harmful to the body. The Lord knew before science did.

missionary.simple: The Word of Wisdom permits meat but encourages using it sparingly and with gratitude — especially in seasons of need. Paul warned against requiring total abstinence from meat as a doctrine of devils.
missionary.explain: 'The principle is moderation, gratitude, and wisdom — not prohibition. We receive meat as God's provision, honor it, and do not over-indulge. Paul himself said everything God created is good and nothing to be refused with thanksgiving.'
missionary.why: The Word of Wisdom is about honoring the body God gave you — not through rigid prohibition, but through wisdom and gratitude.
missionary.invite: Would you like to understand the full scope of the Word of Wisdom and what the science behind it reveals about God's foresight?

questions.question: 83. Why does your church not allow divorce and remarriage freely? Doesn't God forgive broken marriages?
questions.answer:
Christ addressed this directly. “Whosoever shall put away his wife, except it be for fornication, and shall marry another, committeth adultery.”Matthew 19:9 That is not our policy. That is the Saviour's standard. He named one exception — fornication. Outside of that, remarriage while a former spouse lives constitutes adultery in His words.

We do not say God does not forgive the divorced and remarried. He is merciful to all who repent and seek Him. But forgiveness does not lower the standard. Christ forgave the woman taken in adultery — and then said, “Go, and sin no more.”John 8:11 Forgiveness does not endorse the continuation of the sin. It invites departure from it.

Our temples perform eternal sealings — marriages bound by divine authority for time and eternity. Matthew 16:19 These cannot be dissolved by a civil court. A sealing can be cancelled only by the authority that performed it — through the approval of the prophet. This is not bureaucracy. It is the recognition that what God has joined, man has no authority to separate.

The casual approach to divorce in modern Christianity — where marriage is treated as a contract rather than a covenant — is one of the most devastating departures from the gospel. Families are the unit of eternity. Their preservation matters eternally, not just temporally.

missionary.simple: Christ said that whoever divorces and remarries — except for fornication — commits adultery. That is His standard. We take it seriously because marriage is a covenant, not a contract.
missionary.explain: 'Temple sealings can be cancelled only by the authority that performed them. A civil court has no authority over what God has joined. Grace covers the repentant — but grace does not lower the standard Christ set.'
missionary.why: The seriousness with which we treat marriage reflects how seriously we take its eternal nature. Families are not temporary arrangements — they are eternal covenants.
missionary.invite: Would you like to understand what the restored gospel teaches about marriage as an eternal covenant and how that changes the way members approach it?

questions.question: 100. Why do your missionaries travel in pairs? Isn't one person enough to preach the gospel?
questions.answer:
Christ himself established the pattern. “He sent them two and two before his face into every city and place.”Luke 10:1 He sent the twelve out two by two. Mark 6:7 He sent the seventy two by two. The pattern of pairs is deliberate and repeated throughout His ministry.

The reason is both practical and doctrinal. Paul wrote that “in the mouth of two or three witnesses shall every word be established.”2 Corinthians 13:1 The testimony of two witnesses has legal and spiritual weight that a single witness does not. When two missionaries stand at a door bearing the same testimony, that is two witnesses — exactly as the law of God requires.

Practically, two missionaries protect each other, strengthen each other, and keep each other accountable. Paul never traveled alone in his missionary journeys. He had Barnabas, then Silas, then Timothy, then others. The companions of the apostles are named throughout Acts — because they were there, serving alongside the apostle, never sent out in isolation.

Solomon wrote, “Two are better than one; because they have a good reward for their labour. For if they fall, the one will lift up his fellow.”Ecclesiastes 4:9–10 The Lord organised His work from the beginning with this principle in mind. We follow it not because it is convenient — because often it is not — but because it is the Lord's pattern.

missionary.simple: Christ sent His disciples two by two — it is His own established pattern. Two witnesses fulfill the biblical law of testimony. Companions protect, strengthen, and hold each other accountable.
missionary.explain: 'Paul never traveled alone. Peter and John went together. Two missionaries standing at your door are following the exact pattern Christ established when He sent out the seventy two by two.'
missionary.why: The companionship pattern is not a policy — it is a principle of witness, accountability, and mutual strength that goes back to Christ Himself.
missionary.invite: Would you be willing to invite the missionaries into your home and hear what two witnesses of the restored gospel have to share with you?

questions.question: 114. Why do your missionaries commit two years of their lives to this work? Isn't that extreme?
questions.answer:
Paul said, “Woe is unto me, if I preach not the gospel.”1 Corinthians 9:16 He considered it a debt he owed to every person he had not yet reached. He traveled across three continents, endured shipwrecks, floggings, imprisonment, and stoning — and he considered it a privilege.

Christ said, “The harvest truly is plenteous, but the labourers are few; pray ye therefore the Lord of the harvest, that he will send forth labourers into his harvest.”Matthew 9:37–38 The harvest is vast. The work is urgent. Time is short. Two years is not extreme when eternal souls are in the balance.

What is given up in two years? Career advancement. Personal comfort. Social relationships. What is gained? The ability to stand before God and say, "I went. I served. I gave what I had." The twelve apostles gave everything. Paul gave everything. The early saints gave everything — many gave their lives. Two years is a modest offering by comparison.

The young men and women of this Church do not go because they are compelled. They go because they believe. And many of them return with a faith so deep, a testimony so rooted, and a character so forged in service that two years transforms them for the rest of their lives. The investment is not in the mission. The investment is in the person — and through the person, in the kingdom of God.

missionary.simple: Two years is a modest offering compared to what the apostles gave. Paul endured shipwrecks, floggings, and imprisonment. Young missionaries give two years because they believe eternal souls are worth it.
missionary.explain: 'Paul said woe is unto me if I preach not the gospel. He felt a debt to every person he had not yet reached. That same conviction drives every young person who leaves home, family, and career for two years to share what they know is true.'
missionary.why: The sacrifice of a mission shapes character in ways nothing else replicates — and it blesses the lives of every person the missionary reaches.
missionary.invite: Would you be willing to ask a returned missionary what their mission did to them spiritually and whether they would do it again?

questions.question: 115. Why do you believe in the law of consecration? Isn't that just Communism?
questions.answer:
The early Church in Jerusalem practised it — and it was not Communism. “And all that believed were together, and had all things common; and sold their possessions and goods, and parted them to all men, as every man had need.”Acts 2:44–45 That is Acts. That is the New Testament. That is the practice of the apostles.

The difference between the law of consecration and Communism is everything. Communism is coerced by the state. The law of consecration is voluntarily offered to God. Communism removes private ownership. The law of consecration preserves stewardship — individuals manage what they have been given as stewards of God. Communism serves the collective. The law of consecration serves God and through God serves the needy. Communism is compelled by force. The law of consecration is offered in covenant.

Paul wrote in 2 Corinthians 9:7, "Every man according as he purposeth in his heart, so let him give; not grudgingly, or of necessity: for God loveth a cheerful giver." Cheerful. Voluntary. From the heart. That is the spirit of the law of consecration. It is the exact opposite of Communism.

Communism is a distortion of something originally divine — the principle of united order taught by the apostles. The existence of a counterfeit does not invalidate the original. If anything, it confirms that a true principle existed worth imitating.

missionary.simple: The law of consecration is not communism. It is voluntarily offering your time and talents to God and His kingdom. Communism is forced by the state. Consecration is chosen in love.
missionary.explain: 'The early saints in Jerusalem had all things in common — not because the government demanded it, but because they loved each other. Paul said God loves a cheerful giver, not a compelled one. That is the difference between consecration and communism.'
missionary.why: True discipleship involves giving your whole life to God — not because you must, but because you understand what He has given you.
missionary.invite: Would you like to understand what it means to consecrate your life to God and what that kind of discipleship looks like in practice?

questions.question: 128. Why do you believe that tithing is ten percent? The Bible just says a tenth — couldn't that be less?
questions.answer:
A tenth is ten percent. That is the definition of the word. "Tithe" in both Hebrew and Greek means one-tenth. Abraham gave Melchizedek “a tenth of all.”Hebrews 7:2 Malachi commands bringing “all the tithes into the storehouse.”Malachi 3:10 All the tithes — the full tenth.

The calculation is straightforward. Confusion arises only when people want to reduce the obligation — which is why interpretations about "net versus gross" and "increase" versus "income" multiply. The principle is simple: ten percent of what God gives you goes back to God. The remaining ninety percent is yours to manage as a steward.

The wonderful thing about tithing is the promise attached to it. Malachi 3:10 — "Prove me now herewith, saith the Lord of hosts, if I will not open you the windows of heaven, and pour you out a blessing, that there shall not be room enough to receive it." God invites the test. Give the tenth. See what He does with the rest.

Every faithful tithe-payer in this Church has a testimony of this promise. Not because they got rich — but because they found that the ninety percent, managed under God's blessing, stretched further than the hundred percent ever did without it. That is not coincidence. That is the Lord keeping His word.

missionary.simple: A tithe is ten percent — that is the definition of the word in both Hebrew and Greek. Malachi commands bringing all the tithes, and Abraham gave a tenth of all to Melchizedek as the pattern.
missionary.explain: 'The calculation is not complicated. Ten percent of what God gives you goes back to God. The promise attached to it is open to anyone willing to test it: I will open the windows of heaven and pour out a blessing there is not room enough to receive.'
missionary.why: Tithing is one of the clearest ways to demonstrate whether you trust God as your provider or whether you trust your own calculations more.
missionary.invite: Would you be willing to pay a full tithe for three months and see whether God keeps His promise to open the windows of heaven over your life?

questions.question: 132. Why do you practice home and visiting teaching? Isn't Sunday worship enough?
questions.answer:
Paul wrote in Hebrews 10:24–25 — "And let us consider one another to provoke unto love and to good works: not forsaking the assembling of ourselves together, as the manner of some is; but exhorting one another." Exhorting one another — person to person, beyond the Sunday meeting.

The primitive church did not operate through Sunday services alone. They went "from house to house," breaking bread and teaching and fellowshipping. Acts 2:46 Paul taught “publicly, and from house to house.”Acts 20:20 The ministry of the early Church was deeply personal — built on relationships, on regular contact, on knowing people's needs and responding to them.

James wrote, “Pure religion and undefiled before God and the Father is this, To visit the fatherless and widows in their affliction.”James 1:27 Visit. Go to them. Not invite them to a program. Go where they are and minister where they are.

The Lord said to the bishop Newel K. Whitney, “I give unto you a commandment that ye shall visit the poor and the needy and administer to their relief.”Doctrine and Covenants 44:6 The command to visit the members is as old as the Church. A congregation of people who see each other only on Sundays is not a community. It is an audience. We are building something more — a Zion people who know each other, bear each other's burdens, and mourn with those who mourn.

missionary.simple: Home and visiting teaching — now called ministering — follows the exact pattern of the primitive church. Paul taught from house to house. The saints gathered daily in homes. Personal ministry is the Lord's standard.
missionary.explain: 'James said pure religion is to visit the fatherless and widows. Not invite them to a program — visit them. The Church asks members to know each other personally and care for each other the way Christ would.'
missionary.why: A congregation of people who see each other only on Sundays is not a covenant community. Ministering builds the kind of bonds that hold people through every hardship.
missionary.invite: Would you like to experience what it feels like to be part of a community where people genuinely know and care for each other?

questions.question: 142. Why do you teach that a person's social standing, wealth, or education has no bearing on their standing before God?
questions.answer:
Paul wrote to the Galatians, “There is neither Jew nor Greek, there is neither bond nor free, there is neither male nor female: for ye are all one in Christ Jesus.”Galatians 3:28 All one. No ranking by birth, by nationality, by social status, or by gender before God.

James addressed it directly. He described a church meeting where a rich man in fine clothing was given the best seat and a poor man in vile raiment was told to stand or sit on the floor. “Are ye not then partial in yourselves, and are become judges of evil thoughts?”James 2:4 The partiality James condemned is the norm in the world. It has no place in the Church of Jesus Christ.

Peter declared it in Acts 10:34 — "Of a truth I perceive that God is no respecter of persons." This was Peter's conclusion after seeing a vision that broke every social and religious barrier he had grown up with. God does not honour the socially privileged. He looks on the heart. 1 Samuel 16:7

In our Church, a farm labourer and a university professor hold the same priesthood and take the same sacrament from the same tray. A teenage deacon passes the sacrament to a general authority. A convert of three months sits alongside a family with seven generations of membership. In the kingdom of God, the only rank that matters is faithfulness.

missionary.simple: Before God, your bank account, your degrees, and your social connections mean nothing. Paul said there is neither Jew nor Greek, bond nor free — all are one in Christ Jesus.
missionary.explain: 'In our meetings, a farm laborer and a university professor take the same sacrament from the same tray. A teenage deacon passes the sacrament to a general authority. In the kingdom of God, the only rank that matters is faithfulness.'
missionary.why: The gospel creates the most genuinely equal community on earth — not because we ignore differences, but because we recognize that only one thing actually matters before God.
missionary.invite: Would you like to come to a Sunday meeting and experience for yourself what genuine equality in a covenant community feels like?

questions.question: 143. Why does your church place such emphasis on education? Doesn't the Spirit teach all we need to know?
questions.answer:
The Lord himself commanded it. “Seek ye out of the best books words of wisdom; seek learning, even by study and also by faith.”Doctrine and Covenants 88:118 Study and faith. Both. Not faith instead of study. Study anchored in faith.

Paul wrote that he had been “taught according to the perfect manner of the law of the fathers.”Acts 22:3 He was educated. His education made him more effective — he could quote Greek poets to the Athenians Acts 17:28, cite Roman law to the authorities, and engage the intellectual world of his day. His faith was not diminished by learning. It was given a broader range of expression.

The Book of Mormon teaches that “to be learned is good if they hearken unto the counsels of God.”2 Nephi 9:29 The condition is the key. Learning that serves God and mankind is good. Learning that puffs up and turns a person against God is destructive. The difference is not the knowledge — it is the orientation of the heart.

The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints has more college graduates per capita than any other denomination. Our members operate hospitals, universities, research institutions, and businesses across every field of human endeavour. This is not despite the gospel. It is because of it. The same God who created every law in the universe invites His children to understand them — because understanding His creation is another way of knowing Him.

missionary.simple: God commanded His people to seek learning by study and by faith. Paul was highly educated and used that education to serve the gospel. Intelligence and faith are not enemies.
missionary.explain: 'The Church has more college graduates per capita than any other denomination. We believe understanding God's creation is another way of knowing Him. The same God who created every law in the universe invites His children to understand them.'
missionary.why: Faith and knowledge build on each other. The more you understand, the more evidence you find for God — not less.
missionary.invite: Would you like to understand how the restored gospel views the relationship between faith and learning and why both are commanded?

questions.question: 158. Why are your young missionaries called "Elders"?
questions.answer:
Because that is the title the scriptures give to those who hold the Melchizedek Priesthood and are sent forth to preach the gospel. Paul addressed "the elders of the church" in James 5:14 as those who were to anoint the sick and pray over them. Peter called himself a “fellow elder.”1 Peter 5:1 The term refers to the office — not the age.

Mark 6:7 records that Christ sent His apostles out two by two — young men, most of them. John was likely the youngest. None of them were old by modern standards when they began their ministry. Peter was a young fisherman. Yet he held the keys of the kingdom.

The title "Elder" in the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints is the first office of the Melchizedek Priesthood. It carries specific responsibilities — to teach, to testify, to administer ordinances, to preach the gospel to every creature. When a young man is ordained an Elder, he receives a real and valid ordination by the laying on of hands from those who hold that authority in an unbroken line from Christ.

He is not called "Elder" because of his age. He is called "Elder" because of his office. That is the same reason Paul was called an apostle — not because he was old enough, but because he held the office. The title describes the authority, not the biography.

missionary.simple: They are called Elders because that is the scriptural title for those who hold the Melchizedek Priesthood and are sent to preach the gospel — regardless of age.
missionary.explain: 'Peter called himself a fellow elder. Paul ordained Timothy — a young man — as an elder. John was likely a young man when Christ called him as an apostle. The title describes the office, not the biography.'
missionary.why: These young men carry a real and valid ordination to the Melchizedek Priesthood. Their age is irrelevant. Their authority is real.
missionary.invite: Would you be willing to treat the missionaries at your door as what they claim to be — ordained servants of God — and hear their message with that in mind?

questions.question: 159. How can a nineteen-year-old be an Elder?
questions.answer:
By the same method Timothy became one. Paul wrote to Timothy — a young man — “Neglect not the gift that is in thee, which was given thee by prophecy, with the laying on of the hands of the presbytery.”1 Timothy 4:14 The presbytery — the body of elders — ordained Timothy. His youth was no barrier.

Paul told him, “Let no man despise thy youth; but be thou an example of the believers, in word, in conversation, in charity, in spirit, in faith, in purity.”1 Timothy 4:12 Paul acknowledged the youth and addressed it directly — not by saying Timothy should wait until he was older, but by telling him to let his conduct be the proof of his calling.

The question of age confuses the qualification of character with the qualification of authority. The authority of the priesthood is conferred by the laying on of hands from those who hold it. It is not earned by years. David was anointed king as a shepherd boy. Joseph was given his coat and his prophetic dreams as a teenager. John was likely a young man when Christ called him as an apostle.

God does not judge by appearance or by years. He looks on the heart. 1 Samuel 16:7 A faithful nineteen-year-old young man who has been ordained by proper authority is an Elder of the Church of Jesus Christ in every meaningful sense. His age is irrelevant. His authority is real.

missionary.simple: By the same method Timothy became one — through the laying on of hands by those who held the authority. Youth is not a disqualification. God has always called young people to great responsibilities.
missionary.explain: 'Paul told Timothy let no man despise thy youth — not wait until you are older. David was a shepherd boy when Samuel anointed him king. Joseph was seventeen when he received his prophetic calling. God uses who He chooses.'
missionary.why: A 19-year-old elder ordained by proper authority carries the same priesthood Peter carried. Age does not determine the validity of the ordination.
missionary.invite: Would you apply the biblical test — by their fruits — to the young missionaries at your door and judge their message by what it produces in people's lives?

questions.question: 161. Do you practice deliverance or cast out demons?
questions.answer:
Yes. Christ commanded it. “In my name shall they cast out devils.”Mark 16:17 That is one of the signs He said would follow those who believe. The apostles cast out evil spirits. Acts 19:11–12 Paul commanded a spirit of divination to depart. Acts 16:16–18 This is part of the power of the priesthood.

Elders holding the Melchizedek Priesthood have the authority and the responsibility to command evil spirits to depart in the name of Jesus Christ. This happens — quietly, privately, without spectacle, without theatrical performance. We do not make it a public event or a commercial enterprise. We do not sell tickets, build platforms, or record it for broadcast.

The difference between what we do and what many Pentecostal and charismatic groups call "deliverance ministry" is the source of authority. Our authority traces back through Joseph Smith, through Peter, James, and John, to Christ himself. Every elder who commands a spirit to depart does so in the name of Jesus Christ, by the power of the Melchizedek Priesthood.

The magicians before Pharaoh also cast out spirits and performed miracles. Exodus 7:11 That did not validate their authority. Moses performed greater miracles because he held the authority from God. The test is not the miracle. The test is the authority behind it. We have that authority. We exercise it. We simply do not make it a spectacle.

missionary.simple: Yes. Christ commanded that these signs would follow believers — casting out devils, healing the sick, speaking in tongues. We have the authority and we exercise it quietly, without spectacle.
missionary.explain: 'Our elders anoint the sick with oil and lay hands on them in the name of Jesus Christ by the authority of the Melchizedek Priesthood. It happens regularly among members — privately, reverently, without cameras or crowds.'
missionary.why: A church that does not exercise these gifts is missing something the original church had. The restored church has them — because the authority to perform them has been restored.
missionary.invite: Would you like to know someone who has received a healing blessing and ask them what it meant and what happened?

questions.question: 162. Why don't you do crusades or loud street preaching?
questions.answer:
Because that is not the pattern Christ established. He did not rent arenas. He did not stand on street corners screaming at passersby. He went to people where they were — to their homes, to their fishing boats, to their synagogues. He taught. He testified. He invited. He left the choice to the individual.

He sent His apostles out with specific instructions: go to specific cities, knock on specific doors, find those who are "worthy," bless their house, and if they do not receive you, shake the dust from your feet and move on. Matthew 10:11–14 That is a personal, relationship-based, door-to-door ministry — not a mass-event crusade.

Paul reasoned in the synagogues. He spoke in the marketplace. He addressed the Areopagus. In each case he went where people gathered naturally and spoke to them in a way they could receive. He did not use noise and spectacle to generate emotional responses. He taught with “the demonstration of the Spirit and of power.”1 Corinthians 2:4

We have tens of thousands of missionaries in the field at any given time — going door to door, speaking to individuals, visiting homes, teaching families, building relationships. That is the method Christ modelled. Conversions built on a moment of crowd emotion rarely last. Conversions built on personal testimony, personal prayer, and personal commitment to God last forever. We pursue the second kind.

missionary.simple: Because that is the pattern Christ used. He taught in homes, synagogues, and marketplaces. He did not rent arenas. He went to individuals, taught personally, and left the choice to them.
missionary.explain: 'Paul reasoned in synagogues and marketplaces — going where people gathered naturally, speaking in a way they could receive. He did not use noise and emotion to generate responses. He taught with the demonstration of the Spirit and of power.'
missionary.why: The personal, relationship-based missionary approach builds lasting conversion. Emotion fades. A genuine personal witness from the Holy Ghost does not.
missionary.invite: Would you be willing to invite the missionaries to your home for a single conversation — no obligation — and see what the Spirit says to you during it?

questions.question: 186. What is the Church's stance on medical marijuana or vaping?
questions.answer:
The Word of Wisdom — given in 1833 — teaches that the body is a temple of God and that substances harmful to it should be avoided. 1 Corinthians 6:19–20 The principle is clear and consistent: we protect and honour the body God gave us.

Medical marijuana prescribed by a licensed physician for a legitimate medical condition is a matter the Church has left to the conscience of the member and the advice of their doctor. The Church does not prohibit the medical use of substances where the purpose is healing rather than recreation or habit. The same distinction applies to any prescribed medication. Morphine given for post-surgical pain is not a violation of the Word of Wisdom. Marijuana used recreationally to produce a mind-altering state is.

Vaping — the recreational use of nicotine or other inhaled substances — falls under the same principle as tobacco. The Word of Wisdom prohibits tobacco specifically. (Doctrine and Covenants 89:8.) Whether the delivery mechanism is a cigarette or a vaping device is irrelevant to the principle. The substance is harmful. The habit is addictive. The body is a temple. We do not pollute temples.

The broader principle is this: we care for the body because it belongs to God, because we will receive it back in the resurrection, and because physical health enables spiritual service. Everything we put into it should be measured against that purpose.

missionary.simple: Medical marijuana prescribed for a legitimate medical condition is left to the member and their doctor. Recreational use — like recreational tobacco — falls under the Word of Wisdom's principle of protecting the body God gave you.
missionary.explain: 'The principle is simple: the body is a temple. What you put into it should honor that. Medical necessity is different from recreational habit. The distinction is between healing and self-indulgence.'
missionary.why: The Word of Wisdom is not a list of rules — it is a Father's guidance for caring for the sacred instrument God gave you to use in this life.
missionary.invite: Would you like to understand the full scope of the Word of Wisdom and how following it has blessed the health and clarity of millions of members worldwide?

questions.question: 187. Why are the General Authorities almost always older men?
questions.answer:
Because wisdom, experience, and spiritual depth are generally built over time. Paul wrote to Timothy — a young man — that an elder must be “blameless, the husband of one wife, vigilant, sober, of good behaviour, given to hospitality, apt to teach; not given to wine, no striker, not greedy of filthy lucre; but patient.”1 Timothy 3:2–3 These are qualities that are typically developed over decades, not years.

The apostle John was likely young when called. Timothy was young. But the mature leadership of the Church — the First Presidency and Quorum of the Twelve — has generally consisted of men with decades of pastoral experience, professional achievement, and spiritual refinement. That is not ageism. That is wisdom.

Beyond the practical consideration — there is a spiritual one. The calling to apostleship is not an entry-level position. Hebrews 5:4 — "no man taketh this honour unto himself, but he that is called of God, as was Aaron." God calls whom He chooses, when He chooses. The men called to lead this Church have been seasoned by life before God put them in those positions. Their grey hair is not a limitation. It is a credential.

The First Presidency is directed by the Lord. He knows who is needed at each moment in history. The men He has called have led the Church through extraordinary periods of growth and challenge. Their age has been a strength, not a weakness.

missionary.simple: Because wisdom, proven character, and deep spiritual experience are built over decades — not years. Paul's qualifications for a bishop describe a mature person. God calls who He needs, when He needs them.
missionary.explain: 'The apostle John was likely young when called. But the First Presidency and Quorum of the Twelve have generally been men seasoned by life before God placed them in those positions. Grey hair is not a limitation — it is a credential.'
missionary.why: God knows who His church needs at each moment in history. The men He has called have led through extraordinary challenges with wisdom that could only come from a lifetime of faithfulness.
missionary.invite: Would you be willing to listen to a recent message from our current prophet and ask God whether his words carry divine authority?

questions.question: 189. Why does the Church use Stake and Ward instead of Diocese and Parish?
questions.answer:
The terms come from Isaiah. “Enlarge the place of thy tent, and let them stretch forth the curtains of thine habitations: spare not, lengthen thy cords, and strengthen thy stakes.”Isaiah 54:2 The stakes of Zion are the strong, fixed points that hold the tent of God's kingdom secure as it expands across the earth. The term is biblical, prophetic, and deliberate.

A stake corresponds roughly to what a diocese is in other churches — a collection of congregations under one presiding leader. A ward corresponds to a parish. But the terms we use are drawn from the Lord's own prophecy about how His kingdom would grow — staked down, fixed, secure, stretched outward.

The use of our own terminology is also a statement of identity. We are not a reform of existing Christianity. We are the restoration. We use the Lord's own prophetic language for the organisation of His Church rather than the administrative vocabulary inherited by denominations from the Catholic Church. "Diocese" comes from Greek administrative districts. "Stake" comes from Isaiah's vision of Zion. We prefer Isaiah.

missionary.simple: The terms Stake and Ward come directly from Isaiah's prophecy about Zion's tent being enlarged and its stakes strengthened. We use God's own prophetic language for His own organization.
missionary.explain: 'Isaiah told Zion to lengthen her cords and strengthen her stakes. The restored church is that tent of Zion expanding across the earth. The terms are not corporate — they are prophetic fulfillment.'
missionary.why: Every name in the restored church traces back to scripture. We are not building a new institution — we are fulfilling ancient prophecy.
missionary.invite: Would you like to see how the organization of the restored church fulfills specific Old Testament prophecies about the last days?

questions.question: 223. Does the Church still discourage birth control, or is it a private matter?
questions.answer:
The Church's position is that the decision about family planning is a private matter between a husband, a wife, and the Lord. The Church does not issue a specific commandment about birth control.

What the Church does teach is that bearing and raising children is one of the greatest responsibilities and privileges God gives to His children. The family proclamation states that "God's commandment for His children to multiply and replenish the earth remains in force." It also states that husband and wife should rear children "in love and righteousness" — implying that the capacity to provide for and nurture children is a relevant consideration.

Paul wrote, “Let every man be fully persuaded in his own mind.”Romans 14:5 On matters where the Lord has given principles rather than specific rules, the application is between the individual and God. Couples should pray together, counsel with the Lord, and make decisions about their family prayerfully rather than by cultural pressure or convenience.

The general counsel has always been toward openness to children and against making career and financial convenience the primary drivers of family planning decisions. The specific method and timing of family expansion is left to the couple in consultation with God.

missionary.simple: The decision about family planning is between a husband, a wife, and God. The Church teaches that children are a heritage from God and that family decisions deserve prayerful consideration — not cultural convenience.
missionary.explain: 'God's first commandment was to multiply and replenish the earth. That has not been revoked. But the Church also recognizes that real-life family decisions involve health, circumstance, and personal revelation. The principle is openness to children, not a mandated number.'
missionary.why: Children are eternal spirits entrusted to parents. How you approach family planning reflects what you actually believe about the worth of human life.
missionary.invite: Would you like to understand how members approach this deeply personal decision in partnership with God rather than by cultural default?

questions.question: 244. Why do members need to confess to a bishop for sin instead of confessing directly to God?
questions.answer:
We do confess directly to God. Prayer is the first step in repentance — always. The bishop does not substitute for God. He assists in the process that God has ordained for certain categories of sin.

James wrote: “Confess your faults one to another, and pray one for another, that ye may be healed. The effectual fervent prayer of a righteous man availeth much.”James 5:16 Confess your faults to one another. That is the scripture — not our invention. The confessing of serious sins to a priesthood leader is a biblical principle, not a Catholic-style innovation.

Christ gave His apostles specific authority that went beyond merely praying for forgiveness. He said: “Whosoever sins ye remit, they are remitted unto them; and whosoever sins ye retain, they are retained.”John 20:23 That is a judicial authority over the remission of sins — given to men, to be exercised on earth. It is not the power to forgive in God's place. It is the authority to administer the ordinances and processes by which God's forgiveness is made official in terms of Church standing and covenant status.

The bishop is the judge in Israel. (Doctrine and Covenants 107:74.) For serious sins — particularly those that affect a person's temple covenants or their standing in the covenant community — the bishop's involvement is necessary to determine whether Church membership status has been affected and what steps toward full restoration are required.

For everyday sins — weakness, unkind words, failure to pray — a person goes directly to God in prayer. That is repentance in its most common form. The Holy Ghost whispers, the conscience responds, the person kneels and repents, and God forgives. The bishop is not involved.

For serious sins — adultery, abuse, serious moral transgression — the bishop's role is not to replace God's forgiveness but to help restore the person fully to their covenants and to protect the integrity of the covenant community. Just as a doctor does not replace the body's own healing but assists it with tools the body cannot provide alone, the bishop assists the repentance process with authority the individual cannot access alone.

It is also profoundly pastoral. Confession to another human being — particularly a man of God who holds the sealing authority — brings a kind of accountability and healing that private prayer alone sometimes cannot provide. The burden of a secret sin, carried alone, can crush a person. Laying it down before a priesthood holder who has been trained to respond with love and the authority of Christ — that is a gift, not a burden. It is the same gift Christ offered when He said, “Come unto me, all ye that labour and are heavy laden, and I will give you rest.”Matthew 11:28 The bishop is one of the instruments through which that rest is administered.

missionary.simple: For everyday sins, you go directly to God in prayer — repent, change, move forward. For serious sins that affect temple covenants or the covenant community, the bishop helps administer the repentance process.
missionary.explain: 'James commanded confessing faults to one another and praying for each other for healing. Christ gave apostles authority to remit or retain sins. Both direct prayer and pastoral accountability are scriptural. They serve different purposes.'
missionary.why: God designed a complete system of repentance — direct access to Him for daily shortcomings, and priesthood accountability for serious covenantal failures.
missionary.invite: Is there something weighing on you that you have wondered whether God could forgive? Would you like to understand how the atonement and repentance actually work together?

questions.question: 250. What is the Church's stance on gambling?
questions.answer:
The Church teaches clearly that gambling is incompatible with the principles of the gospel. It is discouraged for every member regardless of the form — lotteries, casinos, sports betting, card games played for money, or any activity where financial gain depends on chance at another person's loss.

The reasons are both spiritual and practical. Paul wrote, “The love of money is the root of all evil: which while some coveted after, they have erred from the faith, and pierced themselves through with many sorrows.”1 Timothy 6:10 Gambling is built on the love of money — the desire to gain without working, to profit without contributing, and to do so at the direct expense of someone else who loses what you win.

It also fosters addiction. The same warning the Word of Wisdom gives about substances that harm the body applies in principle to habits that enslave the mind and destroy financial stability. Paul wrote that “all things are lawful for me, but I will not be subject to the power of any.”1 Corinthians 6:12 A person brought under the power of gambling has violated that principle as surely as one brought under the power of alcohol.

Christ said, “Ye cannot serve God and mammon.”Matthew 6:24 Mammon is the love of material wealth pursued as an end in itself. Gambling is one of the purest expressions of that pursuit. We serve God. We work honestly. We trust Him as our provider. And we do not build our financial hope on chance.

missionary.simple: Gambling is incompatible with the gospel because it is built on the love of money — seeking gain at someone else's direct loss, without labor or contribution. Paul identified love of money as the root of all evil.
missionary.explain: 'The principle is simple: we work honestly, give generously, and trust God as our provider. Gambling is the opposite of all three — it seeks to gain without working, at someone else's expense, trusting luck instead of God.'
missionary.why: What you do with money reveals what you actually trust. Gambling and discipleship point in opposite directions.
missionary.invite: Would you like to understand how the gospel shapes how members approach money and financial stewardship in ways that produce peace and security?

questions.question: 251. Are members of the Church allowed to adopt?
questions.answer:
Yes — wholeheartedly. The Church actively supports adoption as a loving and faithful option for families who are unable to have children of their own and for children who need stable, loving homes.

The principle is rooted in the very nature of the gospel. God himself is an adoptive Father in a profound sense — He receives every soul who comes to Him through Christ into His family.
