Paul wrote, “Ye have received the Spirit of adoption, whereby we cry, Abba, Father.”Romans 8:15 We are all adopted into God's family through the covenant. There is no more honoured pattern to follow.

James described pure religion as caring for the fatherless. James 1:27 A child without a family is one of the most vulnerable people in the world. A family willing to open their home and their heart to that child is living the gospel in one of its most practical and beautiful forms.

The Church encourages members who adopt to do so through legal and ethical channels and to raise adopted children with the same love, gospel teaching, and covenant opportunities they would give to any child born in their home. An adopted child can be sealed to their adoptive parents in the temple — becoming part of the eternal family in the fullest sense the gospel provides.

Adoption is not a lesser form of family. In the Church's theology, it is a reflection of how God relates to all of us.

missionary.simple: Absolutely. The Church actively supports adoption. God Himself adopts every soul who comes to Him through Christ into His family. There is no more honored pattern to follow.
missionary.explain: 'Paul said we have received the Spirit of adoption whereby we cry Abba Father. We are all adopted into God's family through the covenant. A family willing to open their home to a child in need is living that principle in its most beautiful form.'
missionary.why: Adoption is not a lesser form of family. In the Church's theology, it is a direct reflection of how God relates to every one of us.
missionary.invite: Would you like to know how an adopted child can be sealed to their adoptive family in the temple — making that relationship eternal?

questions.question: 252. What is the Church's stance on tattooing and body piercing?
questions.answer:
The Church teaches that the body is sacred — a temple of the Holy Ghost, created in the image of God, and the instrument through which we experience mortal life and eventually receive the fulness of the resurrection. Paul wrote, “Know ye not that your body is the temple of the Holy Ghost which is in you... therefore glorify God in your body.”1 Corinthians 6:19–20

On the basis of that principle, the Church discourages tattooing. Permanently marking the body with tattoos is not in keeping with the spirit of caring for the temple God gave us. This is counsel — not a commandment that carries the same weight as the law of chastity or the Word of Wisdom — but it is consistent and clearly stated.

On body piercing, the Church's guidance is that women may pierce their ears modestly — one set of earrings — but discourages other body piercing for both men and women. The principle again is the dignity of the body and the avoidance of practices that reflect worldly trends rather than a gospel-centred approach to physical appearance.

For those who already have tattoos before joining the Church, this is not a barrier to baptism or temple worthiness. The gospel is for everyone. What is asked of members going forward is that they treat the body they have with increasing care and reverence.

The deeper question behind this counsel is one of identity. Are we trying to conform to the world's standards of appearance, or are we trying to reflect our covenant identity as sons and daughters of God? The Church asks us to choose the latter.

missionary.simple: The Church discourages tattoos and multiple piercings because the body is a sacred temple of the Holy Ghost. We treat it with the reverence it deserves.
missionary.explain: 'This is counsel, not a requirement for baptism or temple worthiness. For those who already have tattoos, no judgment — the gospel welcomes everyone. For those going forward, the principle is: honor the temple God gave you.'
missionary.why: How we treat our bodies reflects what we actually believe about their divine origin and eternal destiny.
missionary.invite: Would you like to understand what the gospel teaches about the eternal significance of your physical body and why it deserves such careful treatment?

questions.question: 253. What is the Church's stance on family planning?
questions.answer:
The Lord's first commandment to Adam and Eve was to “multiply and replenish the earth.”Genesis 1:28 The family proclamation reaffirms that this commandment remains in force and that children are a heritage from the Lord. Psalms 127:3 The bearing and raising of children is one of the most sacred responsibilities God gives to His sons and daughters.

At the same time, the Church recognises that decisions about the number and timing of children are deeply personal — to be made prayerfully between husband and wife and the Lord, with consideration for the physical and emotional health of the mother, the family's capacity to provide and nurture, and personal revelation.

The Church does not specify a number of children or forbid any particular method of family planning. It does counsel against allowing purely financial or career-driven motives to become the primary reason for limiting or postponing children. The question to bring to God is not "how few children can we have without feeling guilty?" but "how can we best fulfil the calling He has given us as parents?"

Surgical sterilisation purely for convenience is discouraged. Abortion for family planning purposes is not condoned. Beyond those specific matters, the Church trusts its members to seek the Spirit and make righteous decisions in partnership with God.

The principle is this: children are not inconveniences to be managed. They are eternal spirits entrusted to your care. The family is the most important organisation in eternity — and the decisions surrounding it deserve to be made with the reverence that reflects that reality.

missionary.simple: The Church teaches that multiplying and replenishing the earth remains God's commandment. Family planning decisions are made prayerfully between spouses and God — with openness to children as the guiding principle.
missionary.explain: 'Children are eternal spirits entrusted to parents for their mortal journey. The Church does not specify a number — but it does ask members to approach these decisions with prayer and a heart open to what God directs, not what is most convenient.'
missionary.why: Every child who comes into your family comes as a spirit child of God with an eternal identity. That changes how you make every decision about family.
missionary.invite: Would you like to understand how the restored gospel sees children and family in the context of God's eternal plan for His children?

questions.question: 254. What should I expect from missionaries?
questions.answer:
Missionaries of The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints are young men and women who have set aside a period of their lives — typically two years for men and eighteen months for women — to serve God and share the restored gospel without pay, largely at their own or their family's expense.

When they come to your home or meet you in the community, you can expect them to be respectful and genuine. They will not pressure you. They will teach you in a series of discussions about God the Father, Jesus Christ, the restoration of the gospel, the Book of Mormon, the commandments, and how to prepare for baptism. Each discussion builds on the last.

They will invite you to pray, to read the scriptures — particularly the Book of Mormon — and to attend church. They will answer your questions honestly and will tell you clearly when a question requires more study or when they need to seek guidance from a more experienced leader.

They are not perfect people. They are young servants of God doing their best. Show them the same kindness you would extend to any sincere person who came to your home in good faith to share something they believe is true.

Christ said, “He that receiveth you receiveth me.”Matthew 10:40 The missionaries carry that commission. Receive them with an open heart and see what the Spirit teaches you through them.

missionary.simple: Missionaries will teach you the five lessons about God, Christ, the restoration, the Book of Mormon, and the commandments. They will invite you to pray, read, attend church, and eventually be baptized.
missionary.explain: 'Think of it as a guided conversation with two people who genuinely believe what they are sharing. They are not selling anything. They are sharing something they know changed their lives and believe could change yours.'
missionary.why: Every question you have ever had about God, purpose, and life after death has an answer. The missionaries carry those answers and a genuine desire to share them with you.
missionary.invite: Would you be willing to meet with the missionaries just once — with no obligation — and see for yourself what they actually teach?

questions.question: 255. What is the Church's position on organ donation?
questions.answer:
The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints supports organ and tissue donation as a personal decision made in consultation with family and medical professionals. The General Handbook states that organ donation is "a personal decision" and that members may choose to be donors.

The decision belongs to the individual and the family. The Church neither requires nor forbids it. Many faithful members have chosen to donate organs after death as an act of love — giving life to others. Christ himself taught: "Greater love hath no man than this, that a man lay down his life for his friends."John 15:13 Many members see organ donation as an expression of that love. The body is sacred, but so is the commandment to serve others. This decision is made prayerfully between the individual, their family, and God.

missionary.simple: Organ donation is a personal decision that the Church supports. Members may choose to be donors. It is a private matter between the individual, their family, and God.
missionary.explain: 'The Church does not require organ donation, but it does not forbid it either. Many members see it as an act of love and service — consistent with the Saviour's teaching to lay down one's life for others.'
missionary.why: God values both the sanctity of the body and the commandment to serve. This is a personal decision made with faith and prayer.
missionary.invite: Has your family ever discussed organ donation? It is worth prayerful consideration.

questions.question: 256. What does the Church teach about euthanasia and assisted suicide?
questions.answer:
The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints opposes euthanasia and assisted suicide. The General Handbook states that the Church opposes euthanasia because life is a sacred gift from God, and its end should not be deliberately hastened by human action.

This position rests on the understanding that God is the author of life. Job acknowledged: "The Lord gave, and the Lord hath taken away."Job 1:21 The decision of when a life ends belongs to God alone.

The Church does not require that every possible medical measure be used to extend life when there is no reasonable hope of recovery. Allowing a terminal illness to run its natural course, withdrawing life support when medically appropriate, and using pain management — even if it may indirectly shorten life — are all different from deliberately ending a life. Compassionate care for the dying, including prayer and priesthood blessings, is both encouraged and honoured.

missionary.simple: The Church opposes euthanasia because life is sacred and belongs to God. But allowing natural death with compassion and pain relief is different from deliberately ending a life. The Church honours both positions.
missionary.explain: 'God is the author of life. Deliberately ending a life usurps that authority. But allowing natural death with love and pain relief honours both life and compassion — those are very different things.'
missionary.why: How we treat the dying reflects how deeply we understand the plan of God. Life has divine value from conception to natural death.
missionary.invite: Does the Church's position on the sacredness of life resonate with your own understanding of God's plan?

questions.question: 257. What is the Church's position on suicide?
questions.answer:
The Church teaches that life is sacred and that suicide is contrary to God's plan. Members who struggle with suicidal thoughts are encouraged to seek professional help, speak with family and bishops, and receive priesthood blessings and spiritual care.

At the same time, the General Handbook recognises that many who die by suicide are affected by severe mental illness, depression, or trauma that deeply impairs their judgment. The Church does not take a specific position on the salvation of a person who has died by suicide. God, who knows every heart and every circumstance, will judge each person according to their understanding and agency.

Paul wrote: "The Lord knoweth them that are his."2 Timothy 2:19 Families who have lost someone to suicide are fully supported and welcomed in the Church. Church funerals may be held for members who die by suicide. The Saviour descended below all things — including the deepest suffering — and His atonement reaches there too. D&C 88:6

missionary.simple: Life is sacred and suicide is contrary to God's plan. But God judges each person according to their mental state and circumstances. Families of those lost to suicide are fully supported and welcomed in the Church.
missionary.explain: 'Christ descended below all things. He knows the suffering that drives some to that point. We trust His mercy and His judgment — not our own — in those cases.'
missionary.why: Suicide prevention is both a spiritual and a medical concern. Every life has divine worth, and the Church supports all efforts to help those who are struggling.
missionary.invite: If you or someone you know is struggling, please reach out to a trusted person, a bishop, or a mental health professional.

questions.question: 258. What is the Church's position on in vitro fertilization and surrogacy?
questions.answer:
The Church does not prohibit in vitro fertilization between husband and wife using their own genetic material, but counsels members to consider the spiritual and ethical dimensions carefully. The decision is left to the couple and God.

The General Handbook strongly discourages the use of sperm or eggs from anyone outside the married couple, and discourages surrogacy arrangements because of the complex ethical, emotional, and legal issues they introduce. Children are described in scripture as "an heritage of the Lord."Psalm 127:3 The desire to have children is righteous and good.

Surrogacy — having another woman carry a child — is strongly discouraged. The bearing and rearing of children is a sacred responsibility given to husband and wife together. When medical challenges arise, members are encouraged to seek both professional guidance and personal revelation.

missionary.simple: IVF using a married couple's own genetic material is a personal decision left to the couple and God. The Church strongly discourages using donors outside the couple or surrogacy arrangements.
missionary.explain: 'The Church leaves IVF to the couple and God. But bringing third parties into the conception of a child introduces complex ethical issues the Church counsels against.'
missionary.why: How a family is formed carries moral weight. The Church encourages members to seek God's guidance in these deeply personal decisions.
missionary.invite: Would you like to know more about what the Church teaches on family, children, and the sacred role of parenthood?

questions.question: 259. What is the Church's policy on wearing the garment when a member has a medical condition?
questions.answer:
The August 2024 update to the General Handbook clarified that members with medical conditions or medical devices that prevent them from wearing the temple garment are not spiritually penalised. The handbook states: "When a member cannot wear the garment because of a medical condition or device, his or her religious status is not affected. The Lord focuses on the heart and a willing mind."

In some cases, a member may temporarily stop wearing the garment until the condition improves. In other cases, special-order garments are available through Church distribution services to accommodate specific medical needs. Members are encouraged to wear the garment as fully and as faithfully as their condition allows.

God sees the heart, not the clothing. "The Lord seeth not as man seeth; for man looketh on the outward appearance, but the Lord looketh on the heart."1 Samuel 16:7 A member who cannot wear the garment due to genuine medical necessity remains fully in fellowship with the Church and before God.

missionary.simple: Members who cannot wear the temple garment due to medical conditions are not spiritually penalised. The Lord focuses on the heart and a willing mind. Special-order garments are available for specific medical needs.
missionary.explain: 'A member in a burn unit who cannot wear the garment has not broken their covenant. The covenant is in the heart, and God sees that.'
missionary.why: This policy reflects the Saviour's own approach — ministering to the whole person, not enforcing outward forms when the spirit is willing and the body is limited.
missionary.invite: Would you like to know more about the temple garment and the covenants it represents?

questions.question: 260. What is the Church's position on blood transfusions?
questions.answer:
The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints has no policy against blood transfusions. Members are free to receive blood transfusions and standard medical procedures. The General Handbook states that members are encouraged to receive good medical care from competent doctors.

The Church teaches that God works through both spiritual and medical means. Seeking competent medical help, exercising faith, and receiving priesthood blessings work together for healing, according to the will of the Lord. General Handbook 38.7.8

Members who require blood transfusions face no spiritual restriction. The Church encourages wisdom, faith, and sound medical judgment together.

missionary.simple: The Church has no policy against blood transfusions. Members are free to receive standard medical care. Faith, priesthood blessings, and good medical care all work together.
missionary.explain: God works through doctors and medicine as well as through faith. Seeking good medical care is consistent with the gospel.
missionary.why: God cares about our physical wellbeing. Wisdom in healthcare reflects trust in the gifts He has given to medicine and science.
missionary.invite: Do you have questions about how faith and medicine work together in the Church?

questions.question: 261. What is the Church's position on autopsies?
questions.answer:
The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints permits autopsies when required by law or when the family requests one to determine the cause of death. The General Handbook states that the family of the deceased may request an autopsy and that the body should be treated with dignity throughout.

The decision belongs to the family. The Church imposes no spiritual restriction. Understanding the cause of death or satisfying legal requirements is a legitimate reason to proceed.

Paul wrote: "Know ye not that your body is the temple of the Holy Ghost which is in you?"1 Corinthians 6:19 Even in death, the body is treated with dignity and respect. Church policy trusts families to make this decision wisely and lovingly.

missionary.simple: The Church permits autopsies when required by law or requested by the family. The body is always treated with respect and dignity. The decision belongs to the family.
missionary.explain: The Church does not forbid autopsies. Families make this decision based on their circumstances and need to understand how their loved one died.
missionary.why: Respecting the body and the family are both consistent with Church values. This decision is left entirely to the family.
missionary.invite: Do you have questions about how the Church handles death, burial, and the care of the body?

questions.question: 262. What is the Church's position on surgical sterilization?
questions.answer:
The General Handbook strongly discourages surgical sterilization — including vasectomies and tubal ligation — as a method of birth control. The Church views the ability to bear children as a sacred trust from God, and permanently ending that ability solely for convenience is discouraged.

The handbook makes clear that the decision of how many children to have is extremely intimate and private, left between the couple and the Lord. Members are not condemned for this, but the guidance reflects the Church's deep value of family and life. General Handbook 38.7.12

Medical exceptions are recognised. If sterilization is necessary for genuine health reasons, the decision is left to the individual or couple in consultation with their doctor and personal prayer before God.

missionary.simple: The Church strongly discourages surgical sterilization as birth control because children are a sacred gift. But how many children to have is a private decision between the couple and God. Medical exceptions are fully recognised.
missionary.explain: The Church values family deeply. Permanently ending the ability to have children for convenience is discouraged — but health reasons are a completely different matter.
missionary.why: The Church believes families are central to God's plan and that life is sacred at every stage.
missionary.invite: Would you like to know more about what the Church teaches about family, children, and God's plan for us?

questions.question: 263. What is the Church's policy regarding transgender members?
questions.answer:
The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints teaches that biological sex is an essential characteristic established by Heavenly Father as part of His plan for His children. The August 2024 General Handbook update affirms that gender as used in Church doctrine refers to biological sex at birth. General Handbook 38.6.23

The handbook also states clearly that members who identify as transgender should be treated with sensitivity, kindness, compassion, and Christlike love. The Church does not condone mistreatment of any person. Every individual is a beloved child of God.

Regarding ordinances, those who have surgically or socially transitioned away from their biological sex at birth are not eligible for certain ordinances, including baptism or temple ordinances, without First Presidency approval. Those who wish to return to living in accordance with their biological sex may receive ordinances once appropriate steps are taken. Leaders are given flexibility to minister compassionately to each individual, always pointing toward Christ and the healing His atonement offers.

missionary.simple: Every transgender person is a beloved child of God to be treated with Christlike love and compassion. The Church teaches that biological sex is part of God's plan. Ordinance eligibility is based on biological sex at birth, with compassionate individual consideration.
missionary.explain: God loves every one of His children without exception. The Church's position on biological sex is doctrinal, but its approach to people is always love, compassion, and ministry.
missionary.why: The Church seeks to minister to all people with kindness and Christlike love while remaining true to revealed doctrine.
missionary.invite: Would you like to understand more about how the Church ministers with Christlike love to all of God's children?

questions.question: 264. What is the Church's position on vaccinations?
questions.answer:
The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints actively encourages vaccination. The General Handbook states: "Vaccinations administered by competent medical professionals protect health and preserve life. Members of the Church are encouraged to safeguard themselves, their children, and their communities through vaccination." General Handbook 38.7.13

This is not a new position. The First Presidency has consistently encouraged vaccination since at least 1978. Church President Russell M. Nelson, himself a medical doctor and heart surgeon, publicly received the COVID-19 vaccine in January 2021 alongside other Church leaders, as a demonstration of their confidence in vaccination as a public health measure.

The Church teaches that God works through medicine as well as through faith. Seeking competent medical care — including preventive care like vaccines — is consistent with the gospel principle of caring for the physical body, which Paul described as the temple of the Holy Ghost. 1 Corinthians 6:19

missionary.simple: The Church actively encourages vaccination. The General Handbook says vaccinations protect health and preserve life, and members are encouraged to vaccinate themselves, their children, and their communities.
missionary.explain: The Church has encouraged vaccination since 1978. Church leaders publicly received the COVID-19 vaccine to demonstrate the Church's position. Faith and medicine work together.
missionary.why: Caring for the physical body is a gospel principle. Vaccination is an act of love — protecting yourself, your family, and your community.
missionary.invite: Do you have questions about how faith and modern medicine work together in the Church?

questions.question: 265. What is the Church's official policy on prejudice?
questions.answer:
The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints has a clear, official policy against prejudice of any kind. The General Handbook states: "All people are children of God. All are brothers and sisters who are part of His divine family. Prejudice is not consistent with the revealed word of God. Favor or disfavor with God depends on devotion to Him and His commandments, not on the color of a person's skin or other attributes. The Church calls on all people to abandon attitudes and actions of prejudice toward any group or individual." General Handbook 38.6.14

This policy reflects repeated teachings from the First Presidency on honouring the dignity and divinity of every soul. Paul wrote: "There is neither Jew nor Greek, there is neither bond nor free, there is neither male nor female: for ye are all one in Christ Jesus."Galatians 3:28

The Book of Mormon affirms the same truth: "All are alike unto God."2 Nephi 26:33 Every person, regardless of race, nationality, or background, is of infinite worth to their Heavenly Father.

missionary.simple: The Church has an official policy against prejudice. God values every person equally. Prejudice is inconsistent with the revealed word of God, and the Church calls on all people to abandon it.
missionary.explain: The General Handbook states clearly that favour with God depends on devotion to Him, not skin colour or other attributes. The Church calls on all people to abandon prejudice toward any group or individual.
missionary.why: Every person is a child of God. That truth changes how you see every human being. Prejudice cannot exist alongside a genuine testimony that God loves all His children equally.
missionary.invite: Would you like to know more about how the Church teaches us to love and respect every person as a child of God?

questions.question: 21. If God speaks to prophets, why doesn't He reveal everything clearly to everyone?
questions.answer:
He answered this himself. Christ said to His apostles, “Because it is given unto you to know the mysteries of the kingdom of heaven, but to them it is not given.”Matthew 13:11 God has always distinguished between what He gives to His chosen servants and what He gives to the world at large. That is not favoritism — that is divine order.

Paul was caught up to the third heaven and heard things “not lawful for a man to utter.”2 Corinthians 12:2–4 He received truths not given to everyone. That is the pattern of heaven — light flows through the appointed channel.

If revelation came to everyone equally and individually, there would be no need for prophets. Every man would become his own authority, and every man's revelation would carry equal weight. That is precisely the condition of Christianity today — 800 churches, each claiming the Spirit, none agreeing. It is not unity. It is confusion.

God works through order. He works through covenant. He works through authority. He calls a prophet, speaks through that prophet, and expects His people to receive that message with the same faith they would give to Moses or Elijah or Peter. Those who receive the prophet receive God. Those who reject the prophet reject God. Matthew 10:40 That has not changed.

Nephi described the confirming role of the Holy Ghost: “For he that diligently seeketh shall find; and the mysteries of God shall be unfolded unto them, by the power of the Holy Ghost.”1 Nephi 10:19 And further: “the Holy Ghost… will show unto you all things what ye should do.”2 Nephi 32:5 The prophet speaks. The Holy Ghost confirms. Every sincere seeker can receive that confirmation personally.

missionary.simple: Because God is perfectly organized. He works through order, covenant, and appointed servants. If revelation came to everyone equally and individually, every person's claim would carry equal weight — which produces exactly the chaos Christianity is in.
missionary.explain: 'When Israel refused to receive Moses, they wandered forty years. When they received him, they entered the promised land. Receiving God's appointed servant has always been the test of discipleship — and always produced the blessing.'
missionary.why: God channels revelation through appointed servants so that His children can be united in truth rather than divided by personal interpretation.
missionary.invite: Would you be willing to ask God directly whether He has appointed a living prophet today and whether what that prophet teaches is His word?

questions.question: 29. Why does your church have paid general authorities if the Bible condemns paid ministry?
questions.answer:
Paul said the laborer is worthy of his hire and that those who preach the gospel should live of the gospel. 1 Corinthians 9:14; Luke 10:7 That is a scriptural principle. The question is not whether it is right to support those who serve — the question is how it is done and in what spirit.

General Authorities of the Church receive a modest living allowance — not a salary drawn from tithing. They leave careers, businesses, and professions to serve full time. They do not accumulate wealth through their service. Many take significant financial reductions in their standard of living to serve.

Compare that to the ministers being compared here — who pass collection plates every Sunday, receive salaries, build personal platforms, and in many cases accumulate considerable personal wealth through their ministry. Paul in 1 Corinthians 9:18 said his own reward was to preach the gospel "without charge." He was a tentmaker. Acts 18:3 Peter, James, John, and Andrew were fishermen. Matthew was a tax collector. They worked.

Our full-time missionaries — tens of thousands of them worldwide — pay their own way. They receive no salary. They serve at personal cost. If any church on earth has preserved the spirit of Paul's instruction on unpaid ministry, it is this one.

The Doctrine and Covenants clarifies that those who serve the Lord are sustained modestly as stewards, not enriched as employers: “the laborer in Zion shall labor for Zion; for if they labor for money they shall perish.”2 Nephi 26:31 And the Lord further clarified the principle of equitable support for those who devote their time fully to the work. D&C 70:12–13 The spirit of unpaid, devoted service has been preserved here.

missionary.simple: General Authorities receive a modest living allowance — not a salary — because they leave careers to serve full time. Local bishops, stake presidents, and missionaries serve entirely without pay.
missionary.explain: 'Paul said the laborer is worthy of his hire — the recompense was food and shelter, not a salary. Our most prominent leaders are supported modestly while local leaders across 190 countries serve for free. That is the pattern Paul described.'
missionary.why: The Church operates the largest lay ministry on earth. The system Paul established is alive and functioning here.
missionary.invite: Would you like to compare what Paul taught about financial support for ministers with how the restored church actually operates today?

questions.question: 63. Why do you believe in a prophet who receives revelation but we cannot verify it ourselves?
questions.answer:
The living prophet of the Church stands in that same line of authority. The same question was raised against every prophet in scripture. When Moses came to Pharaoh, Pharaoh could not independently verify that God had spoken to Moses. He had Moses's testimony and the miracles that followed. When Jeremiah prophesied the fall of Jerusalem, the people could not verify it — until it happened. When Paul declared his apostleship, many rejected it and demanded proof.

Deuteronomy 18:21–22 gives the test: if the prophet speaks in the name of the Lord and the thing does not follow, he is answered clearly in scripture. If it follows, he is true. Apply that test to Joseph Smith. He prophesied the Civil War by name — South Carolina, before it began. He prophesied the gathering in the Rocky Mountains when even the US Senate considered that territory worthless. Both came to pass exactly as spoken.

Christ gave a second test: by their fruits. Matthew 7:16 What are the fruits of this prophet? Millions of people living cleaner lives. Families strengthened. Scripture restored. The sick healed. The poor cared for. Ancient promises fulfilled. Those are not the fruits of a fraud.

And beyond all external evidence, we offer the testimony of the Holy Ghost. God promised, through Moroni 10:4–5, that any person who reads the Book of Mormon and asks God in sincerity and faith will receive a personal witness through the Holy Ghost that it is true. The verification is available to everyone who is willing to seek it.

Moroni’s promise stands as the divine verification method: “And when ye shall receive these things, I would exhort you that ye would ask God, the Eternal Father, in the name of Christ, if these things are not true; and if ye shall ask with a sincere heart, with real intent, having faith in Christ, he will manifest the truth of it unto you, by the power of the Holy Ghost.”Moroni 10:4 God Himself is the witness. Every person can receive that witness personally — not secondhand. That is the offer.

The Lord gave further counsel on receiving spiritual confirmation: "Study it out in your mind; then ask me if it be right, and if it is right I will cause that your bosom shall burn within you."D&C 9:8 Study, then ask, then feel. That is God’s own prescribed method for verification.

missionary.simple: Because God provided a way to verify it that does not require taking our word. Read the Book of Mormon, pray sincerely, and ask God directly. He promised to answer. That is the test.
missionary.explain: 'The same test Moses gave us — if a prophet speaks and it comes to pass, he is of God — applies to Joseph Smith. His prophecy of the Civil War by name and state, 29 years before it happened, is one example. The fruits of his ministry are another.'
missionary.why: God made Himself available to answer your question personally. You do not need to depend on our testimony. You can have your own.
missionary.invite: Will you apply Moroni's promise — read and pray — and trust the answer God gives you over every other consideration?

questions.question: 67. Why do Latter-day Saints believe the Garden of Eden was in America?
questions.answer:
The scriptures do not give us a precise map of Eden's location. What they do tell us is that the Garden existed, Adam and Eve lived there, and after the Fall they began to multiply. What has been revealed to us through the prophet Joseph Smith is that Adam and Eve's home after the expulsion from the Garden was in the area we now know as Missouri, in the centre of the North American continent.

This is consistent with what we know about the Book of Mormon record — which describes a people who were descendants of Joseph settling in the Americas. The Lord's dealings with His people have touched both hemispheres. Noah's flood was global. Genesis 7:19 After the flood, peoples scattered to every part of the earth.

The significant point is not the geography. It is the doctrine. Adam was real. Eve was real. The Fall was real. Paul treated it as historical fact in Romans 5:12–19 and 1 Corinthians 15:22. Christ treated it as historical fact. The restoration has given us clarity about things the Bible leaves unexplained — and that is precisely why additional scripture is needed.

The Doctrine and Covenants specifically identifies the land of Zion as being in Missouri: “the land of Missouri… is the land which I have appointed and consecrated for the gathering of the saints.”D&C 57:1–3 This is not speculation. It is specific revelation. The geography of God’s dealings with His children spans both hemispheres — and the restored gospel provides the map.

Lehi affirmed this heritage to his son: "Thou art a remnant of the house of Joseph; wherefore thou shalt be blessed upon the land of thy fathers."2 Nephi 3:2 The Americas are the land of Joseph’s inheritance — exactly as Jacob’s ancient blessing promised.

missionary.simple: Revelation through Joseph Smith identifies the area of Missouri as the centre place of Zion where the New Jerusalem will be built. This is consistent with Old Testament prophecies about Joseph's descendants.
missionary.explain: 'Jacob's blessing to Joseph promised his branches would run over the wall — across the sea — to the utmost bound of the everlasting hills. The great mountain ranges of North America are those hills. Joseph's descendants went there.'
missionary.why: The geography of the restored gospel fulfills specific Old Testament prophecies most Christians have never connected — and it confirms that Joseph Smith was not guessing.
missionary.invite: Would you like to trace Jacob's blessing of Joseph through Genesis and see how the Book of Mormon and the restoration fulfill it precisely?

questions.question: 82. Why do you teach that the devil has power to deceive even good people? Doesn't God protect the faithful?
questions.answer:
God protects the faithful through truth — not by removing their ability to be deceived. If God removed the possibility of deception, He would have to remove free agency. The two cannot be separated.

Paul warned Timothy, “In the latter times some shall depart from the faith, giving heed to seducing spirits, and doctrines of devils.”1 Timothy 4:1 These are people who once had faith. They depart. They are seduced. That is a warning directed at members of the Church, not at pagans who never believed.

Christ himself said, “There shall arise false Christs, and false prophets, and shall shew great signs and wonders; insomuch that, if it were possible, they shall deceive the very elect.”Matthew 24:24 The very elect. The most faithful. If it were possible — meaning the danger is real even for the strongest. The protection against deception is not a divine shield. It is the Holy Ghost, the gift of discernment, the word of God, and obedience to living prophets.

Simon the Sorcerer performed great miracles and deceived an entire city before Philip arrived with the true gospel. Acts 8:9–11 The test is not the sign. The test is the authority — the line of priesthood, the organization matching the primitive church, the fruits of righteousness. Those tests protect the faithful from deception. Abandoning the standard of truth is not freedom. It is vulnerability.

The Book of Mormon names the devil’s most effective tactic: "He cheateth their souls, and leadeth them away carefully down to hell."2 Nephi 28:21 The most dangerous deception is comfortable and quiet. The protection is truth, the Spirit, and obedience to living prophets.

missionary.simple: God protects the faithful through truth and the Holy Ghost — not by removing their ability to be deceived. Without that ability, there is no genuine agency, and without agency, there is no real faith.
missionary.explain: 'Christ warned that false prophets would come with great signs and wonders — strong enough to deceive the very elect if it were possible. The protection against deception is not a divine shield — it is a personal relationship with God through the Holy Ghost.'
missionary.why: God trusts you enough to let you be tested. The test is what builds the character that eternal life requires.
missionary.invite: Would you like to know the tests God gave us to protect against deception — and how to apply them to every truth claim including ours?

questions.question: 84. Why do you believe that Satan is a real being with real power? Isn't evil just an absence of good?
questions.answer:
John wrote, “For this purpose the Son of God was manifested, that he might destroy the works of the devil.”1 John 3:8 The devil has works. Christ was manifested to destroy them. Works require a worker. An absence has no works.

Christ was tempted by Satan personally — three times in the wilderness. Matthew 4:1–11 Satan spoke. Satan offered. Satan departed when commanded. This was not a metaphorical encounter with the absence of good. It was a direct confrontation between two persons — one with a physical body, one without. Both real.

Paul wrote, “For we wrestle not against flesh and blood, but against principalities, against powers, against the rulers of the darkness of this world, against spiritual wickedness in high places.”Ephesians 6:12 Rulers. Principalities. Powers. These are not abstractions. They are organised forces of evil — led by a real being who rebelled against God before the foundation of this world.

Peter warned, “Be sober, be vigilant; because your adversary the devil, as a roaring lion, walketh about, seeking whom he may devour.”1 Peter 5:8 A lion walking and seeking is not an absence. He is present, purposeful, and dangerous. We take him seriously — because the apostles took him seriously.

missionary.simple: Satan is a real being — a spirit son of God the Father who chose rebellion in the pre-mortal life and was cast out. His works are real. Christ came to destroy them.
missionary.explain: 'Christ was tempted by Satan personally in the wilderness. Satan spoke. He offered. He departed when commanded. This was not a metaphor for the absence of good — it was a direct confrontation between two real beings with real wills.'
missionary.why: Understanding that evil has a real source and a real agenda helps you recognize it, resist it, and understand why God's plan of protection through covenants and the Holy Ghost matters.
missionary.invite: Would you like to understand what the restored gospel teaches about the nature and limits of Satan's power — and how God protects those who covenant with Him?

questions.question: 86. Why do you teach that man has a spirit that is separate from the body?
questions.answer:
Isaiah 42:5 separates them plainly: God "giveth breath unto the people upon it, and spirit to them that walk therein." Breath is given to the body. Spirit is a separate gift to the person. These are two different things from the same passage.

Ecclesiastes 12:7 — "Then shall the dust return to the earth as it was: and the spirit shall return unto God who gave it." At death, the body returns to dust. The spirit goes somewhere else. To God. If body and spirit were the same thing, there would be nothing to return to God when the body dies.

James wrote, “The body without the spirit is dead.”James 2:26 Dead means separated. The spirit is the animating life within the body — distinct from it, capable of separating from it, capable of existing without it.

Christ on the cross said, “Father, into thy hands I commend my spirit.”Luke 23:46 And then He died. His spirit went to the Father. His body went to the tomb. Three days later both were reunited in the resurrection. If body and spirit were one thing, that separation and reunion would make no sense.

missionary.simple: The spirit is distinct from the body. At death the body returns to dust but the spirit returns to God who gave it. They are two separate things that together make a complete, eternal soul.
missionary.explain: 'James said the body without the spirit is dead — which means the spirit is the animating life within the body, distinct from it, capable of separating from it. Christ on the cross said Father, into thy hands I commend my spirit — and died.'
missionary.why: You are not just a body with a brief consciousness. You are an eternal spirit temporarily housed in a mortal body — and death is not the end of you.
missionary.invite: Would you like to understand what the restored gospel teaches about the spirit world and what happens to the real you immediately after death?

questions.question: 106. Why do you believe that the Sermon on the Mount applies literally today? Wasn't it just for the disciples?
questions.answer:
Christ said, “Think not that I am come to destroy the law, or the prophets: I am not come to destroy, but to fulfil.”Matthew 5:17 He then proceeded to raise every standard of the law higher — not lower it, not limit it to one audience. "Ye have heard that it was said by them of old time... but I say unto you." He was speaking to all who would follow Him — for all time.

Paul wrote, “All scripture is given by inspiration of God, and is profitable for doctrine, for reproof, for correction, for instruction in righteousness.”2 Timothy 3:16 All scripture. Not just the parts addressed to apostles. Not just the parts addressed to the first century. All.

The Sermon on the Mount is the constitution of the Kingdom of God. It describes what citizens of that kingdom look like — from the inside out. Blessed are the pure in heart. Blessed are the peacemakers. Let your light shine. Love your enemies. These are not optional refinements for advanced disciples. They are the foundational character of every person who claims to follow Christ.

If the Sermon on the Mount was only for the twelve disciples present on that hillside, then every verse in every gospel is limited to its immediate audience — and no one today has any scripture that applies to them. That line of reasoning collapses under its own weight.

missionary.simple: Christ said He came not to abolish the law but to fulfil it — and then raised every standard higher than Moses. The Sermon on the Mount applies to every person who claims to follow Him, in every generation.
missionary.explain: 'Paul said all scripture is profitable for doctrine, for reproof, for correction. All means all — including the Sermon on the Mount. Christ said these things to shape the character of His disciples forever, not just for one afternoon.'
missionary.why: The Sermon on the Mount is the constitution of the kingdom of God — the description of what a person who has been changed by Christ actually looks like from the inside out.
missionary.invite: Would you be willing to read the Sermon on the Mount — or its companion in 3 Nephi 12 — as a personal invitation from Christ to you?

questions.question: 118. Why does your church teach that the gospel was preached to Adam? Isn't the gospel a New Testament concept?
questions.answer:
Paul wrote, “The scripture, foreseeing that God would justify the heathen through faith, preached before the gospel unto Abraham.”Galatians 3:8 The gospel was preached to Abraham — a man who lived two thousand years before Christ. If it was preached to Abraham, why not to Adam who lived before Abraham?

Hebrews 4:2 — "Unto us was the gospel preached, as well as unto them." Them — the people of the Old Testament. The gospel is not a New Testament invention. It is the eternal plan of redemption — the same in every dispensation, taught by every prophet from the beginning.

Moses testified of Christ. John 5:46 Isaiah foresaw His birth, His ministry, His suffering, and His resurrection in extraordinary detail. (Isaiah 53.) The animal sacrifices of the Mosaic law pointed to the sacrifice of the Lamb of God — they were the gospel in symbol, preached through ordinance to every generation of Israel.

Abraham saw Christ's day. “Abraham rejoiced to see my day: and he saw it, and was glad.”John 8:56 If Abraham saw Christ's day, he had been taught about it. By whom? By God, who preached the gospel to him. The gospel has always been the same gospel — the atonement of Christ, faith, repentance, baptism, the Holy Ghost. It did not start with Matthew 1. It started with God.

missionary.simple: The gospel has always been the same — faith in Christ, repentance, baptism, the Holy Ghost. Paul said the gospel was preached to Abraham before the law. The restoration of the gospel was always God's plan from the beginning.
missionary.explain: 'God told Adam and Eve about the atonement of Christ immediately after the fall. Abraham saw Christ's day and rejoiced. Every prophet from Adam to John the Baptist pointed to Christ — the same gospel, the same ordinances, the same plan.'
missionary.why: The restored gospel is not a new invention — it is the original plan of God, unchanged from Adam to today.
missionary.invite: Would you like to trace the gospel of Jesus Christ from Adam through every dispensation and see that God has always offered the same path home?

questions.question: 122. Why do you believe that Adam and Michael are the same being?
questions.answer:
Daniel 10:13 refers to Michael as "one of the chief princes." Daniel 10:21 calls him "Michael your prince." Revelation 12:7–9 records that Michael led the armies of heaven against Satan in the pre-mortal war. He is the chief prince of heaven's armies.

Doctrine and Covenants 107:54 identifies Michael as Adam, the first man. This knowledge comes through the restoration of all things — exactly the kind of truth that was lost in the apostasy and could only be recovered through living prophets.

The logic is consistent with what the Bible tells us about both. Adam was the first and greatest of God's mortal children. He led Israel in the pre-mortal war as Michael. He came to earth as Adam to begin the human family. He will stand as the Ancient of Days in the last days — presiding over the righteous dead before Christ's return, exactly as Daniel describes. Daniel 7:9–10, 13–14

This is not our idea inserted into scripture. It is the clarification of connections that the Bible itself establishes — a great heavenly prince named Michael, a great earthly father named Adam — who the restoration reveals to be one and the same glorious being, faithful to God from the pre-mortal life through mortality and beyond.

missionary.simple: Doctrine and Covenants 107:54 identifies Michael as Adam — the first man, the father of the human race, who also led heaven's armies as Michael the archangel before this world was created.
missionary.explain: 'The same being who led the faithful in the pre-mortal war against Satan became the first mortal man on this earth and will stand as the Ancient of Days before Christ's return. One glorious being with a role in every major act of God's plan.'
missionary.why: Understanding Adam as Michael reveals the coherence of God's plan across eternity — the same key figures serving His purposes from before this world to the end of it.
missionary.invite: Would you like to understand the plan of salvation from before this world began and how the major figures in it connect across time and eternity?

questions.question: 134. Why do you teach that God is just if He punishes the children of Israel for the sins of their ancestors?
questions.answer:
He does not. Ezekiel 18:2–4 makes it explicit: "The soul that sinneth, it shall die. The son shall not bear the iniquity of the father, neither shall the father bear the iniquity of the son: the righteousness of the righteous shall be upon him, and the wickedness of the wicked shall be upon him."

The Lord was correcting a false proverb — "The fathers have eaten sour grapes, and the children's teeth are set on edge." He rejected that proverb. He will judge each person for their own choices. No person is punished for their ancestors' sins.

What is inherited is consequence — not condemnation. Children born into poverty because of their parents' choices are not being punished by God. They are living in the natural consequences of a fallen world. There is a difference between the justice of God and the consequences of human agency that ripple through generations.

The Lord describes Himself as visiting the iniquity of the fathers upon the children “unto the third and fourth generation of them that hate me; and shewing mercy unto thousands of them that love me, and keep my commandments.”Exodus 20:5–6 The context is idolatry — the generational culture of idolatry passed from father to child. Children who are raised in that culture perpetuate it by their own choices. God judges the choice, not the lineage.

missionary.simple: He does not. Ezekiel declared plainly that the son shall not bear the iniquity of the father. Every person is judged for their own choices — not their ancestors'. What is inherited is consequence, not condemnation.
missionary.explain: 'Children born into poverty because of their parents' choices are experiencing the natural consequences of a fallen world — not divine punishment. God distinguishes perfectly between consequence and condemnation. His judgment is individual and perfectly fair.'
missionary.why: God's justice is personal and perfect. Every soul stands before Him alone, accountable only for what they chose with the light they were given.
missionary.invite: Would you like to understand how God's perfect justice and perfect mercy work together in the restored gospel's plan of salvation?

questions.question: 154. Why do you believe that the earth itself is a living creation that feels the effects of righteousness and sin?
questions.answer:
Isaiah wrote, “The earth also is defiled under the inhabitants thereof; because they have transgressed the laws, changed the ordinance, broken the everlasting covenant. Therefore hath the curse devoured the earth.”Isaiah 24:5–6 The earth is defiled. It is cursed. Not as a dead rock indifferent to what happens on its surface — but as a created sphere that responds to the moral condition of those who inhabit it.

Paul wrote that “the whole creation groaneth and travaileth in pain together until now. And not only they, but ourselves also... waiting for the adoption, to wit, the redemption of our body.”Romans 8:22–23 The creation groans. The earth groans along with humanity — waiting for redemption. It is not passive. It is participating in the fallen condition and waiting for the restoration.

Moses was shown that the earth "shall be renewed and receive its paradisiacal glory" at the coming of the millennium. (Articles of Faith 10.) The earth will be redeemed just as its inhabitants are redeemed — transformed from a fallen state to a celestial one.

John saw “a new heaven and a new earth.”Revelation 21:1 Not a destroyed one. A renewed one. The same earth, glorified. Just as the body of Christ was the same body glorified in the resurrection — recognisable but transformed — the earth will be the same earth glorified. The creation that fell with Adam will be exalted with His redeemed children.

missionary.simple: Isaiah, Paul, and John all taught that the earth responds to the moral condition of those who live on it. The earth fell with Adam and will be renewed and glorified in the millennium and celestial era.
missionary.explain: 'Paul said the whole creation groans and travails, waiting for the manifestation of the sons of God. The earth is not indifferent to what happens on its surface. When the sons of God are glorified, the earth is glorified with them.'
missionary.why: This earth — this specific creation — has an eternal future. You will live on a glorified earth with God and your family, not float in clouds.
missionary.invite: Would you like to understand the full eternal destiny of this earth and what that means for the physical life and resurrection you have been promised?

questions.question: 157. Do you believe in the same Jesus Christ as other Christians?
questions.answer:
We believe in Jesus Christ as He described Himself — not as councils of uninspired men defined Him centuries after His death.

We believe He was born of the Virgin Mary, conceived by the power of God the Father. Luke 1:35 We believe He performed miracles, taught the gospel, suffered in Gethsemane, was crucified on Calvary, and rose from the dead on the third day with a glorified body of flesh and bone. Luke 24:39 We believe He ascended to heaven with that body, sits at the right hand of the Father, and will come again in glory. We believe He is the Saviour of the world, the only name given under heaven whereby man can be saved. Acts 4:12

Where we differ from most of Christianity is in what councils invented about Him after the apostasy. The Nicene and Athenasian Creeds — composed by uninspired men at the command of a pagan emperor — describe a Christ who is "incomprehensible," co-substantial with the Father in a way that defies explanation, and merged into a Trinity that no scripture ever defines in those terms.

The Christ of the New Testament has a Father He prays to, a Father He is separate from, a Father whose voice came from heaven at His baptism while He stood in the water. Matthew 3:16–17 That Christ — distinct from the Father, obedient to the Father, one with the Father in purpose and love — is the Christ we worship.

The answer depends on which understanding of Jesus is being asked about. The Jesus of scripture — yes, absolutely. We follow the Christ of the New Testament — the one who prayed, who wept, who rose with a body of flesh and bone, and who sits at the right hand of the Father. That is the Christ we worship and testify of.

🕮 Scripture Evidence: God Has Been Seen

  • Moses — Exodus 33:11: The LORD spoke to Moses face to face, as a man speaks to his friend.
  • Moses — Exodus 33:18-23: Moses is permitted to see God's back, confirming a directional, tangible presence.
  • Moses & the elders — Exodus 24:9-11: Moses, Aaron, and seventy elders saw the God of Israel and ate and drank in His presence.
  • Jacob — Genesis 32:30: “I have seen God face to face, and yet my life is preserved.”
  • Isaiah — Isaiah 6:1-5: Isaiah beheld the Lord sitting upon a throne, high and lifted up.
  • Ezekiel — Ezekiel 1:26-28: Ezekiel describes the appearance of the likeness of the glory of the LORD as a human form.
  • Abraham — Genesis 18:1-2: The LORD appeared to Abraham in visible, tangible form at the oaks of Mamre.
  • Job — Job 42:5: “My eyes have seen you.”

missionary.simple: We believe in Jesus Christ as He described Himself in the New Testament — not as uninspired councils redefined Him centuries later. His resurrection was literal, His body was real, and He is alive today.
missionary.explain: 'The Christ of the New Testament has a Father He prays to, a Father He is separate from, a voice that spoke from heaven at His baptism while He stood in the water. That Christ — exactly as described — is the Christ we worship.'
missionary.why: Knowing the true Christ — personal, physical, living, knowable — changes how you pray, how you live, and what you hope for.
missionary.invite: Would you like to compare the Christ of the New Testament with the Christ of the Nicene Creed and ask God which one He actually is?

questions.question: 199. Who is Jesus Christ? If He is the Son of God, does it mean God gave birth to Him during His heavenly life before becoming God and being sealed to Him?
questions.answer:
This question touches the deepest doctrine of the plan of salvation. Let us answer it plainly with what has been revealed.

Jesus Christ is the Firstborn of the Father in the spirit. Colossians 1:15 Before this world was created, all spirit children of God the Father were born as spirits in the pre-mortal life. Christ was the first — the greatest, the mightiest, the most faithful of all. He was not created from nothing. He was born of the Father, as every spirit child is born of the Father, in the eternal world before mortality.

In that pre-mortal world, He was chosen and foreordained to be the Saviour. 1 Peter 1:18–20 He created this earth under the direction of the Father. Colossians 1:16 He was the God of the Old Testament — Jehovah — the God of Israel, the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob.

In the fulness of time, He was born into mortality. Mary was His mother. God the Father was His literal Father — providing the divine element of His nature. That is what Luke 1:35 means when it says "the power of the Highest shall overshadow thee." He came to earth as both fully mortal and fully divine — carrying the capacity to suffer, to die, and to overcome both.

He was not "sealed" to the Father after becoming God. He is the Son eternally — eternally begotten, eternally the Firstborn, eternally united with the Father in purpose and love. The Father and the Son have always been Father and Son. That relationship is eternal and fundamental to the nature of both. He is the Son. The Father is the Father. And through their love for us, They provided the plan, the atonement, and the resurrection — the gifts that make it possible for every child of God to return home.

missionary.simple: Jesus Christ is the literal Son of God — the Firstborn of the Father in the spirit, born of Mary in the flesh. He is the Savior, the Redeemer, and through His atonement the path back to God is open to every person.
missionary.explain: 'In the pre-mortal life He was chosen before the world was created. He created this earth under the Father's direction. He came to earth as both fully mortal and fully divine — the only being who could do what He did.'
missionary.why: Jesus Christ is not just a great teacher or a good example. He is the literal Son of God, and His atonement is the only path to eternal life.
missionary.invite: Would you like to know what the restored gospel teaches about who Jesus Christ actually is and what His atonement means for your life right now?

questions.question: 215. How does the Church view the Theory of Everything in physics versus the concept of miracles?
questions.answer:
God is the author of every law in the universe. He did not create a universe and then stand outside it, occasionally poking holes in its laws to perform miracles. He operates through laws — some of which we understand, most of which we do not.

Joseph Smith taught that “there is no such thing as immaterial matter. All spirit is matter, but it is more fine or pure, and can only be discerned by purer eyes.”Doctrine and Covenants 131:7 What we call spiritual reality and what we call physical reality are both part of a single, unified order — governed by laws that are higher and more comprehensive than anything current physics has described.

What we call miracles are events that operate according to laws we have not yet discovered. When Christ walked on water, He did not violate physics. He operated by a higher physics. When the Red Sea parted, it was not chaos. It was divine order operating at a level beyond our current instruments. Einstein expanded our understanding of space and time in ways that Newtonian physics could not accommodate. God's laws expand the same framework infinitely beyond where Einstein stopped.

A Theory of Everything — if physics ever achieves one — will still be an approximation of God's laws, not a description of all of them. The more science discovers, the larger the unknown becomes. That is not a threat to faith. It is an invitation to reverence.

missionary.simple: God is the author of every law in the universe. What we call miracles operate by higher laws we have not yet discovered. A Theory of Everything would still be an approximation of God's laws — not a description of all of them.
missionary.explain: 'When Christ walked on water, He did not violate physics — He operated by a higher physics. Einstein expanded Newton's framework infinitely. God's laws expand Einstein's framework infinitely again. The more science discovers, the larger the unknown becomes.'
missionary.why: Science and faith are not enemies — they are two different paths toward the same ultimate truth. Both point to an intelligence behind the universe.
missionary.invite: Would you like to explore how the restored gospel's view of truth — that all truth is one — connects what science discovers with what God has revealed?

questions.question: 1. Why don't members of The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints use the title Reverend for their clergy?
questions.answer:
The title Reverend comes from the Latin reverendus, meaning worthy of reverence. It entered common Christian use through medieval Catholic tradition, which created a professional clergy separate from ordinary members. But the Bible uses the word reverend only once — in Psalm 111:9 — and there it refers exclusively to God: “holy and reverend is his name.” Applying it to a human being has no scriptural basis.

Christ warned directly against religious titles: “But be not ye called Rabbi: for one is your Master, even Christ; and all ye are brethren. And call no man your father upon the earth: for one is your Father, which is in heaven. Neither be ye called masters: for one is your Master, even Christ.”Matthew 23:8–10

The early church had no professional clergy. Every qualified male member could hold the priesthood and serve. The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints restores that pattern. Leaders are called from the congregation, serve without pay at the local level, and return to ordinary life when their service ends. The title Elder used by missionaries and the title President used by leaders come directly from New Testament usage. 1 Timothy 5:17; Acts 14:23

missionary.simple: The Bible uses the word reverend only once — referring to God alone. Christ specifically forbade religious titles. Our leaders serve without pay, called from the congregation, following the New Testament pattern.
missionary.explain: 'Christ said call no man your father, for one is your Father in heaven. That is not a suggestion. It is a direct command — and it eliminates the entire system of professional clergy titles at a stroke.'
missionary.why: The absence of paid clergy is evidence of the restored church, not a lack of organization. Every member serves because every member has a priesthood role.
missionary.invite: Would you like to understand how the priesthood organization of the restored church matches the pattern Christ set in the New Testament?

questions.question: 2. How did sprinkling and pouring come to replace immersion in Christian baptism?
questions.answer:
The New Testament presents baptism clearly as full immersion. The Greek word baptizo means to dip, plunge, or immerse — never to sprinkle. John baptized in the River Jordan “because there was much water there.”John 3:23 Jesus himself went “up straightway out of the water” after his baptism. Matthew 3:16 Paul described it as a burial and resurrection: “Therefore we are buried with him by baptism into death: that like as Christ was raised up from the dead...even so we also should walk in newness of life.”Romans 6:4

Sprinkling — known as aspersion — was first introduced in the Didache, an early Christian document around AD 100, as an emergency allowance only when enough water was not available. The Council of Ravenna in AD 1311 officially declared sprinkling equally valid as immersion. That was a human decision with no scriptural basis. Pouring followed a similar path.

Many traditions then adopted infant baptism, which further shifted the practice. The Latter-day Saint position simply returns to what Christ demonstrated and Paul described — full immersion by one holding priesthood authority.

missionary.simple: Baptizo in Greek means to immerse, never to sprinkle. Christ was immersed. Paul called it a burial. Sprinkling was an emergency allowance in AD 100 that councils later declared equally valid — without any scriptural basis.
missionary.explain: 'If baptism is a burial with Christ and a resurrection with Christ, as Paul says, then a few drops of water on the forehead does not symbolize burial. Immersion does. The symbolism only works one way.'
missionary.why: The mode of baptism matters because the ordinance is meant to mirror the death, burial, and resurrection of Jesus Christ. The symbol must match the reality.
missionary.invite: Would you be willing to be baptized by immersion, as Christ was, by someone holding the same priesthood authority John the Baptist held?

questions.question: 3. How were the twelve apostles of Jesus Christ killed?
questions.answer:
Early church history and Christian writings give us accounts of most of the apostles. Peter was crucified upside down in Rome under Emperor Nero around AD 64–68, reportedly requesting this because he felt unworthy to die as his Lord had. John 21:18–19 Andrew was crucified on an X-shaped cross in Patras, Greece. James, son of Zebedee, was beheaded by Herod Agrippa I — the only apostle whose death is directly recorded in scripture. Acts 12:2

John is the only apostle widely believed to have died of old age, reportedly surviving being boiled in oil. Philip was crucified or stoned in Hierapolis. Bartholomew was skinned alive and then crucified in Armenia. Matthew was killed by sword in Ethiopia or Persia. Thomas was speared to death in India around AD 72. Simon the Zealot was sawn in half or crucified in Persia. Paul, though not one of the original twelve, was beheaded in Rome under Nero.

Their willingness to die rather than deny the resurrection is one of the strongest evidences for that event. People will die for what they believe is true. These men died for what they had personally seen. Matthew 10:22; 2 Timothy 4:6–7

missionary.simple: Almost every apostle died rather than deny the resurrection. Men will die for what they believe. These men died for what they had personally seen and touched. That distinction is the strongest evidence for the resurrection.
missionary.explain: 'You can die for a belief. But the apostles were not dying for a belief — they were dying for a personal eyewitness experience. No conspiracy of frightened men who stole the body would hold together under that kind of pressure.'
missionary.why: The deaths of the apostles are not just history. They are evidence. Twelve men, scattered across the ancient world, all chose death over denial.
missionary.invite: Would you be willing to ask God directly whether the resurrection of Jesus Christ is real, and listen for His answer?

questions.question: 4. How did the worship of Mary enter Christianity, and what does scripture actually teach about her?
questions.answer:
Mary holds a genuinely honoured place in scripture. The angel called her “blessed among women.”Luke 1:28 Elizabeth, filled with the Holy Ghost, confirmed this. Luke 1:42 Mary’s own words say “all generations shall call me blessed.”Luke 1:48 Honouring her memory is appropriate.

What is not found in scripture is prayer to Mary, intercession through Mary, or doctrines such as the Immaculate Conception, declared by Pope Pius IX in 1854, or the Assumption of Mary, defined by Pope Pius XII in 1950. These developed gradually as Christianity absorbed converts from pagan traditions across Egypt, Greece, and Rome — where goddess worship was central — and devotion to Mary began filling that theological space. The Council of Ephesus in AD 431 declared Mary Theotokos — God-bearer — raising her status significantly.

Christ himself, when a woman praised his mother in the crowd, redirected the honour: “Yea rather, blessed are they that hear the word of God and keep it.”Luke 11:27–28 Scripture names one mediator: “For there is one God, and one mediator between God and men, the man Christ Jesus.”1 Timothy 2:5

missionary.simple: Mary is genuinely honoured in scripture as blessed among women. But prayer to Mary, intercession through Mary, and Marian doctrines like the Immaculate Conception have no New Testament foundation — they developed from pagan influence and church councils.
missionary.explain: 'When a woman in the crowd praised his mother, Christ redirected it: blessed are those who hear the word of God and keep it. He honoured her. He did not elevate her to mediator. Scripture names one mediator — Christ himself.'
missionary.why: Understanding the historical origin of Marian doctrine is not an attack on Mary. It is an honest acknowledgment that scripture and Catholic tradition diverged on this point.
missionary.invite: Would you like to compare what the New Testament actually says about Mary with what centuries of tradition added, and ask God what He thinks?

questions.question: 5. What is the origin of Purgatory, and what does the Bible actually say about the state of the dead?
questions.answer:
Purgatory is the Catholic teaching that souls of the faithful dead undergo purification before entering heaven. It has no clear foundation in the Old or New Testament. Its origins lie partly in 2 Maccabees 12:46, which speaks of praying for the dead — but that book belongs to the Catholic Apocrypha, not the Protestant or Latter-day Saint canon.

Early writers like Tertullian and Origen wrote about prayers for the dead and intermediate states, but no formal doctrine existed until the Second Council of Lyon in AD 1274 and the Council of Trent, 1545–1563, which fully defined it. The financial abuse of this doctrine — the selling of indulgences to reduce time in purgatory — was one of Martin Luther’s central complaints in his 95 Theses in 1517, which sparked the Protestant Reformation.

Scripture teaches that after death comes judgment Hebrews 9:27 and that the blood of Christ cleanses from all sin. 1 John 1:7 The Latter-day Saint understanding of the spirit world — where the gospel is preached to those who did not receive it in life 1 Peter 3:18–20; 4:6 — addresses the real concern about the dead but is grounded directly in scripture, not medieval theology.

missionary.simple: Purgatory was formally defined by councils in AD 1274 and 1545. It has no direct biblical basis. The selling of indulgences to shorten time there was what triggered the Reformation. The spirit world as taught in scripture is a very different — and more merciful — reality.
missionary.explain: 'Peter says Christ went and preached to spirits in prison so the dead could be judged. That is not purgatory — it is the gospel being preached after death, exactly as the restored gospel teaches. Scripture had this right long before any council.'
missionary.why: The Latter-day Saint understanding of the spirit world answers the deepest concern people have about those who died without hearing the gospel — with scripture, not tradition.
missionary.invite: Would you like to understand what the restored gospel teaches happens after death, and how it compares to what the Bible actually says?

questions.question: 6. Why did Christianity split into so many different churches?
questions.answer:
The New Testament church was one body. Ephesians 4:4–5 The divisions that followed are exactly what Paul and Christ warned against. 1 Corinthians 1:10–13; John 17:21

The first major split was the Great Schism of AD 1054, when the Western Roman Catholic and Eastern Orthodox churches formally divided over the authority of the Pope, the filioque controversy about the Holy Ghost, and political tensions between Rome and Constantinople.

The Protestant Reformation began in 1517 when Martin Luther, a German monk, posted his 95 Theses challenging the sale of indulgences. John Calvin, Ulrich Zwingli, and others followed with their own theologies. Without a living prophet to settle disputes, each reformer relied on his own reading of scripture. Within decades, hundreds of Protestant denominations existed.

The Latter-day Saint understanding is that the reformers were inspired men doing vital work — preparing the world for a full restoration. But reformation alone could not restore what had been lost. That required new revelation and new authority, not just better interpretation of old texts. Amos 3:7

missionary.simple: The reformers were inspired men who broke from corruption. But without a living prophet to settle doctrine by revelation, every individual’s reading of scripture became its own denomination. Only a restoration — not a reformation — could bring back what was lost.
missionary.explain: 'Christ prayed that they all may be one. He did not pray for five hundred denominations. The divisions exist because men without prophetic authority settled doctrine by debate instead of revelation.'
missionary.why: The existence of thousands of Christian denominations is not evidence that Christianity is false. It is evidence that the apostasy prophesied by Paul actually happened.
missionary.invite: Would you like to understand how the restored church answers the question that the reformers raised but could not fully resolve?

questions.question: 7. What is the origin of the Pope, and does this office have a biblical foundation?
questions.answer:
The word Pope comes from the Latin papa, meaning father — itself a violation of Matthew 23:9: “call no man your father upon the earth.” The bishop of Rome gradually claimed supreme authority over all Christianity, arguing from Matthew 16:18: “Thou art Peter, and upon this rock I will build my church.” Catholics interpret this as making Peter the first pope.

But Peter himself never claimed such supremacy. Paul publicly rebuked Peter to his face when he was wrong. Galatians 2:11 The early church was governed by councils, not by a single bishop. The claim to papal supremacy developed over centuries, particularly under Pope Leo I in 440–461 and Gregory I in 590–604.

The doctrine of papal infallibility — that the pope cannot err when speaking formally on matters of faith and morals — was not defined until the First Vatican Council in 1870. No New Testament text supports this. The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints teaches that Christ stands at the head of his church, directing it through living prophets and apostles by revelation — not through inherited office. Ephesians 2:20

missionary.simple: Papal infallibility was not declared until 1870 — nearly 1,800 years after Peter. The title ‘father’ for a religious leader directly contradicts Matthew 23:9. The New Testament shows a church governed by apostles and prophets, not a single inherited office.
missionary.explain: 'Paul rebuked Peter to his face and said so publicly. That is not how supreme authority works. The early church was led by a body of apostles, not a single successor. The papal office is a post-apostolic development.'
missionary.why: Understanding where the papacy came from historically does not diminish sincere Catholics. It does clarify what the New Testament actually describes as church governance.
missionary.invite: Would you like to compare the governance of the New Testament church with both the Catholic and restored models, and ask God which pattern He established?

questions.question: 8. How did infant baptism begin, and is it supported by scripture?
questions.answer:
