Infant baptism has no clear scriptural command. Every instance of baptism in the New Testament involves adults who heard the gospel, believed, and chose to be baptized. Acts 2:38; Acts 8:12 The Ethiopian eunuch is a clear example — understanding first, then faith, then baptism. Acts 8:35–38

The practice likely began in the 2nd and 3rd centuries out of concern for infants who died before baptism. Tertullian mentioned it around AD 200 and actually argued against it at the time. By Augustine around AD 400, it had become widespread partly because of his doctrine of original sin — that all people inherit Adam’s guilt and therefore infants needed washing without delay. The Council of Carthage in AD 418 formally affirmed infant baptism.

Christ himself said, “Suffer little children, and forbid them not, to come unto me: for of such is the kingdom of heaven.”Matthew 19:14 Children do not need baptism. They are already in a state of innocence. The Book of Mormon states plainly: “their little children need no repentance, neither baptism...it is solemn mockery before God, that ye should baptize little children.” Children become accountable before God at age eight.

missionary.simple: Every New Testament baptism involves an adult who heard, believed, and chose. Infant baptism arose from Augustine’s doctrine of inherited guilt and was formally adopted in AD 418. Christ said children are already of the kingdom of heaven — they do not need washing.
missionary.explain: 'Baptism requires faith, repentance, and a covenant — none of which an infant can form. The Lord told us that little children are alive in Christ. Baptizing them implies they need saving from something they have not done.'
missionary.why: The doctrine of child accountability at age eight is both merciful and scripturally grounded. Children are safe. The covenant waits until they can choose it.
missionary.invite: Would you like to understand what the restored gospel teaches about the innocence of children and when baptism becomes meaningful and necessary?

questions.question: 9. What is the Trinity doctrine, and how does it differ from the Latter-day Saint view of the Godhead?
questions.answer:
The Trinity doctrine teaches that God is one being in three persons — Father, Son, and Holy Ghost — coequal, coeternal, and of the same substance. This exact formulation does not appear in scripture. The Bible clearly shows three distinct beings: a Father who sends John 3:16, a Son who prays to the Father John 17:1–3, and a Holy Ghost who comes after the Son departs. John 16:7

The Trinity as a formal doctrine was largely shaped at the Council of Nicaea in AD 325, convened by Emperor Constantine to settle whether Christ was eternal or created. The resulting Nicene Creed described Christ as homoousios — of one substance with the Father — a Greek philosophical term not found in scripture.

Stephen’s vision shows two separate beings clearly: “I see the heavens opened, and the Son of man standing on the right hand of God.”Acts 7:55–56 Joseph Smith’s First Vision confirmed the same pattern. The Latter-day Saint position is not polytheism. It is that the Father, Son, and Holy Ghost are three separate, divine persons — perfectly united in purpose and will John 17:21–22 — but not merged into one substance.

missionary.simple: The Trinity was formally defined at Nicaea in AD 325 using the Greek philosophical term homoousios, not found in scripture. Stephen’s vision shows two separate beings. The restored gospel teaches three distinct divine persons, one in purpose — exactly as scripture describes.
missionary.explain: 'At Christ’s baptism, He stands in the water. The Father speaks from heaven. The Spirit descends as a dove. Three events, three places, three persons. No philosophical merging into one substance can account for that scene.'
missionary.why: Whether God is one merged being or three separate persons directly affects how we pray, how we understand Christ’s atonement, and what eternal life actually means.
missionary.invite: Would you be willing to read the account of Christ’s baptism in Matthew 3 and ask God to show you the nature of the Godhead as it is revealed there?

questions.question: 10. Why do some Christians pray to saints, and what does scripture say about it?
questions.answer:
Prayer to saints — asking deceased holy persons to intercede with God on your behalf — developed as an extension of asking living Christians to pray for you. James 5:16 It entered Catholic and Orthodox practice gradually. By the 4th and 5th centuries, martyrs’ tombs had become sites of prayer, and by the medieval period each saint had assigned areas of intercession.

The practice has no explicit New Testament command. Scripture consistently directs prayer to God the Father through Jesus Christ: “Whatsoever ye shall ask in my name, that will I do.”John 14:13 Paul’s instruction is clear: “For there is one God, and one mediator between God and men, the man Christ Jesus.”1 Timothy 2:5 The dead are not shown in scripture as active intermediaries for the living.

In the New Testament, all baptized believers are called saints. Romans 1:7; 1 Corinthians 1:2 The canonisation process by which the Catholic Church officially declares someone a saint has no scriptural precedent. The Latter-day Saint position is direct: we pray to the Father in the name of Christ. No other mediator is needed or appointed.

missionary.simple: Scripture names one mediator — Christ Jesus. It directs all prayer to the Father through Him. The practice of praying to deceased saints entered Christianity through veneration of martyrs’ tombs in the 4th century — not through any New Testament command.
missionary.explain: 'Paul says there is one God and one mediator. Not one primary mediator and several helpful intercessors on the side. One. The simplicity and directness of that teaching is unmistakable.'
missionary.why: Direct access to God through Christ is one of the most precious gifts of the gospel. No one needs to stand in between you and your Father.
missionary.invite: Would you like to experience what it feels like to pray directly to God the Father in the name of Jesus Christ, with no intermediary, and hear His answer?

questions.question: 11. What is the origin of Christmas, and is December 25 the actual birth date of Christ?
questions.answer:
December 25th is not confirmed anywhere in scripture as the birth date of Christ. The Gospels give no specific date. Luke 2:8 says shepherds were “abiding in the field” — activity consistent with spring or autumn in Judea, not the cold of winter, when flocks were typically penned indoors.

Early Christians did not celebrate Christ’s birth as a major feast. The celebration began taking formal shape in the 4th century. In AD 336, the Roman Church first recorded December 25th as the Nativity. That date corresponds with the Roman feast of Sol Invictus and the earlier feast of Saturnalia. As Christianity spread through the Roman Empire, it was common practice to attach Christian meaning to existing celebrations rather than ban them. Pope Gregory I explicitly endorsed this approach for missionaries to England in AD 601.

Many scholars acknowledge the pagan roots of December 25th. The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints has no official position requiring members to observe Christmas on any specific date. What matters is that Christ was born. Isaiah 7:14; Matthew 1:21–23 The exact date is far less important than the reality of His divine mission.

There is one additional detail worth noting. Section 20 of the Doctrine and Covenants opens: “The rise of the Church of Christ in these last days, being one thousand eight hundred and thirty years since the coming of our Lord and Savior Jesus Christ in the flesh” (D&C 20:1). Some past Church leaders, including James E. Talmage and Bruce R. McConkie, interpreted this verse to mean Christ was born on April 6, 1 BC, making April 6 a date commonly discussed in Latter-day Saint culture as a possible birth date. However, the Church has never declared this official doctrine. Many Latter-day Saint scholars note that D&C 20:1 may simply be using formal language to describe the year 1830, not revealing a specific calendar date for the Saviour's birth. The Church's current manuals and official publications do not make a definitive claim about the exact day or year. Non-Latter-day Saint biblical scholars generally place the birth of Jesus between 6 BC and 4 BC, largely because Herod the Great died around 4 BC according to historical records. The honest answer is that no one knows the precise date. What is certain is that He was born, He lived, and He rose.

missionary.simple: December 25 is not given in scripture. Shepherds abiding in fields points to spring or autumn. The date was chosen in AD 336 to correspond with Roman solstice celebrations. Christ was born — the exact date matters far less than what His birth means.
missionary.explain: 'The importance of Christmas is not the date. It is the fact. God came to earth. He was born of a woman. He lived among us. Whether that happened in spring or winter does not change what it means.'
missionary.why: Knowing the historical origins of December 25 does not diminish the celebration — it clarifies what you are actually celebrating: the reality of the incarnation, not the calendar date.
missionary.invite: Would you like to understand what the restored gospel teaches about the birth of Christ and what His coming means for you personally?

questions.question: 12. What is the origin of Easter, and what does the resurrection mean for Christians?
questions.answer:
Easter commemorates the resurrection of Jesus Christ — the foundational event of Christianity. 1 Corinthians 15:14–17 Without the resurrection, Paul says, faith is pointless. The event itself is supported by multiple witnesses, an empty tomb, and more than five hundred people who saw the risen Lord. 1 Corinthians 15:6

The date of Easter was disputed from the earliest centuries. The First Council of Nicaea in AD 325 set it as the first Sunday after the first full moon following the spring equinox. The name Easter in English likely derives from Eostre, an Anglo-Saxon spring goddess. In most other languages, the celebration is named from the Hebrew Passover, which is historically accurate since Christ died as the Passover lamb. 1 Corinthians 5:7

The eggs, bunnies, and spring imagery common in popular Easter culture come from spring fertility traditions, not scripture. Members of The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints celebrate the resurrection as the most significant event in human history. The weekly sacrament is, in many ways, a weekly Easter — a remembrance of His death and resurrection. John 20:1–18; Luke 24:1–8

missionary.simple: The resurrection is the best-attested event in early Christian literature. Easter as a celebration has complex historical roots, but the reality it points to is simple: Christ rose from the dead with a glorified body, and that changes everything about human destiny.
missionary.explain: 'Paul says if Christ be not raised, your faith is vain. He does not say your faith is slightly weakened. He says it is worthless. The resurrection is not a secondary doctrine — it is the foundation everything else rests on.'
missionary.why: The resurrection means physical death is not the end. Every person who has ever lived will be resurrected. That is the gift Christ gave to the entire human family.
missionary.invite: Would you like to study the resurrection accounts in the Gospels and ask God what it means that Christ physically rose from the dead?

questions.question: 13. Why do some Christian churches mix Old and New Testament practices inconsistently?
questions.answer:
Paul is explicit: the law of Moses — dietary laws, animal sacrifice, circumcision, feast days — served as “a schoolmaster to bring us unto Christ; but after that faith is come, we are no longer under a schoolmaster.”Galatians 3:24–25 Christ himself declared “It is finished,” marking the completion of the Mosaic dispensation. John 19:30

That does not mean Christians discard the Old Testament. Paul wrote that “All scripture is given by inspiration of God, and is profitable for doctrine, for reproof, for correction, for instruction in righteousness.”2 Timothy 3:16 Christ said he came not to destroy the law but to fulfil it. Matthew 5:17 The moral law — the Ten Commandments, the commands to love God and neighbour — remains. The ceremonial and sacrificial law was fulfilled in Christ.

The problem in many congregations comes from inconsistent application. A preacher may quote Malachi 3:10 to require tithes while ignoring Leviticus food restrictions entirely. The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints approaches this carefully: the principles of the Old Testament are eternal and fully relevant; the specific ordinances of the Mosaic law were preparatory and were fulfilled in Christ.

missionary.simple: The law of Moses was a schoolmaster to bring us to Christ — fulfilled in Him. The moral law is eternal. The ceremonial law was preparatory. Inconsistent application — requiring tithes but ignoring food laws — shows what happens without a living prophet to clarify boundaries.
missionary.explain: 'If the Mosaic law is still binding, keep all of it. If it was fulfilled in Christ, explain why. The inconsistency reveals a deeper problem: without continuing revelation, every preacher draws the line where it is convenient for them.'
missionary.why: Understanding which parts of the law were fulfilled in Christ and which principles are eternal is essential to sound doctrine. A living prophet removes the guesswork.
missionary.invite: Would you like to understand how the restored gospel distinguishes between eternal principles and fulfilled ordinances in the Bible?

questions.question: 14. Where did the image of hell as eternal fire come from, and what does the Bible actually teach?
questions.answer:
The popular image of hell — eternal fire, demons, pitchforks, the devil ruling below — owes more to medieval literature, particularly Dante’s Inferno written around 1308, and ancient mythology than to careful scripture study.

The New Testament uses three different words commonly translated as hell. Sheol in Hebrew and Hades in Greek both simply mean the realm of the dead — a spirit world, not necessarily a place of torment. Gehenna refers to the Valley of Hinnom outside Jerusalem, a perpetually burning rubbish dump that became a metaphor for destruction and judgment. Christ’s teaching in Luke 16 depicts post-mortal states. Revelation describes a “lake of fire” which is “the second death” — spiritual separation from God. Revelation 20:14

The Latter-day Saint understanding is more nuanced and more scriptural. Those who have a perfect knowledge of God’s reality and then deliberately deny it face outer darkness. For others, the kingdoms of glory vary in closeness to God. 1 Corinthians 15:40–42 Both the justice and the mercy of God are honoured in this view.

missionary.simple: The Bible uses three different words translated as hell — only one of which involves fire. The medieval image of eternal torment owes more to Dante’s Inferno than to scripture. The restored gospel’s view of kingdoms of glory honours both God’s justice and His mercy.
missionary.explain: 'Paul describes celestial, terrestrial, and telestial bodies — three levels of glory. He is describing what happens after the judgment. The restored gospel has taught this from the beginning, and it matches exactly what Paul wrote.'
missionary.why: A God who condemns billions to eternal fire for the accident of where they were born is not the God of the Bible. The restored view of the afterlife is far more just, far more merciful, and far more scriptural.
missionary.invite: Would you like to understand what the restored gospel teaches about the kingdoms of glory and how they reflect the justice and mercy of God?

questions.question: 15. Did Jesus Christ really rise physically from the dead? What is the evidence?
questions.answer:
The resurrection of Jesus Christ is the best-attested event in early Christian literature. Within the New Testament, Paul — writing within 20–25 years of the crucifixion — lists eyewitnesses by name: Peter, the twelve, five hundred brethren at once, James, and Paul himself. 1 Corinthians 15:5–8 This is not legend. Paul personally knew those witnesses.

The empty tomb is acknowledged even by enemies. The Jewish authorities never denied it was empty — they claimed the disciples stole the body. Matthew 28:12–13 If the body were still there, producing it would have stopped Christianity immediately.

The transformation of the disciples is also compelling. Men who fled and hid at Christ’s arrest Mark 14:50 became bold enough to preach publicly at risk of death within weeks of the resurrection. And non-Christian sources — Josephus, Tacitus, and Pliny the Younger — confirm that a group of people believed Christ had risen and would not deny it under persecution. Paul states the stakes clearly: “if Christ be not raised, your faith is vain; ye are yet in your sins.”1 Corinthians 15:17 It is the centre of everything.

missionary.simple: More than 500 people saw the risen Christ. His enemies admitted the tomb was empty. Men who fled in fear became willing to die for what they had personally seen. Non-Christian historians confirm the movement. The evidence for the resurrection is extraordinary.
missionary.explain: 'Five hundred witnesses at once. Named witnesses Paul personally knew, still alive when he wrote. An empty tomb even enemies acknowledged. Frightened men who became fearless unto death. You cannot account for those facts without the resurrection.'
missionary.why: If Christ rose from the dead, everything He said is true, everything He promised is certain, and death has lost its power over every person who follows Him.
missionary.invite: Would you be willing to ask God directly whether Jesus Christ rose from the dead, and trust that He will answer?

questions.question: 16. Why does the Bible say God is a Spirit if members of The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints believe He has a body?
questions.answer:
John 4:24 — “God is a Spirit: and they that worship him must worship him in spirit and in truth” — is the verse most often cited. But reading it in context shows that Christ was explaining how to worship, not defining God’s physical nature. He was contrasting true worship with location-based worship at Jerusalem or on a mountain. The verse describes how we approach God, not what God is made of.

Throughout the Bible, God is described in ways that suggest form and substance. Moses spoke to God “face to face, as a man speaketh unto his friend.”Exodus 33:11 Abraham saw the Lord. Genesis 18:1 Isaiah “saw also the Lord sitting upon a throne.”Isaiah 6:1 Stephen saw “the glory of God, and Jesus standing on the right hand of God.”Acts 7:55–56 These are eyewitness accounts, not metaphors.

The idea that God is without body, parts, or passions entered Christian theology through the influence of Greek philosophy and was formalised in the Westminster Confession in 1646. It is a post-biblical development. The restored gospel simply confirms what careful scripture readers have always noticed: God the Father is a glorified, embodied being, and that truth makes prayer and the concept of being made in His image fully meaningful.

missionary.simple: John 4:24 describes how to worship God — in spirit and truth — not the composition of His body. Moses spoke to God face to face. Isaiah saw Him on a throne. Stephen saw Him with Christ at His right hand. These are eyewitness accounts, not metaphors.
missionary.explain: 'The Westminster Confession of 1646 says God is without body, parts, or passions. That is a 17th-century theological document. The Bible says Moses spoke to God face to face, as a man speaks to his friend. Which one are you going to trust?'
missionary.why: Knowing that God has a body means He is not a philosophical abstraction. He is a personal Father who sees, hears, and answers. That changes everything about how you approach Him.
missionary.invite: Would you be willing to read Joseph Smith’s account of the First Vision and ask God whether what Joseph saw is consistent with what the Bible teaches about the nature of God?

questions.question: 17. What does the Bible teach about the role of women in the Church?
questions.answer:
Certain passages appear restrictive: 1 Corinthians 14:34 says women should keep silent in churches; 1 Timothy 2:12 says women should not teach or have authority over men. But these must be read in their historical context. Paul was addressing specific problems in specific congregations in Corinth and Ephesus, not setting a universal rule for all time.

Other passages tell a different story. Women were the first witnesses of the resurrection and the first commissioned to proclaim it. Matthew 28:7–8; John 20:17 Phoebe is called a deaconess. Romans 16:1 Priscilla co-taught Apollos, described as “an eloquent man, and mighty in the scriptures.”Acts 18:26 Philip’s four daughters prophesied. Acts 21:9 Deborah was a judge and prophet over all Israel. Judges 4:4 Joel prophesied that in the last days “your sons and your daughters shall prophesy.”Joel 2:28

Latter-day Saint women do not hold the Melchizedek Priesthood, but they participate fully in priesthood work, serve in leadership callings, perform ordinances in the temple, speak and teach in all meetings, and serve as auxiliary presidents. Their roles are substantive, not ceremonial.

missionary.simple: Women were the first witnesses of the resurrection and the first to proclaim it. Phoebe was a deaconess. Priscilla taught scripture. Deborah led Israel. Joel prophesied that daughters would prophesy in the last days. The restored gospel gives women substantial roles, not ceremonial ones.
missionary.explain: 'God said through Joel that in the last days daughters shall prophesy. He did not say daughters shall sit quietly. The restored church fulfils that prophecy — women lead, teach, serve, and exercise real authority in their callings.'
missionary.why: Understanding the full biblical picture of women in the church removes centuries of cultural overlay and reveals a God who values and uses women’s gifts across every age of scripture.
missionary.invite: Would you like to understand how women serve and lead in the restored church, and how that reflects the biblical pattern from Deborah to Mary Magdalene?

questions.question: 18. Is speaking in tongues required as evidence of receiving the Holy Ghost?
questions.answer:
The belief that speaking in tongues is the required sign of receiving the Holy Ghost is primarily a Pentecostal doctrine, emerging from the Azusa Street Revival in Los Angeles in 1906. It rests largely on Acts 2:4, when on the day of Pentecost “they were all filled with the Holy Ghost, and began to speak with other tongues.”

But the pattern in the New Testament is not consistent with that reading. When the Samaritans received the Holy Ghost Acts 8:17, no tongues are mentioned. Paul’s conversion records no tongues at all. Acts 9:17–18 Paul, who spoke in tongues more than anyone, 1 Corinthians 14:18 explicitly cautioned against making it the central gift: “I would that ye all spake with tongues, but rather that ye prophesied.”1 Corinthians 14:5

The gifts listed in 1 Corinthians 12–14 include tongues, but also healing, prophecy, wisdom, knowledge, faith, and discernment. No single gift is made mandatory for all. The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints teaches that the Holy Ghost confirms truth and manifests in every gift listed — tongues included, but not tongues alone.

missionary.simple: Paul spoke in tongues more than anyone and ranked prophecy above it. When the Samaritans received the Holy Ghost, no tongues are mentioned. Making tongues the mandatory sign of the Spirit is a Pentecostal doctrine from 1906, not a New Testament teaching.
missionary.explain: 'Paul said he would rather speak five words with understanding than ten thousand in an unknown tongue. He was not downplaying the gift — he was correcting its misuse. The Spirit manifests in wisdom, faith, healing, prophecy, and discernment — not tongues alone.'
missionary.why: Defining the Spirit by one manifestation narrows what God can do. The restored gospel recognises every gift of the Spirit as real and present, without requiring any single one as proof.
missionary.invite: Would you like to understand how the Holy Ghost works in the restored church, and what a genuine confirmation of truth feels like?

questions.question: 19. Who wrote the Bible, and how was it assembled?
questions.answer:
The Bible was written over roughly 1,500 years, from around 1400 BC to approximately AD 95. About 40 human authors were involved — kings, fishermen, physicians, priests, prophets — writing in Hebrew, Aramaic, and Greek. The consistent theological thread running through all of it testifies to divine inspiration. 2 Timothy 3:16; 2 Peter 1:21

The Old Testament canon was largely settled among Jewish scholars by the Council of Jamnia around AD 90. The New Testament canon developed more gradually. The Council of Carthage in AD 397 formally affirmed the 27-book New Testament canon that Protestants use today. The Catholic Bible includes additional books — the Deuterocanon — that most Protestants do not accept. Jerome’s Latin Vulgate translation completed in AD 405 served as the standard Bible for over a thousand years. The King James Version of 1611 put it in the hands of ordinary people.

The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints affirms that the Bible is the word of God “as far as it is translated correctly.” This is not a rejection of the Bible. It is an honest recognition that manuscripts, copies, and translations involve human hands, and that additional scripture provides further witness. Amos 3:7

missionary.simple: 40 authors over 1,500 years, three languages, and a consistent theological thread pointing to Christ. The canon was settled by councils in AD 90 and AD 397. The Latter-day Saint acknowledgment that translation is imperfect is not doubt — it is honesty about how books are copied and translated across millennia.
missionary.explain: 'God inspired the writers. He did not control every copyist and council member across fifteen centuries. The Book of Mormon is a second witness of Christ precisely because a second witness strengthens the first — not contradicts it.'
missionary.why: Understanding how the Bible was assembled actually increases respect for it. God preserved His word across extraordinary obstacles. And He provided additional scripture for this dispensation.
missionary.invite: Would you be willing to read the Book of Mormon as a second witness alongside the Bible and ask God whether both are His word?

questions.question: 20. What is the origin of tithing, and is 10% the correct amount for Christians today?
questions.answer:
Tithing predates the law of Moses. Abraham paid tithes to Melchizedek Genesis 14:20 and Jacob vowed a tenth to the Lord Genesis 28:22 long before Moses received the law. The Mosaic law then formalised this practice. Malachi 3:10 frames it as the one area where God invites His people to test Him: “Bring ye all the tithes into the storehouse...and prove me now herewith, saith the Lord of hosts, if I will not open you the windows of heaven.”

Christ commended tithing while also calling for inward change: “these ought ye to have done, and not to leave the other undone.”Matthew 23:23 Paul taught proportionate, willing giving: “Every man according as he purposeth in his heart, so let him give; not grudgingly, or of necessity: for God loveth a cheerful giver.”2 Corinthians 9:7

Members of The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints pay ten percent of their income as tithing — consistent with the biblical pattern and the Lord’s direct instruction through the restored gospel. Tithing funds are used for meetinghouses, temples, education, and humanitarian work worldwide. The principle is simple: put God first financially, and trust His promise of blessings.

missionary.simple: Tithing predates Moses — Abraham paid it, Jacob vowed it. Malachi says it is the one area where God invites you to test Him. Christ commended it. The restored church follows the biblical pattern of a full tenth, with the promise attached.
missionary.explain: 'God said prove me now herewith. That is not a demand — it is an invitation. He is saying: try it. Trust me with a tenth, and see what I do with the nine. That promise has never been withdrawn.'
missionary.why: Tithing is not about Church finances. It is about where your trust sits. It is the practice of putting God first with the resource most people hold most tightly.
missionary.invite: Would you be willing to accept the invitation of Malachi 3:10 and test God’s promise about tithing for one year?

questions.question: 21. What is Africa's place in the history of Christianity?
questions.answer:
Africa’s Christian history is ancient and largely left out of Western accounts. It begins in the New Testament itself. Simon of Cyrene, from modern Libya, carried Christ’s cross. Mark 15:21 The Ethiopian eunuch — baptized by Philip — carried the gospel back to Africa. Acts 8:27–39 Egypt, particularly Alexandria, was one of the intellectual centres of early Christianity, home to Clement of Alexandria, Origen, and Athanasius, whose defence of Christ’s divinity shaped the Nicene Creed.

North Africa also produced three of the most influential theologians in church history: Tertullian, Cyprian, and Augustine — all from the region of modern Tunisia and Algeria. The Coptic Church in Egypt and the Ethiopian Orthodox Church are among the oldest continuously existing Christian institutions on earth.

Islam’s spread across North Africa from the 7th century onward significantly reduced Christianity there, though Egypt’s Coptic community has survived to this day. Today Africa has more Christians than any other continent. The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints has grown quickly across West, East, and Southern Africa, fulfilling Isaiah’s and the Psalmist’s prophecies about Ethiopia and Africa stretching their hands to God. Psalm 68:31; Zephaniah 3:10

missionary.simple: Africa was present at the cross and at the first baptism after Christ’s resurrection. Augustine, Tertullian, and Cyprian — three of history’s greatest theologians — were African. Today Africa has more Christians than any continent. The gospel is coming home to where it has always had deep roots.
missionary.explain: 'The Ethiopian eunuch was baptized before Paul was converted. Africa did not receive Christianity from Europe. Africa received it from Philip, directly from the original church, and carried it home centuries before European missionaries arrived.'
missionary.why: Understanding Africa’s role in Christian history restores a dignity and depth to African believers that centuries of colonial history tried to obscure. The gospel has always been at home in Africa.
missionary.invite: Would you like to know how the prophecies of Isaiah and the Psalms about Africa and Ethiopia are being fulfilled in the growth of the restored church today?

questions.question: 22. What is the significance of Jerusalem in the Bible and in the last days?
questions.answer:
Jerusalem appears more than 800 times in the Bible — the most mentioned city in all of scripture. David captured it and made it Israel’s capital around 1000 BC. 2 Samuel 5:6–9 Solomon built the First Temple there. It is the city where Christ was crucified, buried, and resurrected, and where the Holy Ghost descended at Pentecost. Acts 2

For the last days, Jerusalem holds central importance. Zechariah 12:3 says it will become “a burdensome stone for all people.” Zechariah 14:4 states that when Christ returns, his feet will stand on the Mount of Olives east of Jerusalem, which will split. Ezekiel 48 describes a millennial Israel centred on Jerusalem. Revelation 21

The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints holds a distinct perspective: Christ will appear at Jerusalem, fulfilling Old Testament prophecy, and also at New Jerusalem in the Americas, fulfilling latter-day scripture. 3 Nephi 20:22 Jerusalem’s importance is not merely symbolic. It is the stage for the fulfilment of God’s covenant with Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob.

missionary.simple: Jerusalem is the most mentioned city in scripture — over 800 times. Christ died, rose, and will return there. Zechariah, Ezekiel, and Revelation all describe its central role in the last days. God’s covenant with Abraham will be fulfilled in Jerusalem.
missionary.explain: 'Zechariah said Jerusalem would become a burdensome stone for all people in the last days. Look at the headlines. Every major geopolitical conflict in the region centres on that city. Prophecy does not get more specific than that.'
missionary.why: Understanding Jerusalem’s prophetic role connects current world events to God’s ancient promises and helps believers see that history is moving toward a purpose.
missionary.invite: Would you like to study the Old Testament prophecies about Jerusalem in the last days and see how both the Bible and latter-day scripture describe the same events?

questions.question: 23. Did Jesus Christ really exist? What does history outside the Bible say?
questions.answer:
The historical existence of Jesus Christ is among the best-attested facts of ancient history. Professional historians — whether Christian or not — do not seriously question it.

Josephus, a Jewish historian writing around AD 93, refers to Jesus in two passages of his Antiquities of the Jews, describing him as “a wise man” and noting his execution under Pontius Pilate. A second passage refers to “James the brother of Jesus who was called Christ.” Tacitus, a Roman historian writing around AD 116, describes Nero blaming Christians for the great fire of Rome and notes that Christ “suffered the extreme penalty during the reign of Tiberius at the hands of one of our procurators, Pontius Pilatus.” Pliny the Younger, writing around AD 112, describes Christians singing hymns to Christ as to a god.

These are not Christian sources. They are Roman and Jewish historians with no motivation to support Christian claims. Their testimony confirms what the New Testament records. Matthew 1:1; Luke 3:1–2; 1 Corinthians 15:3–8

missionary.simple: Josephus, Tacitus, and Pliny the Younger — Roman and Jewish historians with no reason to support Christianity — all confirm Jesus lived, was crucified under Pontius Pilate, and was worshipped by His followers as God. The historical existence of Christ is beyond serious question.
missionary.explain: 'Tacitus was hostile to Christianity. He called it a superstition. Yet he confirmed Christ was executed under Pilate. When your critics confirm your core historical claim, you have very strong evidence.'
missionary.why: The historical confirmation of Christ’s existence is the foundation everything else stands on. Once you know He was real, the question becomes: was He who He said He was?
missionary.invite: Would you be willing to examine the historical evidence for Christ’s existence and then ask God directly whether He is the Son of God?

questions.question: 24. What does the Bible teach about Satan, and how much power does he actually have?
questions.answer:
Satan is a real being, not a symbol. His names reveal his nature: Satan means adversary in Hebrew; Devil comes from the Greek diabolos, meaning slanderer or accuser. He is called “the prince of this world”John 12:31 and “the god of this world”2 Corinthians 4:4 — titles that acknowledge his real but limited authority.

His origins: Isaiah 14:12–15 describes the fall of “Lucifer, son of the morning,” cast down for aspiring to be like the Most High. Revelation 12:9 identifies him as the great dragon, that old serpent, cast out of heaven with a third of the host. Revelation 12:4 He rebelled against the Father’s plan and sought to destroy human agency.

His power is real but bounded. He cannot force anyone’s will. Job 1:12 shows he must receive divine permission before acting. Christ defeated him through the Atonement. Hebrews 2:14 Resist him and he will flee. James 4:7 He will be bound for a thousand years during the Millennium Revelation 20:2 and ultimately cast into outer darkness. His power over the righteous is exactly as much as they give him — no more.

missionary.simple: Satan is a real being who must receive divine permission before acting — Job proves this. Christ defeated him through the Atonement. Resist him and he flees. His power over the righteous is exactly what they give him — nothing more.
missionary.explain: 'God told Satan exactly how far he could go with Job and no further. Satan did not like it, but he obeyed. He is not God’s equal. He is a rebellious creature who lost the war in heaven and continues fighting a battle he already lost.'
missionary.why: Understanding Satan’s real but limited power removes both the dangerous extremes: treating him as omnipotent, or dismissing him as a myth. He is real, he is defeated, and you have power over him through Christ.
missionary.invite: Would you like to understand what the restored gospel teaches about the war in heaven, the fall of Satan, and the power Christ’s atonement gives you over his influence?

questions.question: 25. What does the Bible actually teach about life after death?
questions.answer:
Scripture presents a richer picture of life after death than most sermons suggest. At death, the spirit separates from the body. The body returns to dust Ecclesiastes 12:7; the spirit goes to the spirit world — a real place where the righteous rest in paradise Luke 23:43 while others continue to learn.

Peter says Christ preached to spirits in prison so the dead could be “judged according to men in the flesh.”1 Peter 4:6 This directly supports the teaching that the gospel is preached after death — not everyone receives a fair opportunity in this life.

Resurrection reunites spirit with a perfected, immortal body. 1 Corinthians 15:42–44 After resurrection comes judgment. Revelation 20:12–13 Paul’s description of celestial, terrestrial, and telestial bodies in 1 Corinthians 15:40–42 corresponds precisely with the Latter-day Saint teaching on three kingdoms of glory. Most of humanity — including billions who never heard of Christ — will not be condemned to darkness. God’s justice and mercy are both honoured in a plan that accounts for every soul. 1 Peter 3:18–20

missionary.simple: The Bible describes a spirit world, a resurrection, and three levels of glory after judgment. Peter says Christ preached to spirits in prison so the dead could be judged fairly. The restored gospel matches scripture in every detail of what happens after death.
missionary.explain: 'Paul describes three different bodies of glory — celestial, terrestrial, and telestial — in the resurrection. He was not writing poetry. He was describing real distinctions in the state of the dead after judgment. The restored gospel taught exactly this from the beginning.'
missionary.why: Knowing what actually happens after death removes both the terror of a simplistic heaven-or-hell choice and the despair of thinking billions of honest souls are condemned for geography.
missionary.invite: Would you like to study what the scriptures teach about the spirit world and kingdoms of glory, and ask God whether this picture of His mercy and justice is true?

questions.question: 26. What is the origin of Sunday worship, and why do some Christians still keep Saturday as the Sabbath?
questions.answer:
The commandment given to Moses was to remember the Sabbath — the seventh day, Saturday — to keep it holy. Exodus 20:8–11 Jewish communities have observed the Sabbath on Saturday for more than three thousand years.

Early Christians — most of whom were Jewish — initially continued Saturday worship. But Sunday worship began early in the Christian era in honour of the resurrection, which took place on the first day of the week. Matthew 28:1; John 20:19 Paul met with saints on “the first day of the week.”Acts 20:7 The Didache around AD 100 and Justin Martyr around AD 155 both describe Sunday as the regular day of Christian assembly. Emperor Constantine made Sunday a legal rest day in AD 321.

Seventh-day Adventists and some other groups keep Saturday worship, arguing that no scriptural authority changed the Sabbath day. Members of The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints worship on Sunday in honour of the resurrection, but treat the Sabbath principles — rest, worship, family time, avoidance of commercial activity — as fully applicable to the day set apart for the Lord. D&C 59:9–13

missionary.simple: Sunday worship began because the resurrection happened on the first day of the week. The early church, Paul, and the Didache all confirm Sunday assembly within decades of the resurrection. The Sabbath principles of rest and worship remain fully binding — the day was changed in honour of Christ’s rising.
missionary.explain: 'The resurrection is the greatest event in human history. It happened on Sunday. The early church kept Sunday as the Lord’s Day in response to that reality. Keeping Sunday holy is not an abandonment of the Sabbath — it is a recognition of what Sunday is.'
missionary.why: The Sabbath exists to turn your attention to God and away from the world. Whether that is Saturday or Sunday, the principle is the same: one day in seven belongs to Him.
missionary.invite: Would you like to understand what the restored gospel teaches about keeping Sunday holy and what that practice does for a family over years and decades?

questions.question: 27. What does the Bible say about wealth, and is the prosperity gospel scriptural?
questions.answer:
No doctrine is more widely misused today than prosperity theology — the teaching that financial blessing is always God’s will for the faithful, and that poverty signals spiritual failure. This is not biblical Christianity.

Christ was direct: “Lay not up for yourselves treasures upon earth, where moth and rust doth corrupt...But lay up for yourselves treasures in heaven.”Matthew 6:19–20 He told the rich young ruler to sell everything and follow him. Matthew 19:21 Paul wrote that “the love of money is the root of all evil”1 Timothy 6:10 and warned specifically about preachers who see financial gain as a sign of godliness. 1 Timothy 6:5–8

The prosperity gospel arose largely in 20th-century American Christianity. Its teachers cite Abraham’s wealth and Solomon’s riches while ignoring the poverty of the prophets, the apostles, and Christ himself, who had “not where to lay his head.”Matthew 8:20 Latter-day Saint teaching is balanced: be financially responsible, avoid debt, pay tithing, maintain a year’s supply, and be generous — but never equate material success with divine favour.

missionary.simple: Christ told the rich young ruler to sell everything. Paul called the love of money the root of all evil. Christ himself had nowhere to lay his head. The prosperity gospel is a 20th-century American invention that cherry-picks Abraham’s wealth while ignoring the poverty of every apostle.
missionary.explain: 'Christ said you cannot serve God and mammon. That is a binary choice. The prosperity gospel says you can serve God and get rich as a reward. Those two teachings cannot both be true. One of them came from Christ. The other came from a TV studio.'
missionary.why: The restored gospel teaches financial principles that actually work — tithing, avoiding debt, storing reserves, and generous giving — rooted in scripture, not in promises of prosperity.
missionary.invite: Would you like to understand what the restored gospel teaches about money, stewardship, and the real blessings God promises to the financially faithful?

questions.question: 28. What is the origin of the cross as a Christian symbol, and why don't members of The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints use it?
questions.answer:
In the first two centuries, the cross was rarely used as a Christian symbol — it was an instrument of shameful execution, and early believers more commonly used the fish or the chi-rho symbol. The cross became prominent after Constantine’s conversion in AD 312.

There is genuine spiritual meaning in the cross. Christ died on it for our sins. Paul gloried in “the cross of our Lord Jesus Christ”Galatians 6:14 and Christ commanded his followers to “take up their cross.”Matthew 16:24 These are expressions of sacrifice and discipleship, not instructions to display the instrument itself.

The Latter-day Saint position is that the living Christ matters more than the dying one. President Gordon B. Hinckley explained it plainly: the cross is the symbol of the dying Christ, while the message of the restored gospel is a declaration of the living Christ. The empty tomb, not the cross, is the defining symbol of Christianity. The sacrament, taken weekly, provides a more intimate and scriptural remembrance of Christ’s sacrifice than a piece of jewellery or a building ornament. 1 Corinthians 1:17–18; Romans 6:5–6

missionary.simple: The cross became a major symbol after Constantine in AD 312, not from the earliest church. The restored gospel focuses on the living Christ rather than the dying one. The weekly sacrament is a more personal and scriptural remembrance of His sacrifice than a symbol on a building.
missionary.explain: 'Taking up your cross is a call to sacrifice — not a call to wear a symbol. The empty tomb is what changed history. We do not put empty tombs on our buildings either — because He is not in the tomb. He is alive, and His church is led by a living prophet.'
missionary.why: The absence of the cross in Latter-day Saint chapels is not a rejection of Christ’s sacrifice. It is a declaration of His resurrection and continued leadership of His church.
missionary.invite: Would you like to understand how the weekly sacrament in the restored church functions as a living, personal remembrance of Christ’s atonement more meaningful than any symbol?

questions.question: 29. What were the Crusades, and what do they mean for Christians today?
questions.answer:
The Crusades were a series of religiously motivated military campaigns sanctioned by the papacy, primarily between AD 1095 and 1291, aimed at recapturing the Holy Land from Muslim control. Pope Urban II launched the First Crusade in 1095 at Clermont, promising spiritual rewards to those who fought.

The First Crusade ended with the 1099 siege of Jerusalem, during which Crusaders massacred both Muslim and Jewish inhabitants. The Fourth Crusade in 1204 never reached the Holy Land — it sacked Constantinople, the centre of Eastern Christianity, deepening the East-West divide.

None of this reflects the teaching of Jesus Christ, who commanded: “Love your enemies, bless them that curse you, do good to them that hate you.”Matthew 5:44 Paul wrote that “the weapons of our warfare are not carnal.”2 Corinthians 10:4 The sword has never been the tool of genuine gospel work.

For Christians today, the Crusades stand as a sobering reminder of what happens when institutional religion, political power, and worldly ambition push the actual gospel aside. The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints has no history of forced conversion or religious violence. The gospel spreads by persuasion, love, and testimony. D&C 121:41–43; John 18:36

missionary.simple: The Crusades were launched by popes who promised heaven to soldiers who killed in God’s name. Christ said love your enemies. Paul said our weapons are not carnal. The Crusades are what happens when institutional power replaces actual gospel teaching.
missionary.explain: 'Christ told Pilate: my kingdom is not of this world, else would my servants fight. Every sword raised in the name of Christianity since that moment contradicts the explicit teaching of the King those swords claimed to serve.'
missionary.why: Understanding the Crusades helps honest seekers separate the failures of institutional religion from the actual teachings of Jesus Christ — which have always been about love, not conquest.
missionary.invite: Would you like to understand how the restored gospel defines the correct means of spreading the gospel, and why persuasion and love are the only tools Christ authorised?

questions.question: 31. Do angels truly have wings? What the Bible actually says — and when winged angels entered religion.
questions.answer:
The popular image of angels — white-robed figures with large feathered wings hovering in the sky — is one of the most widespread pictures in religious art. But when you go to the Bible itself and read carefully, the picture is far more precise, and far more interesting, than the image most people carry in their minds.

Scripture describes several distinct categories of heavenly beings. The seraphim — literally "burning ones" — appear in Isaiah's vision of the throne room of God. Each seraph had six wings: two to cover their face, two to cover their feet, and two to fly with. Isaiah 6:2 These are not messenger angels. They are celestial beings stationed before God's throne, crying "Holy, holy, holy, is the LORD of hosts." Their wings are described in detail because their function — concealment before the holiness of God — is the point.

The cherubim appear throughout scripture as guardians and throne-bearers, not messengers. God placed cherubim with a flaming sword at the gate of Eden after the Fall. Genesis 3:24 The ark of the covenant had two golden cherubim with wings stretched over the mercy seat. Exodus 25:20 Ezekiel's vision describes four living creatures — each with four faces and four wings — as part of the chariot-throne of God. Ezekiel 1:5–6 These are the cherubim. They are not the angels who knock on Abraham's door or speak to Mary.

Now here is what the Bible actually says about the ordinary messenger angels — the ones sent to human beings with a word from God. Not one of them is described with wings. The angel Gabriel appears to Mary Luke 1:26–28 and to Zacharias Luke 1:11–13 — no wings mentioned. The angel who rolls back the stone at the resurrection is described as having an appearance like lightning and clothing white as snow Matthew 28:2–3 — no wings. The angel who releases Peter from prison Acts 12:7 — no wings. The angel who speaks to Cornelius Acts 10:3 — no wings. Hebrews 13:2 says some have "entertained angels unawares" — meaning angels who appeared completely human, indistinguishable from ordinary people. Hebrews 13:2

So where did the winged angel come from?

The answer lies in art history and cultural borrowing, not scripture. The ancient Near East was filled with winged divine beings. In Assyria and Babylon, massive winged bull-men called lamassu guarded palace gates. Egypt had winged deities. Greek mythology had winged messengers — the god Hermes wore winged sandals and a winged helmet. As Christianity spread into the Roman world and then through Europe, artists began borrowing the visual language of winged divine beings from surrounding cultures to depict angels — because audiences immediately understood what a winged figure meant: a heavenly messenger. The earliest Christian art shows angels without wings. By the 4th century AD, after Constantine, winged angels began appearing in Christian iconography and became dominant over the following centuries.

The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints has received remarkable additional clarity on this subject through revelation. The Prophet Joseph Smith taught that there are two types of beings who can appear as angels: resurrected beings with physical, glorified bodies, and translated beings or spirits of the just. D&C 129:1–3 The angel Moroni, who appeared repeatedly to Joseph Smith, was a resurrected being — a former prophet who once lived on earth. D&C 27:5 He had a body. He stood in the room. He did not fly in through a window on feathered wings. The restored gospel thus answers this question in the most grounded way possible: angels are real, they are personal, they have identity — and they are not the winged creatures of medieval painting.

The Psalmist wrote that God "shall give his angels charge over thee."Psalm 91:11 Hebrews confirms that angels are "ministering spirits, sent forth to minister for them who shall be heirs of salvation."Hebrews 1:14 Their reality is certain. Their nature is grander and more personal than the greeting-card image suggests. Wings were the language of ancient art. The reality of angels is the language of scripture and restored revelation.

📖 Scripture Summary: Angels in the Bible

  • Isaiah 6:2 — Seraphim: six-winged celestial beings before God's throne — not messenger angels
  • Genesis 3:24 — Cherubim placed as guardians at the gate of Eden
  • Ezekiel 1:5–6 — Four living creatures with four wings each — the cherubim of God's chariot-throne
  • Exodus 25:20 — Cherubim with wings over the ark of the covenant
  • Luke 1:26–28 — Gabriel appears to Mary — no wings described
  • Matthew 28:2–3 — Angel at the resurrection — appearance like lightning, no wings
  • Hebrews 13:2 — Some have entertained angels without recognising them as such
  • Hebrews 1:14 — Angels are ministering spirits sent to those who are heirs of salvation
  • Psalm 91:11 — God commands His angels concerning you
  • D&C 129:1–3 — Restored gospel clarifies two types of angelic beings: resurrected and translated

missionary.simple: The Bible never describes messenger angels with wings. Seraphim and cherubim have wings, but they are heavenly throne-guardians, not messengers. The winged angel image came from ancient Near Eastern and Greek art absorbed into Christian iconography by the 4th century. The restored gospel teaches that angels like Moroni were resurrected beings — real, personal, and physical.
missionary.explain: 'Not one messenger angel in the Bible is described with wings. Gabriel visits Mary — no wings. The angel at the tomb — no wings. Peter is freed from prison by an angel — no wings. Hebrews says some entertained angels without knowing it, meaning they looked completely human. The winged angel is a work of art, not a work of scripture.'
missionary.why: Understanding the difference between seraphim, cherubim, and messenger angels shows that scripture is far more precise than tradition. It also reveals that the restored gospel's teaching about angels as resurrected beings is the most scriptural position of all.
missionary.invite: Would you like to understand how the restored gospel's teaching about angels, resurrection, and heavenly messengers brings clarity to questions that art and tradition have made confusing for centuries?

questions.question: 30. Is there evidence in scripture and history that a complete apostasy from Christ's church actually happened?
questions.answer:
Far from being a surprising claim, the Great Apostasy is one of the most thoroughly predicted events in all of scripture. Christ himself warned: “Beware of false prophets...many will say to me in that day, Lord, Lord...and then will I profess unto them, I never knew you.”Matthew 7:15, 22–23 Paul wrote: “that day shall not come, except there come a falling away first” — the Greek word is apostasia. 2 Thessalonians 2:3 He warned Ephesian elders: “after my departing shall grievous wolves enter in among you...speaking perverse things, to draw away disciples after them.”Acts 20:29–30

The historical evidence is equally clear. Within one generation of the apostles, deep doctrinal disputes had fractured the church. By the 2nd century, Gnosticism had heavily infiltrated Christian communities. The Arian controversy of the 4th century nearly tore the church apart over the nature of Christ. No living apostle could step in and resolve it by revelation — because there were none. Councils of men, bishops, and emperors made those decisions.

The Restoration was not an invention — it was a necessity, prophesied by Peter himself: “he shall send Jesus Christ...whom the heaven must receive until the times of restitution of all things.”Acts 3:20–21 The word restitution demands a prior loss. Something had to be lost before it could be restored. Amos 8:11–12

missionary.simple: Paul used the word apostasia — apostasy — and said it would come before the Lord’s return. Christ warned of false prophets. Peter prophesied a restitution of all things. The historical record shows the church fractured within a generation of the apostles. The Restoration was prophesied, not invented.
missionary.explain: 'Peter said the heavens must receive Christ until the times of restitution of all things. Restitution requires a prior loss. You cannot restore something that was never taken. Peter was prophesying the apostasy and the restoration in one sentence.'
missionary.why: Understanding the apostasy is not an attack on sincere Christians. It is the explanation of why a restoration was necessary, why there are thousands of denominations, and why a living prophet matters.
missionary.invite: Would you be willing to study the biblical prophecies of the apostasy alongside the historical record, and ask God whether the restoration Joseph Smith brought is the fulfilment of what Peter prophesied?

questions.question: 1. What are the main differences between the Catholic Church and The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints?
questions.answer:
Both traditions trace their origins to Jesus Christ and regard Him as the Saviour of the world. That is where the similarities become more complicated, because the paths each tradition claims to authority, doctrine, and ordinances are fundamentally different.

Authority and succession. The Catholic Church claims apostolic succession — that priestly authority has passed unbroken from Peter through every Pope to the present day. The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints teaches that this succession was broken during the Great Apostasy, that the priesthood was lost from the earth, and that it was literally restored in the 1820s and 1830s by John the Baptist and by Peter, James, and John as resurrected beings who appeared to Joseph Smith and Oliver Cowdery. D&C 27:7–8 One tradition claims continuity. The other claims restoration after a genuine break.

The nature of God. Catholic theology teaches the doctrine of the Trinity — one God existing as three co-equal, co-eternal persons sharing one substance, with no physical body. This doctrine was formally defined at the Council of Nicaea in AD 325. Latter-day Saint theology teaches that God the Father is a glorified, embodied being; that His Son Jesus Christ has a resurrected, physical body; and that the Holy Ghost is a personage of Spirit. D&C 130:22 These are three separate and distinct beings, perfectly unified in will and purpose but not in substance. Joseph Smith's First Vision resolved this question by direct experience — he saw them both.

Scripture and revelation. Catholics accept the Bible plus Sacred Tradition plus the Magisterium — the teaching authority of the Church. Latter-day Saints accept the Bible, the Book of Mormon, the Doctrine and Covenants, the Pearl of Great Price, and the words of a living prophet. Both traditions acknowledge that scripture requires authoritative interpretation. The difference is in who holds that authority and how they received it.

Salvation. Catholic theology teaches that salvation involves faith, the sacraments, and cooperation with grace. Purgatory — a state of purification after death — allows those who die imperfectly prepared to be cleansed before heaven. Latter-day Saint theology teaches the plan of salvation — three degrees of glory, a spirit world where the gospel continues to be preached, and temple ordinances that extend saving blessings to the dead through proxy work. 1 Corinthians 15:29 Both traditions share a deep concern for the dead and for the sufficiency of God's mercy. The mechanisms differ significantly.

Clergy and worship. The Catholic Church has a professional, celibate priesthood. Latter-day Saint congregations are led entirely by lay members who serve without pay and return to ordinary life when their calling ends — the New Testament pattern Paul described. 1 Timothy 3:1–7 Both celebrate a form of the Lord's Supper as the central act of worship — Catholics in the Mass as the literal body and blood of Christ; Latter-day Saints in the sacrament of bread and water as a covenant renewal.

The LDS position is not that Catholics are wrong to love God or sincerely follow Christ. It is that the authority and fulness of the gospel, lost in the apostasy, have been restored — and that this restoration makes available blessings the Catholic tradition sincerely seeks but cannot fully offer without that authority.

missionary.simple: Both traditions claim to follow Christ. The key difference is authority — Catholics claim unbroken succession from Peter; Latter-day Saints teach that succession was broken in the apostasy and literally restored through Joseph Smith by heavenly messengers. That difference in authority changes everything.
missionary.explain: 'A Catholic will tell you authority passed from Peter to every Pope in an unbroken line. We agree that Peter held the keys. We part ways on whether those keys were preserved or lost and restored. The evidence from history and scripture strongly supports that they were lost — and the evidence from Joseph Smith's experience is that they were restored.'
missionary.why: If priesthood authority was genuinely lost and genuinely restored, then the fulness of the gospel is available again in our day — including ordinances that link families together across eternity and extend saving blessings to those who died without the gospel.
missionary.invite: Would you like to compare what the early Christian church looked like with what exists today — and see whether restoration or continuity better describes what happened between then and now?

questions.question: 2. How does The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints differ from Protestant Christianity?
questions.answer:
Protestantism emerged from the 16th-century Reformation as a protest against Catholic corruption and error. Martin Luther, John Calvin, Ulrich Zwingli, and others broke from Rome and began reading scripture for themselves, producing hundreds of new denominations within decades. The Latter-day Saint position is that the reformers were inspired men doing essential preparatory work — but that reformation alone could not restore what the apostasy had taken. What was lost required restoration, not reinterpretation.

Scripture alone vs. continuing revelation. Most Protestant traditions hold to sola scriptura — scripture is the sole, sufficient authority for faith and practice. The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints teaches that God continues to speak through living prophets and apostles, and that the scriptural canon is not closed. Amos 3:7 The Book of Mormon, Doctrine and Covenants, and Pearl of Great Price are additional scripture — not replacements for the Bible but witnesses alongside it.

Faith alone vs. faith expressed through covenant. Luther's doctrine of sola fide — salvation by faith alone — was a response to Catholic works-righteousness. Latter-day Saint theology teaches that grace is absolutely essential and primary — that no person is saved without the infinite Atonement of Jesus Christ. But the gospel also involves covenant-making, ordinances, and discipleship. 2 Nephi 25:23 Faith is not just a belief — it is an action that leads to baptism, covenant-keeping, and transformation of character.

Priesthood authority. Most Protestant traditions rejected the Catholic priesthood entirely as corrupted. Latter-day Saint theology teaches that the problem was not priesthood itself but the loss of proper authority — and that the Melchizedek and Aaronic Priesthoods were literally restored, not simply reinstituted by human decision. Hebrews 5:4 No man takes this honour to himself.

The nature of the afterlife. Most Protestant traditions teach heaven or hell as the final destination. The Latter-day Saint understanding — grounded in 1 Corinthians 15:40–42 — teaches three degrees of glory corresponding to different levels of light received and covenants kept. The mercy of God extends to billions who never heard the gospel in mortality through the spirit world and proxy ordinances.

The closed canon. Protestants generally hold that revelation ended with the apostles and that no new scripture is possible or needed. The Restoration directly challenges this — and the Book of Mormon, translated by Joseph Smith, is itself the most tangible evidence that God has spoken again.

missionary.simple: Protestants reformed Catholicism using the Bible. Latter-day Saints teach that reformation was necessary but insufficient — what was lost required restoration. A living prophet, continuing revelation, priesthood authority, and additional scripture are what restoration means.
missionary.explain: 'Luther nailed 95 theses to a door. That took courage. But he had no apostles, no living prophet, no restored priesthood. He had the Bible and his own inspired reading of it. The same God who spoke through Paul and Peter can speak again — and He did.'
missionary.why: The existence of thousands of Protestant denominations — all reading the same Bible and reaching different conclusions — is exactly the evidence the Restoration addresses. Without a living prophet to settle doctrine by revelation, every man's interpretation becomes its own denomination.
missionary.invite: Would you like to read the account of Joseph Smith's First Vision and ask God whether He has spoken again in our day through a living prophet?

questions.question: 3. What is the difference between the Trinity and the LDS understanding of the Godhead?
questions.answer:
This is one of the most theologically significant differences between Latter-day Saint teaching and most of historic Christianity — and it is one where the biblical evidence is far more complex than most people realise.

What the Trinity teaches. The doctrine of the Trinity — formulated at the Council of Nicaea in AD 325 and further refined at Chalcedon in AD 451 — teaches that God is one divine substance or essence existing in three co-equal, co-eternal persons: Father, Son, and Holy Spirit. They share one substance but are distinct persons. God has no physical body, no parts, no passions. This is the doctrine of most Catholic, Protestant, and Orthodox Christianity.

Where the Trinity came from. The word "Trinity" appears nowhere in scripture. The Council of Nicaea was called by Emperor Constantine to resolve the Arian controversy — whether Christ was of the same substance as the Father. Three hundred bishops voted. The resulting creed used the Greek philosophical term homoousios (same substance) — a concept borrowed from Platonic thought, not drawn from the text of the New Testament. This does not make the councillors dishonest — they were doing their best without a living apostle to settle the question by revelation. But it does show that the Trinity is a theological formulation, not a direct biblical teaching.

What the Bible actually shows. At the baptism of Jesus, three distinct things happen simultaneously: Christ stands in the Jordan River, the Spirit descends as a dove, and the Father speaks from heaven. Matthew 3:16–17 Three separate manifestations, at the same moment, in the same place. Stephen, being stoned, looks up and sees "the glory of God, and Jesus standing on the right hand of God." Acts 7:55–56 Two distinct beings, separately visible. Christ prays to His Father — not to Himself. John 17:1–5 These are not the actions of a single substance in three modes. They are the actions of three separate, distinct, conscious beings.

What the Godhead teaches. Latter-day Saint theology holds that God the Father, Jesus Christ, and the Holy Ghost are three separate beings — perfectly unified in will, purpose, testimony, and love, but not merged into one substance. D&C 130:22 This is what Joseph Smith saw in the First Vision — not a theological abstraction but two actual persons, distinct and visible. The LDS position is simply a more literal reading of the biblical text, confirmed by direct revelation.

They are one in the same profound sense that Christ prayed His disciples would be one — "that they all may be one; as thou, Father, art in me, and I in thee." John 17:21 Christ was not praying that His disciples would merge into a single substance. He was praying for unity of heart, mind, and purpose. That is how the Godhead is one.

missionary.simple: The Trinity was defined by councils in AD 325 using Greek philosophical terms not found in the Bible. The LDS Godhead is three separate, distinct beings — Father, Son, and Holy Ghost — as the Bible literally shows at Christ's baptism and in Stephen's vision. Joseph Smith saw them both in the First Vision.
missionary.explain: 'At Christ's baptism, the Father speaks from heaven, the Spirit descends as a dove, and the Son stands in the water — three distinct things at the same moment in the same place. Stephen sees two beings, separately visible. These are not the acts of one substance in three modes. They are three distinct beings who are perfectly unified in will and purpose.'
missionary.why: The nature of God matters because it determines how you relate to Him. An abstract, bodiless philosophical substance cannot be your Father. A real, loving, glorified Father with a face and a voice — who knows you by name — transforms prayer from ritual into relationship.
missionary.invite: Would you be willing to read the account of Joseph Smith's First Vision, where he literally saw the Father and the Son as two separate beings, and ask God whether what Joseph described is consistent with what the Bible actually teaches?

questions.question: 4. Do Latter-day Saints believe in salvation by grace or by works?
questions.answer:
This question is one of the most misunderstood about Latter-day Saint theology — and one of the most important to answer clearly.

Grace is primary, essential, and non-negotiable. No Latter-day Saint is saved by their own effort. The Atonement of Jesus Christ is the only source of salvation for the entire human family. Without it, no amount of human effort, covenant-keeping, or good works could save a single soul. This is not a secondary point in LDS theology — it is the foundation of everything. 2 Nephi 2:6–8

What "after all we can do" actually means. The most frequently cited LDS scripture on this topic is 2 Nephi 25:23 — "it is by grace that we are saved, after all we can do." This verse calls us to full effort — to exercise faith, repent sincerely, keep covenants, and live the gospel to the best of our ability. Grace then covers the gap between our sincere best and what God requires for exaltation. President Dieter F. Uchtdorf taught that the Atonement both enables our righteous efforts and completes what we cannot finish on our own. Grace is not a last resort after effort is spent — it is the enabling power behind every righteous act we perform and the completing power that brings us home.

Ordinances are not works-merit — they are acts of faith. Baptism, confirmation, temple covenants — these are not payments for salvation. They are the channel through which grace operates in the life of a covenant person. God designates the ordinances. We participate in them in faith. The power is entirely His. Moroni 10:32–33

Where LDS and Protestant theology genuinely differ. Most Protestant traditions, following Luther, teach that faith alone saves — and that works are evidence of saving faith, not contributors to it. Latter-day Saint theology teaches that genuine faith always produces action, and that specific covenants and ordinances are required because God has prescribed them — not because the human effort merits anything. The difference is not grace vs. works. It is how and through what channels grace operates.

Paul and James are not contradicting each other. Paul writes that Abraham was "justified by faith" Romans 4:3 — not by circumcision or law. James writes that Abraham was "justified by works" James 2:21 — by the act of offering Isaac. Both are correct. Paul addresses how God sees us — through faith in Christ. James addresses how faith proves itself real — through action. Dead faith saves no one. James 2:17

missionary.simple: Grace is the foundation — no one is saved without the Atonement of Jesus Christ. Ordinances and covenants are the channel through which grace operates in a covenant person's life, not payments for salvation. The difference from Protestantism is not grace versus works — it is how grace is applied and through what covenant channels it flows.
missionary.explain: 'A Protestant will say saved by grace, not works. An LDS member agrees completely. The question is not whether grace saves — it absolutely does. The question is what God has asked us to do in response to that grace. Covenants and ordinances are faith made visible, not merit accumulated.'
missionary.why: Understanding grace correctly removes two equal and opposite errors: the pride of thinking you earned salvation, and the passivity of thinking nothing is required of you. The covenant path threads between both.
missionary.invite: Would you like to study what the Atonement of Jesus Christ actually covers — and how the ordinances of the restored gospel are the channel through which that infinite grace becomes personal for you?

questions.question: 5. Does the Bible actually teach that faith alone saves?
questions.answer:
The phrase "faith alone" — in Latin, sola fide — was the theological cornerstone of Luther's Reformation. It was his answer to a Catholic system that had, in his view, made salvation dependent on human merit, indulgences, and priestly mediation. His instinct was right. Salvation is not earned. But the biblical picture is more layered than either extreme suggests.

The only time "faith alone" appears in the Bible. The phrase "faith alone" appears exactly once in the entire New Testament — in James 2:24: "Ye see then how that by works a man is justified, and not by faith only." James 2:24 James says explicitly that a man is NOT justified by faith alone. This is so uncomfortable for Lutheran theology that Luther famously called James "an epistle of straw" and wanted to remove it from the canon. He did not remove it, but his discomfort reveals the tension.

What Paul actually argues. Paul's argument in Romans and Galatians is not that works are irrelevant to salvation — it is that the works of the law of Moses (circumcision, dietary laws, ritual observances) do not justify a Gentile before God. His contrast is between trust in Christ and trust in ethnic/ceremonial identity. Romans 3:28 He is not saying that covenant action is unnecessary. He is saying that checking the boxes of Mosaic law does not save anyone.

Faith that saves is always active. Abraham "believed God, and it was counted unto him for righteousness" Romans 4:3 — but that belief moved him to leave Ur, to receive circumcision, and ultimately to offer Isaac. His faith was never passive. James makes this explicit: "Even so faith, if it hath not works, is dead, being alone." James 2:17 A faith that changes nothing about how you live is not saving faith. It is intellectual agreement dressed as belief.

The LDS position. Faith is the first principle of the gospel — foundational, primary, and essential. But genuine faith in Christ leads naturally to repentance, then to baptism, then to receiving the Holy Ghost, then to covenant discipleship throughout life. These are not earned merits — they are the natural movement of a soul that has genuinely encountered Christ and trusted Him enough to obey. 3 Nephi 27:20

Without a living prophet to settle this debate by revelation, Protestantism has produced thousands of competing answers to the question of what faith alone means. The Restoration provides the clarity no council could supply.

missionary.simple: The only time "faith alone" appears in the Bible is in James 2:24 — where James says salvation is NOT by faith alone. Paul's argument was against Mosaic law-keeping, not against covenant action. Genuine faith is always active — it produces repentance, baptism, and discipleship.
missionary.explain: 'Luther wanted to remove James from the Bible because it directly contradicts his theology. He kept it — which means every Bible printed since the Reformation contains the verse that says salvation is not by faith alone. That tension has never been resolved without a living prophet. The Restoration resolves it.'
missionary.why: The faith-alone debate reveals exactly what happens when sincere, intelligent people read the same Bible without prophetic authority to clarify doctrine. Thousands of denominations, all claiming scripture, all reaching different conclusions. That is the Apostasy made visible.
missionary.invite: Would you be willing to read James 2 and Romans 4 side by side — and then ask God through prayer what He actually requires of someone who genuinely believes in Jesus Christ?

questions.question: 6. How does The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints compare to the Jehovah's Witnesses?
questions.answer:
Both the Jehovah's Witnesses and The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints are sometimes grouped together as non-traditional Christian movements. Beyond a few surface similarities — both reject the Trinity, both emphasize literal Bible reading, both engage in door-to-door outreach — the two traditions are doctrinally quite different.

Origins and authority. The Jehovah's Witnesses were founded in the 1870s by Charles Taze Russell, a Pittsburgh haberdasher who reinterpreted the Bible independently. His successor Joseph Rutherford shaped the organisation significantly. No claim is made to divine authority from heaven — the Governing Body interprets scripture as a body of sincere students. The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints was founded by Joseph Smith through what he described as direct visitations from God the Father, Jesus Christ, the angel Moroni, John the Baptist, and Peter, James and John. The claim is literal restoration of heavenly authority, not human reinterpretation.

The nature of Christ. Jehovah's Witnesses teach that Jesus is a created being — the first of God's creations, subordinate to Jehovah, not co-equal and not God. They translate John 1:1 as "the Word was a god" — lowercase. Latter-day Saint theology teaches that Jesus Christ is the divine Son of God — separate from the Father but fully divine, the Jehovah of the Old Testament, the Creator of the world under the Father's direction. Moses 1:33 He is not a created being. He is the Firstborn in the spirit and the Only Begotten in the flesh.

The afterlife. Jehovah's Witnesses teach that only 144,000 go to heaven; the rest of the faithful will live on a restored paradise earth. Latter-day Saint theology teaches three degrees of glory — celestial, terrestrial, and telestial — with the celestial kingdom being life with God and the eternal family. 1 Corinthians 15:40–42 Proxy temple ordinances extend blessings to those who died without the gospel — a doctrine with no parallel in Witness theology.

Blood transfusions and flag saluting. Witnesses prohibit blood transfusions based on Acts 15:29 and abstain from any military involvement or national allegiance. Latter-day Saints have no such prohibitions — members serve in military service and participate in civic life.
