The Holy Ghost. Witnesses teach that the Holy Spirit is an active force — not a person. Latter-day Saint theology teaches that the Holy Ghost is a distinct personage of Spirit — the third member of the Godhead, a real being with personality and will. D&C 130:22

missionary.simple: Jehovah's Witnesses were founded by Charles Taze Russell in the 1870s through biblical reinterpretation — no claim of divine authority. Latter-day Saints claim literal restoration of heavenly authority through Joseph Smith. Both reject the Trinity, but for different reasons and with different conclusions about who Christ actually is.
missionary.explain: 'Witnesses say Jesus is the first created being — a god but not God. We say He is the divine Son — the Jehovah of the Old Testament, the Creator of the world, fully divine and separate from the Father. The difference in how you understand Christ changes everything about how you understand the gospel.'
missionary.why: Understanding the distinction between the Jehovah's Witnesses and the restored church matters because both claim to restore original Christianity. The difference is in how they received their authority — by human reinterpretation or by literal divine commission.
missionary.invite: Would you like to understand what the restored gospel teaches about the true nature of Jesus Christ — who He is, what He did, and what He means for your eternal future?

questions.question: 7. How do Islam and the restored gospel of Jesus Christ compare?
questions.answer:
Islam and the restored gospel of Jesus Christ are both Abrahamic faiths that trace their heritage to Abraham, the great patriarch of faith. Both are monotheistic — believing in one God. Both take their prophets seriously as real human beings with genuine divine commissions. Both require prayer, fasting, and charitable giving as core religious practice. And both explicitly reject the Trinitarian formulation of God that mainstream Christianity adopted in the 4th century. That much they share.

Where they diverge. The foundational difference is the identity and mission of Jesus Christ. Islam honors Jesus — known as Isa — as one of the greatest prophets and as the Messiah. But Islam teaches that Jesus was not crucified (the Quran states that it only appeared so), that He is not the Son of God in a literal sense, and that He is not divine. Muhammad, not Jesus, is the final and greatest prophet in the Islamic tradition, and the Quran is the final and complete revelation from God.

The restored gospel teaches that Jesus Christ is literally the Son of God — divine, resurrected, and the only name by which salvation comes. Acts 4:12 His Atonement, death, and literal resurrection are not symbolic events — they are the central facts of human history upon which every person's eternal destiny rests. Joseph Smith was a prophet in the Abrahamic tradition — but not the final prophet, and not one who supersedes Christ. Christ is not one prophet among many. He is the Lord of all.

Additional revelation vs. supersession. Islam teaches that the Bible was corrupted over time and that the Quran corrects and completes it. The restored gospel also acknowledges that the Bible has imperfections in transmission — the 8th Article of Faith states it is the word of God "as far as it is translated correctly." But rather than replacing the Bible, the restoration adds additional witnesses — the Book of Mormon, the Doctrine and Covenants — that testify alongside the Bible of the same Jesus Christ. The Quran points away from Christ. Additional LDS scripture points toward Him.

Prayer and practice. Islam requires the five pillars: declaration of faith, five daily prayers (Salat), fasting during Ramadan, charitable giving (Zakat), and pilgrimage to Mecca (Hajj) once in a lifetime. Latter-day Saints pray daily, fast monthly, pay tithing, and attend the temple — a different form of sacred practice but recognisably Abrahamic in spirit.

Both traditions believe in the same God of Abraham. The conversation between them ultimately returns to one question: who is Jesus Christ? That question alone separates the two traditions at the deepest level.

missionary.simple: Both Islam and the restored gospel are Abrahamic, monotheistic, and prophetic traditions that take prayer, fasting, and scripture seriously. The foundational difference is Jesus Christ — Islam honours Him as a prophet; the restored gospel teaches He is the divine Son of God whose Atonement and resurrection are the centre of all human history.
missionary.explain: 'A Muslim who sincerely follows God will recognise something familiar in Latter-day Saint theology — the prophetic call, the additional scripture, the rejection of the Trinity, the emphasis on prayer and fasting. The conversation between us returns to one question: who is Jesus Christ? On that question everything else turns.'
missionary.why: Understanding where Islam and the restored gospel agree and diverge matters because billions of sincere people are searching for God through Islam. The restored gospel does not dismiss their sincerity — it invites them to know the fullness of what God has revealed about His Son.
missionary.invite: If you have Muslim friends or colleagues, would you like to understand how to speak respectfully about the restored gospel — honouring what they believe while sharing what you know about Jesus Christ?

questions.question: 1. Did James and Paul contradict each other on faith and works?
questions.answer:
This is one of the oldest and most debated questions in Christian theology. Martin Luther called the book of James "an epistle of straw" and seriously considered removing it from the Protestant Bible — precisely because he believed it contradicted Paul's teaching on salvation by faith. He kept it, but the tension he identified is real and worth addressing directly.

What Paul says. Paul writes in Romans 3:28: "Therefore we conclude that a man is justified by faith without the deeds of the law." Romans 3:28 And in Galatians 2:16 he states that no one is justified by the works of the law but by faith in Jesus Christ. This sounds like a clear statement that works are irrelevant to salvation.

What James says. James writes in James 2:24: "Ye see then how that by works a man is justified, and not by faith only." James 2:24 And in James 2:17: "Even so faith, if it hath not works, is dead, being alone." James 2:17 This sounds like the direct opposite of Paul.

The resolution — they are answering different questions. Paul is writing to Jewish and Gentile Christians who believed that circumcision and adherence to the Mosaic law were required for salvation. His argument is that keeping the ritual laws of Moses does not make anyone righteous before God — only faith in Jesus Christ does. He is arguing against legal merit-making.

James is writing to Christians who have taken Paul's message too far — who believe that intellectual agreement with the gospel is sufficient, that they can believe in Jesus without any corresponding change in life. His argument is that genuine faith always produces action. A faith that changes nothing about how you live is not saving faith — it is empty intellectual assent. He uses Abraham as his example — Abraham's faith expressed itself in the willingness to offer Isaac. That act did not earn his salvation. It proved his faith was real.

Both are right. Paul and James are addressing opposite errors in opposite directions from the same truth. Paul corrects the error of thinking works of law earn salvation. James corrects the error of thinking passive belief is sufficient faith. Together they describe what genuine saving faith looks like: it is not earned by ritual performance, and it is not satisfied by passive belief. It is alive, active, and expressed in life.

The Latter-day Saint framework holds both together naturally. Grace saves — fully, completely, and primarily. And genuine faith in Christ produces repentance, covenant, and discipleship. The ordinances are not payments. They are faith made visible. 2 Nephi 25:23

missionary.simple: Paul and James are not contradicting — they are correcting opposite errors. Paul corrects the idea that ritual law-keeping earns salvation. James corrects the idea that passive intellectual belief is saving faith. Together they describe genuine faith: it is not earned by ritual, and it is not satisfied by belief alone. It lives and moves.
missionary.explain: 'Luther wanted to remove James because it said faith alone doesn't save. He kept it — which means every Protestant Bible contains the verse that directly challenges his core doctrine. The tension is not in the Bible. It is in the absence of a living prophet to clarify what both men were actually arguing.'
missionary.why: Understanding the James-Paul debate reveals why a living prophet matters. Without authoritative interpretation, sincere people read the same text in opposite directions. The Restoration provides the clarity that councils, reformers, and theologians have debated for 500 years.
missionary.invite: Would you like to read James 2 and Romans 3–4 together and ask God what genuine saving faith actually looks like in a person's life?

questions.question: 2. What does the book of Revelation actually mean?
questions.answer:
The book of Revelation — also called the Apocalypse of John — is simultaneously the most quoted and most misunderstood book in the Bible. It has fuelled centuries of end-times predictions, elaborate charts, and conflicting interpretations. Understanding what it is, who wrote it, and why helps the reader approach it with both reverence and clarity.

What kind of literature is it? Revelation belongs to a genre called apocalyptic literature — a form of writing common in Jewish tradition that uses symbolic imagery, visions, numbers, and coded language to communicate spiritual truth. Daniel, Ezekiel, and Zechariah use the same techniques. The imagery is intentionally symbolic. A seven-headed beast is not meant to be taken as a biological description — it is a code for Roman imperial power threatening the early church. This was deliberate — writing in code protected Christians from Roman persecution by making the text opaque to Roman officials while clear to believers familiar with the symbolism.

Key symbols and their meaning. The number seven represents completeness or perfection throughout Revelation — seven churches, seven seals, seven trumpets. Twelve represents the tribes of Israel or the apostles. The 144,000 is twelve times twelve times one thousand — a symbolic number representing the fulness of God's people, not a literal headcount. The harlot of Babylon represents corrupt political and religious power. The dragon is Satan. The beast from the sea represents Rome. The Lamb is Jesus Christ.

The central message. Beneath all the imagery, Revelation carries a single, clear, sustaining message: Christ will ultimately triumph over every earthly power that opposes Him. Revelation 19:11–16 Written to Christians facing Roman persecution, it promises that their suffering is not the end of the story. The kingdoms of this world will become the kingdoms of Christ. Revelation 11:15 That message has not changed and does not require a decoded timeline to be received.

The LDS advantage. Joseph Smith asked God about key passages in Revelation, and the Lord provided interpretation directly. Doctrine and Covenants 77 gives a question-and-answer key to several of Revelation's most debated passages — including the identity of the four beasts D&C 77:2–3 and the meaning of the sealed book. D&C 77:6 This is not speculation. It is revelation. The restored gospel has resources for understanding Revelation that no other tradition can offer.

missionary.simple: Revelation is apocalyptic literature — intentionally symbolic, written in code to protect early Christians. The central message is simple: Christ will triumph over every opposing power. The numbers, beasts, and imagery are symbols, not literal descriptions. D&C 77 provides a prophetically revealed key to several of its most debated passages.
missionary.explain: 'The beast with seven heads and ten horns is a code for Rome — the dominant world power persecuting Christians when John wrote. Every Christian reading it then understood immediately. We have lost that cultural context. The book was not written to confuse. It was written to protect and to promise — and the promise is that Christ wins.'
missionary.why: Understanding Revelation correctly removes the anxiety that end-times speculation produces and restores its original purpose: comfort for people under pressure, certainty that God is in control, and confidence that Christ's ultimate victory is guaranteed.
missionary.invite: Would you like to study Doctrine and Covenants 77 alongside the corresponding passages in Revelation and see how a living prophet's revealed interpretation illuminates what scholars have debated for centuries?

questions.question: 3. Why does the Old Testament show God commanding violence?
questions.answer:
This is one of the most honest and difficult questions a thoughtful reader of the Bible can ask. The conquest of Canaan, the flood, the destruction of entire peoples — these events disturb modern readers and are frequently used by critics as evidence against the moral character of God. The question deserves a serious, honest answer.

Context matters enormously. The ancient Near East in which these events occurred operated by entirely different norms. Warfare, conquest, and the displacement of peoples were standard features of ancient life across every culture. The question is not whether God's people used violence — every nation did. The question is whether God's interaction with Israel reflects a coherent moral purpose or simply tribal bloodlust given divine sanction.

The trajectory of biblical law is toward dignity. Even within the Mosaic legal system — which modern readers find harsh — several features were radically unusual for the ancient world. The command to love the stranger as oneself appeared in Leviticus. Leviticus 19:34 The Year of Jubilee released debts and returned land. Leviticus 25:10 Protection was granted to servants in ways that had no parallel in surrounding cultures. The arc of biblical law moves consistently toward increased human dignity — not away from it.

The Latter-day Saint framework. The 8th Article of Faith acknowledges that the Bible is the word of God "as far as it is translated correctly" — and this applies equally to cultural transmission over time. God's revealed will unfolds progressively as human capacity to receive it grows. The Joseph Smith Translation restores or clarifies many difficult Old Testament passages, and the 8th Article of Faith acknowledges that imperfect human transmission across centuries can affect how we read scripture. What is consistent across all scripture is that God's ultimate character is revealed most fully in Jesus Christ — and no Old Testament passage should be read in a way that contradicts the mercy, love, and justice that Christ embodies.

Christ as the full revelation. The clearest picture of God's character is not found in Joshua's campaigns. It is found in Jesus Christ — who healed lepers, forgave adulterers, blessed children, and died for those who crucified Him. Hebrews 1:1–3 Christ is the fullest revelation of who God actually is. Any reading of the Old Testament that contradicts the character of Christ revealed in the Gospels must be held lightly. God has not changed. Our understanding of Him deepens across time.

missionary.simple: The Old Testament was written in an ancient world where violence was the norm across every culture. The trajectory of biblical law moves toward dignity, not away from it. The fullest revelation of God's character is Jesus Christ — whose love, mercy, and sacrifice define who God actually is. Difficult passages are held in that light.
missionary.explain: 'Christ told His disciples to love their enemies. That is not the command of a tribal war-god. That is the full revelation of who God is. When you read the Old Testament through the lens of Christ — not the other way around — the trajectory becomes clear. God did not change. Our capacity to understand Him deepened.'
missionary.why: Taking this question seriously — rather than dismissing it — shows that the restored gospel can hold difficult questions with intellectual honesty. The 8th Article of Faith gives Latter-day Saints permission to acknowledge complexity in biblical texts without their faith collapsing.
missionary.invite: Would you like to understand how the LDS 8th Article of Faith gives a principled, honest framework for approaching difficult Old Testament passages — one that neither dismisses the Bible nor pretends the hard questions don't exist?

questions.question: 4. How do you answer the Bible contradictions that skeptics raise?
questions.answer:
Skeptics who raise Bible contradictions are often making a legitimate observation — different passages, different accounts, and different numbers appear throughout the text. The honest response is not to pretend these differences do not exist. It is to understand what they are, where they come from, and what they actually mean for the reliability of the Bible's central claims.

Most apparent contradictions are differences in emphasis, not errors. The four Gospels record the resurrection differently. Matthew says one angel was at the tomb. John says two. Mark says a young man in white. Luke says two men. Skeptics call this a contradiction. Historians call it corroborating eyewitness testimony. Multiple accounts of the same event that emphasise different details and mention different witnesses are exactly what we expect from genuine eyewitness reporting — not fabricated narratives, which tend to be suspiciously consistent. No reasonable court dismisses eyewitness testimony because three witnesses noticed different things.

Transmission introduces variation. The Bible was copied by hand, across multiple languages, over fifteen hundred years, by thousands of scribes with varying accuracy and occasional theological motivations. Textual scholars — most of whom are not hostile to Christianity — acknowledge that the manuscript tradition contains variants. The Latter-day Saint 8th Article of Faith addresses this honestly: the Bible is the word of God "as far as it is translated correctly." Articles of Faith 1:8 This is not a dismissal of the Bible. It is an honest acknowledgment that inspired originals passed through imperfect human hands.

The core claims are remarkably consistent. Across hundreds of manuscripts, spanning centuries, the central testimony of the New Testament does not waver: Jesus of Nazareth was crucified under Pontius Pilate, was buried, and rose from the dead on the third day. 1 Corinthians 15:3–8 The names, the dates, the locations, the witnesses — these details are consistent across every major manuscript tradition. The variations that exist are peripheral, not foundational.

The Restoration as the answer. The existence of textual variation is precisely why additional scripture and living prophets matter. The Book of Mormon serves as a second witness to the core truths of the Bible. Where the Bible has been obscured, additional revelation clarifies. 1 Nephi 13:40 This is not a criticism of the Bible — it is the provision God made for its inevitable human transmission.

missionary.simple: Most apparent Bible contradictions are differences in emphasis between genuine eyewitness accounts — exactly what historians expect from real testimony. The 8th Article of Faith honestly acknowledges transmission imperfections. The core claims — crucifixion, burial, resurrection — are consistent across every major manuscript. The Restoration provides additional witnesses precisely because the Bible's transmission is imperfect.
missionary.scripture: 1 Corinthians 15:3–8 | Articles of Faith 1:8 | 1 Nephi 13:40 | 2 Peter 1:21
missionary.explain: 'If four people witness the same car accident and then describe it, their accounts will differ in detail. One notices the colour of the car. Another notices the number of passengers. A skeptic could call those accounts contradictory. A historian calls them corroborating. The gospel accounts work the same way.'
missionary.why: Taking Bible contradictions seriously — rather than dismissing them — demonstrates intellectual honesty. And the honest answer is that the Restoration anticipated exactly this problem and provided its solution: living prophets, additional scripture, and direct revelation.
missionary.invite: Would you be willing to study the 8th Article of Faith and the role of the Book of Mormon as a second witness — and ask God whether He provided additional scripture precisely because He knew the Bible would face these challenges?

questions.question: 5. Why does the Bible appear to permit slavery?
questions.answer:
This is one of the most difficult questions the Bible raises for modern readers — and one that has been used to justify enormous evil in human history. The honest answer requires both historical honesty and theological clarity.

God did not invent slavery — He regulated an existing institution. Slavery existed throughout the ancient Near East millennia before the Mosaic law was given. When God gave Israel the law at Sinai, He did not introduce slavery. He inherited it as an existing social reality and began regulating it in ways that were, by the standards of the ancient world, remarkably more humane than anything in surrounding cultures. Exodus 21 — often cited as evidence of God endorsing slavery — actually contains protections with no parallel anywhere in the ancient Near East: a Hebrew slave was to be released after six years; a slave who was permanently injured by a master was to be freed immediately; killing a slave carried penalties. These were radical restrictions by ancient standards, not endorsements.

The trajectory moves toward freedom. The biblical narrative moves consistently toward liberation — from the Exodus itself (the paradigmatic act of God freeing enslaved people) Exodus 20:2 to the Year of Jubilee, which freed all bonded servants every fifty years Leviticus 25:10, to Paul's letter to Philemon — a masterpiece of implicit abolitionism in which Paul returns a runaway slave to his Christian master with a barely concealed appeal to free him. The New Testament contains no command to keep slaves. It contains the command to love every person as made in the image of God. Galatians 3:28

Christ's declaration. When Christ began His ministry He read from Isaiah: "The Spirit of the Lord is upon me... to set at liberty them that are bruised." Luke 4:18 That is not the language of an institution endorser. It is the language of liberation.

The LDS framework. The Doctrine and Covenants 101:79 teaches that God established the Constitution to protect the moral agency of every person — that all may act according to God-given agency and moral principle. Latter-day Saint theology explicitly affirms the dignity and eternal worth of every human soul regardless of race, nationality, or background. D&C 18:10 The history of human slavery is a tragedy that the restored gospel — with its emphasis on agency, eternal worth of souls, and continuing revelation — is equipped to address honestly and without defensiveness.

missionary.simple: God did not create slavery — He regulated an existing institution with protections that were radically humane by ancient standards. The biblical trajectory moves toward liberation — from the Exodus to Jubilee to Paul's letter to Philemon to Christ's declaration that He came to set the captive free. The restored gospel explicitly affirms the eternal worth and equality of every human soul.
missionary.explain: 'The Exodus is the centre of the Old Testament — God freeing enslaved people with a mighty hand. That is who God is. Every regulation of slavery in the Mosaic law must be read in the light of a God whose most defining act was liberation. He regulated what He could not yet eliminate from human culture. The trajectory of everything He revealed moved toward freedom.'
missionary.why: Taking this question seriously demonstrates that the restored gospel can engage honestly with the hardest questions in human history — without dismissing them and without losing faith. A God who works progressively within human culture is a God whose continuing revelation makes sense.
missionary.invite: Would you like to understand how the principle of continuing revelation in the restored gospel gives a framework for understanding why God works progressively in human history rather than imposing a perfect social order all at once?

questions.question: 6. What happened to Bible verses that appear in some translations but not others?
questions.answer:
Many readers encounter a footnote in a modern Bible that reads "the earliest manuscripts do not include these verses" — and it raises a legitimate question. Understanding what happened is both honest and, from a Latter-day Saint perspective, expected. Nephi prophesied it centuries before it occurred. 1 Nephi 13:26–29 Here are the most significant passages that were either added by later scribes or are missing from the oldest manuscripts.

Passages added that do not appear in the earliest manuscripts:

1. Mark 16:9–20 — The longer ending of Mark. The Gospel of Mark in the oldest Greek manuscripts — Codex Sinaiticus and Codex Vaticanus, both dating to the 4th century — ends abruptly at verse 8: "for they were afraid." Verses 9 through 20, which include Christ’s post-resurrection appearances, the Great Commission, and the promise that believers would speak in new tongues and handle serpents, are absent. Most modern Bible footnotes acknowledge this directly. These 12 verses were added by a later scribe to provide a more complete ending to the Gospel.

2. John 7:53–8:11 — The woman taken in adultery. This beloved story — where Christ says "He that is without sin among you, let him first cast a stone" — does not appear in the oldest Greek manuscripts of John. It is absent from Papyrus 66, Papyrus 75, Codex Sinaiticus, and Codex Vaticanus. When it does appear in later manuscripts, it is sometimes placed after Luke 21:38, not in John at all. Most modern Bible footnotes flag it as not in the earliest manuscripts. The story may preserve a genuine tradition about Jesus, but it was not part of the original Gospel of John as written.

3. 1 John 5:7–8 — The Johannine Comma (the Trinitarian proof text). The King James Bible reads: "For there are three that bear record in heaven, the Father, the Word, and the Holy Ghost: and these three are one. And there are three that bear witness in earth, the Spirit, and the water, and the blood." The italicised Trinitarian phrase — "the Father, the Word, and the Holy Ghost: and these three are one" — appears in no Greek manuscript before the 14th century. It is absent from all early manuscripts, all early church fathers’ writings, and all early translations. It was inserted into the Latin text to support Trinitarian doctrine and eventually found its way into the Greek tradition and then the KJV. Modern translations omit it entirely.

4. Luke 22:43–44 — The angel strengthening Christ in Gethsemane. The passage where an angel appears to strengthen Jesus, and where his sweat is described as "great drops of blood," is absent from several important early manuscripts, including Papyrus 75 and Codex Vaticanus. Other manuscripts include it. Scholars believe it may have been removed by scribes who were uncomfortable with the image of Jesus needing angelic help — which seemed to them to diminish His divinity. It was later restored in many manuscript traditions. The Joseph Smith Translation confirms its authenticity.

5. Acts 8:37 — Philip’s baptismal confession. In the story of Philip and the Ethiopian eunuch, the KJV includes verse 37: "And Philip said, If thou believest with all thine heart, thou mayest. And he answered and said, I believe that Jesus Christ is the Son of God." This entire verse is missing from the oldest Greek manuscripts. It does not appear in Codex Sinaiticus, Codex Vaticanus, or most early manuscripts. It was added to provide an explicit confession of faith before baptism — likely to support the practice of requiring a creedal statement before the ordinance. Modern Bibles either omit it entirely or place it in a footnote.

6. Matthew 17:21 and Mark 9:29 — Prayer and fasting. The KJV Matthew 17:21 reads: "Howbeit this kind goeth not out but by prayer and fasting." This verse is entirely absent from the oldest manuscripts and most modern Bibles omit it or relegate it to a footnote. Mark 9:29 in the KJV reads "prayer and fasting" — but the oldest manuscripts read only "prayer." The word "fasting" was added to both passages, likely by scribes who wanted to emphasise the practice.

Why this happened. The New Testament was hand-copied by scribes for over a thousand years before the printing press. Some scribes made honest errors. Some made deliberate additions — harmonising one Gospel with another, inserting marginal notes into the text, or adding theologically motivated clarifications. None of this was deceptive by intent. It was the inevitable result of human transmission across centuries.

The LDS framework. The 8th Article of Faith states: "We believe the Bible to be the word of God as far as it is translated correctly." Articles of Faith 1:8 This is not a dismissal of the Bible. It is an honest acknowledgment that God’s inspired word passed through imperfect human hands. The Joseph Smith Translation restores or clarifies dozens of passages. 1 Nephi 13:40 And the Book of Mormon stands as a second witness alongside the Bible, testifying of the same Jesus Christ whose words and works need no scribe’s addition to be true.

missionary.simple: Six key passages are missing from the oldest Bible manuscripts or were added by later scribes: Mark 16:9–20 (the longer ending, absent from the oldest Greek manuscripts), John 7:53–8:11 (the woman taken in adultery, absent from early manuscripts), 1 John 5:7–8 (the Trinitarian Comma, absent from all Greek manuscripts before the 14th century), Luke 22:43–44 (the angel in Gethsemane, absent from several key manuscripts), Acts 8:37 (Philip’s baptismal confession, absent from oldest manuscripts), and Matthew 17:21 (prayer and fasting, absent from oldest manuscripts). Nephi prophesied this would happen. The 8th Article of Faith acknowledges it honestly. The Book of Mormon was given in part to restore what was lost.
missionary.scripture: Articles of Faith 1:8 | 1 Nephi 13:26–29 | 1 Nephi 13:40 | 2 Nephi 29:6
missionary.explain: 'Nephi saw in a vision, 600 years before Christ, that plain and precious things would be taken from the Bible before it reached the nations. Modern textual scholarship has confirmed exactly that — passages were added, removed, and altered across centuries of hand-copying. This is not a crisis of faith. It is a prophecy fulfilled and a reason why additional scripture matters.'
missionary.why: The existence of textual variants in the Bible is precisely why God prepared additional witnesses — the Book of Mormon, the Joseph Smith Translation, living prophets. The Restoration anticipated the problem of biblical transmission and provided its solution.
missionary.invite: Would you like to read 1 Nephi 13 — where Nephi saw exactly what would happen to the Bible centuries before it happened — and consider what it means that the restoration of plain and precious truths was prophesied long before Joseph Smith was born?

questions.question: 7. Are there errors in the Bible, and does that undermine Christian faith?
questions.answer:
The honest answer is yes — the Bible as we have it today contains documented variations, manuscript inconsistencies, and copyist alterations accumulated over fifteen centuries of hand-transmission. The question is not whether these exist — they do, and textual scholars of every faith background confirm it. The question is whether they undermine the foundational claims of Christianity — and the answer to that is no. Here are five well-documented examples that any honest Bible student should know.

1. Two different accounts of Judas’s death. Matthew 27:5 says Judas “went and hanged himself.” Acts 1:18 says he “falling headlong, he burst asunder in the midst, and all his bowels gushed out.” Same event, same person, two completely different accounts of how he died. One says he hanged himself. The other says he fell and his body burst open. These cannot both be a literal description of the same death. This is a factual contradiction between two New Testament books.

2. Different numbers for the same census. 2 Samuel 24:9 records that Joab counted 800,000 fighting men in Israel and 500,000 in Judah. The parallel account in 1 Chronicles 21:5 gives different numbers — 1,100,000 in Israel and 470,000 in Judah. These are the same census, the same time, the same king. The numbers disagree by hundreds of thousands. One account has been transmitted with different figures from the other.

3. Who moved David to number Israel — God or Satan? 2 Samuel 24:1 says: “the anger of the Lord was kindled against Israel, and he moved David against them to say, Go, number Israel.” 1 Chronicles 21:1 says: “Satan stood up against Israel, and provoked David to number Israel.” One text says God moved David. The other says Satan did. This is a direct theological contradiction on the same event.

4. The age of King Ahaziah. 2 Kings 8:26 states that Ahaziah was 22 years old when he began to reign. 2 Chronicles 22:2 states he was 42 years old. The same king, the same beginning of his reign, two books give ages that differ by 20 years. At 42, he would have been older than his father Jehoram. This is a clear copyist error that entered the manuscript tradition.

5. Peter’s denial — before one or two cock crows? Mark 14:30 records Jesus saying Peter would deny Him “before the cock crow twice.” Matthew 26:34, Luke 22:34, and John 13:38 all record Jesus saying Peter would deny Him “before the cock crow” — just once, with no mention of twice. Three Gospels say once. Mark says twice. This is a small but documented textual discrepancy across four parallel Gospel accounts of the exact same moment.

What these examples mean. These five examples confirm exactly what the Latter-day Saint 8th Article of Faith states: the Bible is the word of God “as far as it is translated correctly.” Articles of Faith 1:8 Inspired originals passed through imperfect human hands across fifteen centuries. Numbers were miscopied. Parallel accounts diverged. A scribe unfamiliar with Hebrew idiom substituted a wrong name or number. None of these errors touch the core testimony of the New Testament — that Jesus Christ was crucified, buried, and rose from the dead. 1 Corinthians 15:3–8 That testimony is consistent across every major manuscript tradition.

The Latter-day Saint framework. Rather than requiring Latter-day Saints to defend a claim of perfect biblical preservation that textual scholarship cannot support, the 8th Article of Faith gives a principled, honest foundation. God inspired the originals. Humans transmitted them imperfectly. And God prepared additional scripture — the Book of Mormon, the Joseph Smith Translation, living prophets — precisely because He knew imperfect transmission would happen. 1 Nephi 13:28–29 The Apostasy produced textual drift. The Restoration corrects it. The 8th Article of Faith is not a concession — it is a strength.

missionary.simple: Yes, the Bible contains documented transmission errors — including contradictions between parallel accounts (Judas’s death, David’s census numbers, who moved David to number Israel, Ahaziah’s age, the cock crow count). These are factual discrepancies between books of the Bible itself. They do not destroy faith — the core testimony of Christ’s death and resurrection is consistent across every manuscript. The 8th Article of Faith gives Latter-day Saints a principled, honest framework: the Bible is the word of God as far as it is translated correctly.
missionary.scripture: 1 Corinthians 15:3–8 | Articles of Faith 1:8 | 2 Peter 1:20–21 | 2 Nephi 29:6–7
missionary.explain: 'The 8th Article of Faith does not say the Bible is wrong. It says the Bible was transmitted by imperfect human hands across fifteen centuries — which is historically accurate and intellectually honest. Rather than forcing members to defend a claim of perfect preservation that scholarship cannot support, it gives a principled framework: God inspired the originals, humans transmitted them imperfectly, and God provided additional witnesses and living prophets to preserve the fulness of truth.'
missionary.why: An approach to the Bible that requires ignoring textual scholarship is fragile — it collapses the moment a member encounters that scholarship. The LDS framework is robust: it acknowledges the reality, explains why it happened, and shows how God prepared for it in advance.
missionary.invite: Would you like to study what the Joseph Smith Translation adds or clarifies in key passages — and see how a living prophet's inspired revision of scripture gives us a clearer window into what was originally revealed?

questions.question: 8. What is the unforgivable sin, and how do you know if you have committed it?
questions.answer:
Few passages in the Gospels cause more anxiety than Matthew 12:31–32, where Christ says: "Wherefore I say unto you, All manner of sin and blasphemy shall be forgiven unto men: but the blasphemy against the Holy Ghost shall not be forgiven unto men. And whosoever speaketh a word against the Son of Man, it shall be forgiven him: but whosoever speaketh against the Holy Ghost, it shall not be forgiven him, neither in this world, neither in the world to come." Matthew 12:31–32 This has caused genuine spiritual anguish for millions of sincere people who worry they may have crossed an irreversible line.

What this sin actually is. Latter-day Saint theology provides the clearest and most doctrinally grounded answer available. Doctrine and Covenants 76:31–35 describes those who commit the unpardonable sin as people who had received a perfect, personal knowledge of God — who had experienced divine witness at the highest level — and then deliberately, willfully, and completely denied that knowledge. They are described as having been "of the Church of the Firstborn," having "known God's power," and having "suffered themselves through the power of the devil to be overcome." D&C 76:31

This is not an ordinary sin. It is not blasphemous speech in anger. It is not doubt. It is not apostasy from the Church. It is not even persistent, repeated sin. It requires a level of personal spiritual experience — a direct, undeniable, perfect knowledge of divine reality — that places a person beyond the reach of normal human ignorance or weakness. Most people, including most members of the Church, will never be in a position to commit this sin. They have not received sufficient divine knowledge to make such a deliberate, knowing rejection possible.

How do you know you have not committed it? The very fact that a person worries about having committed the unforgivable sin is strong evidence that they have not. A person who has truly committed it has, by definition, rejected the witness of the Holy Ghost so completely that spiritual concern about God is no longer possible for them. The anxiety you feel about your standing before God is itself the Holy Ghost still working with you. That is not the condition of someone past feeling.

The Atonement of Jesus Christ is broader, deeper, and more merciful than most people realise. It covers every sin committed in weakness, ignorance, or partial understanding. Moroni 10:32 Come to Christ. He turns no one away who genuinely comes.

missionary.simple: The unforgivable sin requires a direct, undeniable, perfect personal knowledge of God — followed by deliberate, complete rejection of that knowledge. It is not ordinary sin, doubt, blasphemous speech, or apostasy. The fact that a person worries about having committed it is strong evidence they have not — a person who has truly committed it is beyond spiritual concern.
missionary.explain: 'D&C 76 says those who commit the unpardonable sin had a perfect knowledge of God and then chose deliberately to deny it. Most people have never had that level of divine knowledge. The very worry about this sin — the fear that you've crossed a line — is the Holy Ghost still reaching you. That is the opposite of the condition D&C 76 describes.'
missionary.why: Understanding the unforgivable sin correctly removes spiritual anxiety from people who have carried guilt far heavier than they needed to. The Atonement is infinitely broader than most people realise. Almost no one who worries about this has committed it.
missionary.invite: Is there a sin or a past moment you have been carrying as though it were unforgivable? Would you like to understand what the Atonement actually covers — and allow yourself to receive the mercy that has always been waiting for you?

questions.question: 9. Does the Bible teach predestination, and what does the restored gospel say about it?
questions.answer:
Predestination is the theological teaching — associated most closely with John Calvin and Reformed Christianity — that God, before the foundation of the world, chose certain individuals for salvation and others for condemnation, entirely apart from any foreseen human choice or merit. The five points of Calvinism spell this out: Total depravity, Unconditional election, Limited atonement, Irresistible grace, Perseverance of saints — the TULIP acronym.

The biblical passages used. The primary texts are Romans 8:29–30 — "For whom he did foreknow, he also did predestinate to be conformed to the image of his Son" Romans 8:29 — and Ephesians 1:4–5 — "According as he hath chosen us in him before the foundation of the world... Having predestinated us unto the adoption of children." Ephesians 1:4–5 These passages genuinely describe God's advance knowledge and purpose in choosing His people.

What Calvinism concludes. Calvin concluded that because God foreknows who will be saved, He must have determined it — and because He determined it, human will has no meaningful role. The elect are saved irresistibly. The reprobate are condemned irreversibly. This appears to make God the author of damnation.

The Latter-day Saint distinction — foreordination, not predestination. The restored gospel teaches foreordination — not predestination. God, being omniscient and existing outside of linear time, knows the choices every person will make throughout their life. Because He knows, He calls certain individuals to specific missions before they are born — as He called Jeremiah Jeremiah 1:5 and Abraham Abraham 3:22–23 and reserved others for the last days. But this foreknowledge does not remove agency. God does not cause the choices He foreknows. He simply knows them.

Agency is the bedrock. The entire plan of salvation rests on the premise that human agency is real. Without genuine choice, there is no genuine faith, no genuine repentance, no genuine discipleship. A God who predetermines who is saved and who is damned does not need a plan of salvation — He needs only a decree. The restored gospel teaches that every person born into mortality has the capacity to choose, and that God's desire is that all should be saved. Moses 1:39 The elect are those who choose Him. Foreordination calls them to that choice. It does not make it for them.

missionary.simple: The Bible teaches God's foreknowledge — that He knew before the foundation of the world who would come to Him. Calvin concluded this means God pre-determined who is saved. The restored gospel teaches foreordination, not predestination — God knows the choices people will make, but that knowledge does not remove their agency or make the choice for them. Agency is the bedrock of the plan of salvation.
missionary.explain: 'Foreknowledge is not causation. A God who knows what you will choose has not taken away your choice — He simply exists outside of time in a way that makes all moments equally present to Him. The difference between foreordination and predestination is the difference between a God who knows your future and a God who forced it. One honours agency. The other eliminates it.'
missionary.why: The distinction between foreordination and predestination matters because it determines who God is. A God who predestines some to damnation is not the God who weeps at the death of Lazarus, heals the ten lepers, or whose work and glory is the immortality and eternal life of man. The restored gospel reveals a God whose love for every soul is genuine and whose invitation is universal.
missionary.invite: Would you like to understand the pre-mortal life as the restored gospel teaches it — and see how foreordination, agency, and God's plan for you work together in a way that predestination never could?

questions.question: 300. At what age can I serve a full-time mission?
questions.answer:
Young men may serve beginning at age 18. Young women may begin serving at age 19. Senior couples may serve at any age after retirement or when family circumstances allow. There is no upper age limit for senior missionaries — the Church welcomes mature couples whose life experience makes them powerful instruments in the Lord's hands.

The age for young men was lowered from 19 to 18 in October 2012 by President Thomas S. Monson. This allows young men to serve before or immediately after their first year of college or university, reducing disruption to their education and making it easier to serve at the right time in their lives.

Young women serve for 18 months. Young men serve for 24 months. Senior missionaries serve for terms ranging from 6 to 23 months depending on the type of call and their circumstances. Whatever the length, a mission is one of the most formative experiences of a person's life — and the Lord uses every missionary regardless of age or background.

missionary.simple: Young men may serve beginning at age 18. Young women at age 19. Senior couples may serve at any stage of life. The call is open to anyone who is worthy and willing.
missionary.explain: 'Isaiah said here am I, send me. That is the spirit of missionary service — not I am old enough now, but I am willing. Age opens the door. Willingness and worthiness walk through it.'
missionary.why: Every missionary who serves carries that call for the rest of their life. It shapes who they become more than almost any other experience.
missionary.invite: Are you thinking about serving a mission? Would you like to know what you can do right now to prepare yourself spiritually and practically?

questions.question: 301. What are the worthiness requirements to serve a mission?
questions.answer:
To serve a full-time mission, a missionary must be morally clean, be a full tithe payer, keep the Word of Wisdom, and be living in harmony with the commandments of God. They must have a testimony of the restored gospel and a genuine desire to serve — not simply parental or social pressure.

The bishop and stake president interview each prospective missionary. These interviews are private, honest, and designed to help the candidate assess their own readiness. The goal is not to disqualify people — it is to help each person prepare to serve with full confidence and a clean conscience.

If there are past sins to resolve, the repentance process is the path forward. Repentance is real. The Atonement of Jesus Christ is real. Many missionaries who have repented wholeheartedly serve with extraordinary power — because they know from personal experience what the gospel can do. The Lord does not require perfection. He requires a broken heart, a contrite spirit, and a genuine commitment to move forward in righteousness.

Physical and mental health requirements also apply. The Church has broadened its approach to mental health in recent years, and many missionaries with managed conditions serve effectively. The missionary department works with candidates and their doctors to determine what assignments and environments will set each missionary up for success.

missionary.simple: A missionary must be morally clean, keep the Word of Wisdom, be a full tithe payer, have a testimony of the gospel, and be living in harmony with God's commandments.
missionary.explain: 'Paul said if a man purges himself of dishonorable things he will be a vessel fit for the Master's use. The worthiness requirements are not barriers — they are preparation to be the kind of instrument God can work through.'
missionary.why: The Lord does not call perfect people — He calls willing people and then works with them. Worthiness is about direction, not perfection.
missionary.invite: Are there worthiness concerns you want to resolve before serving? Would you be willing to meet with your bishop and take that first step?

questions.question: 302. How do I submit mission papers and get a mission call?
questions.answer:
The process begins with a conversation with your bishop. He will schedule a worthiness interview and guide you through the application process. The application is completed online through the Church's missionary recommendation system and includes a medical questionnaire, dental records, and bishop and stake president interviews.

Once your application is submitted, it goes to the missionary department at Church headquarters in Salt Lake City. The Apostles oversee the assignment of missionaries to their fields of labor. Many members of the Quorum of the Twelve have testified that they feel inspired as they assign missionaries — that specific missionaries are matched to specific areas and peoples by the Spirit of the Lord.

Your mission call arrives by email and includes your assigned mission, the language you will speak, your report date, and the Missionary Training Center you will attend. Opening your call is one of the most memorable moments in a young person's life — often done surrounded by family and friends.

From submission to receiving your call typically takes two to eight weeks. Begin the process early. Medical clearances sometimes require additional time, so do not wait until the last minute.

missionary.simple: Start with a conversation with your bishop. He will guide you through the online application, the medical forms, and the worthiness interviews. From submission to receiving your call is typically two to eight weeks.
missionary.explain: 'Nephi said I will go and do. He did not wait for perfect conditions. Start the process now. Medical clearances take time. The sooner you begin, the sooner you will be in the field.'
missionary.why: Every week you spend preparing is a week invested in being the most effective instrument God can use from the moment you arrive.
missionary.invite: Have you spoken with your bishop about serving a mission? Would you be willing to make that appointment this week?

questions.question: 303. What happens at the Missionary Training Center (MTC)?
questions.answer:
The Missionary Training Center is where missionaries go to prepare before entering their assigned field. The main MTC is in Provo, Utah, but there are MTCs in dozens of countries worldwide — including Ghana, Nigeria, Brazil, Mexico, and the Philippines. Missionaries usually attend the MTC closest to their home country.

At the MTC, missionaries study the gospel, learn how to teach the missionary discussions, practice companionship unity, and — if assigned to a foreign language area — begin intensive language study. Language missionaries typically spend eight to twelve weeks at the MTC. English-speaking missionaries usually spend two to three weeks.

Life at the MTC is structured and focused. Missionaries wake early, study scripture, attend classes, practice teaching with companions, and attend devotionals. The spirit in those devotionals is often described by returned missionaries as some of the most powerful spiritual experiences of their lives.

The MTC is not just training — it is a spiritual furnace. Missionaries who arrive uncertain often leave with a burning testimony. The combination of scripture study, prayer, fasting, and unified purpose accelerates spiritual growth in ways that ordinary life rarely replicates.

missionary.simple: The MTC is where missionaries study the gospel, learn the lessons, begin language study if needed, and spiritually prepare before entering their assigned field. Most attend two to twelve weeks.
missionary.explain: 'Christ said I send you as lambs among wolves. The MTC is where lambs learn to move with confidence and purpose. Missionaries who arrive prepared use the MTC to build on what they know. Those who arrive unprepared spend it catching up.'
missionary.why: The spiritual intensity of the MTC accelerates growth in ways ordinary life rarely replicates. Many missionaries describe it as the most powerful weeks of their spiritual lives.
missionary.invite: Are you preparing for the MTC right now? Would you like to know the single most important thing you can do to be ready when you arrive?

questions.question: 304. What should I do now to prepare spiritually for a mission?
questions.answer:
Read the Book of Mormon — all of it, before you go. Not as a school assignment but as a personal conversation with God. Moroni 10:4-5 promises that God will reveal the truth of it to any sincere seeker. Before you ask anyone else to pray about the Book of Mormon, know for yourself that it is true. Your testimony of that book will be the foundation of every lesson you teach.

Develop a consistent prayer life. Learn to speak to God honestly, specifically, and with real intent. Missionaries who do not know how to pray when they arrive struggle to teach others to pray. Practice now. Pray morning and evening. Pray about specific things. Record answers when they come.

Study Preach My Gospel — the missionary handbook. Read it cover to cover. Know the five lessons well enough to teach them without notes. Know the key scriptures. The missionaries who hit the ground running are the ones who arrive already knowing the material.

Live the gospel fully — not as a performance for others but as genuine discipleship. Keep the Word of Wisdom perfectly. Pay a full tithe. Keep the Sabbath day holy. Attend the temple as often as you can. These habits formed before your mission will sustain you through it. Missionaries who struggle in the field often trace their struggles to habits they failed to establish at home.

missionary.simple: Read the Book of Mormon cover to cover. Develop a consistent daily prayer habit. Study Preach My Gospel fully. Live the gospel completely — not as a performance, but as genuine discipleship.
missionary.explain: 'Paul said scripture equips the man of God for every good work. A missionary who arrives already knowing the Book of Mormon and the five lessons can focus on people from day one. A missionary who arrives unprepared has to learn what they should have already known.'
missionary.why: The habits you build before your mission will sustain you through it. The ones you fail to build will be the source of your greatest struggles.
missionary.invite: Will you commit today to reading the Book of Mormon in full before you enter the MTC — and to praying about it as you go?

questions.question: 305. What should I do to prepare physically and practically for a mission?
questions.answer:
Get your health in order before you submit papers. See a dentist. Handle any medical issues that might slow the process. The missionary department will require full dental records and a medical history — address any outstanding issues early so they do not delay your call.

Begin building physical endurance. Missionaries walk. In many areas they walk several miles a day in heat, rain, and difficult terrain. Start walking or exercising regularly now. Missionaries who are physically fit handle the demands of the work far better than those who are not.

Learn basic life skills. Cook simple meals. Do your own laundry. Manage a simple budget. Missionaries who have never lived independently often spend the first weeks of their mission just surviving. The more capable you are at basic adult living, the more energy you can direct toward the work.

Start saving money. Missionaries and their families are expected to contribute to the cost of the mission. Begin putting aside whatever you can. And learn to be comfortable with people you have never met. Practice starting conversations with strangers. Get comfortable with silence, with rejection, and with persistence. The social skills you build before your mission will be among your most valuable tools in the field.

The Lord promised: "If ye are prepared ye shall not fear."D&C 38:30 Physical and practical preparation is not unspiritual. A missionary who arrives prepared can give everything to the Lord from the first day.

missionary.simple: Get a full medical and dental check-up now. Build physical endurance through daily walking. Learn to cook basic meals and manage your own finances. Address everything before you submit papers.
missionary.explain: 'God said if you are prepared, you shall not fear. Physical and practical preparation is not unspiritual — it is stewardship. A missionary who is physically fit, financially prepared, and practically capable can direct all their energy toward the work.'
missionary.why: The mission will demand everything from you. Going in strong means you can give everything from the beginning rather than spending the first months just surviving.
missionary.invite: What is the one area of preparation — physical, financial, or practical — that you most need to address right now?

questions.question: 306. What is Preach My Gospel and why does it matter?
questions.answer:
Preach My Gospel is the official guide for full-time missionaries of the Church. It contains the missionary discussions, guidance on how to teach, how to study, how to work with members, how to plan effectively, and how to develop Christlike attributes. Every missionary is expected to know it deeply.

It is not a script. It is a framework. The discussions within it — on the Restoration, the Plan of Salvation, the Gospel of Jesus Christ, the commandments, and the laws and ordinances — are designed to be taught as Spirit-led conversations, not memorized speeches. A missionary who understands the framework can teach flexibly to the needs of any investigator.

Study it before your mission. Read a chapter a week. Teach the lessons to family members or friends. The more familiar you are with the content before you enter the MTC, the more the MTC can build on what you already know rather than starting from scratch.

Beyond the lesson content, Preach My Gospel contains some of the most practical spiritual wisdom you will ever encounter — on faith, diligence, obedience, and the attributes of Christ. Many returned missionaries return to it years after their mission and find it speaks to their lives in entirely new ways. The Lord commanded missionaries to teach as directed by the Spirit. D&C 42:13 Preach My Gospel embodies that principle — the book gives you the doctrine, the Holy Ghost gives you the application.

missionary.simple: Preach My Gospel is the official missionary guide — containing the five lessons, guidance on how to study, how to teach, and how to develop the attributes of Christ. Know it deeply before you go.
missionary.explain: 'Christ stood up in the synagogue and read from the scriptures — He knew the material. A missionary who knows Preach My Gospel well can teach flexibly, spirit-led, to any person's needs. One who does not is always one step behind.'
missionary.why: Preach My Gospel is not a script — it is a framework for spirit-led teaching that has produced the most effective missionaries in the Church's history.
missionary.invite: Do you have a copy of Preach My Gospel? Would you commit to reading one chapter per week until you enter the MTC?

questions.question: 307. Will I be assigned to serve in my home country or abroad?
questions.answer:
You will be assigned wherever the Lord needs you. Missionaries are called to specific missions — which may be in your home country or on another continent entirely. You have no control over where you are sent, and that is precisely the point. The call is not a reward for preference. It is an assignment from God.

Some missionaries serve in countries they never imagined visiting, speaking languages they had never studied, among people whose culture is completely unlike anything they knew growing up. Those are often the missionaries who grow the most — because they cannot rely on familiarity. They have to rely on the Lord.

In your mission papers you may note any languages you already speak, medical or family considerations that are relevant, and any personal circumstances the missionary department should know. The Church takes that information seriously. But the final assignment rests with those called to make it — and Apostles who have participated in the process consistently affirm that those assignments are inspired.

Go willing to serve anywhere. If you receive a call to a place you did not expect, take that as confirmation that the Lord has a specific work for you there. The missionaries who serve with the greatest effectiveness are those who arrive fully committed to their assignment — not wishing they had been sent somewhere else.

The Lord declared to early missionaries: "Go ye and preach my gospel, whether to the north or to the south, to the east or to the west, it mattereth not, for ye cannot go amiss."D&C 80:3 Every direction is the right direction when the Lord sends you.

missionary.simple: You will be sent wherever the Lord needs you. The call is not a reward for preference — it is an assignment from God. Some serve in their home country. Others go to the other side of the world.
missionary.explain: 'Paul saw a man from Macedonia in vision calling him there. He did not choose Macedonia. God sent him there because that is where he was needed. The missionaries who serve most effectively are those who arrive fully committed to wherever God sent them.'
missionary.why: The place God sends you is not random. He has people prepared to hear your specific voice, your specific testimony, in that specific place.
missionary.invite: Are you willing to go wherever the Lord sends you — and trust that He knows exactly where you are needed most?

questions.question: 310. How do I teach Lesson One — The Restoration of the Gospel?
questions.answer:
The Restoration lesson answers one foundational question: How did God restore His church in our day? Begin by helping the investigator see that God has always communicated with His children through prophets — Moses, Isaiah, Peter, Paul. Then help them see that after the apostles died, that authority was lost. The doctrines of the early church were changed. The organization was altered. Men were left with sincere intentions but no divine commission.

The key events to teach: the Great Apostasy — the loss of priesthood authority; Joseph Smith's First Vision — God the Father and Jesus Christ appearing to a young boy and calling him as a prophet; the translation of the Book of Mormon; the restoration of the priesthood; and the organization of the Church in 1830.

Key scriptures: Amos 8:11-12 — the famine of hearing the word of the Lord. Acts 3:19-21 — the restitution of all things before Christ's return. James 1:5 — ask God. Matthew 17:11 — Elias shall restore all things. These scriptures show that the restoration was prophesied, not invented.

Invite the investigator to read Joseph Smith's account of the First Vision in Joseph Smith — History 1:15-20. Invite them to pray and ask God if Joseph Smith was truly called as a prophet. The Holy Ghost is the real teacher. Your job is to open the door for that witness to come.

The anchor scripture is Joseph Smith's own account: "I saw two Personages, whose brightness and glory defy all description, standing above me in the air."Joseph Smith—History 1:17 That account, read by an investigator who then prays sincerely, is the most powerful teaching tool you have. The Holy Ghost does the rest.

missionary.simple: Lesson One answers how God restored His church in our day — through the apostasy, the First Vision, Joseph Smith, the Book of Mormon, and the restoration of priesthood authority.
missionary.explain: 'Start by helping the investigator see that God has always spoken through prophets. Then show that those prophets were killed or ignored, the church fell away, and God promised to restore everything. Then show how He kept that promise.'
missionary.why: The Restoration lesson is the foundation of everything else. If the investigator accepts the First Vision, every other lesson builds naturally on it.
missionary.invite: Would you like to practice teaching Lesson One with a family member or friend so that when you are in the field you can teach it naturally, from the heart?

questions.question: 311. How do I teach Lesson Two — The Plan of Salvation?
questions.answer:
The Plan of Salvation answers questions every human being eventually asks: Where did I come from? Why am I here? Where am I going? These are not just religious questions — they are human ones. And the restored gospel answers all three with clarity that no other church on earth can match.

Where did I come from? Before this earth, we lived with God as His spirit children. We knew Him. We loved Him. We chose to come to earth to receive a body and to grow by experience. (Jeremiah 1:5 — "Before I formed thee in the belly I knew thee." Job 38:7 — the sons of God shouted for joy at the creation of this earth.)

Why am I here? To gain a body. To be tested. To learn to choose good over evil by actual experience. To form families. To grow into the kind of person who can live with God eternally. (2 Nephi 2:25 — "Men are that they might have joy.")

Where am I going? After death the spirit goes to the spirit world — a place of continued learning. After resurrection, all will be judged and assigned to one of three degrees of glory — celestial, terrestrial, or telestial — according to their choices and faith. The goal is the celestial kingdom — to live with God and family forever.

Use a simple diagram if it helps. Show the investigator where they are on that map. Then invite them to pray and ask God if this plan is true.

Abraham 3:22–23 grounds the pre-mortal portion of the lesson: God showed Abraham the spirits awaiting birth, and among them were noble and great souls foreordained to serve. Abraham 3:22–23 Help your investigators feel that their life has a purpose that began before birth and extends beyond death. That truth alone changes how a person sees themselves.

missionary.simple: Lesson Two answers where we came from, why we are here, and where we are going — the pre-mortal life, mortality, the atonement, death, the spirit world, resurrection, and judgment.
missionary.explain: 'Draw the simple map on a piece of paper — before earth, earth life, spirit world, resurrection, kingdoms of glory. Every person has wondered about these questions. This lesson answers them all with the clarity no other religion offers.'
missionary.why: The plan of salvation is the most complete and comforting answer to the deepest questions of the human heart. People respond to it because it rings true.
missionary.invite: Will you practice teaching the plan of salvation to someone before you enter the MTC — so it lives in your heart, not just your notes?

questions.question: 312. How do I teach Lesson Three — The Gospel of Jesus Christ?
questions.answer:
The Gospel of Jesus Christ lesson teaches the five principles and ordinances that bring a person to Christ: Faith, Repentance, Baptism, the Gift of the Holy Ghost, and Enduring to the end. This is the core of what you are inviting people to do — not just to believe, but to act.

Faith: Not just belief in God but trust in Jesus Christ — enough to act on what He taught. Faith is always followed by action. Abraham believed, and he moved. Nephi believed, and he built. Faith without works is dead. James 2:17 Invite investigators to act on their beginning faith — to pray, to read, to come to church — and watch their faith grow.

Repentance: Not a punishment but a gift. It means turning away from what separates us from God and turning toward Him. Every person who has ever lived needs this gift. The Atonement makes repentance possible. Without it, sin would be permanent. With it, the most burdened soul can be made completely clean.

Baptism: Not optional. Christ himself was baptized to fulfil all righteousness and to set the example. Matthew 3:15 John 3:5 — except a man be born of water and of the Spirit, he cannot enter the kingdom of God. Invite investigators to baptism early — ideally by the first or second lesson — not as pressure but as direction. Help them see it is the next step, not a distant destination.

The Holy Ghost: After baptism, the gift of the Holy Ghost is given by the laying on of hands. A companion for life — a guide, a comforter, and a witness of truth. Help investigators understand that the feelings they have been experiencing as they study are the Holy Ghost already working with them.

missionary.simple: Lesson Three teaches faith, repentance, baptism, the gift of the Holy Ghost, and enduring to the end — the five principles that bring a person to Christ and into His covenant.
missionary.explain: 'Invite to baptism early — by the first or second lesson if possible. Not as pressure, but as direction. Help them see that baptism is the next step, not a distant destination. A specific date given in faith changes the whole dynamic of the lessons.'
missionary.why: The gospel of Jesus Christ is the path. Lesson Three teaches the path. Every lesson before and after this one exists to help the investigator commit to walk it.
missionary.invite: Do you know the five principles of the gospel of Christ well enough to teach them without notes? Would you commit to knowing them before you enter the MTC?

questions.question: 313. How do I teach Lesson Four — The Commandments?
questions.answer:
The commandments lesson introduces investigators to specific laws of the gospel they will need to live to be baptized and remain in full fellowship. The major commandments taught here are the Law of Chastity, the Word of Wisdom, the Law of Tithing, the Sabbath Day, and general obedience to God.

Present the commandments not as restrictions but as gifts. The Word of Wisdom protects the body. The Law of Chastity protects families and the sacred power of creation. Tithing teaches faith and connects us to God's work. The Sabbath gives rest and spiritual renewal. Every commandment God has given has a reason — and that reason is always love.

Explain clearly what these commandments require. Investigators with coffee, tea, tobacco, or alcohol habits will need to change. Those living with partners outside marriage will need to make different arrangements. These are real changes. It is better to discuss them openly and early than to have them become surprises at the baptismal interview.

Make clear that the Atonement covers the transition. No one is expected to be perfect on day one. What God asks is a willing heart and sincere effort. The Spirit that comes with genuine repentance and obedience is so transforming that many investigators who initially resist a commandment find, within weeks, that they are grateful for it.

missionary.simple: Lesson Four teaches the Law of Chastity, the Word of Wisdom, Tithing, the Sabbath, and obedience to God's commandments — the laws an investigator needs to be living before baptism.
missionary.explain: 'Present the commandments as gifts, not restrictions. The Word of Wisdom protects the body. Chastity protects families. Tithing builds faith. Each one is a Father's loving guidance. Explain what is required clearly — better to discuss it now than at the baptismal interview.'
missionary.why: The commandments are the evidence that the gospel is changing someone. A person who genuinely loves God will want to keep them.
missionary.invite: Is there a specific commandment you are still working to fully live? Would you be willing to talk with your bishop about taking that step before your mission?

questions.question: 314. How do I teach Lesson Five — Laws and Ordinances?
questions.answer:
The fifth lesson covers the sacred ordinances that connect members to God's covenants — baptism, confirmation and the Gift of the Holy Ghost, and an introduction to temple ordinances. It also addresses the principle of consecration and service in the Church.

Help investigators understand that ordinances are not just rituals. They are two-way covenants between a person and God, performed by authorized servants, that bind heaven and earth. When a person is baptized by proper authority, heaven records it. When the Holy Ghost is given by the laying on of hands, a divine gift is conferred — not just a symbol.

Introduce the temple gently. Most investigators will not be ready for a full explanation of temple ordinances at this point. But planting the seed — that there is a sacred place where families are sealed together for eternity, where the highest covenants are made — gives investigators a vision of what lies ahead on the covenant path.

Close with a direct and warm invitation to baptism on a specific date. By this point the investigator has been taught the Restoration, the Plan of Salvation, the Gospel of Christ, the commandments, and the ordinances. They have what they need to choose. Help them identify any remaining concerns, then invite them by name and date. Specificity is an act of faith — for them and for you.

missionary.simple: Lesson Five covers baptism, confirmation, the sacrament, the endowment, and eternal marriage — the sacred ordinances that connect us to God's covenants and lead to eternal life.
missionary.explain: 'Plant the seed of the temple early. Tell investigators in the first lessons that there is a sacred place where families are sealed for eternity — and that you want them to be able to go there. Give them a vision of where the covenant path leads.'
missionary.why: The fulness of the gospel is not just baptism. The ordinances of the temple complete the covenant path and open the door to the highest degree of the celestial kingdom.
missionary.invite: Does the covenant path leading to the temple feel like something you genuinely desire? Would you be willing to make sure you are personally on that path before you invite others to walk it?

questions.question: 315. How do I bear a powerful testimony at the end of a lesson?
questions.answer:
A testimony is not a summary of the lesson. It is a personal witness — what you know, not what you learned. There is a difference between saying "The Church teaches that Joseph Smith was a prophet" and saying "I know that Joseph Smith was a prophet." The first is information. The second is fire.

A missionary testimony should cover the core truths: that God is our Heavenly Father and that He lives; that Jesus Christ is His Son, the Saviour of the world; that the gospel was restored through Joseph Smith; that the Book of Mormon is the word of God; and that there is a living prophet on the earth today. Speak from your own experience with each one.

Keep it short. A testimony that takes ten minutes loses its power. Three to five sentences spoken with conviction carry more weight than a ten-minute speech. Say what you know. Say why you know it. Express your love for the investigator. Invite them to seek their own witness.

Then be quiet. One of the most powerful things a missionary can do after bearing testimony is to sit in silence for a moment. The Holy Ghost works in quiet. Do not rush to fill the silence. Let the Spirit finish what your words began.

missionary.simple: A testimony is not a summary of what you learned — it is a personal declaration of what you know. Say what you know, why you know it, express love for the investigator, and then be quiet.
missionary.explain: 'Peter did not testify by reviewing the lesson. He declared: thou art the Christ, the Son of the living God. Short. Certain. Personal. Bear your testimony the same way — not a speech, but a witness.'
missionary.why: The Holy Ghost confirms a personal testimony in a way that no amount of explanation can replicate. Your witness is what opens the door for the Spirit to do His work.
missionary.invite: Would you practice bearing your testimony right now — out loud, in your own words — and work on making it shorter, more certain, and more personal?

questions.question: 316. How do I invite someone to be baptized for the first time?
questions.answer:
Ask directly, warmly, and specifically. Do not hint. Do not say "What do you think about baptism someday?" Say: "We would like to invite you to be baptized on [date]. Will you prepare to be baptized on that day?"

A specific date does several things. It gives the investigator something concrete to pray about. It creates a clear milestone in their journey. And it signals that you believe they are ready — which is itself an act of faith in them. Many investigators who are not sure they are ready will rise to a specific invitation that treats them as capable of choosing.

If they hesitate, do not back down. Ask what concerns remain. Listen fully. Then address each concern with scripture, testimony, and love. Often the hesitation is not a real objection — it is fear. Fear of what their family will think. Fear that they are not ready. Fear of commitment. Help them see that these fears are normal, that the Atonement covers imperfection, and that the best way to feel ready is to move forward in faith.

If they say no, ask when they think they will be ready. Keep the conversation open. A "not yet" is not a "never." Commit to continuing to meet and study. The Lord will do His part. Do yours.

missionary.simple: Ask directly, warmly, and specifically. Say: We would like to invite you to be baptized on this date. Will you prepare to be baptized on that day? Specificity is an act of faith in them.
missionary.explain: 'A vague invitation gets a vague response. A specific date gives the investigator something concrete to pray about and creates a clear milestone in their journey. It also signals that you believe they are ready — and that belief is itself an act of love.'
missionary.why: The baptismal invitation is the moment when the investigator becomes a potential convert. Everything in the lessons leads to this moment. Do not soften it.
missionary.invite: Have you ever made a direct baptismal invitation to someone? Would you be willing to practice it right now so it feels natural when the moment comes in the field?

questions.question: 320. What does a typical missionary day look like?
questions.answer:
A missionary's day is built on structure. The Church publishes a standard daily schedule, and while mission presidents may adjust it for local conditions, the framework is the same worldwide. It is designed to maximize spiritual power, physical health, and effective work.

The day begins at 6:30 AM. Missionaries exercise for 30 minutes, shower, dress, and are ready for the day by 7:30. Personal study runs from 7:30 to 8:30 — one hour in the scriptures and Preach My Gospel. Companion study runs from 8:30 to 9:00. Language study follows for those in foreign language missions.
