From 9:30 AM until 9:30 PM missionaries are in the field — teaching lessons, meeting with members, visiting investigators, attending baptismal services, and doing the work of the Lord. Dinner is usually at 6:00 PM, often with a member family who hosts the missionaries.

Planning for the next day happens from 9:30 to 10:00 PM. Missionaries are in bed by 10:30 PM. This schedule is not optional. Missionaries who keep it consistently report more effective work and greater spiritual power than those who treat it loosely. On a mission, obedience and productivity are directly connected.

missionary.simple: Wake at 6:30, exercise, personal study from 7:30–8:30, companion study 8:30–9:00, then in the field teaching, visiting, and working until 9:30 PM. In bed by 10:30.
missionary.explain: 'God commanded missionaries to retire to bed early and arise early to do His work. The schedule is not a cage — it is a channel. Missionaries who keep it consistently report more effective work and greater spiritual power than those who treat it loosely.'
missionary.why: The discipline of the daily schedule is itself a form of consecration. How you spend your hours is how you spend your mission.
missionary.invite: Are you living a schedule now that resembles missionary discipline? Would you be willing to start practicing that structure before you enter the MTC?

questions.question: 321. How do I handle rejection as a missionary?
questions.answer:
Rejection is not personal, even when it feels personal. When a door slams in your face, it is not rejection of you — it is rejection of the message. And sometimes it is not even that. It is exhaustion, fear, bad timing, a hard day, or a person who is simply not ready yet. You cannot know what is behind the door. The Lord knows. Your job is to knock.

Christ prepared His disciples for rejection specifically. "Whosoever shall not receive you, nor hear your words, when ye depart out of that house or city, shake off the dust of your feet." Matthew 10:14 He did not say apologize, beg, or collapse. He said move on. The same gospel that one person rejects today, another person — perhaps the very next door — will receive with tears of joy.

Track your reactions honestly. If rejection is making you bitter, bring it to the Lord. If it is making you reluctant to knock, talk to your companion and your mission president. A missionary who stops knocking because of fear has let Satan win the real battle. The battle is never at the door. It is in your own heart.

Keep a record of moments of grace — the investigator who invited you in, the family who listened for two hours, the person who was baptized after others gave up on them. When rejection weighs heavy, return to those records. The Lord is gathering His people. Every knock, accepted or not, is part of that gathering.

missionary.simple: Rejection is not personal. When a door closes in your face, it is not rejection of you — it is the response of a person who is not yet ready. Your job is to knock on the next door.
missionary.explain: 'Christ said where they do not receive you, shake the dust from your feet and move on. He did not say collapse, beg, or become bitter. The same gospel rejected at one door will be received with tears of joy at the next.'
missionary.why: Rejection is preparation, not failure. Every closed door sharpens your testimony and strengthens your dependence on God rather than on your own persuasion.
missionary.invite: How do you currently handle rejection in your daily life? Would you be willing to practice moving forward with faith instead of taking it personally?

questions.question: 322. How do I build a strong relationship with my missionary companion?
questions.answer:
Your companion is not chosen by you. They are assigned by the Lord through your mission president. That means you have no grounds to wish for a different companion. You have every reason to treat the one you have as a divine appointment. Paul wrote, "I can do all things through Christ which strengtheneth me." Philippians 4:13 That includes serving with a difficult companion.

Pray with your companion every day — morning and night. Not as a ritual but as a genuine conversation with God about your work together. Companions who pray together in faith build a spiritual bond that makes the assignment feel like partnership rather than obligation.

Study together every day. Use companion study time to actually discuss the scriptures, prepare lessons, and share what you are learning. Companions who study together build shared language, shared testimony, and shared purpose.

Talk to each other. Not just about the work — about how you are doing. About what is hard. About what is meaningful. Many companionship problems are simply communication problems. Most issues that would take ten minutes to address directly become major conflicts when left to simmer.

Defend your companion to others. Never speak negatively about them to members, investigators, or other missionaries. The unity you protect outwardly will eventually create genuine unity inwardly.

missionary.simple: Your companion is assigned by the Lord through your mission president. Treat them as a divine appointment — pray with them daily, study with them honestly, and communicate before small problems become large ones.
missionary.explain: 'Solomon said two are better than one — if one falls, the other lifts him up. Your companion is there to be that for you. Companions who pray and study together build a spiritual bond that makes the assignment feel like partnership rather than obligation.'
missionary.why: How you serve with your companion will determine more than almost anything else how effective your mission is. Invest in that relationship from day one.
missionary.invite: Are there areas of your character — patience, communication, flexibility — that you know you need to develop before you serve with a companion for 24 hours a day?

questions.question: 323. How do I deal with homesickness on my mission?
questions.answer:
Homesickness is normal. It is not weakness. It is love — love for your family, your friends, the familiar. That love is good. But on a mission, it must be directed properly. You are there because you love something even more — the souls you have been sent to find.

Write home. The Church allows weekly contact with family. Use that time fully. Pour out what is on your heart. Read what your family sends back. Then close the device and return to the work. The contact home is the valve. Open it once a week, then close it and go serve.

Throw yourself into the work. Missionaries who stay busy have the least time for homesickness. When you are knocking doors, teaching lessons, studying, and serving with full energy, the ache is quieter. Idleness amplifies it. Work silences it.

Build a mission family. Your companion, your zone leaders, your district — these people will become some of the closest friends of your life. Let them in. Be vulnerable. Support each other. The bond formed between missionaries is unlike any other because it is forged in shared consecration.

Pray specifically about your homesickness. Tell God exactly what you miss and what it feels like. He left His home too — and He came here for love. He understands the ache of separation. And He will fill the void with the peace that passes all understanding. Philippians 4:7

missionary.simple: Homesickness is love — love for your family and what is familiar. Direct that love outward toward the souls you were sent to find. And write home every week, fully, then close the device and go serve.
missionary.explain: 'Paul said he had learned in whatsoever state he was to be content. Contentment is a learned skill. Throw yourself into the work. Missionaries who stay busy have the least time for homesickness. Idleness amplifies it. Work silences it.'
missionary.why: God sent you there for a reason. Every week you are on your mission, someone in your area needs to hear your specific testimony. That purpose is greater than the ache.
missionary.invite: Is there a specific relationship or situation at home that you fear will pull you emotionally off your mission? Would you be willing to talk through that with your bishop before you leave?

questions.question: 324. How do I maintain my testimony during a hard mission?
questions.answer:
Hard missions produce the strongest missionaries. The mission that breaks you down and then shows you that the Lord can rebuild you is the one that changes you permanently. Easy missions produce testimonies of comfort. Hard missions produce testimonies of power.

Do not skip personal study when you are discouraged. That is precisely when you need it most. Open the Book of Mormon to wherever you are and read until something speaks to you. It always does. The scriptures are not a general comfort — they are a personal letter from God to you in your moment of need.

Serve your companion. When your own testimony feels fragile, carry someone else's burden. Help a struggling companion. Visit a less-active member. Paul wrote, "Bear ye one another's burdens, and so fulfil the law of Christ." Galatians 6:2 When you give strength to another, you discover you had more than you thought.

Seek your mission president. That is what he is there for. He carries authority and the Spirit in his office. A missionary who is struggling and does not seek the mission president is refusing help that God has placed directly in their path.

And remember: doubt is not the opposite of faith. Acting in faith when you doubt — continuing to pray, to study, to work, to testify — is among the highest expressions of faith that exist. Many of the strongest returned missionaries will tell you their testimony was forged precisely in the season when they were not sure they still had one.

missionary.simple: Hard missions produce the strongest missionaries. Do not skip personal study when you are discouraged — that is precisely when you need it most. Open the Book of Mormon and read until something speaks to you. It always does.
missionary.explain: 'God told Paul His strength is made perfect in weakness. Moroni said God gives weaknesses so that they become strengths through humility. The mission that breaks you down and shows you that God can rebuild you is the one that changes you permanently.'
missionary.why: Doubt during a mission is not the end of your testimony — it is the beginning of a deeper one. Acting in faith when you are not certain is the highest expression of faith there is.
missionary.invite: What is the area of your faith that feels most fragile right now? Would you be willing to address it directly — through scripture, prayer, and honest conversation — before you leave?

questions.question: 325. How do I work effectively with members in the mission field?
questions.answer:
Members are the most powerful resource a missionary has — and the most underused. A referral from a trusted friend is worth ten cold contacts at the door. A member sitting in on a lesson gives the investigator a living witness — not just missionaries who are supposed to be there, but a neighbor who chose this faith freely.

Treat members as partners, not as an audience. When you visit a member family, ask who they know who might be ready to hear the gospel. Ask them to pray for specific people. Ask them to invite those people to a meal where you will also be present. That is missionary work done the way the Lord intended it.

Be punctual, respectful, and genuinely interested in the family you are visiting — not a missionary checking a box on a daily planner. The relationship you build with a member family will outlast your mission. Some of them will still be writing to you twenty years later.

Attend ward or branch activities when invited. Show up for sacrament meeting every week fully engaged. Members watch how missionaries participate. A missionary who sits in the back distracted teaches members nothing. A missionary who sings every hymn, sustains every leader, and listens with full attention is a living lesson in discipleship.

missionary.simple: Members are your most powerful resource. A referral from a trusted neighbor is worth ten cold door contacts. Treat members as partners, ask who they know, and invite them to your lessons.
missionary.explain: 'God said every man is warned who has been warned — meaning members who know the gospel have a responsibility to share it. Your job is to help them do that naturally, without pressure, by building real relationships with the families you visit.'
missionary.why: The most effective missionary work happens when a friend invites a friend. Members open doors that missionaries alone never could.
missionary.invite: Are you currently building relationships with non-member friends and neighbors that could become referrals when you are in the field — or when you return?

questions.question: 326. What should I expect spiritually on my mission?
questions.answer:
Expect experiences you cannot explain to anyone who was not there. Missionaries consistently describe moments — at the door, in a lesson, in personal study, in prayer — that fall outside the ordinary. A prompting to turn down a street you had not planned to visit. A name that comes to mind during prayer who turns out to be a golden investigator. A prayer answered so specifically that doubt becomes difficult. These are not coincidences. They are the Lord at work.

Also expect opposition. The adversary is real and he opposes this work directly. Missionaries who are most effective often face the strongest resistance — difficult companions, unproductive areas, personal struggles, discouragement that seems to have no natural cause. This is not evidence that God has abandoned you. It is evidence that you are in the right battle.

Expect your understanding of the scriptures to grow dramatically. Missionaries who study one to two hours every day for two years read the standard works multiple times and teach the same truths hundreds of times. Each repetition reveals something new. The missionaries who study most diligently return home with a depth of gospel knowledge that no classroom can replicate.

Expect to be changed — not just informed. The discipline, the faith, the obedience, the compassion, the love for people you had never met — these reshape a person at the level of character. That is the real purpose of a mission. Not just to baptize, but to become.

Alma described the joy of missionary work in terms every faithful missionary will recognise: “Now have we not reason to rejoice? Yea, I say unto you, there is not one soul among all our brethren who has perished; and we have brought thousands of souls to repentance.”Alma 26:4 And then: “I do not boast in my own strength… but behold, my joy is full, yea, my heart is brim with joy.”Alma 26:11–12 That joy is what awaits a faithful missionary. It is unlike anything else on earth.

missionary.simple: Expect experiences you cannot fully explain. Expect opposition. Expect your understanding of scripture to deepen dramatically. And expect to be fundamentally changed — not just informed.
missionary.explain: 'Jeremiah said God's word was like a burning fire shut up in his bones — he could not hold it back. That fire is what a mission produces. The discipline, the faith, the love for strangers — these reshape a person at the level of character.'
missionary.why: The real purpose of a mission is not only to baptize — it is to become. The person who returns from a faithful mission is not the same person who left. That transformation is one of God's greatest gifts.
missionary.invite: Are you going on a mission to check a box or to become someone? Would you be willing to make that distinction clear in your own heart before you submit your papers?
